FELONIES . " Offenses are either felonies or misdemeanors .
Such offensesas are punishable with death or confinement in
the penitentiaryare felonies. All other offenses, whether at
common law or made so by statute, are misdemeanors .
" 1. No crime shall be punished with death unless it be
directedby the statute.
" 2. A common law offense, for which punishment is pre-
scribedby statute, shallbe punished only in the mode so pre-
scribed .
" 3. The commission of a felony shall not stay or merge
any civilremedy of the party aggrieved against the felon.
" 4. When an indictment is dismissed, the reasons therefor
shallbe stated and entered of record." Ky . Stats. (1915),
Sec . 1127 .
Felony. " A felony is an offenseof which the punishment
isdeath, or confinement in the penitentiary." Crim . Code ,
Sec . 6 .
The term felonyused in the definitionof the crime of bur-
glaryembracesany crime which was a felonyat common law,
whetheritisnow punishedas a felonyor not,and itwas not
errorfor the courtto charge the jury that breakinginto a
dwellingin the nighttime with intentto stealany property
of another therefrom was burglary. Williams v. Com ., 10 Ky .
Opin. 245 .
" In all felonies, accessoriesbeforethe factshallbe liable
tothe same punishmentas principals, and may be prosecuted
jointlywith principals, or severally, though the principalsbe

FELONIOUS FELONIOUSLY1348
not taken or tried, unlessotherwiseprovidedin thischapter."
Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec. 1128.
FELONIOUS .- The common definitionof this word is,
" Proceeding from an evilheart or purpose; done with a
deliberatepurposeto commit a crime." Hocker v. Com ., 70
S. W. 293, 24 R. 936 .
FELONIOUSLY .- Indictmentfor statutoryfelony. An in-
dictmentcharginga statutorycrime, definedby the statute
itself, in thelanguageof thestatute, issufficientwithoutusing
the words " feloniously," or " with feloniousintent." Hower-
ton v .Com ., 129 Ky .482, 112 S. W. 606,33 R. 1008.
An indictmentfor a felonyenacted by statuteneed not allege
that the acts complained of were done " feloniously," unless
the statuterequiresthat the act should be done " feloniously"
in orderto constitutethe crime. Higgins v. Com ., 94 Ky . 54,
14 R. 729 .
An indictment charging defendant, in the language of Sec .
1170,Ky . Stats., with the offenseof " willfully, maliciously, and
unlawfully burning a dwelling house occupied as a residence,"
isgood, though the word " feloniously" is not used. Collierv .
Com ., 62 S. W. 4,22 R. 1929.
IndictmentFor Common Law Felony. An indictmentfor
a common law felony should charge that the act was done
" feloniously," or " with a feloniousintent." The use of no
other words willsupply the omissionof such an allegation.
Kaelin v . Com ., 84 Ky . 354, 8 R. 293 ; Hall v . Com ., 15 R. 856.
Though it was held in Jane v . Com ., 3 Met . 18, that itwas
not necessaryto the validityof an indictment for a felony that
the word " feloniously" shouldbe used in chargingthe intent
with which the offensewas committed , the latercaseshold that
in common law feloniesthat word should be used in the indict-
ment .
Indictment For Felony. The word " feloniously" must be
used in every indictmentfor a felony, whether the offenseis
a statutoryor common law offense. It is a term of art pecu-
liarly adapted to define one of the constituentelements of
a felony. Hocker v. Com .,111 S. W. 676, 33 R. 944. But see
Howerton v . Com ., supra.

FELONIOUSLY FELONIOUSLY1349
Indictment for Murder . In an indictment for murder , the
useof the word " feloniously" is necessary because it defines
oneof the constituentelements which go to make up the crime
of murder . There is no other word in the English language
which is a perfect synonym for it that can be used to supply
itsplace. Hocker v . Com ., 111 S. W. 676, 33 R. 944.
In an indictment for murder the omission of the word
" feloniously" renders it fatallydefective. Hoskins v . Com .,
145 Ky .580 ,140 S. W. 1040 .
IndictmentforRape . An indictmentfor rape which omits
the word " feloniously" is fatallydefective. Reed v . Com .,
76 S. W. 838 , 25 R. 1029 .
Indictment for Chicken Stealing. In an indictment for
stealingchickens it is necessary to allege that the defendant
feloniouslytook the chickens. Allen v. Com ., 144 Ky . 222,
137 S. W. 1060 .
In Instructions . In a trial for murder the court instructed
that" the word felonious, as used in these instructions, means
a deliberateor a well-formed intenton the part of the defendant
to do an act known by him to be wrong ." Held , that this
definitionis sufficientwhen considered in connection with the
chargein the indictment, and couldnot have been prejudicial
todefendant. Hocker v . Com ., 70 S. W. 291, 24 R. 936.
It is not reversibleerror to omit the word " feloniously" in
instructionin a murder trial. Stout v . Com ., 123 Ky . 184,
94 S. W. 15, 29 R. 627; O'Day v. Com ., 99 S. W. 937, 30 R.
848; Carson v . Com ., 149 Ky . 294, 148 S. W. 30 ; Bunnell v .
Com .,30 S. W. 604, 17 R. 106.
Defendant was not prejudicedby the omissionof "felon-
iously" and " maliciously" from the firstinstruction, where
thisidea was carefully expressed in all other instructions.
Ward v.Com .,91 S.W. 700,29 R. 62.
On a trialfor murder , the court should instructthat the
word " feloniously" means proceedingfrom an evilheart or
purpose. Ewing v.Com .,129 Ky .237,111 S.W. 352,33 R. 749.
Itwas not error for the courttodefine" willful," " willfully,"
" malice," and " malice aforethought," and failto define" fel-
oniously." Hutsellv . Com .,75 S. W. 225,25 R. 262.

FELONIOUSLY FELONIOUSLY1350
An instructionon manslaughterisnot bad for failingto use
the word " feloniously." Cook v. Com ., 72 S. W. 283, 24 R.
1731 .
Where defendantisindictedfor "willfully,feloniously, and
with malice aforethought" killinganother, and the evidence
for the stateshows thatdefendantlay in wait for his victim,
and the only defenseis self-defense, defendant'ssubstantial
rightsarenot prejudicedby the failureof the courtto use the
word " feloniously" in itscharge describingthe offense. Brooks
v . Com ., 16 R. 356.
The Court ofAppeals has not held that itiserrorto omit the
word " feloniously" from an instructionupon the charge of
murder , but on the contrary has frequentlyheld that the omis-
sion of the word from an instruction is not error. Carson v .
Com ., 149 Ky . 294, 148 S. W. 30.
In a trialfor murder it was proper to instructthat the
word " feloniously" means proceeding from an evil heart or
purpose, done with the deliberateintentionof committing a
crime. Carnesv.Com .,146Ky .425,142S.W. 723.
On trialfor murder the omission of the word " feloniously"
from the instructionsdefiningmurder and manslaughter is not
reversibleerror. Bunnell v . Com ., 30 S. W. 604, 17 R. 106.
In an indictment for stealingchickens it is necessary to
allegethat the defendant feloniouslytook the chickens, but it

The failureto definethe terms " feloniously" and " malice
aforethought" isnotprejudicialerror. Collierv.Com .,160 Ky .
338, 169 S. W. 740.
The failureto use the word " feloniously" in the instruction,
although it isused in the statuteunder which the prosecution

FEME SOLE . A court of equity has no jurisdictionto
declarea wife a feme soleas to her property, for the solepur-
poseof enabling her to deprive her husband of the use of her

astoher property,and the husband ismade a partydefendant,

52,Art . 2, Sec. 6, relievedthem of the necessityof applying

A ferry is a highway across a stream by boat instead of a

In CivilCases . " In Aikman v. South,et al, 97 S. W. 4 ,29

were the same used in the instant case, 'filedaway .' In the
opinion it is said: 'Counsel for appelleecontend that there is
no such order known to the code as " filingaway " a suit, and
that consequently such an order was ineffectualto dispose of
the case. The language used by differentcourts in different
sectionsof the State to express the same thing is not always
uniform. For example, some use the more correctexpression
"dismissed," which is undeniablya finalorder, which after
the adjournment of the term at which it is rendered divests
the court of furtherjurisdictionin the action, except after
notice, and upon a motion for a new trialor in an action for a
new trialbrought pursuant to specificsectionsof the code.
Some courts, however, use the expression"discontinued,"
others, " strickenfrom the docket," while othersyet use the
one used in this case, " filedaway." They all mean sub-
stantiallythe same thing; that is, that the action has been
stricken from the docket for want of prosecution, or upon
the motion of the plaintiff, or upon the motion of the court.
It is a finalorder, however worded , when it may be gathered
from the language of it that the court intended to dismiss the
actionand the partiesfrom further considerationof the court.
The partiesmay thereafterassume that they need not look
further to the records of the court to see what steps might
be taken in the action, as they were thus notifiedthat the
courtwould not take any furtherstepsin it. The fairintent
of the order was to dismiss the action and the partieshence-
forth and without pay . " Phillipsv . Arnett, Admr ., 164 Ky .
426 , 175 S. W. 660 .
The words " filedaway ," " dismissed," " strickenfrom the
docket," " discontinued," and so on, mean substantiallythe
same thing. An ordercontainingsuch words is a finalorder,
however worded , and the fair intent of such an order is to
dismiss the action. Aikman v . South, 97 S. W. 4, 29 R. 1201.
An order that a case be filedaway is a finalorder of dis-
missal. Aikman v. South, 97 S. W. 40, 29 R. 1201 .

FILUM AQUAE .- A thread of water, used generally to
denote the middle line of a stream . Cochran's Law Lexicon .
Thread of the stream. Ford v . Commonwealth , 156 Ky .428,
160 S. W. 1080 .
" In respectto streams and riverswhich are not navigable
-that is, in which the tide does not ebb and flow- the rule
seems to be universalthat describingland as running to the
stream or the bank , and by it or along the stream or the bank ,

Confirming Report of Master Commissioner . An order

ofthe Code . A judgment which not only dismissedthe petition

Divorce. By the provisionof Sec.950,Ky . Stats.,no appeal

Divorce a mensa et thoro, and Allowance . A judgment

Election Held Void . A contested election board has

itto the effectthat the Act under which the election was held

a responseto a ruleto pay money intocourtand making the

FINAL IN THE ACTION .- Civil Code , Sec. 177, provides
that a defendant against whom an order of arrest has been
obtained may apply for a vacation of the order, and that the
decisionof the motion shallbe finalin the action. Held , the

language" the decision of the motion shall be final in the

FINAL JUDGMENT AGAINST HIM .– Under an indemnity

rendered against another for a tort committed by him , the
insurednot being a party to the action, but having paid the
judgment. Rock SpringsDistillingCo. v. Employers' Indem-
nityCo., 160 Ky . 317, 169 S. W. 730.
FINAL PASSAGE.- Where a bill passed by one house of
theGeneral Assembly , afterbeing amended by the other house,
isreturned to the house by which it was originallypassed, its
passageby thathouseas amended isits" finalpassage" within
the meaning of Sec. 46 of the Constitution, and it must , in
order to become a law, there receivethe vote prescribed by
thatsection,and thatvotemust be takenby yeasand nays and
entered on the journal. Norman v . Ky . Board Mgrs .

FINAL SUBMISSION .- Under Sec. 371, Civil Code , there
ean be no finalsubmission of a case to the jury until all the

and papers pertainingto the casehave been actuallydeliveredto

Where a motion for a peremptory instructionhas been sus-
tained, a verdictwritten out and the foreman of the jury di-
rectedto sign it this will constitutea finalsubmission of the

FINAL TERMINATION1376 FINANCIALLY GOOD
After the courthas sustaineda motion by defendant for
a peremptory instructionto the jury, but before such an
instructionhas been given, the plaintiffhas a right to dismiss
his action without prejudice, as there has been at that time no
" finalsubmission" of the case to the jury within the meaning
of Subsec . 1 of Sec . 371 of the Civil Code . Vertrees v . N. N.
& M. V. Co., 95 Ky . 314, 15 R. 680 .
FINAL TERMINATION .- There is no final termination of
a case in the Court of Appeals until the time for filinga
petitionfor rehearinghas expired, or until a petitionhas been
filedand overruled. Cline v . Wrightson, 7 R. 215.
FINAL TRIAL OR HEARING .- Motion to remove a suit
from the State to the United States Circuit Court must be
made before " finaltrialor hearing" in the State Court . The
words " finaltrialor hearing" in the Act of 1867 mean the
same as the words " trialor finalhearing" in the Act of
Congress,1866. Hall v .Rickets, 9 Bush ,369.
FINALLY . A provisionin a citychartergiving the City
Court the right to hear, try, and determine " finally" all
prosecutionsfor violationsof city ordinances does not pro-
hibit an appeal to the Circuit Court, but simply means that
the City Court shallproceed to judgment on the matter, and
not stop at a preliminary investigation. Payne v. Com .,
14 R. 302 .
FINANCIAL CIRCUMSTANCES .- In a suit to recover
forpersonalinjury, the financialcircumstancesof eitherparty
isnot a matter for consideration,and itwas not error to refuse
to admit evidence of the financialabilityof the injured party
to endure the loss. Andonique v . Carmen , 162 Ky . 154, 172
S. W. 112 .
FINANCIALLY GOOD . Effect of belief that person was
financially" good," as estoppel. Advance Thresher Co. v.
Fishback,157 Ky .427,163 S.W. 228.

FINDING FINDING OF THE1377
FINDING . Acts 1906, page 115, Chap . 22, Art . 3, Sec. 1,
makes it the duty of an owner or claimant of land to pay
all the taxes which have been or should have been assessed
againsthim as of the years 1901-1905, inclusive, and provides
thatfailureto listfor assessment or failureto pay the taxes
charged for any three of the years shallbe cause for forfeiture
to the Commonwealth of his title, which cause of forfeiture
shallbe extinguished if the owner shallcause the land to be
assessedfor taxation before March 1, 1907. Sec. 2 provides
that the ascertainment of the amount of taxes unpaid and
assessmentsrequiredby the precedingsectionshallbe made by
the County Court on the applicationof the owner ; that either
thepetitioneror the Commonwealth may appeal to the Circuit
Court, and that the finding of the Circuit Court shall be
conclusiveand not subjectto appeal. Ky . Stats., Sec. 950,
allowsappealsto the Court of Appeals where the titleto land
isinvolvedor the amount in controversyis not lessthan $200,
and from all other final orders and judgments . Held , the
Court of Appeals had jurisdictionof an appeal from a judg-
ment of the CircuitCourt, on appealfrom the County Court,
refusingto listlands on petitionunder Sec. 2 , in that the
petition was not sufficientunder the statute; the word
" finding" relatingto the fixingof the value of the property
assessed alone. Eastern Ky . Coal Lands Corporation v .
Commonwealth , 127 Ky . 667, 106 S. W. 260, 108 S. W. 1138,
32 R. 129 , 33 R. 49 .
FINDING OF THE COURT .- The finding of the court
where the law and the facts are submitted to him will be
treatedas the verdictof a properly instructedjury where there
isno separationof his findingsof law and fact. Cincinnati
Grain Co. v .L. & N. R. Co., 146 Ky .237,142 S. W. 374.
The Court of Appeals ordinarilyaccords great weight to the
findingof the chancelloron the facts. Caudill v . Bays , 89
S. W. 114, 28 R. 182 ; Bourne v.Salin,89 S. W. 673,28 R. 555 ;
Moore v . Moore , 98 S. W. 1027, 30 R. 383 ; Adair v. Adair ,
104 S. W. 365, 31 R. 956.

FINDING OF THE 1378 FINES
It is a rule of long standing in the Court of Appeals that
where both the law and factsare submitted to the trialjudge
without the interventionof a jury, his findingof fact will be
treatedas theverdictof a properlyinstructedjury. Excelsior
Coal Mining Co. v . Gatliff, 69 S. W. 798, 24 R. 682 .
The law and facts having been submitted to the court, his
specialfindings of fact must be treated as the verdict of a
properly instructedjury, and can not be set aside unless
clearlyand palpably against the evidence. Board of Trustees
v .Morris, 71 S. W. 654, 24 R. 1420.
The rule that the chancellor'sjudgment on questionsof
factwill be regarded as favorably as the verdictof a properly
instructedjury appliesonlyinordinaryactions;in an equitable
action the court will, on appeal, determine the weight of the
evidence. Wood v.Howk ,79 S. W. 1184,25 R. 2109.
While in a purely ordinary or common law action the rule
is that on appeal the judgment of the chancellorwill be as
favorably regarded as would be the verdict of a properly
instructedjury, the rule is differentin equitableactions,
which upon appeal will be determined by the weight of the
evidence. List'sExtx .v. List,82 S. W. 446,26 R. 691 .
Where an issue of equitablecognizance is submitted to a
jury, in aid of the chancellor, and the findingsof the jury are
disregarded by him , the judgment of the chancellorwill be
considered by the Appellate Court without reference to the
verdict of the jury, and the same effectwill be given to it
as if there had been no finding of the facts. The Court of
Appeals will consider the evidence and determine the case
accordingto the truthof the matter as itshallappear from
the whole record, and where the evidenceis conflictingthe
chancellor'sverdictwill not be disturbed. Wisdom v . Nichols,
139 Ky .506,97 S. W. 18, 29 R. 1128.
See, also, Digests, titleAPPEAL AND ERROR (REVIEW ).

lawful tribunal upon person convicted of a crime or
misdemeanor. Com . v. French, 130 Ky . 744, 114 S. W. 255,
quoting from Bouvier's Law Dictionary.

"The Supreme Court of North Carolina, in Statev.Oswald,

ora contemptcommitted, imposedby thejudgment of a court;

Blackstonesays: 'A " fine" is so called because it puts an

The governor's remission of all of a fine, " except commis-
sioner'sfees and costs," does not releasethe suretiesin the
bailbond , it not appearing that the defendant had satisfied

depend upon the amount of the judgment againstthe principal.

The penalty of ten per cent. prescribed by Sec. 4091, Ky .

within the meaning of Sec. 1721, Ky . Stats., which provides

easeseach circuit clerk shall receive from the State treasury

ered in their respectivecourts and paid into the State treas-
ury." Ford v. Stone, 102 Ky . 471, 43 S. W. 721, 19 R. 1537.
The provisionof a charter " that allfinesimposed for viola-

Court, but must be regarded as referringonly to finesimposed

FISCAL COURT.- " Each county in the Commonwealth of
Kentucky shallhave a FiscalCourt, which shallconsistof
the judge of the County Court and the justicesof the peace
of said county, and theirsuccessorsin office, in which court
the judge of the County Court shallpreside, if present. If
saidjudge isnot presentand can not preside,then a majority
of the justicesof the peace shallelectone of theirnumber to
preside." Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec. 1833.

FISCAL COURT 1383 FISCAL COURT
FiscalCourts consistof the judge of the County Court and
thejusticesof the peace of the county. Ky . Constitution, Sec.
144 .
" The term 'Fiscal Court ' where so used in this chapter
(PublicRoad Act ), shallmean the court having charge of the
fiscalaffairsof the county, whether composed of the county
judge and the justicesof the peace or of the county judge
and commissioners." Acts 1914, p. 339; Ky . Stats. (1915),
Sec .4291 .
The judgeof theCounty Court,asjudge of theFiscalCourt,
isa member of the FiscalCourt, and must be included to deter-
mine whether or not a majority of the court has acted. Bath
County, ex rel., Brown v. Daugherty , 113 Ky . 518 ; Sec. 144,
Constitution; Sec.1833,Ky .Statutes, 1915;Barnettv.Gilbert,
164 Ky . 564 , 175 S. W. 1029.
Under Ky . Constitution, Sec.144, providingthatthe Fiscal
Court shall" consistof the judge of the County Court and the
justicesof the peace, in which courtthe judge of the County
Courtshallpreside, ifpresent," and that" a majorityof the
members of said court shallconstitutea quorum for the trans-
actionof business," the judge of the County Court isa mem-
ber of the FiscalCourt, and not merely a presidingofficer,
and therefore, where thereare fivejusticesof the peace, the
FiscalCourt consistsof six members, and threemembers do
not constitutea quorum for the transactionof business, even
thoughthe Legislaturemay have attemptedto limitthe duty
of thejudge of the County Court to thatof presiding. Bath
Co.v.Daugherty,113 Ky .518,68 S. W. 436,24 R. 350.
The FiscalCourt isone of specialand limitedjurisdiction,
and ithas no power to appropriatethe money of the county,
exceptas authorized by law. There is no statuteauthorizing
the Fiscal Court to make any kind of a contract with the
jailer. Mitchellv . Henry Co., 124 Ky . 833, 100 S. W. 220, 30
R. 1051 .
The Fiscalaffairsof the county are not confidedto a certain

of the county, which tribunalacts as a body, and can act

of the magistratesmust be presentand in which a majority

FISCAL COURT 1384 FISCAL COURT
of those present must concur. The court must keep a record

null. McDonald's Admx .v. FranklinCo.,125 Ky .205,100 S.
W. 861 , 30 R. 1245.
Neither the county judge nor his son is competent to sitas
a member of the Fiscal Court in fixingthe salary of the county
judge. Grayson County v.Rogers, 122 S. W. 866.
A County or Fiscal Court can contractonly by itsrecords.
Montgomery County v . Taylor, 142 Ky . 547, 134 S. W. 894.
A Fiscal Court can not bind itselfby parol. Fiscal Court
v .Board of Trustees, 118 S. W. 298.
The Fiscal Court, likeother courts, must speak through its

An orderof theFiscalCourt of PulaskiCounty making the

The authority of the Fiscal Court to levy taxes can not be

mond v . Lester, 159 Ky . 310, 166 S. W. 976.
The Fiscal Court has no authority to open a public road.
This power islodged exclusivelyin the County Court under

156 Ky .507, 161 S. W. 508.
The Fiscal Court of a county has authority, under Sec. 1840
of the Kentucky Statutes,to employ an accountantto examine
and investigatethe books and accounts and records of the

the controlof or who are charged with the collectionor expen-
diture of the funds of the county arisingfrom taxationor any

The Fiscal Court isinvestedwith powers that are judicialor

discretionthat can not be controlledby other judicialtribunals

The FiscalCourt isa court of limitedjurisdictionor powers ,
and it had no right or power to appropriateor expend

The Fiscal Court is a court of limitedjurisdiction, and its

Where the recordshows the names of the justicespresent

FIXED . During recessthe presidingjudge saw a juror
and a son of defendant conferringin a suspiciousmanner . He

FIXTURE . An articlewhich was a personalchattel, but
which, by being physically annexed to a building or land,
becomesaccessoryto it, and part and parcelof it. Formerly
itwas treatedas belongingto the owner of the freehold, and
passedwith it to a vendee; and , though annexed by a tenant
forhis own conveniencein the occupationof the premises,
couldnot be removed by him. The rulehas been modifiedby
statuteinEngland and many of thestates,and ismuch relaxed
inpractice,especiallyas betweenlandlordand tenant, so that
tradefixturesand ornamental fixturesmay generallybe removed
at the end of his term by the tenant, provided he does thereby
no materialinjury to the freehold. Cochran's Law Lexicon.

FIXTURE FIXTURE1390
The three classesof casesin which questionsrespectingthe
right to fixtures arise are : 1. Between heir and executor-
there the rule obtains with most rigor in favor of the inherit-
ance and against the right to consider as a personal chattel
any thing affixedto the freehold; 2. Between executor of
tenant for life and remainderman or reversioner - there the
rightto fixturesisconsideredmore favorablyto the executor;
3. Between landlord and tenant - here the claim to have
articlesconsideredas personalpropertyis receivedwith the
greatest latitude. There is stillan exception of a broader
character in respect to fixtureserected for the purpose of
trade. Johnson v . Wiseman , 4 Met . 359.
In determining whether or not a thing is a fixtureseveral
tests are to be regarded . These are the real or constructive
annexation of the thing to the freehold, the relationthe parties
in interesthave to each other, the characterof the article, the
use which may be made of it in connection with the realty,
and the intentionof the owner as to itsuse, the testlastnamed
beingof controllingimportance;and,asbetweenthevendorand
vendee, the law favors the latter. Hill v . Mundy , 89 Ky . 36,
11 R. 248, 10 R. 541.
Chandeliers attached to freehold are subjects of larceny.
The rulemaking such appendages part of the realtyas between
vendor and vendee, and personalty as between landlord and
tenant, does not apply in a case of larceny. Smith v . Com .,
14 Bush ,31.
Neither the reservation of the mill and fixtures in a mortgage
of the land on which the mill is located, nor theirtemporary
severance from the land, changes theirlegal characteras part
of the realty; and in sellingsuch fixturesunder execution all
the statutory requirements for the sale of real property must
be followed. Davisv.Eastham,81 Ky .116,4 R. 850.
Chattels attached by mortgagor after executing mortgage ,
and which are not mentioned therein,are not subjectto the lien
unless so attached that a removal will impair the freehold.
But chattelsannexed to the freeholdprior to the mortgage,
which are essentialto its enjoyment though not mentioned in
themortgage, aresubjecttothelien. Clorev.Lambert,78 Ky .
224 .

FIXTURE FIXTURE1391
Chattels annexed to the freehold which are essential to its

FLOATING INTENTION.- If a person actuallyremoves
to another place with the intentionof remaining there for an
indefinitetime and as a place of fixed domicile, it is to be
deemed his place of domicile, notwithstanding the fact that
he might entertain a " floatingintention" to return to his
former home at some future period. Where a man abandons
his former home and takes up a new home with a view of
remaining there indefinitely, or until he actuallypurchases a
new farm , and does remain there for almost three years, the
fact that he may entertaina " floatingintention" to purchase
property and go elsewhere will not be sufficientto show that
no change of domicilehas taken place. Saunders v. City of
Flemingsburg , 163 Ky . 680, 174 S. W. 51.
If a person has actuallyremoved to another place with the
intentionof remaining there for an indefinitetime and as a
place of fixeddomicile, it is to be deemed his place of domicile,
notwithstanding he may entertain a " floatingintention" to
returnat some futureperiod. Baker v .Baker, Eccles& Co.,
162 Ky . 683,173 S. W. 109.

ance is not guilty of conversion by sellingthe same . Finnell
v .Sage,9 Ky . Opin .249.

thefirstforcibleentry was made .

Under the Code, exclusiveoriginaljurisdictionin case of

FORGED CHECK .- Where a bank cashed a check drawn

The exceptionalrule is that a bank is bound to know the
signatureof itsdepositors; and if it pay the check of a depos-
itorittherebyadmitsthe genuinenessof hissignature, and is
estopped to afterwards deny itto the detriment of an innocent
third party. Farmers National Bank of Augusta v . Farmers
& TradersBank ,159 Ky .141, 166 S. W. 986.
The exceptionalruledoes not, however, requirethe drawee

the partieswhose names appear upon the check, or were duly
authorized by them . Farmers National Bank of Augusta v .
Farmers & Traders Bank , 159 Ky .141, 166 S. W. 986.

" The conventional fixing of twenty-one years, by the
common law, when man's estateof full responsibilityis begun ,
has been adopted generally where the common law has gone .
It was adopted in deference to considerationsof expediency
similarto thoseupon which restthe maxim that the law takes
no notice of a fraction of a day . Inasmuch as a man is a man
when he is twenty-one,he is then entitledto allthe privileges,
as he is charged with the responsibilitiesof full age. At
twenty-one he may lawfully do what he could after he is
twenty-one. Time iscontinuous, and, of course, isnot in fact
severable. There is no instant between the ending of one
period and the beginning of the succeeding one. When twenty-
one years have passed, the twenty-second year had begun . So ,
if it were said that twenty-one years must actuallypass before
one isof fullage, it would follow that he would be more than
twenty-one in fact before he attained to the privilegeswhich
the common law gives to one who isjust twenty-one years old.
The law notes no fraction of any day . In law a man is
twenty-one years old on the day preceding his twenty-first
birthday, and may then do whatever is allowed to an adult
to do." Erwin v.Benton ,120 Ky .549,87 S. W. 291,27 R. 909 .
FRANCHISE.- " By the common law a franchisewas said

itisunderstood to be a particularprivilegeconferredby grant
from government and vestedin individuals. (3 Kent's Com .
458; Bouvier'sLaw Dictionary, 611.) It is thus definedby

(15 Johnson's Report, page 387), "If there are certain

immunitiesand privilegesin which the public have no interest,

power, it would seem to me that such immunities and

13 Bush , 189.
A privilegeinwhichthepublichavean interest,and which
can not be exercised without authority from the sovereign
power, isa franchise. Cumberland R. L. Co. v . Com ., 6 R. 295.
What is commonly termed the granting of a franchiseby a

thenature of a contractby the city with the grantee for the

ata particularpoint where it is proposed to locate a college

Generally. "No actionshallbe brought to charge any
person: 1. For a representationor assurance concerning the
character,conduct,credit,ability, trade,or dealingsof another,
made with intent that such other may obtain thereby credit,
money or goods ; nor, 2. Upon a promise to pay a debt con-
tractedduring infancy,or a ratificationof a contractor prom-
isemade during infancy; nor,3. Upon a promise of a personal
representativeas such to answer any liabilityof his decedent
out of his own estate; nor,4. Upon a promise to answer for the
debt, default, or misdoing of another; nor, 5. Upon any agree-
ment made in considerationof marriage, except mutual prom-
isesto marry ; nor, 6. Upon any contract for the sale of real
estate, or any leasethereoffor longer term than one year ; nor,
7. Upon any agreement which is not to be performed within
one year from the making thereof, unless the promise, con-
tract, agreement, representation, assurance, or ratification, or
some memorandum or note thereof, be in writing, and signed
by the party to be charged therewith, or by his authorized
agent; but the considerationneed not be expressed in the

writing; it may be proved when necessary, or disproved by

The statutemerely withholds a right of action upon con-

anceof the party signing affectthe matter. King v. Cheat-

Thestatuteof fraudsrelatesto the remedy, and a contract

154 S. W. 900 .

408. And although not enforcibleas a right of action in the

anactionto recoverthe land. Cornellisonv. Cornellison, 1

The statuteis only availableso long as promise remains
executory; when themoney ispaid,no actionliesfor itsrecov-
eryback. Craigv. Vanpelt, 3 J. J. M. 490.

lutelyvoid; they are validfor many purposes. McCampbell

Gray , 2 J. J. M. 22 ; Hill v. Spalding, 1 Duv . 219.
Contractfor the saleof land must be so far evidencedby

withouttheaidofparoltestimony. Kay v.Curd,6 B.M. 103.
Ten acres" adjoininghim on the north" in a bond for title

cient. Hanley v . Blackford, 1 Dana , 2.
The law will not permit a plaintiffto take advantage of

The statuteof fraudsand perjuriesrelatesto the remedy or

The statutedoes not apply to promises raised by implica-

gener v. Howsley's Admr ., 164 Ky . 113, 175 S. W. 4.
It is not within the statute of frauds for one who has

poseof preventingfrauds and perjuries, and we apprehend,

hendedby sufferingthe admission of parol evidence in other
casesof the saleof land, would equally apply to the case of a
purchase, a constructioncalculatedto meet the evilintended
tobe remedied by the makers of the statute, requiresthat such
a case should be held to come within the statute .
" If the trust is consideredas created by the agreement of
theparties, if it does not come within the letter, that liber-
alityof constructionjustmentioned, which isalonecalculated
to prevent the mischiefs intended to be prevented by the
statuteemphaticallyrequiresitshouldbe brought within the

Partnershipto Deal inReal Estate. An agreementbetween

theterms of the contract, and itsnature, and the situationof

notand could not be performed by the partieswithin the
year." East Tenn . Tel. Co. v . Paris Electric Co., 156 Ky .

" If the contract provides for performance upon the hap-
peningof some event which may or may not take place within
theyear, such contract is not within the statute." East

" If thereis in the terms of the contract, or any reasonable

" 'It now seems to us the statute was intended and does
properlyapply only to an agreement that is not to be per-
formedby eitherparty withina year, but not to one which is
tobe orhas been performedby one or eitherof them within
suchperiod,and thatconstructionhas been adoptedelsewhere.
Forifthepracticaleffectand operationof the statuteis, as
hasbeenuniformlyheld by thiscourt, in every case where
onepartyhas performedan agreementwithina year, to hold
theotherparty liableon such agreement, althoughhe is not
toperformwithina year, such shouldbe construedand held
tobe themeaning and import of the languageused. In fact,
thestatuteproperlyappliesto agreementsthatare wholly
executory; and one which has been performed by one of the

partieswithina year is, to that extent, executed, and cannot,

908, the rule laid down in this case was approved ." East

530,quoting from Dant v . Head , 90 Kу . 255.

by the death of the obligorwithin that time, and is therefore

FRIGHT . No recovery of damages is warranted for mere
fright, unaccompaniedby physicalinjury. Morse v. C. &
0.R.Co.,117 Ky .11, 77 S. W. 361, 25 R. 1159.
No recoverycan be had for personal injury resultingfrom
frightoccasionedby the negligenceof an other, where there
isno immediatepersonalinjury, trespassto real estate, or
somecontractrelation. Morse v . C. & O. Ry . Co., 117 Ky .
11,77S.W. 361,25 R. 1159; McGee v.Vanover,148 Ky . 737,
147S.W. 742, and casescited.
It iswell settledthat there can be no recovery for fright
unaccompaniedby physicalinjury: Morse v. C. & O. Ry .
Co.,117Ky . 11; Reed v. Maley, 115 Ky . 816; Reed v. Ford,
129Ky.471; Chesapeake& Ohio Ry . Co. v. Robinett, 151 Ky .
778,45 L. R. A. (N.S.) 433; Southern Ry . v. Owen , 156
Ky.827,162 S. W. 110.

painand sufferingresultingsolelyfrom fright, unaccompanied
byphysicalinjury, superinducedby an assaultand battery
committedupon her husband in her presence; the damages
beingtooremoteand speculative. McGee v. Vanover, 148 Ky .
737,147S.W. 742.
In order to relievea person from the consequences of his
own acts on the ground that they were done suddenly or
throughfright, they must be done under a senseof impend

FRIGHT FRIGHT1468
ing danger, and that danger must be a realone, or the circum-
stancessuch as to createin the mind a reasonableapprehension
of danger . Courier-Journal Printing Co. v . Haag , 155 Ky .

10 Ky . Opin . 303 .
There can be no recovery for fright not accompanied with
personal injury, but if there is a physical personal injury
from a jar or fallbackward , fright may be recovered for as
mental suffering. Ky . Traction& Terminal Co. v. Bain, 161
Ky . 44 , 170 S. W. 499.
Defendant illegallydischargedfirearmson a publichighway,

manslaughter. Com .v . Couch , 106 S. W. 830, 32 R. 638.
A cause of action will not lie in favor of an infant female
for pain and suffering resulting solely from fright, unac-
companied by physicalinjury, superinducedby a wrongful
assaultand battery committed upon her father in her presence,
by the conductor of a train and his assistants; the damages in
such a case being too remote and speculative. C. & O. Ry .
Co. v .Robinett, 151 Ky .778 , 152 S. W. 976.
A cause of action willnot liein favor of a woman for pain
and sufferingresultingsolelyfrom fright, unaccompanied by
physicalinjury, superinducedby one who , withoutseeingher
or knowing of her presence, and withouttrespassingon her
premises, assaulteda thirdpersonwho occupieda room in her
house ; the damages being too remote and speculative. Reed v .

personalinjury is received. Id .
FROG .- A frogis" a devicenow usuallymade of several
railsectionssecured to a plate or bolted together through dis-
tance(distinct) pieces, forming a connectionof one track with
anotherbranching from or crossingit. There are many special
forms." Com .v . L. & N. R. Co., 143 Ky . 503, 136 S. W. 869.
" A frog isso calledfrom itsshape as originallymade . The
guardrailto keep the wheel from slipping off the other rail
whileit ispassing over the frog is in no sense a part of the
frog. The guard rail is put there to keep the wheel on the
track. Guard railsare in common use among railroads. The
statutewas not aimed to secure the blocking of guard rails."
" A frog, in railroadparlance, is a sectionof a rail, or of
severalrailscombined , at a point where two railways cross,
or at the point of a switch from a line to a siding or to
anotherline, and its function is to enable a car or train to
be turned from one track to another. In a blocked frog the
pointof space between the rails, at the point where the car is
switchedfrom one track to another, is filledwith wood or
othermaterial, so that the foot will not be held. There is a
formof castiron frog, in which the space between the rails
attheapex of the frog is filledwith cast iron." Com . v . L.
& N. R. Co.,143 Ky . 502, 136 S. W. 869.
In Ky. Stats., Sec. 780, providing that every railroad
companyshalladjust, fix, or block the frogson itstracksto
preventthe feet of its employes from being caught therein,
theword " frog" does not includethe opening at the end of
theguard rail, maintainedoppositethe frogand used to keep
thewheelfrom slippingoffthe otherrailwhile passingover
thefrog. Com .v . L. & N. R. Co., 143 Ky . 501, 136 S. W. 869.
Railroadcompanies are not requiredto furnishthe best
and latestimproved machinery, but only that which is
reasonablyproper when judged by what has been shown to

be best by experienceand use. The use of the frog known
as the T ironor steelrailisnot negligence, itappearingthat
therearevariouskindsoffrogsinuse,and expertsdifferas to
which isbest. Young v .L. & N. R. Co.,6 R. 308.
In an actionfor injuriesto a person by having his foot

or whether he did not see it until his foot was caught in it.
I. C. R. Co. v . Crockett, 79 S. W. 235, 25 R. 1989.
Section 780, Kentucky Statutes, as to unblocked frogs is
for the protection of employes and affectsin no way the
liabilityof a railroadcompany for an unsafe conditionof the

Sieber, 157 Ky . 151, 162 S. W. 804.
Under Sec. 780 of the Kentucky Statutes, which requires

of itsfrogs. L. & N. R. Co. v. Com ., 154 Ky . 293, 157
S. W. 369 .
Section780 ,Ky . Stats., providing that " before the firstday

R. 1342 .
An indictment against a railroad company for failing to

FROM AND AFTER . The act authorizingcontractsfor the

as follows: " this Act shall take effect and be in force from
andafterthe firstday of September , 1871."

thelanguagewould have been " on and after" thatday, etc.

& T. P. Ry . Co., 127 Ky . 159, 105 S. W. 379, 32 R. 67.
GATES AT CROSSINGS .- Where a railroadcompany
maintains gates at a streetcrossing, which are lowered when
a trainisapproachingit, theraisingof thegateisan invita-
tion to use the crossing; but such raisingdoes not relieveone
using the crossingfrom the obligationto use ordinarycare
forhissafety. L. & N. R. Co. v. Wilson, 124 Ky .836, 100 S.
W. 302 , 30 R. 1048 .
The fact that crossing gates are raised is an invitation
to a travelerto cross. L. & N. R. Co. v. Roth , 130 Ky . 766 ;
Crossv.I.C. R. Co., 110 S. W. 292; LouisvilleBridge Co. v .
Moroney , 32 R. 707.

GATHERED GENERAL ALPHA .1499
When gates at a crossingare kept closed when trains are
approachingand open when they are not, the fact that they
areopen isan invitationto the publicto cross, and persons
desiringto crosshave a right to assume that they can do so
withoutbeing struckby approachingtrains, though they are
notthereby relievedfrom exercisingordinary care for their
ownsafetyor from attending to other noticeor warning of the
approachof trains. Schultev. L. & N. R. Co., 128 Ky . 627,
108S. W. 941, 33 R. 31.
GATHERED .- Implied power of executor or trusteeto
sellrealproperty; effectof word " gathered." See note on
thissubjectin 32 L. R. A. (N.S.) 682.
GENDER . In construingthe Civil Code each gender
includesevery gender. Civil Code , Sec. 732 .
Itisprovidedin Ky . Stats., Secs.446, 457,thatin the con-
structionof statutes" a word importing the masculine gender
onlymay intendand be appliedto femalesas well as males."
Itisprovided in the Tenement House Act, that for the
purposesof that Act words in the masculine gender include
thefeminineand neuter. Acts 1912,p .573 ; Ky . Stats. (1915),
Sec. 3037g.
GENERAL ACCEPTANCE .- See ACCEPTANCE .
GENERAL AGENT . See, also,AGENT .
The law impliesan agreementupon the part of a railroad
company to furnish necessary cars on a particularday when
a requestthereforhas in due time been made by a shipper
ofthestationagent, who , for the purpose, has the authority
ofa generalagent. N. N. & M. V. Co. v.Mercer, 96 Ky .475,
16 R. 555 .
If one who is himself engaged in a particularcalling or
businessbe employed to do certainactsin thattradeor busi-
ness,he will,as a generalrule,be regardedwith respectto his
employmenta generalagent. Bellv. Offutt,10 Bush, 635.
GENERAL ALPHABETICAL CROSS INDEX .- Under Sec .
513, Ky . Stats., itisthe duty of the county court clerkto

keep a general alphabeticalcross index of all instruments
lodged for record in his office. Bentley v. Letcher County , 143
Ky . 585, 136 S. W. 1008.
GENERAL ASSEMBLY . It is provided in Ky . Constitu-
tion, Sec. 29, that " The legislativepower shall be vested in
a House of Representativesand a Senate, which, together,
shall be styled the 'General Assembly of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky ."
GENERAL AVERAGE . General average, as applied in
marine insurance, is a loss arising out of extraordinary sac-
rificesmade or extraordinary expenses incurred for the joint
benefitof the ship and cargo. LouisvilleUnderwritersv.
Pence , 93 Ky . 96, 14 R. 21.
Where there is a common danger to the vesseland cargo,
everythingwhich issaved by one continued, unremittedeffort,
must by way of general average pay the expense in proportion
to its value. But this principle is not applicablewhere ex-
penses are incurred for a separate interest, the other interests
not being involved in the danger . Id .
GENERAL CLAIM FUND . The requirement of the Act of
May 20, 1897, that guards appointed to protect property
against mobs shall be paid " out of the county treasury and
levy of that year," means that they are to be paid out of the
general fund , and a designationof this fund in an order of
the fiscalcourt levying the tax as the " General Claim Fund "
issufficientlyspecificunder Sec. 180, Ky . Constitution. Cahill
v. Perrine, 105 Ky . 531, 49 S. W. 344, 20 R. 1454.
GENERAL COSTS .- Fees which constitute what are called
general costs, though the service was not directed by either
party, should be taxed against the successfulparty, who is
entitledto recoverthem of hisadversary, but the factthat
the latteris insolventis no reason why the former should not
pay them . Brown v . Young , 8 R. 523.
GENERAL DAMAGES . “ General damages" are such as
the law presumes to have accruedfrom the wrong complained

GENERAL DEMUR . 1501 GENERAL ELECTION
of,and as a necessary resultthereof, and need not be alleged.
L.& N. R. Co. v . Roney , 108 S. W. 343, 32 R. 1326 .
Under an allegationof generaldamages, recoverymay be
hadonlyforsuch as proximatelyand naturallyflowfrom the
wrongscomplainedof,and not forany specialdamages. Louis-
villeGas Co. v. Kentucky Heating Co., 132 Ky . 435, 111 S.
W. 374, 33 R. 912.
General damages need not be averred, being such as the
law presumes to have accrued from the wrong . Cumberland
Tel. & Tel. Co. v. Overfield, 127 Ky . 548, 106 S. W. 242, 32
R. 421. See, also, DAMAGES .
GENERAL DEMURRER .-" A generaldemurrer isan objec-
tionto a pleading because it does not statefacts sufficientto
constitutea cause of action or a defense, or because it does
not state facts sufficientto support a cause of action or a
defense." Civil Code , Sec. 93 .
GENERAL DEPOSIT . “ Deposits of money with banking
corporations, or with bankers, are eithergeneral or special. A
specialdeposit is where the specificmoney , the very silveror
goldcoin, or billsdeposited, are to be restored, and not an
equivalent. A general deposit is said to amount to a mere
loan, and the bank is to restore, not the same money , but an
equivalentsum, whenever itisdemanded." Keene v. Collier,
1 Met .415, 417 .
Bank's failureto appropriategeneraldepositto payment of
noteheld a releaseof sureties. Faulkner v . Cumberland Valley
Bank,14 R. 923 .
GENERAL ELECTION .- An electionto filla vacancy in
a county officeis not a general electionin the meaning of an
Act providing that the questionmay be submitted at a general
electionto be held for county officers. Hutchinson v . Miller,
158Ky . 363 , 164 S. W. 961.
Where an Act provided for the submission to the people
ofthequestionwhetherthey wished to have the FiscalCourt
composed of three commissioners, " at a general electionto be
heldfor county officers," the questioncouldnot be submitted

GENERAL ELECTRIC 1502 GENERAL FUND
at a general electionat which no county officerswere to be
elected. Hutchinson v. Miller, 158 Ky . 363, 164 S. W. 961.
See, also, LAST GENERAL ELECTION .
GENERAL ELECTRIC BUSINESS.- " The statute does not
except corporationsorganized for the purpose of conducting
a 'general electricbusiness.' A general electricbusinesswould
include the manufacture of, the buying and sellingof, every
electriccontrivance or apparatus known to modern science;
and there are no exceptionsin the statutesin favor of corpor-
ationsorganizedfor the purpose of 'acquiringstocksand bonds
or other obligationsof other corporations, and sellingand trans-
ferring the same .' '" Gamewell Fire Alarm Tel. Co. v . Fire
& Police Tel. Co., 116 Ky . 779 , 76 S. W. 862, 25 R. 1010 .
GENERAL ESTATE . The general estateof a wife, in
personalty, held by her free from any restrictionsor limita-
tions, upon her death, without leaving a will, passes to her
administrator. Lee v.Belknap, 163 Ky . 432, 173 S. W. 1129.
GENERAL EXPRESSIONS . " It is a maxim , not to be
disregarded, that general expressions, in every opinion, are
to be taken in connectionwith the case in which those expres-
sions are used. If they go beyond the case, they may be
respected, but oughtnot to controlthe judgmentin a sub-
sequentsuit, when the very point is presentedfor decision."
Allison v . Cocke's Exr ., 106 Ky . 779 .
GENERAL FUND . “ General fund " of the county held to
consistof a surplus left remaining from funds appropriated
for other purposes after the purposes for which the fund was
appropriated had been satisfied. Whaley v . Com ., 110 Ky .
154 ; Taylor v.Riney , 156 Ky . 393, 161 S. W. 203.
The requirement of the charter of citiesof the fifthclass
(Sec. 3646, Ky .Stats.), thatallmoney receivedfrom licenses
shallbe paid intothe " generalfund," repealedthe provision
oftheamendmentofApril26,1880,tothecharterofthetown
of Vanceburg, now a cityof the fifthclass, requiringthat all
money receivedfrom liquorlicensesshallbe paid to the com-
mon schoolcommissionerforthecounty,tobe usedsolelyforthe

GENERAL ISSUE 1503 GENERAL MERCHAN .
purposeof employing teachersfor the common schoolsin the
cityof Vanceburg . Trustees v. City of Vanceburg , 46 S. W.
1, 20 R. 369 .
GENERAL ISSUE .- As the general issue is abolished by
theCode, an answer denying that the defendant is indebted to
theplaintiffis not good . LouisvilleHotel Co. v . Taylor, 10
R. 150 .
GENERAL KNOWLEDGE.- Knowledge of defectin a street
willnot prevent a person injured thereby from recovering
damageswhere the injury is caused by his momentarily for-
gettingthe existence of the defect. The knowledge referred
toinTown of Belleviewv. England, 118 S. W. 994, isnot a
generalknowledge, but a present knowledge . City of Hender-
sonv.Dennis, 157 Ky . 192, 162 S. W. 820.
GENERAL LAWS OF LOCAL APPLICATION .- Acts
empoweringa localboard of education to levy a tax to pay
bondsissuedfor schoolpurposes are not private statuteswhich
mustbe speciallypleaded,but are" generallaws of localappli-
cation." Board of Education v . Ry . Co., 110 Ky . 932, 62
S.W. 1125,23 R. 376.
GENERAL MANAGEMENT OF THE BUSINESS . It is
withintheapparent authorityof a generalmanager of a cor-
porationto make contracts for labor. Forked Deer Pants Co.
v.Shipley,80 S. W. 476,25 R. 2299.
Such authority is given by a by -law providing that he
shouldhave the " general management of the businessof this
association, with power to buy and sell." Id .
GENERAL MERCHANDISE.- Where a policyon a stock
of" generalmerchandise" providesthat it shallbe void if
theinsuredkeepsgunpowderon thepremiseswithoutwritten
permissionin the policy, the fact that gunpowder is usually
keptas a part of sucha stock, or thatthe agentwho took
theapplicationtoldtheinsuredthathe couldkeep gunpowder
forsale,willnot precludethe company from relyingon the
conditionin the policy. Parol testimonycan not vary or con

GENERAL POWER 1504 GENERAL VERDICT
tradicta written contract. Western Assurance Co. v . Rector,
85 Ky . 294, 9 R. 3. See 7 R. 523. See and compare Wright
v . N. W. M. L. Ins. Co., 91 Ky . 208, 12 R. 850.
GENERAL POWER OF APPOINTMENT .- See POWERS .
GENERAL RELIEF .- In an actionto enjoindefendants from
transferringto otherscertainsharesof stockin a corporation,
and to compel them to transferthe stock to plaintiffs, a prayer
for" generalrelief," defensebeing made , willauthorizejudg-
ment for the value of the stock, if itbelonged to plaintiffsand
the defendants have disposed of it. Lillard v . Brannin , 91
Ky . 511, 13 R. 74.
Where the facts set out in the petitionshow grounds for
only partial rescissionof a contract, complete rescissioncan
not be granted under a prayer for general relief. Under such

the facts set out in the pleading. Crow v . O. & N. R. Co. ,
82 Ky . 134, 6 R. 6. See, also, ALL PROPER AND EQUITABLE
RELIEF .
GENERAL REPUTATION .- Proof of generalreputationis
not admissible to establisha partnership. Marks & Stix v .
Hardy's Admr ., 117 Ky .669, 78 S. W. 864,1112,25 R. 1770,
1909 .
GENERAL STATUTES .- The objectof the revisionof the
statutesof Kentucky was to enact a system of laws in a con-
densed form embracing all statutoryenactments of a general
nature other than those excepted in the Act adopting the Gen-
eral Statutes. Broaddus v . Broaddus , 10 Bush , 306.
The General Statutes must be regarded as containing all
the law on the subjectsindicatedin the titlestherein. When ,
therefore, a sectionin the Revised Statuteshas been omitted
in the General Statutes, or any change made , however slight,
the whole law as found in the Revised Statutes on that sub-
ject must be considered and treated as repugnant to the pro-
visions of the General Statutes . Id .
GENERAL VERDICT . “ A generalverdictisthatby which
the jury pronounces generally, upon all the issues, for the

plaintiffor for the defendant." Civil Code , Sec. 326 ; Witty
V.C.& O. Ry . Co., 83 Ky . 27 .
"The jury may render eithera generalor a specialver-
diet."
"A general verdict- 1. Upon a plea of guilty is 'guilty,'
and affixingthe punishment in cases in which the jury is
requiredto determine the degree of punishment. (Punish-
ment by labor, Ky . Stats., Sec. 1377). 2. Upon a plea of
'notguilty,' is'guilty,' or 'not guilty;' and if'guilty,' fixing

theCommonwealth,fixingtheoffenseand degreeof the offense,
andthepunishment in casesin which the jury is required
todeterminethe degree of punishment." Crim. Code, Secs.
256,257.
In civilcasesa generalverdictis one that findsfor the
plaintiffor thedefandant,as the casemay be,accordingto the
pleaor declarationof eitherparty. It is commonly for a
certainsum. Juriesin such casesfrequentlymake a mistake
by adding to their verdict irrelevantmatter which the law
termssurplusage. Thus it is not rare that juriesadd costs
toa verdict, with which they have nothingto do, as that is
a matterwholly within the regulationsof law. Thus, it is

GENERAL WARRANTY .—" A covenantby a grantorin a
deed'thathe will warrant the property hereby conveyed'
orwordsof likeimport; or the words 'with warranty,' or

The " general warranty " clause in a conveyance is equiva-

A covenant of general warranty in a deed is, in substance,

GENERALIA SPECIALIBUS NON DEROGANT .- General

GENEROUS JUST .- There must be in every contract, to
renderit valid, a good, or a valuable consideration. The
"quidpro quo," is the delightof the law , so much so, that out
ofthevarious considerationswhich influencehuman action,
ithasselectedand consideredthe two foregoing only the good
andthevaluable. Indeed, so adhesiveisthe" quid pro quo"
principle, that while it will not lend its aid to the donor, to
reclaiman executed gift- it refuses its assistance to the
enforcementof a giftexecutory- it permits generosity, but
exactsjustice- you may be generous- you must be just.
Shackelfordv .Handley's Exrs ., 1 A. K. Mar. 503.
GETTING AHEAD OF .- Where the attorneyfor an execu-
tioncreditornotified the debtor of the execution and told him
thatunlesshe replevied the debt the sheriffwould have to
closehisstore, and the debtorthereuponmade an assignment
forthebenefitof allhis creditors,his admission that he made
theassignmentto preventthe executioncreditorfrom " getting
ahead" of his other creditorsdoes not show that the assignment
wasmade with a fraudulent intent. Deposit Bank of Carlisle
V.Lee, 13 R. 495.
GET BY . See TO GET BY .
GET THEM BOTH . On trialfor maliciouslyshootingat
D.withoutwounding him, evidencethat defendant, afterhis
arrest, and when told that he had shot at D. and had shot
Mrs.D.,statedthathe was sorrythathe did not " get" them
both,was admissible, thoughirrelevantas to Mrs. D., since
thestatementwas not severable, so as to show regretmerely
fornotshootingD. Cogswellv.Com.,32 S. W. 935,17 R. 822.
GETTING MONEY .- The fact that the offense in the
accusativepart of an indictment is stated to be " getting
money under falsepretenses" insteadof " obtainingmoney by
falsepretenses" accordingto thewordingof thestatute,does
notrendertheindictmentdefective, the offensebeing properly
setoutinthedescriptivepartof theindictment. Hale v.Com .,
151Ky . 639, 152 S. W. 773 .

GIFT CAUSA MORTIS 1508 GIFT CAUSA MORTIS
GIFT CAUSA MORTIS .— A giftcausamortisis a giftby
which a person in sickness, who , apprehendinghis dissolution,
deliversor causes to be deliveredto another the possessionof
any personal goods, to keep as his own in case of the donor's
death, and it takeseffectonly in case the giver dies. It is
conditional,and thedonormay at any time revokeit. McCoy's
Admr .v . McCoy , 126 Ky . 783 , 104 S. W. 1031, 31 R. 1189.
" According to the well-settleddoctrineon the subject, to
constitutea donatiomortiscausa, theremust be threeattri-
butes: (1) The giftmust be with a view to the donor's death .
(2 ) It must be conditionedto take effectonly on the donor's
death by his existingdisorder, or in his existingillness. (3 )
There must be an actual delivery of the subject of the dona-
tion. 1 Story'sEquit .,Sec.607.
" Heretofore it has been doubted whether choses in action,
bonds, bills, notes, etc., could pass by a donation mortis causa.
But it is now the establisheddoctrinethat not only negotiable
notes, and billsof exchange, payable to bearer, or endorsed
in blank, and bank notes, but promissory notes not made pay-
able to 'bearer' without an assignment from the payee's
donor to the donor, may be the subject of a donatio mortis
causa, as the beneficialinteresttherein may pass by delivery.
Turpin v . Thompson , 2 Met . 420 ; Kemper v . Kemper , 1 Duv .
401, and authoritiescited." Briscoev. Briscoe'sAdmr ., 2
Ky . Opin . 380.
A gift causa mortis existswhere property is given in the
donor's last sickness or in other imminent peril, and takes
effectonly on hisdeath through the disorderor peril, and is
revocableduring his life. Stark v . Kelley, 132 Ky . 376, 113
S. W. 498 .
That several months before dying decedent gave notes to
defendant, excludes the idea of a gift causa mortis. Id .
Gifts causa mortis are against the policy of the law, and
defeatitspurposes. They are regardedwith scrutiny,and will
not be enforcedwhere the evidenceisnot clearand satisfactory.
Albro v.Albro, 65 S. W. 592, 23 R. 1555.
What is gift causa mortis. See note on this subject in 11
L. R. A. 684 .

GIFT CAUSA MORTIS 1509 GIFT CAUSA MORTIS
A giftcausa mortis is not made out by a statement by the
givertwo or three days prior to his death and at the time he
deliveredcertainpersonalproperty to a friend, " that he wanted
himto take charge of his effects," and said he wanted it for
theboys,meaning his grandchildren. To convey titleby a gift
causamortis, the language and acts of the giver must indicate
morethanhis intentionin the future to give itmust be a gift
inthepresent. McCame's Admr . v . McCame's Admr ., 8 Ky .
Opin.554.
A giftcausa mortis is made upon the implied conditionthat
ifthedonor recovers or the donee dies firstthe gift shallbe
void,and mere delay willnot bar the donor's right to recover
thegiftif it is demanded before the right is barred by
limitation; but the donee may rely upon any act or conduct
ofthedonor that will estop him to claim the gift. Lisle v .
Tribble,92 Ky .304,13 R. 595.
Itisessentialto the validityof a giftcausa mortis that the
propertygiven should pass immediately to the donee, and that
itshouldbe irrevocableby the donor . Duncan v . Duncan , 5
Litt.12 .
Giftscausamortisare, in general, conditional,likelegacies;
butit is absolutelyessentialto them that they be made by
thedonor in his last illnessor in expectation of death . Knott
v.Hogan, 4 Met . 101.
The rule in regard to delivery is that there must be an
actual,constructiveor symbolicaldeliveryof the property in
questionin order to make it a valid gift causa mortis. This,
however,doesnotalwaysmean thattheremust be themanual
transferof the property. The ruleis that the deliverymust
beaccordingto the nature of the thing, and actualdelivery
sofaras the subjectis capable of delivery. If actual delivery
beimpracticable,then theremust be some actequivalentto it.
Brownv.Brown's Admr ., 4 B. Mon. 535; Weber v.Salisbury,
Trustee,149 Ky .327,148 S. W. 34.
Where one, in anticipationof death from a mortal wound ,
sendsfor an intimate friend, and tellshim that he has
certainmoney on depositin a certainbank, and directshim
toget it and divide it equally between his wife, his mother ,

GIFT CAUSA MORTIS 1510 GIFT INTER VIVOS
his son and Irma Salyers, and then signs the followingorder,
addressed to the bank : " Please give all the money which I
have on depositwithyou,which issomethingmore than $2,000,
to W. M. Salisbury, who is to divide it equally between my
wife, my mother, my son and Irma Salyers," and the order

GIFT INTER VIVOS .— То constitutea valid giftintervivos

"The authoritieson the subjectare to the effectthat to

**GOOD AND SUFFI.** 1521 **GOOD BEHAVIOR**

A contract to convey land by "a good and sufficient deed" obliges the grantor to furnish the grantee a marketable deed—one that will pass a good title to the land it purports to convey. *Hall v. McKee*, 147 Ky. 841, 145 S. W. 1129.

**GOOD AND SUFFICIENT CONVEYANCE.**—Vendors of land, having agreed to make a "good and sufficient conveyance," conveyed their interests, and stipulated that the vendees might retain part of the price till the vendors perfected the title on an eighth of the land by obtaining a deed from certain heirs. The heirs were paid for their interest, but died without having made a deed. Held, that when the vendees had had peaceful adverse possession long enough to perfect their title the vendors were entitled to the balance of the price without obtaining the deed from said heirs. *Moyers v. Arthur*, 15 R. 684.

**GOOD AND WORKMANLIKE MANNER.**—The phrase "good and workmanlike manner," is employed in every building contract, and its meaning is so well known and generally understood that any attempt on the part of the court to define it could but result in confusing the minds of the jury, and making doubtful that which was already clear and free from doubt. *Kleiderer & Son v. Aldridge's Extr.*, 160 Ky. 638, 170 S. W. 23.

"The stipulation 'good, workmanlike manner' means something more than a promise to do a job which shall look well—something more than a good example of pipe-fitting. A grist-mill which will not grind; a reaper which will not cut grain; a locomotive which will not move when proper power is applied —can hardly be said to have been constructed in a good and workmanlike manner. Even so, a heating apparatus that will heat nothing but the owner's temper must be said not to fill that condition." *International Harvester Co. v. Bean*, 159 Ky. 842, 169 S. W. 549.

**GOOD BEHAVIOR.**—Act March 11, 1902, Sec. 3 (Acts 1902, p. 42, Chap. 14.), providing that on the second or any subsequent conviction for a violation of the local option law the

**GOOD ENGLISH** 1523 **GOOD FAITH**

**GOOD ENGLISH ORDER.**—T. through S. bought of M. tobacco which was guaranteed to be sound and prized in “good English order.” T. transferred this contract to C., and an addition was made to the contract that M. guaranteed the weights of the tobacco sold to T. and now sold to C. Held, that there was no new contract made which changed M.’s duty as to the condition of the tobacco prized, and the proof showing that the greater part of the tobacco was prized under the supervision and inspection of S., before the transfer of the contract, the jury were authorized under proper instructions to find the verdict rendered. *Thompson v. T. J. Melton & Co.*, 74 S. W. 192, 24 R. 2461.

**GOOD FAITH.**—“By ‘good faith,’ as judicially interpreted, is meant a purchase made not merely for a consideration, but also without notice to the purchaser of an adverse claim to the property by others; for the taking of an estate after notice of a prior right makes one a *mala fide* purchaser.” (Referring to Civil Code, Sec. 417. *King v. Huni*, 118 Ky. 454, 81 S. W. 254, 25 R. 2266, quoting from *Kellar v. Stanley*, 86 Ky. 246, 5 S. W. 480, 9 R. 388.)

The rule is that a statute will not make an act criminal unless the offender’s intent concurred with his act, and whether he acted in good faith or not is a question for the jury. *Com. v. Williams*, 120 Ky. 314, 86 S. W. 553, 27 R. 695.

Under an indictment for shooting and wounding O., an instruction telling the jury that if defendant, in good faith, went to arrest O., “and not to kill him,” he had the right to defend himself, is not liable to the objection that it requires defendant to prove a negative, as the expression quoted is used as merely explanatory of the expression “in good faith.” *Hart v. Com.*, 60 S. W. 298, 22 R. 1183.

Where a partner attempts unfairly to acquire a gain at the expense of his co-partner, he commits a breach of faith for which the law gives an action to the defrauded partner. *White v. Jouett*, 147 Ky. 197, 144 S. W. 55.

“One who, while careful and circumspect, is led into mistake of facts, and, doing what would be in no way reprehensible

as to the defendant'sintent or knowledge , the indictment need

the fact. His being misled concerning it is matter for him

GOVERNMENT OF THE UNITED STATES . “ If any
one propositioncould command the universal assent of man-
kind , we might expect it would be this that the government
of the Union, though limitedin itspowers, issupreme within
its sphere of action. This would seem to result necessarily
from itsnature. It is the government of all; itspowers are
delegatedby all; it representsall, and actsfor all. Though
any one state may be willing to control its operations, no
state is willing to allow others to control them . The nation
on those subjectson which it can act, must necessarilybind

itscomponent parts. But this question is not left to mere
reason; the people have, in express terms, decided it, by
saying, 'this constitutionand the laws of the United States,
whichshallbe made in pursuance thereof, shallbe the supreme
lawof the land; and by requiring that the members of the
statelegislatures, and the officersof the executiveand judicial
departmentsof the statesshalltake the oath of fidelityto it.
Thegovernment of the United States, then, though limited by
itspowers, issupreme; and thelaws,when made in pursuance
oftheconstitution, form the supreme law of the land,' any-
thingin the constitutionor laws of any state to the contrary
notwithstanding. 'This great principle is that the
constitutionand the laws made in pursuance thereof, are
supreme; that they control the constitutionand laws of the
respectivestatesand can not be controlledby them . " І. С.
R.Co.v.Doherty'sAdmr .,153 Ky .363,155 S. W. 1119.

GRANARY.- The terms " warehouse " and " storehouse," as

boat, in which goods, wares, and merchandise are usually
depositedforsafekeeping or forsale, and-
Farming implements and products are to be deemed goods
withinthe meaning of the statute.
A granary built and used for keeping and preserving

GRANDCHILDREN . The term, " grandchildren" includes

Grandchildrenmay claim a devise under the description

v.Handley, 4 Litt. 349.
Theword " children" willnot ordinarilyinclude" grand-
children," and willnever be construedto embrace them,
exceptin cases where it is necessary to give that construction
to theword in order to effectuate the obvious intention of
thetestator. Pratherv. Gault, 7 Ky . Opin. 186.
Testatorby thethirdclauseof thecodicilof hiswillcreated

fortheuseof hisgrandchildren,declaringthatifany of the

word"grandchildren" as there used should be construedto
includenotonlythosebornand livingor havingdiedleaving
issueattestator'sdeath,butalsoany who mightbe bornto
testator'sdaughterduringher lifetime. U. S. Fidelity&
Guar.Co.v.Douglas' Trustee, 134Ky .374, 120 S. W. 328.
As towhether"child," or " children" willbe held to

GRANDSONS .- While the word children is not like " heirs"
a word of limitation, importingby itsown forcea fee-simple
estate, itwill be so construed when necessary to effectuatethe
intention of the testator as made manifest from a consideration
of his whole will. Under a devise to " grandsons and their
children," the grandsons take a fee, as is manifest from a
considerationof the whole will. Williams v . Duncan , 92 Ky .
125, 13 R. 389.
GRAND JURY .-" A grand jury shallconsistof twelve
persons, and no person shallbe qualifiedto serve as a grand
juryman unless he be a citizenand a housekeeper of the
county in which he may be calledto serve, and over the age
of twenty-one years. No civil officer(except trustees of
schools), no surveyorof a highway, tavern-keeper, venderof
ardentspiritsby license, or personwho is under indictment,
or who has been convicted of a felony, and not pardoned , or
who, forreward,has, withintheprecedingsixmonths, stood
a horse, jack or bull, shallbe competent to serve as la grand
juror; but the fact that a person not qualifiedor competent
served on a grand jury, shall not be cause for setting aside
indictments found by such grand jury." Ky . Stats. (1915),
Sec . 2248 .
It is provided in Ky . Constitution (1891), Sec. 248 that
" A grand jury shallconsistof twelvepersons, nine of whom ,
concurring, may find an indictment."
Under the new Constitution an indictment found by a
grand jury consistingof more than twelve persons is void.
Downs v . Com ., 92 Ky . 605, 13 R. 820 ; Sanders v . Com ., 13
R. 820 .
Constitution, Sec. 248, limitingthe number of grand jurors
to twelve, is self-executing and mandatory , and only that
number is necessary, although no law has been enacted to
regulatethe selectionof the grand jury since the adoption of
thepresentconstitution. Wellsv. Com ., 15 R. 179.

GRANT . The words " give, grant, and convey" are as
comprehensiveas any that can be used to convey the legaltitle.

bepresumed. Browning v. Wickliffe, 15 B. Mon. 84; Beallv.
Clore, 6 Bush , 676; Cahillv. Mangold, 151 Ky . 156, 151
S.W. 373 .
The use of a rightof way by one personover the land of
anothermust be continued at least twenty years before any
presumptionof grant of the right can arise merely from the
use. And then the use must have been enjoyed under such
circumstancesas would indicate that it had been claimed as
a right, and had not been regarded by the parties merely as
a privilegerevocableat the pleasure of the owner of the land.
Downingv. Benedict, 147 Ky . 8, 143 S W. 756. See, also,
ADVERSE POSSESSION .

estateorinterestpassesin likemanner.

GRATUITY .- The rule is that when a man takes a child

her of itsapproach, or the failure, afterdiscoveringher peril,

Ky . 648, 149 S. W. 954.

contract of which it assumes performance by the party
primarilybound ,and the two agreements can not be so blended
as to constitutebut one. Kellog v . Dunn , 2 Met . 217.
A guaranty imports ex vi termini a collateralundertaking.
It is an accessory agreement distinctfrom the principal con-
tract, the performance of which it assures. It is not a
constituentpart of, though it may be considered a branch of
such contract, and to enforce its performance it is usually
necessary to sue the guarantor in a separate action from that
prosecuted against the party for whose default he agrees to
answer. Marshallv.Peck,etc., 1 Dana ,609; Yeatesv. Walker,
1 Duvall , 84.
It is essentialthat the guaranty or the promise to
assurethe performanceof the originalundertaking, shallbe
made the foundation of the action, and the recovery must be
basedupon thispromise. Jonesv. Turner, 6 Ky . Opin. 501.
The legal effectof a contract of a guarantor on a note is

GUARD RAILS . " A frog is so calledfrom itsshape as
originallymade . The guard rail to keep the wheel from
slippingoffthe otherrailwhileit ispassingover the frog is
in no sense a part of the frog. The guard rail is put there

use among railroads. The statutewas not aimed to secure
the blocking of guard rails." Com . v. L. & N. R. Co., 143
Ky . 502, 136 S. W. 869.
" If guard railsare reasonablynecessaryfor the safetyof
travelersand their property in crossing a bridge, then the
owners are liablefor an injury which is caused by a failureto
construct them . The guard rails, furthermore, should be
effectivefor the purpose for which they are constructed. They
should be reasonablystrong, and maintained so as to withstand
the ordinary weights and forces to which they are subjected,
and the safeguard should be suited to the character of its
ordinarytraffic." Cincinnati,N. O. & T. P. Ry .Co.v.Dungan ,
162 Ky . 36, 171 S. W. 1007.
GUARDIAN .- Guardians are not officersof the court, but
trustees, with such interestas entitlethem to protectionfrom

The guardian stands in place of the parent, and under

A guardian in the management of his ward's estateshould

intheappointmentas theirguardian. By naturehe is

andoversightas much as he isentitledto have thecareand

GUILTY .- " The plea of guiltycan only be enteredby the
defendant himself in open court." Crim . Code , Sec. 173 .
A verdictwhich findsthe defendant " guilty as charged in

Sec. 257, Criminal Code , provides that a general verdict on

HABEAS CORPUS.- (That you have the body.) Words
used in various writs, commanding one who detains another

" All prisonersshallbe bailableby sufficientsecurities,unless

not be suspended unless when , in case of rebellionor invasion,
the public safety may require it." Ky . Constitution, Sec. 16.
Code provisionsas to writ of habeas corpus. Crim . Code , Sec.
399 , et seq.
A person in prison isnot entitledto a writ of habeas corpus,
as, of course, itis a discretionarywrit, to be issuedonly upon
probable cause being shown ; and if upon the face of a petition
therefor it appears that there is no sufficientground for the
releaseof the prisoner, the writ willbe denied. Bethuram v .
Black,11 Bush,629.
The writ of habeas corpus is a discretionarywrit, to be
issued only when it is shown by the petitionor otherwise that
there isprobable cause to believethat the applicantisdetained
without lawful authority. That one is held " under a judg-
ment obtainedby fraud and forgeryon a forgedindictment"

conclusiveuntil reversed or set aside by the proper tribunal.
Cornelisonv. Toney , 12 R. 746 .

S. W. 1128 .
The proceedings in habeas corpus are not limitedto a deter-
mination of the sufficiencyof the extraditionpapers and the
identificationof the prisoner, but may determine the question
whether the person demanded is a fugitive from justice.

HABENDUM.- The clausefollowingthe grantingpart of a

" The officeof the habendum clause in a deed is to limit

HABIT . The word " practice," with referenceto intoxicants,
when used in an applicationfor insurance means " custom,"
' habit," and if the applicant'suse of intoxicantshas not been

perate use of such liquors, the rule being different, however ,

The representationof assured in his applicationthat he was

Where plaintiffsues for personal injuries, he may prove

Though the word " heirs" in its strictlegal sense denotes
the person upon whom the law casts the inheritanceon the

the word being referred to persons both in being and ascer-

As Distributee or Successor . The term " heirs" in a will
shouldbe interpretedaccordingto the subject-matter where

realty, the term shouldbe understoodin itstechnicalimport;
and if the subject be personalty, the same term should be

not used to designatea classor collectivebody , but as a desig-

HEIRS OF THE BODY .- The words "heirsof the body"
are properly used as words of limitation, and for the creation
of an estatetail, which is an estateto the person and the heirs
general or specialof his body . It is also true that the words
" heirsof the body," "heirsmale" or female of the body
or of two bodies, may be used as words of purchase. It is
a question of intentionin what sense they are used. When
used to designate persons, they are construed as words of
purchase; when used to designate the whole line of succession,
they are words of limitation. Prescott v . Prescott, 10 B. M.
59 ; Johnson v . Johnson , 2 Met . 335.
The term " heirsof the body ," when used in wills, is very
generally understood to be used as words of purchase and to
convey an interestto children, and not used as words of limi-
tation. The same words are so taken when it clearlyappears
to be the intentionto give a present interestto children. Such
was the constructiongiven by the English courts, especially
wher used with referenceto a chattel. Jarvis v. Quigly, 10
B. M. 106 .

mother , and this is true although the wife " and the heirs of

latterembraces descendants existentat every period. Johnson

HELP THEM OUT .- A contractby a railroadcompany with
a former employe to " give him back his old place as con-
ductor and retainhim in itsserviceas long as he did faithful
and honest work " in considerationof his agreement to " help
them out" in a strike was valid, and the company is liable
in damages for a breach of the contract. L. & N. R. R. Co. v .
Offutt, 15 R. 301. Upon appealto the Court of Appeals, that
court in an opinion deliveredJune , 1896, held that such con-
tractwas a contractfor employment for an indefinitetime and
could be terminated at any time by eitherparty. 99 Ky . 427,
36 S. W. 181, 18 R. 303.
HELPERS . It is quite common for head -miners to employ
assistantsor helpers, who stand in the same relationto the
mine owner and the mine boss as the head miner stands . It
is the duty of the mine owner to protect the helpers in every
way , and to preciselythe same extent, as he should protect
the head miners . In such cases the head miner or workman
is not an independent contractor, but a servant whose com-
pensation depends upon the amount of coal he gets out ; and
while he pays his helpers, theirpay is taken out of his pay .
And , where both are subject to the controlof the mine owner ,
as here, they are equallyhisservants. Curvin v. Grimes, 132
Ky . 555 ; InterstateCoal Co. v .Baxavenie, 144 Ky . 172 ; Inter-
stateCoal Co. v. Trivett, 155 Ky . 795; Employers' Indemnity
Co. v. Kelly Coal Co., 156 Ky . 74 ; Borderland Coal Co. v.
Small,Admr ., 160 Ky .738, 170 S. W. 8.

" In 2 Greenleaf Ev ., Sec. 261a, the distinctionis marked
between 'contractsfor specificwork and contractsfor the hire
of clerks, agents, laborersand domestic servants for a year
or shorterdeterminateperiods.' In that case the learned
authorshows thatthedefendantmay prove,on a breachof the
contract, 'either that the plaintiffwas actually engaged in
other profitableserviceduring the term, or that such employ-
ment was offered to him , and he rejected it.' There is an
evident distinctionbetween such a hiring and a contract
for the performance of some specificundertaking. In the one
case, the party can earn and expect to earn no more than
singlewages, and if he gets that his losswillgenerallybe
but nominal . King v . Steiren, 8 Wright , 99, was of this
nature. Whereas , in the other case the loss of the party is
the loss of the benefitsof the contract he is prepared to
perform. In Costiganv. The RailroadCompany , 2 Denio,609,
in a caseof hiringfor personalservice, where the party was
dismissed before his term had expired, it was held he was
not obligedto seek employment, nor perform servicesoffered
him of a differentnature from that he had engaged to perform ,
in order to recover full damages for disappointment." Har-
nessv.Ky .Fluor Spar Co.,149 Ky .65, 147 S.W. 934.
" There is a well-defineddistinctiondrawn by allthe author-
itiesbetween contractsfor hire, or for personalservice, and
the contractsto do a specificact. In the former case, if the
plaintiffgetsemployment at the same wages, it isplain that
he is not damaged , and when he does not his damages are
easily ascertained. In the latter species of contract, where
the employer refusesto acceptof the services, or to have the
work contractedfor performed, or preventthe employe from
performing the same in any manner, the usual measure of
damages, where the contractrelatesto the manufactureof an
article, or the constructionof a building, or the perform-
ance of some otherspecifiedact, isthe differencebetween the
price agreed to be paid and what it would have cost the
employe to complete it, provided such costswould be less
than the contractprice. Field on Law of Damages , Sec. 339."

Testator gave certain property to his wife " to have, to

HOLD THE HOUSE . A contractfor the buildingof a

renteduntil the rent cancels the debt. Trustees v . Marshall,
10 R. 402 .
HOLDER . It is provided in NegotiableInst. Law , Ky .
Stats. (1915), Sec. 3720b, Subsec. 190, that, unless the con-
textotherwise requires, the word " holder" means the payee
orendorsee of a bill or note, who is in possessionof it, or
