" The holder of a billis he who is in possessionof the bill,
and is legallyentitledto the benefitof it; and therefore the
drawer, as well as any other party, may become the holder.
Ricev.Hogan ,8 Dana , 135.
HOLDER FOR VALUE . " Where value has at any time
beengiven for the instrument, the holderisdeemed a holder
forvaluein respectto allpartieswho became such prior to
thattime." NegotiableInst. Law , Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec.
37206,Subsec.26.
" Where the holderhas a lienon the instrument,arisingeither
fromcontractor by implicationof law,he isdeemed a holder
forvaluetotheextentof hislien." NegotiableInst.Law ,Ky .
Stats. (1915), Sec. 3720b,Subsec. 27.
Where a bank took a note as collateralsecurityfor a note
itthenheld, itwas a holderforvalue, as alsoitwas if it
tookthenotein exchangefor the noteof the indorser. Camp-
bellv.FourthNationalBank of Cincinnati, 137 Ky . 555, 126
S.W. 114.
A payeeof a note,who on receivingitpaida specifiedsum
tobanks,which the maker owed, isa holderfor value within

instrument,theholderisa holderforvalue. Herman'sExr.

HOLDER FOR VALUE 1604 HOLDER IN DUE
A bona fideholder for value takes a negotiableinstrument
free from the equitiesbetween the parties. American Nat .
Bank v .Madison , 144 Ky . 152, 137 S. W. 1076.
A party who takes a negotiablenote as collateralsecurity
is a holder for value to the extent of his lien, but defenses
availableagainst the payee may be made against the holder
after his lien is satisfied. Jett v . Standafer, 143 Ky . 787,
137 S. W. 513 .
One who holds a note as collateral to secure another note
is a holder for value only to the extent of his lien. Elk Valley
Coal Co. v .Third NationalBank , 157 Ky .617, 163 S. W. 766.
The purpose of the statutewas to place commercial paper
on such footing that it would be fully and freely accepted
without question in all commercial transactionsand thereby
facilitatetrade, and with that purpose in view to protectthe
holder for value before maturity from any defenses that might
existbetween the payor and payee, unless the holder before
negotiationhad knowledge of such defense or infirmityor was
in possessionof such facts as would make his negotiationof
the paper amount to bad faith. Farmers Bank of Lynnville
v. First National Bank of Dickson, 164 Ky . 548, 175 S. W.
1019 .
The holderof commercialpaper who receivesit simply as
collateralfor an antecedent debt, without giving additional
consideration, is not a holder for value, and his titleis sub-
ordinate to that of the true holder. Greenwell v .Haydon , 78
Ky . 332 .
HOLDER IN DUE COURSE . “ A holder in due course
is a holder who has taken the instrument under the following
conditions: (1) that the instrument is complete and regular
upon its face; (2) that he became the holder of it before it
was overdue, and without notice that it had been previously
dishonored, if such was the fact; (3) that he took it in good
faith and for value; (4 ) that at the time it was negotiated
to him he had no noticeof any infirmityin the instrument or
defect in the titleof the person negotiatingit." Negotiable
Inst. Law , Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec. 3720b, Subsec. 52.

HOLDER IN DUE 1605 HOLDER IN DUE
"Every holder is deemed prima facie to be a holder in due
course; (a) but when it is shown that the titleof any person
who has negotiated the instrument was defective, the burden
ison the holder to prove that he or some person under whom
heclaims, acquired the titleas a holder in due course." Nego-
tiableInst. Law , Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec. 3720b, Subsec. 59.
Muir v. Edelen , 156 Ky . 212, 160 S. W. 1048.
The enactment of the negotiable instruments statute does
notserveto protecta holder in due courseof a note void
under Sec . 4223 of the statutes. Citizens' Bank v . Crittenden
Record-Press, 150 Ky . 634, 150 S. W. 814; Lawson v. Bank ,
102S. W. 324, 31 R. 318.
Under Subsec. 59 of the new NegotiableInstrumentsActs,
Sec.3720b,Kentucky Statutes, when the maker of a note pro-
ducesproofof fraud inducingitsexecution, the burden shifts

HOMESTEAD . Generally. By Ky .Stats.,Sec.1702,there
isexempt from sale under execution, attachment or judg-
ment, so much land, including the dwelling house and

The word homestead means only a home place, and has

A homesteadrightisnot an estatein land, but a privilege

shellbe exempt. Turnerv. Browning'sAdmr ., 128 Ky . 79,

Right of homesteadexemptionis purely statutory, and
ifnotfoundin theletterand spiritof thelaw itcan not

The mortgage of a " homestead interest" was no convey-ance of the legal estate, but of the homestead exemption

The right to a homestead exemption depends upon the
present and actual purpose and intention of the debtor to
use and enjoy the property sought to be exempted as a home
for himself and family, and that right does not exist where

Brown v . Martin, 4 Bush , 50 ; Carter v . Goodman , 11 Bush ,
231 .
Building may be a " homestead," though part of it is leased
to others. Garrison v . Penn , 66 S. W. 14, 23 R. 1775.
The homestead law merely exempts the homestead from sale
under coerciveprocess, and leavesthe husband'spower of
alienationunchanged . Pribble v. Hall, 13 Bush , 62.
The homestead exemption is not an estatein the land, but
only a privilegeof occupying the same by a housekeeper
with a family as against his creditors; and though the home-
stead is not subject to coercivesale by a creditor, a sale and
conveyance of the homestead is not prohibited by the stat-
ute. Brame v . Craig, 12 Bush , 405 .
The law did not create for the debtor a new estate; its
only effectwas to protect the title, which he retainsjust as
it is, from creditors. It is only where an insolventdebtor
dies, and his land is sold subjectto a homestead for his
widow and children, that there arises a new interestwhich
may be called a right of homestead. Eby v . Lovelace, 4 R.
449 .
A right of homestead in a living debtor is merely a right
to exemption. In its nature it is inalienable; because per-
sonal, it is extinguishedby the act transferringit. As a
thing that may be granted separate from the titleit does not
exist . Id .
Homestead Purchased When Paid For. Under Ky . Stats.,

Housekeeper With a Family. Ifthedebtorisa housekeeper

In order to constitutea " family" within the meaning

Where a debtoris a housekeeper, and a granddaughter

One who , having a wife and infant child, and a house

Sale and Reinvestment of Proceeds . The law is well settled
that the person who owns a homestead may sell it and

preservehis rightto a homestead in the new tractof land,

able time after sellingthe firsthomestead. Lee & Hester v.

653, 30 R. 816; Baker v. Kash, 113 S. W. 820; Farmers &
Traders Bank v. Childers, 150 Ky . 719, 150 S. W. 840.

Attempted Conveyance . A widow's homestead right in
her husband's property is a right of occupancy only, and an

The grantee under such conveyance does not take the widow's
dower right, for the electionby the widow to take homestead

Co., 62 S. W. 494, 23 R. 42 .
deceasedAn attempted conveyance by a widow of a
husband'shomestead passesno title, and is an abandonment
of the homestead . Bryant v . Bennett, 61 S. W. 1004,

death, in the absenceof any evidenceshowing thathis father
either occupied the land or intended within a reasonable
time to occupy it. Bailey v. Joseph, 143 Ky . 579, 136 S. W.
1009 .
An infant can not abandon his homestead right in land
of a deceased ancestor worth less than $1,000. Baker v.
Lane , 118 S. W. 963.

The statute does not require any particular form of
contract. It is enough if the parties each contemplate that
the lodger is to pay for his entertainment. The law then
implies an agreement, a contract, to pay reasonable
compensation if none other is settled upon . The statute
relievesthe implicationof a promise that would otherwise
arise from the mere fact of one party's furnishing the
entertainment to the other, but after there has been an
understanding between them by which the agreement to pay
is either expressly made by the lodger or it is a necessary
implicationfrom his express contract for entertainment, then
the statutedoes not apply. Id .

HOSTILITY.- " But it is not enough to merely assert the

facts which he alleges constitutethe state of feeling com-
plained of. We have held that the truthfulnessof the facts
stated can not be questioned by the judge (Vance v. Field,
89 Ky . 178 , 12 S. W. 190) ; therefore, it is all the more
importantthat the facts, and not the litigant'sconclusions
or suspicions, be set forth, that this court may have an
opportunity of testing their sufficiency, if the trial judge
should hold them insufficient." Massie v . Com ., 93 Ky . 588 ;
McDonald v . Walsend Coal Co., 135 Ky . 624 ; Anderson v .
Com ., 117 S. W. 364; Rush v. Denhardt, 138 Ky . 238;
Wathen v . Com ., 133 Ky . 94, 116 S. W. 336, support the
doctrines above announced ." Tolliverv. Com ., 165 Ky . 318,
176 S. W. 1190 .

A notary is liable for a loss occasioned by his negligence in failing to discharge his duty in protesting and delivering or mailing notices of protest as required by law. In order to fix such liability, the loss must be shown to have been on account of the want of skill or diligence on the part of the notary. *Neal v. Taylor*, 9 Bush, 385.

It was evidently intended by the act of 1864 to alter the law merchant in regard to giving notice of protest, but the act is so indefinite as to require judicial construction to enable notaries to know what their legal duties were by reason of its provision; the maxim "*ignorantia legis non excusat*" did not apply. *Id.*

Party failing to prepare for trial because he believed an order for a change of venue would remove a cause, though lodged at any time, is no ground for a continuance. *Shipp v. Gale*, Hard. 224.

**ILLS WE HAVE.**—“Such a thought ‘makes us rather bear those ills we have, than fly to others that we know not of.’” *Com. v. I. C. R. Co.*, 160 Ky. 745, 170 S. W. 171.

**ILLUSORY APPOINTMENT DOCTRINE.**—See statement of this doctrine in *Barret's Exor. v. Barret*, 166 Ky. 413.

**ILL-WILL.**—It is a uniform rule in criminal practice in homicide cases to permit the Commonwealth to give evidence of any pertinent facts or circumstances tending to show ill-will or bad feeling on the part of the accused toward the deceased, or a motive that may have contributed to influence the commission of the act. *Childers v. Com.*, 161 Ky. 440, 171 S. W. 149.

**IMMATERIAL ISSUE.**—An issue is immaterial where a plea to the whole declaration does not answer the whole cause of action. *Shields v. Henderson*, 1 Litt. 239. Or where that which is materially alleged is not traversed, or an issue taken on such a point as will not determine the cause. *Garrard v. Willet*, 4 J. J. M. 629.

**IMMEDIATE—IMMEDIATELY—IMMEDIATE NOTICE.**—“Immediately,” held not to mean, as used in an answer, instantly; but within a reasonable time. *Whitecotton v. Simpson*, 4 J. J. Mar. 12.

While the contract of insurance required the bank upon discovery of any speculation on the part of its cashier to give immediate notice, notice by telegram fourteen days after the discovery was sufficient, in view of the fact that in the meantime the officials were trying to secure enough money and property from the cashier to make good the shortage, and did get all he had. Moreover, the question of whether the notice was reasonable was submitted to the jury under proper instructions. It was not error for the court to instruct the jury that the words “immediate notice” as used in the contract of insurance mean notice given as soon as practicable and within a reasonable time, nor was it error to state in an instruction that it is admitted in the pleadings that the cashier appropriated a certain sum of money, though the pleading states that the appropriation was with the consent of the officials of the bank. *Employers' Liability Assurance Corporation v. Stanley Deposit Bank*, 149 Ky. 735, 149 S. W. 1025.

word "immediately" is used in the sense of "promptly," and a notice given eight days after the last item of material or labor is furnished, is not sufficient. *Wolflin-Luhring Lumber Co. v. Mosely*, 152 Ky. 701, 154 S. W. 22.

"The word 'immediately' is used in the statute in question with reference to time. Ordinarily it is defined as 'instantaneously,' 'instanter,' 'instantly,' 'forthwith,' 'without the intervention of time,' 'without the lapse of any appreciable time.' However, it is not a word of very precise signification, and is rarely employed to designate an exact portion of time. Thus, it is often construed to mean 'within a reasonable time.' *Solomon v. Continental Fire Insurance Company*, 160 N. Y. 595, 46 L. R. A. 682; or 'within a reasonable time under all the facts and circumstances of the case.'" *Peoples Accident Association v. Smith*, 126 Pa. St. 317, 12 Am. St. Rep. 870. It is also held to mean 'within such convenient time as is reasonably requisite.' *Martin v. Pifer*, 96 Ind. 245; or 'may be reasonably necessary under the circumstances to do the thing required.' *Foster v. New York Fidelity, etc. Co.*, 99 Wis. 447, 40 L. R. A. 833; *Woodmen Accident Association v. Pratt*, 62 Neb. 673, 89 Am. St. Rep. 777, 55 L. R. A. 291. Again, it is held to mean 'without any delay except such as is necessary in the usual course of the particular business in hand.' *Inman, etc., v. Barnum*, 115 Ga. 117. The broader meaning of 'within a reasonable time under all the facts and circumstances of the case' is usually applied in the case of accident insurance policies requiring immediate notice of the accident to be given. In this character of cases the policy is construed most favorably to the insured. While it is true that the word 'immediately,' as used in the statute in question, is not used in the narrow sense of 'instantaneously,' it is evidence that it is not used in the enlarged sense of 'within a reasonable time.' Our conclusion is that it was used in the sense of "promptly" and without attempting to fix a precise time within which the notice should have been given, it is sufficient to say that the notice of December 21st, which was given eight days after the last item of the material was furnished, was not given 'immediately' within the meaning of the statute." *Wolflin-Luhring Lumber Co. v. Mosely*, 152 Ky. 701, 154 S. W. 22. Fol-

**IMPAIRING.**—The word impairing in the Constitution does not mean destroying; consequently every law which weakens the obligation of a contract previously made, or renders it less operative, or so changes the remedy as to impair the obligation, is a violation of the constitution. *Lapsley v. Brashears*, 4 Litt. 53; *Berry v. Ransdell*, 4 Met. 294.

"No *ex post facto* law, nor any law impairing the obligation of contracts, shall be enacted." Ky. Constitution (1891), Sec. 19.

We have in Sec. 19, of the Constitution of the State a clause declaring that "no law impairing the obligation of contract, shall be enacted." But, this provision can only be relied upon or be effective when private contracts or legislative enactments of the State are called in question as it does not and could not apply to changes made in the Constitution of the State, and so, when in the obligations of a contract to which the State is a party the Constitution of the United States must be invoked to protect the contract, and reliance had on that provision in Sec. 10, declaring that: "No State shall pass any bill of attainder, *ex post facto* law, or law impairing the obligation of contracts." *German Ins. Co. v. Com.*, 141 Ky. 611, 133 S. W. 793. See, also, **OBLIGATION OF CONTRACTS**.

**IMPAIRMENT OF EARNING CAPACITY.**—Where a minor sued by his sister as his next friend for damages for loss of time and for the impairment of his earning capacity, and the record failed to show that the minor's father had actual notice of the suit, or that he failed to interpose any objection before trial and judgment of the son's suit, there was no waiver by the father of his right to claim or sue for damages for the son's loss of time, or an impairment of his earning capacity during his minority. *Helm v. Phelps*, 157 Ky. 795, 164 S. W. 92. See, also, **PERMANENT IMPAIRMENT OF EARNING POWER**.

" It is true thatin casesof executed contracts, if the parties
be in pari delicto,they willbe leftwhere they have placed them

IN PARI DELICTO 1687 IN PARI DELICTO
selves. They do not come into court with clean hands . If,
however, one party is but an instrument in the hands of the
other, then they are not in pari delicto. Judge Story says:
'One party may act under circumstancesof oppression, impo-
sition, hardship, undue influence, or greatinequalityof condi-

thatof his associatesin the offense.' In such a casethey are,

indeed, be substantiallythatof the one party. Thus the law
forbidsthepayment of usury;but ifthe borrowerseeksequity
forrelief, it willbe afforded; or if he has paid it, he may
recoverit back. The rule particeps criminisdoes not apply.
He isnot in pari delicto. He is the slave of the lender- is in
vinculis; and must submittohisnecessities." Harper v.Harper,
85 Ky . 165.
A vendee,not knowing that the land purchased was adversely
held, is not in pari delicto. Breckinridge v . Moore , 3 В. М.
637 .
Where parties are equally concerned in illegalagreements
or transactions, whether mala prohibitaor mala in se, courts
of equity, likecourts of law, will not interposeto grant relief

in pari delicto, no rightof actioncan be predicatedthereon by

The general rule is that the law will leave persons in pari
delictoin the same conditionthat it found them , not affording
to eitherreliefagainstor protectionfrom the other, but where
partiesoccupy toward each otherclosepersonaland businessrela-
tions, one of them willnot be allowed to take advantage of his
positionand influenceand the confidencereposed in him by the

Breyfogle v .Bowman , 157 Ky . 62, 162 S. W. 787.
Generallyin casesof executedcontracts,where both parties

to either. There isthough to thisrule an exception, where the
mind of one of the actors exercises an undue influence on the
other. Anderson v . Meredith, 82 Ky .564,6 R. 622.
" The law , both in this country and in England , now un-
doubtedlyis, in general, that where partiesare equallycon-
cerned in illegalagreements or transactions,whether such agree-
ments or transactionsbe mala prohibitaor mala in se, courts
of equity, likecourts of law , will not interposeto grant relief

606 .

chaser from him can not recover from the company or itssuc-
cessorson account of injury to the remainder of the land from
the proper use of a water tank constructedon the rightof way .
IllinoisCent. R. Co. v . Hodge , 55 S. W. 688, 21 R. 1479.
Ky . Stats., Sec. 836, provides that the commissioners shall
award to the owner " the value of the land or material taken,
which shallbe statedseparately; and they shallalso award the
damages , if any , resultingto the adjacent lands of the owner ,
consideringthe purposes for which itis taken;but shalldeduct
from such incidentaldamages the value, if any , of the advant-
ages and benefitsthat willaccrue to such adjacent lands from
the constructionand prudent operationof the railroadproposed
to be constructed ."
INCLUDING .- Where the lesseeof a road undertook to per-
form each and all stipulationsof every contract theretofore
enteredintoby the lessor, " including" certaindesignatedcon-
tracts, " as well as such other contractsas are not specifically
mentioned herein," the obligationof the lesseewas not limited
to contractsof the lessorof the same kind as those specifically
mentioned, but includeda contractby the lessorto give to
another a pass over its road during his life, and to secure to
him this right in the event there should be a change in the
ownership of the road. LouisvilleSouthern R. Co. v .Ragland ,
15 R. 814 .
INCLUDING FRAME ADDITION . A policyof insurance
on a brick building, includingframe addition," embraces only
the frame attachedto the brick and forming a continuous struc-
ture, and not a separate building twenty feet away . Franklin
Fire Ins. Co. v .Hellerick, 49 S. W. 1066, 20 R. 1703 .
In an action on a policy on a brick building, " including
frame addition," evidence as to the property intended to be
coveredis not admissible, therebeing no ambiguity and no
allegationof fraud or mistake. Franklin Fire Ins Co. v.
Hellerick, 49 S. W. 1066, 20 R. 1703.

INCLUSIO UNIUS 1702 INCOMPATIBLE
INCLUSIO UNIUS EST EXCLUSIO ALTERIUS .- The in-
clusion of one is the exclusion of another . Cochran's Law
Lexicon .
INCOME . The privilegegiven by a corporationto itsstock-
holders to take, additionalstock at par, when the stock is worth
more , can not be regarded as income, and thereforedoes not
belong to the lifetenant. Hite v. Hite, 93 Ky . 257, 14 R. 385.
The rule that a devise of the income of personal property
passes the principalapplies only in the absence of anything
in the willindicatinga contrary intention. It does not apply
where the testatorplainly indicatesthat only a part of the
corpus is to be used, if necessaryto make the deviseecom-
fortable. Watkins ' Admr . v . Watkins ' Exrs ., 120 S. W. 341.
Storage accounts not income or rent. Storage accounts that
are assessableat the assessingperiod are accrued and not accru-
ing accounts; and it is the earned and accrued charge that is
the subject of assessment and not the storage that may be
earnedin any succeedingyear. Com .v.Ky .D. & W. Co., 143
Ky . 315, 136 S. W. 1032.
INCOME AND REVENUE .- Under Ky . Const., Sec. 157 ,
" income and revenue" includescollectibledelinquent taxes of
previousyears. Overallv. City of Madisonville, 125 Ky . 684,
102 S. W. 278,31 R. 278,12 L. R. A. (N.S.) 433.
Fines and license fees are too uncertain and indefinite to
be estimatedin the beginning of the year as part of the income
of thatyear. Overallv. City of Madisonville, 125 Ky . 684,
102 S. W. 278, 31 R. 278, 12 L. R. A. (N.S.) 433.
INCOMMODUM NON SOLVIT ARGUMENTUM . Incon-
veniencedoes not destroyan argument. Cochran'sLaw Lex-
icon .
INCOMPATIBLE OFFICES . " The officesof justiceof the
peace, county judge, surveyor, sheriff, deputy sheriff, coroner,
constable,jailer,assessor,marshalof a town and clerkor deputy
clerkof a court, shallbe deemed incompatible, the one with
eitherof the others; and the officeof countyjudge and school
superintendentare incompatible. Nor shallany person, at the

INCOMPETENCY INCOMPETENT1703
same time, be a State officeror a deputy officer, or member of
theGeneral Assembly , and an officerof any county, city, town ,
or other municipality, or an employe thereof. And no person
shall, at the same time, filltwo municipal offices, eitherin the
same or differentmunicipalities; nor shall any person, at the
same time, filla municipal officeand a county office." Ky .
Stats. (1915), Sec. 3746.
" The acceptance by one in officeof another office, or em-
ploymentsincompatiblewith the one he holds, shalloperate
tovacatethe first." Ky .Stats. (1915), Sec.3744.
What officesare incompatible. Ky . Constitution(1891),
Secs.165,237 .
One holding the officeof city councilman vacates his office
by accepting the appointment of health officerof the county,
as under Ky . Stats., Secs.3744 and 3746, the two officesare in-
compatible. Vickers v . Sory, 102 S. W. 272, 31 R. 277.
The officesof postmaster and school trusteeare, under the
law, incompatible, and both can not be held at the same time
by one person. Johnson v. Sanders, 131 Ky . 537, 115 S. W.
772 .
The officeof county commissionerisnot incompatiblewith
thatof a county judge, within the meaning of Sec. 165 of the
Constitution. Vogt v. Beauchamp , 153 Ky . 64, 154 S. W. 393.
INCOMPETENCY OF FELLOW SERVANT .- A servant
may recoverfor injury caused by incompetency of fellow serv-
ant,of which complaint had been made , and assurance of com-
petency given, unless the danger of continuing the work with

prudent man would not have continued the work . Bell Cog-
geshallCo. v. Lewis, 89 S. W. 135, 28 R. 149; Standard Oil
Co.v.Leach, 138 Ky .594,128 S. W. 885; Crane v. Congleton
& Bro ., 116 S. W. 341. See, also, FELLOW SERVANTS , in this
book .
INCOMPETENT PERSONS .-Ky .Stats.,Sec.2156,author-
izingproceedingsto inquireintothe stateof personsalleged
to be of unsound mind or imbecileor incompetent to manage

theirestates, and to appointa committee, appliesto persons
who are incompetent by reason of mental unsoundness or
imbecility,and not to incompetencyby mere physicalinfirmity
because of sickness, infancy, or age. Upton's Committee v.
Bush , 135 Ky .102,121 S. W. 1005.
Power of courtsto appointcommitteesfor. Ky . Stats., Sec.
2149 .
INCONSISTENT ALLEGATIONS .- Two ormore pleasmay
be made if allof them may be shown to be true, and are incon-
sistentonly when the proving of one necessarilydisproves the
other. It isonlywhen a pleaisa directand unequivocalcon-
tradiction, in factas wellas in law, of anotherplea, thatthe
pleaswillbe inconsistent. Jonesv. Whitaker,141 Ky .484,133
S. W. 223 .
Under CivilCode , Sec.113 , where an administratorsued on

in considerationof care, lodging,etc., was not inconsistentwith
a counter claim for the reasonablevalue of his care, lodging,
etc. Tapp's Admr . v. Poindexter, 128 S. W. 594.
Under the rule now in force, very great latitudeis allowed
a defendant in the number and characterof defenses he may
interposeto an action. For the purpose of determining whether
defenses are inconsistentor not, the law dividesthem into two
classes: first, those which are inconsistentand contradictoryin
pointof fact; second,thosewhich are merelytechnicallyincon-
sistentby implicationof law . Where the defensesinvolvemere
logicalinconsistenciesby implicationof law, they may be
pleadedtogether; but defensescontradictoryor repugnant in
factcan not be joined. Caruso v.Brown , 142 Ky . 76, 133 S.
W. 948 .
In otherwords, a defendantwillonly be requiredto elect
between defenseswhere the factsstatedin the pleadings are so
inconsistentthat if the truth of one defense be admitted it
would disprove the other. Id .
In an actionon a note, defendant answered that the note was
not dated when executed, thatno due date was fixed, thatthe
note was given in renewalwithoutknowledge by defendant's

sureties, and that they had been discharged by a previous

pleaded that the suretieswere discharged by an extension of

138 S. W. 246 .
In an action on notes given for the differencebetween the
priceof a new and an old engine, defendant in one paragraph
setup a breach of warranty,and in another paragraph pleaded
a settlement. Held , that the defensesare not inconsistent. Rob-
inson& Co. v .Hill, 66 S. W. 623, 23 R. 2095.
Plaintiffsued for the infringementof a trade-mark , and
asked for an injunction and an account of profitsmade by
defendant, and damages for the simulationof plaintiff'sgoods.
Held, that defendant'smotion to requireplaintiffto elect
whether he would prosecutethe claim for profitsor for damages
shouldbe sustained. E. H. Taylor, Jr., v. Taylor, 85 S. W.
1085, 27 R. 625.
A traverse of an alleged agreement establishinga division

89 S. W. 12, 28 R. 22.
See,also,Sec.63,infra.
Where ,in an actionby plaintifffor thepriceof goods sold,

as a counterclaimdamages for delayto the goods, was incon-
sistentwith the firstanswer, and defendant should have been

Sons v .Paragon PlasterCo., 124 S. W. 268 .
In an actionto recoverthepriceon jewelry,where defendant
in his answer denied that he made the contract sued on except
as thereaftersetout, and in a subsequentpart of his answer
statedthe factsas to what contractwas made , the answer was

" Permanent inabilityto labor" isnot the preciseequivalent

Personal inconvenienceand annoyance are elements of com-

The questionof inconveniencecausedby the lossof a home-

ation under the name alleged. Any evidence tending to show

An independent contractoris one " who renders service in
thecourse of an occupationrepresentingthe willof his employer
only as to the resultof his work , and not as to the means by
which it is accomplished." Jahn's Admr . v . McKnight &
Co., 117 Ky . 655, 78 S. W. 862, 25 R. 1758, quoted approv-

An independent contractoris one who , carrying on an inde-
pendent business,contractsto do a pieceof work accordingtohis
own methods , and without being subject to the controlof his
employer as to the means by which the resultis to be accom-
plished, but only as to the result of the work . Williams v .

The rule is that one who contractsto do a specificpiece of
work, furnishing his own assistants, and executing the work
entirelyin accordance with his own ideas, or in accordance with
the plan previouslygiven to him by the person for whom the
work is done , without being subjectto the orders of the latter

tractor, and not a servant. See v . Leidecker, 152 Ky . 724, 154
S. W. 10 .

ingthework eitherentirelyin accordancewith hisown ideasor
in accordance with the plan previouslygiven to him by the per-
son for whom the work isdone, without being subjectto the or-
ders of the latterin respectto the detailsof the work ." Mason
& Hoge Co. v .Highland , 116 S. W. 320.
While the one for whom the work isdone may not directthe
manner in which the work isto be done without destroyingthe

may give to the work a general supervision, eitherin person or
throughengineersof hisselection,toseethatitisdone in strict

Ordinarily, where one employs anotherto executea pieceof

of the employe and no rightto directthemanner in which the

To thisrulethereare the followingwell-recognizedexcep-
tions: First,iftheemployerisnegligentin theselectionof his

self on the ground that the one to whom he has let it is an

acterof the businessundertaking isof such a hazardous nature

he cannotescapeliabilityon the ground thatthe work isdone

tor; and the men working under him are employes of the com-
pany within the meaning of that term as used in a policy of
indemnityinsurance. Employers' Indemnity Co. of Philadel-
phiav.Kelly Coal Co., 156 Ky . 74,16C S. W. 914.
Where the manner and method of mining coal is subject
to the controlof the master, one who is paid by the ton is not
an independent contractor. InterstateCoal Co. v. Trivett, 155
Ky . 825 , 160 S. W. 728 .

INDEPENDENT CON . 1728 INDEPENDENT CON .
A miner who gets out coal by the ton under the direction
and supervisionof thecoalcompany isnot an independentcon-
tractoralthough he pays his own assistants, and their pay is
taken out of his. InterstateCoal Co. v . Trivett, 155 Ky . 795,
160 S. W. 731 .
If the landlord voluntarilyundertakes to repair the leased
premises and employs a contractorto do the work , he will be
liableto the tenant for damages sustained by the negligence
of the contractor, althoughthe tenantmay have consentedto
the making of the repairsand the contractormay have been
what is usually calledan independent contractor. Michael &
Bro .v .BillingsPrinting Co., 150 Ky . 253,150 S : W. 77.
An employer is not relievedof liabilityby employing an
independent contractorto do work where the statuterequires
the employer to do the work in a certainmanner , or where it
is contrary to law or creates a nuisance. L. & N. R. Co. v .
Smith'sAdmr .,134 Ky .47,119 S. W. 241.
The court, in this case, held that a contractordoing work
under the supervisionof a railroadcompany was an independ-
ent contractor, because " the railroadcompany had no control
or authorityover him in the conduct of saidwork , further than
to see that it was done according to specificationswhich ithad
furnished him ."
See, also, M. , H. & E. R. Co. v . Owen , 147 Ky . 1, 143 S.
W. 421 .
" There are certainwell-definedcaseswhere,from thenature
of the undertakingor the characterof the employment, an
employer cannot relievehimselffrom liabilityon the ground
that the work was done by an independent contractor. These
cases for convenience may be grouped into fiveclasses: (1)
Where a statuteimposes a duty upon the employer to perform
the work in a given manner ; (2) where the undertaking is
a matter of contract,imposingupon the employera fixedduty;
(3) where itiscontraryto law; (4) where itcreatesa nui-
sance; and (5 ) where , from the nature and surroundings of the
work proposed, itsexecutionis necessarilyattended with such
hazardsas thatinjuriousresultsmay reasonablybe expected

INDEPENDENT COV . 1729 INDEPENDENT TELE .
to follow." L. & N. R. Co. v. Smith's Admr ., 134 Ky . 56, 119
S. W. 241 .
A contractoremployed by a city to build an engine house,
the contractstipulatingthat the work was to be done by him
" under directionof the committees of the firedepartment and
publicbuildings, who shallhave entirecontrolover the manner
of doing or shaping alland every part of said work ," can not
be regarded as an independent contractorso as to relievethe
city from liabilityfor injuriesresultingfrom his negligent
performance of the work . City of Covington v . Geyler, 93 Ky .
275, 12 R. 466 .
The owner of a building is not liablefor negligence of an
independent contractorin the manner of doing the work . Bel-
lamy v .F. A. Ames Co., 140 Ky . 98, 130 S. W. 980.
Who are independent contractors. See note on this subject
in 65 L. R. A. 445, 17 L. R. A. (N.S.) 371.
INDEPENDENT COVENANT .- An independent covenant
iswhere a promise goes only to a part of the considerationon
both sides, and a breach of a covenant of that kind may be
compensatedforin damages,and theparty suingforthebreach
may maintain his suitwithout averring the performance of the
otherconditions, and a failureto perform an independent cove-
nant will not prevent recovery from the other party who has
broken his covenant. Big Run Coal Co. v . Employers ' Indem-
nity Co., 163 Ky . 596, 174 S. W. 25.
INDEPENDENT RECOLLECTION . “ A witnessmay look
ata paper not made by himself,but by some otherperson, for
thepurpose of refreshinghis recollectionas to some past event;
but the paper must be used alone to refresh his memory and
he can not testifyas to any factssetforthin suchpaper unless
he has an independent recollectionof such facts- a recollection
independentfrom the paper." InternationalHarvesterCo. of
America v . Com .,147 Ky . 557, 144 S. W. 1070.
INDEPENDENT TELEPHONE COMPANY . An indepen-
dent telephone company is one not using the Bell patents.
CampbellsvilleTel. Co. v. Lebanon, etc., Tel. Co., 118 Ky . 277,
80 S. W. 1114 .

INDEPENDENTLY 1730 INDETERMINATE
INDEPENDENTLY OF ALL OTHER CAUSES .- In an
actionon a policyinsuringagainstdisablementby diabetes
" independentlyof allother causes," itwas not errorto instruct
thatthejury shouldfindforplaintiffifhe was whollydisabled
and prevented by diabetes from transactinghis business, as
plaintiffwould be entitledto recover if the diabetes disabled
him , though he had some other malady . TravelersIns. Co. v .
Duvall ,74 S. W. 740,25 R. 137.
INDETERMINATE SENTENCE LAW .- The Indeterminate
Sentence Law contemplatesthe reformationas well as the pun-
ishment of the criminal. Board of Prison Commissioners v.De
Moss ,157 Ky . 289,163 S. W. 183.
Under the Indeterminate Sentence Law of 1914 (Ky . Stats.,
Sec. 1136 ), the verdict of the jury must fixa minimum and
a maximum sentence, and the judgment must conform to the
verdict. Gardner v . Com ., 164 Ky . 196, 175 S. W. 362.
A verdictfixinga sentencefor hog stealingat " not lessthan
one year and not more than one year in the stateprison" does
not conform to the Indeterminate Sentence Law . Stephens v .
Com ., 164 Ky . 265, 175 S. W. 353.
Under the IndeterminateSentenceAct of 1914 itis preju-
dicialerror to authorizethe jury in the instructionsto fix a
definiteterm of imprisonment for defendant. Under that Act
the judgment of the court must conform to the verdictof the
jury. Adams v . Com ., 164 Ky . 148, 175 S. W. 10.
Under the Indeterminate Sentence Law the jury, afterbeing
firstadvised of the minimum and maximum punishment fixed
by law , should be instructedif they find the defendant guilty
to fixa minimum sentencefor any time in theirdiscretionunder
the maximum and not less than the minimum sentence fixed
by law and a maximum sentencefor any time in theirdiscretion
greaterthan the minimum sentencebut not more than the maxi-
mum punishment fixedby law. Biggs v. Com ., 162 Ky . 103,
172 S. W. 117 .
Under Sec. 1136, of the Ky . Stats., the jury and not the
judge must fixtheindeterminatesentence. Satterfieldv. Com .,
165 Ky . 216, 176 S. W. 980 .

INDETERMINATE 1731 INDEX
A verdictfixingdefendant'spunishment at not lessthan one
year nor more than one year in the penitentiary, does not fix
an indefinitesentence within the purview of the Indeterminate
SentenceLaw, Ky .Stats., Sec. 1136. Klettev. Com ., 165 Ку .
430, 177 S. W. 258 .
The Court of Appeals has preparedthe followinginstruc-
tions: " If the jury believefrom the evidence beyond a reason-
abledoubt that the defendant, in Kenton County , Kentucky ,
beforethe findingof the indictmentherein,did unlawfully take,
stealand drive away one red cow with three teats, the personal
property of Paul W Flynn , of the value of twenty dollarsor
more, with the feloniousand fraudulent intentthen and there
of convertingthe said cow to his own use and of permanently
depriving the said owner of his property therein without his
consent, they willfindthe defendantguiltyof grand larceny
and fixhis punishment at confinement in the penitentiaryfor
an indefiniteterm, to be fixedby the jury and statedin the
verdict, fixingthe minimum or shortestperiod of said sentence
for any time in the discretionof the jury, so that it be less
than the maximum so fixedby them , but not lessthan one year ;
and fixingthe maximum or longestperiod of said sentence for
any time in theirdiscretion, so thatitbe more than the mini-
mum so fixedby the jury, but not more than fiveyears."
Klettev .Com ., 165 Ку .431, 177 S. W.. 258 .
INDEX . A partialindex found in about the middle of
the record on appeal is not a compliance with the statute
requiringsuch record to be indexed. L. N. A. & C. Ry . Co. v .
Schick, 94 Ky . 191, 14 R. 833.
A general index of conveyanceswhich does not show the name
of the grantor to the grantee and that of the grantee from the
grantor, under the initialletterof the party's surname , is not
suchindexas thelaw requires. Smith v.Chapman ,153 Ky .70,
154 S. W. 915 .
The failureto learn of the existenceof an unindexed deed,
judgment or Act of the Legislatureisnot such negligenceas
willdebar a party from a new trial,upon itsdiscovery, ifit be
controllingin itseffect. CallahanConstructionCo. v.Thomas,
160Ky .496,169 S..W. 828.

INDEX ANIMI INDICTMENT1732
A county clerkwho indexesa mortgage from L. O. Stephen-
son to Jennie P. Stevenson as from " L. O. Stephens" to " Jen-
nieP. Stephens" is guiltyof negligencewhich willrenderthe
suretyon the clerk'sbond liablein damages. TitleGuaranty
& Surety Co.v. Com .,141 Ky .570,133 S. W. 577.
A partialdischargeof duty is, as to the omittedpart, a
neglectof duty . The indexing of deeds one way does not sat-
isfythe requirementsof thelaw. Smith v. Chapman , 153 Ky .
70 , 154 S. W. 915 .
INDEX ANIMI SERMO .- Speech is the index of the mind .
Cochran's Law Lexicon .
INDICTABLE OFFENSES.- " All publicoffensesmay be
prosecutedby indictment, except- 1. Offensesof publicoffi-
cers,where a differentmode ofprocedureisprescribedby law;
2. Offensesexclusivelywithin the jurisdictionof justicesof
the peace,or of policeor citycourts; 3. Offensesarisingin the
militia, of which a militarycourthas exclusivejurisdiction."
Crim . Code , Sec. 9 .
As Sec.12,Ky . Constitution,declaringthat" no personfor
an indictableoffenseshallbe proceededagainstby information,
except," etc., appliesonly to offenseswhich were indictableat
common law,Sec.1073,etseq.,Ky .Stats.,providingthatwhen the
circuitcourt isnot in sessionpersons charged with misdemean-
ors and lodged in jailin default of bail shallbe triedby the
countyjudge, etc., are not in conflictwith saidconstitutional
provision. Lowry v. Com ., 36 S. W. 1117,18 R. 481.
An actionto recovermoney lostat gaming is not an " indict-
ableoffense." Com .v.Prall,146 Ky .109, 142 S. W. 202.
INDICTMENT .- " An indictmentisan accusationin writing,
found and presented by a grand jury to the court in which
they are empaneled , charging a person with the commission of
a public offense." Crim .Code ,Sec.118 .
" The indictment must be presented by the foreman , in the
presence of the grand jury, to the court, and filedwith the
clerk,and remaininhisofficeas a publicrecord." Crim.Code,
Sec . 121 .
Code provisionas to what an indictmentmust contain. Crim .
Code , Sec.122 .

INDICTMENT INDICTMENT1733
Form of indictmentisgivenin Crim. Code, Sec.123.
" The essentialthings are that the indictment shallcontain
(a) the name of the party charged; (b) the offensecharged;
(c) thecountyin which itwas committed; (d ) and a statement
of the actsconstitutingthe offense, in ordinary and conciselan-
guage and in such a manner as to enable a person of common
understanding to know what is intended, and with such degree
of certaintyas to enable the court to pronounce judgment on
convictionaccording to the rightsof the case." Head v. Com .,
165 Ky .342, 176 S. W. 1162 , quoting from Overstreetv . Com .,
147 Ky .471 ,144 S. W. 751.
An indictmentmay contain more than is necessaryor itmay
be phrased in inapt words , or the sentencesbe ungrammatically
expressedor the spellingnot good ; but, if when considered as
a whole the charge is stated with sufficientclearnessand cer-
taintyto enable the accused to know what he is charged with,
and toenablethecourttopronouncejudgment,no errorinform
of expression will make the indictment bad . In considering
the sufficiencyof an indictment it willbe read as a whole ; and
ifwhen so read it substantiallyconforms to the requirements
of the Code in respect to material and substantialmatters, it
willbe a good indictment. Overstreetv. Com ., 147 Ky . 471,
144 S. W. 751 .
The purpose of an indictment is to advise the accused of
the offensewith which he stands charged and for which he is
to be tried. Daniel v . Com .,154 Ky . 601, 157 S. W. 1127.
A proceedingby indictmentis an actionat law. Com . v.
Spiller, 165 Ky . 761.
An indictment can only be found and presented by a grand
jury, and thereforeno amendment of an indictment can be
allowed. But a warrant which issuesupon information may be
amended in the same manner as a warrant in a civilcase. Com .
v. Vanmeter , 8 Ky . Opin .755.
The courtisnot inclinedto favorhypertechnicalcriticisms
of indictments. Rutland v .Com ., 160 Ky . 77, 169 S. W. 584.
Under Sec.793, Ky .Stats., providingthatthe fineimposed
on railway companies for failureto give the statutory signal
atgrade crossings(Sec 786 ) is" to be recoveredby indictment,"

INDICTMENT INDICTMENT1734
such finecan onlybe recoveredon indictment,though Sec.1139
providesthatallfinesmay be recoveredby civilproceedings
or on an indictment. Com . v . L. & N. R. Co., 37 S. W. 589,
18 R 610 .
An indictment is not necessary in the prosecutionof a mis-
demeanor which was not an indictable offense at the common
law. Ford v.Moss ,98 S. W. 1015,30 R. 428 .
When the statutedoes not prescribethe form of indictment
or the words that shallbe used, it is only necessarythat it con-
form to thegeneralrulesof law applicableto such indictments,
and be soplainand explicitthattherecan not be any reason-
able doubt as to itsmeaning . Smith v . Com ., 141 Ky .534, 133
S. W. 228 .
The U. S. Constitution, providingthat no person shallbe
held to answer for a capital or otherwise infamous crime,
unless on the presentment or indictmentof a grand jury,has no
applicationto prosecutionsin the State courts. Jane v . Com .,
3 Met . 19 .
An indictmentin the words of the statuteisnot always suffi-
cient. Whether sufficientor not depends upon the manner of
stating the offensein the statute. If every fact necessary to
constitutethe offenseis charged, or necessarilyimplied by fol-
lowingthe languageof the statute,an indictmentin the words
of the statuteis sufficient; otherwise it is not. Com .v . Stout,
7 B. Mon. 247 ; Com . v . McCrory , 3 Ky . Law Rep . 241 ; Com .
v. Shaurer, 4 Ky .Law Rep .343 ; Com .v .Gregory ,121 Ky .458 ,
89 S. W. 477; Com .v. White, 109 S. W. 324; Tudor v.Com .,
134 Ky . 186, 119 S. W. 816 ; Com . v . Barney , 115 Ky . 475 ;
Com .v. Lowe, 116 Ky .338; Com .v.Huff, 141 Ky .459; Lan-
casterHotel Co. v . Com ., 149 Ky . 443, 149 S. W. 942 ; Carroll
v . Com ., 164 Ky .599, 175 S. W. 1043.
A prosecution by indictment is a litigationin which the
State is plaintiffor complainant, and is representedby the
Commonwealth's Attorney . The judge does not represent the
State, any more than he doesthedefendantin theprosecution.
His right to controlthe prosecutiongoes only to the extent of
determining whether or not the indictmentisgood on demurrer .
If he holds it to be a good indictment he is without power to

INDIFFERENT INDISPENSABLE1735
directitsdismissal. Com . v. Cundiff, 149 Ky . 37, 147 S. W.
767 .
Sec. 120 of the Crim . Code of Practice, which requiresthat
when an indictmentisfound ,the names of allthe witnesseswho
were examined by the grand jury must be written at the foot
of,or on the indictment,doesnot renderincompetentotherwit-
nesses for the Commonwealth whose names were not in a sub-
pœna or at the foot of the indictment. The purpose of this
statute is to inform the defendant of the names of his accusers ;
itis directory, and ought to be complied with; but a failureto
do so neither invalidatesthe indictment, nor prevents the intro-
duction of witnesseswhose names do not so appear. Hendrick-
son v .Com ., 146 Ky .742,143 S. W. 433.
For forms in indictments, with annotations, see Kentucky
Form Book ,Forms Nos . 1764, 1872 .
Many of the paragraphs under this titleare taken, by per-
mission, from Digest Kentucky Reports (Morton & Co.).
INDIFFERENT .-Negligence or carelessnesssignifieswant
of care, caution, attention, diligence, or discretionin one hav-
ingno positiveintentiontoinjurethepersoncomplaining. The
words, " reckless," " indifferent," " careless," and "wanton"
are never understood to signifypositivewillor intention, unless
when joined with other words which show that they are to
receivean artificialor unusual, if not an unnatural, interpreta-
tion. Lexington v . Lewis,10 Bush , 679.
INDIGNITY . For note on meaning of thisword, see 43
L. R. A. (N.S.) 260 .
INDIRECTION .- " What is prohibitedcan not be done by
indirection, any more than it could have been by directviola-
tionof the constitution." Com .v.LouisvillePropertyCo.,139
Ky . 603 , 121 S. W. 399, 132 S. W. 413.
INDISPENSABLE .- Where a person or corporationpays
a licensetax for the privilegeof doing businessin a city, and
in carrying on the businessuses vehiclesthat are not indispen-
sableto the conductof itsbusiness,itmay be requiredto pay
the tax leviedby an ordinance upon allvehiclesin use in the

INDISPUTABLE INDORSE1736
city. Adams Express Co. v. Boldrick, 141 Ky . 111, 132 S. W.
174 .
The Adams Express Company obtained a licenseauthoriz-
ing it to do businessin the city of Louisville. In the conduct
of its business it deliveredin its wagons packages in certain
parts of the city, and in other partsit did not. The citysought
to tax the wagons under a general vehicletax. Held , that it
had the right to do this, as wagons , although used in the bus-
iness, were not indispensableto carrying it on . Id.
INDISPUTABLE .-A stipulationin an insurance policy
providing that " afterone year thispolicyshallbe indisputable
for the breach of any of the provisionshereof" means indis-
putable if the policy itselfis in force, and does not include a
provision avoiding the policy for nonpayment of premiums .
Noble v.SouthernStatesMutual LifeIns.Co.,157 Ky .46,162
S. W. 528 .
INDIVIDUAL LIBERTY.- " However comprehensive the
terms'individualliberty,' so frequentlymade use of,are, and
however broad the claim which may be advanced that everyone
may employ his time in a lawful undertaking as may best serve
his own interests, stillthe libertyreferredto is a relativeterm ,
and ,at most,means libertyregulatedby just and impartiallaws,
while all sortsof reasonablerestrictionsare imposed upon the
actions of men for the common welfare and good of society.
Statev.Freeman,38 N. H. 426." Hyman v.Boldrick,153 Ky .
77 , 154 S. W. 369.
INDORSEMENT .-See , also, ENDORSEMENT .
While technicallythere is a differencebetween the meaning
of the words " indorsement" and " suretyship," yet they are
popularlyused interchangeably, and where a debtor executes
a mortgage to secure one from lossby reason of any " indorse-
ment " made by him forthemortgagor, the word willbe given
itspopularmeaning. Josephv.Lyon,9 R. 324.
INDORSE AND RENEW .- Authorityto indorseand renew
any paper in which the principalis responsibleas the surety

INDORSER INDULGENCE1737
of P., and to render him liableas surety for same on any new
paper not exceeding $2,000 empowers the agent to bind his
principalas suretyof P. by the renewalof debtsthen existing,
and also to bind him in debts subsequently created by P. not
exceeding$2,000. Prestonv.Henning & Speed, 6 Bush , 558.
INDORSER .- See ENDORSER .
INDUCING.- See INVITING, PERSUADING OR INDUCING .
INDUCEMENT .- " It is the officeof the inducement to nar-
ratethe extrinsiccircumstanceswhich ,coupled with the language
published, affectsitsconstructionand rendersit actionable;
where standing alone and not thus explained, the language
would appear eithernot to concerntheplaintiff,or ifconcern-
ing him not to affecthim injuriously. This being the office
of the inducement, it followsthat if the language published
does not naturallyand per se refer to the plaintiff, nor convey
the meaning the plaintiffcontends for, or if it is ambiguous or
equivocal, and requiresexplanationby some extrinsicmatter
to itsrelationto theplaintiff, and make itactionable, the com-
plaintmust allege,by way of inducement, the existenceof such
extrinsicmatter." Newell on Libel and Slander, 2d Ed ., p .
603. Moore v. Johnson , 147 Ky . 584 , 144 S. W. 765 .
In an actionfor slander, whenever the words have a slander-
ous meaning alone,not by theirown intrinsicforcebut by rea-
son of the existenceof some extraneous fact, thisfact must be
averred in a traversableform , which averment is calledthe in-
ducement . Where the words charged to have been spoken are:
" You are a d- d old vote seller," the petitionisdefectivewhich
does not allege that there had been an electionand that the
plaintiffhad voted or failedto vote. Case v . Mackey , 13 R.
334 .
INDULGENCE . A receiptfor $80.00 for " indulgence" on
a note, where shown by the pleadings to have been given for
usury paid, and was for forbearance, would not releasethe
sureties,exceptfortheusury thuspaid. Davisonv.Gee,3 Ky .
Opin . 461 .
Forbearanceor passiveindulgenceby obligeewillnot exon-
eratea surety,though givenwithouthisknowledge or consent.

INDULGENCE INEBRIATES1738
Sneed v.White, 3 J. J. M. 526; Brinagarv. Phillips, 1 B. M.
285 ; Nicholsv.McDowell , 14 B. M. 7.
A contract for indulgence should be mutual and capable
of ascertainmentas to the time when it is to be performed , or
itwillbe construedas a contractto be performed then or within
a reasonabletime. Rasco v.Sheet, 8 R. 703 .
INEBRIATES .- The statute makes three requisites for
recovery in action for sale of liquor to inebriate: (1) That
there was a sellingor furnishing of the liquor; (2 ) That the
persongettingtheliquorwas an inebriateor habitualdrunkard;
(3 ) That itwas sold or furnished in violationof a notice for-
bidding it. Keyser v . Damron , 159 Ky . 444, 167 S. W. 381.
In an actionfor damages for the saleof liquorto an inebri-
ate, the chief requisiteof the action is the notice; if the sale
ismade in violationof the notice, it may be deemed a wilful
or malicious violationof it. For this reason, and by express
provision of the statute, a punitive damage instructionis
authorized. Keyser v . Damron , 159 Ky . 444, 167 S. W. 381.
An indictment for sellingliquor to an inebriate, under the
Act of 1878,must allegethat the defendant knew that the party
to whom the liquor was sold was an inebriate. Com . v . Bell,
14 Bush ,433 .
A city ordinance defininginebriates, and depriving a liquor
sellerof the defense that he did not " knowingly " sellto an
inebriate,held,void as in contraventionof Ky . Stats., Sec.1307.
Com . v . Preston, 165 Ky . 200, 176 S. W. 963.
In many of the States there are specialstatutescalled" civil
damage acts," which give in certaincases a right of action to
one injuredbecauseof theunlawfulsaleof intoxicatingliquors.
In thisState we have no such act. The nearestapproach thereto
is Sec. 1307 , which gives to the wife, or the father, or the
mother, or thechildof such inebriate,a rightof actionagainst
a person who , afterwrittennoticeforbiddinghim to do so,
" shallsell, lend,give, procure for,or furnish spirituous,vinous
or malt liquors, or any mixture of either, knowingly, to any
person who is an inebriateor in the habit of becoming intoxi-
cated or drunk ." Waller's Admr .v . Collinsworth, 144 Ку . 4,
137 S. W. 766 .

INEVITABLE CAS . 1739 INFANTS
INEVITABLE CASUALTY .- See, also, ACCIDENT; ACT OF
GOD .
A tenant is bound to pay the rent, and is entitledto no
abatement unless he, by his contract, provides against his lia-
bility, though the premises should be destroyed by inevitable
casualty. Redding v. Hall, 1 Bibb , 536 ; Harrison v. Murrell,
5Mon. 359;Proctorv.Keith,12 B. M. 254;Helburn v.Mofford,
7 Bush , 174.
Where tenant expressly covenants to restorethe premises
in repair, he will be bound to do so though the premises be
destroyedby inevitablecasualty. Redding v.Hall,1 Bibb,536.
Averment thatgoods were lostby unforeseen,inevitableacci-
dent, without averring why the accident was unforeseen and
inevitable, isnot good. Bentleyv.Bustard, 16 B. M. 686.
INFAMOUS . " The term 'infamous' has a technicalimport
more extensivethan mere degradationor reproach." Sodusky
v.McGee ,5 J. J. Mar. 621.
INFANTS . It isprovidedin CivilCode, Sec. 732.Subsec.
31, that in the constructionof the Code the word " disability"
means coverture, infancy, unsoundness of mind , or imprison-
ment in a penitentiaryof thisState or of any other country.
A person is fullage the day preceding the twenty-firstanni-
versary of his birth. Hamlin v. Stevenson, 4 Dana , 597 ;
Erwin v . Benton , 27 R. 915.
The privilegeof infancy is a shieldfor the protectionof the
infant, and not a weapon of attack; nor is it to be used as a
means of defraudingothers. Smith v. Cole, 148 Ky . 138, 146
S. W. 30 .
Infants under seven years of age are prima facie unac-
quaintedwith guilt,and cannotbe convicted,unlessat thetime
the offensewas committed they had a guilty knowledge that
theywere doingwrong. Willetv.Com .,13 Bush,230.
Between seven years and fourteen the Commonwealth may
rebutthe presumptionby showing a guiltyknowledge on the
part of the accused. Willetv. Com ., 13 Bush , 230.
The statuteprovidingthat if an infantdieswithoutissue,
having titleto realestatederivedby gift, devise, or descent

INFECTION INFERIOR STATE1740
from one of his parents, the whole shalldescend to that parent
and his or her kindred, etc., appliesonly to infantsborn in
lawful wedlock. Blankenship v .Ross, 95 Ky . 306, 15 R. 708 .
In the statute providing that certain articlesshall be set
apart for the widow and infantchildor children, the words
" infantchildor children," referto the infant childor children
of intestate,and do not include his wife'sinfant childrenby a
former marriage. Howland's Admr .v. Hart ,123 Ky .732 .
Infancy is a personalprivilege, which can only be taken
advantage of by the infant or his representativesor one who
is privy in estate. Cannon v . Alsbury, 1 Mar. 77 ; Beeler v .
Bullitt, 3 Mar. 281.
Contracts of infants. See titleVOID - VOIDABLE in this book .
INFECTION .- Death from infectedwound liabilityunder
accidentpolicy. Casualty Co. v . Taylor, 164 Ky . 786, 176 S.
W. 194 .
INFERENCE .- Where reasonablemen may reasonablydiffer
as to the inferenceto be drawn from the facts, the question
is for the jury although there may be practicallyno dispute
about the facts. Cincinnati, N. O. & T. P. Ry . Co. v . Black,
157 Ky . 149, 162 S. W. 800 .
Where proof isentirelycircumstantial, any logicalinference
that might be deduced from the circumstances shown may be
acceptedby thejury as a proven fact. C., N. O. & T. P. Ry .
Co. v . Falconer, 97 S. W.. 727, 30 R. 152.
In order to work an estoppel, the representationsreliedon
shouldbe plainand positive, such as would misleada person
of ordinaryprudence. It isnot sufficientthata partywas mis-
led by drawing an inferencenot warranted by the words used.
McClure's Exr . v. King , 126 Ky . 675, 104 S. W.. 711, 31 R.
1085 .
It is the peculiarprovinceof the jury to draw inferences
from proven facts. C., N. O. & T. P. Ry . Co. v. Veatch, 162
Ky .136 ,172 S. W. 89.
INFERIOR STATE OFFICERS .- Under Sec. 93, Constitu-

INHERENT DEFECT OR VICE . In the shipmentof live

and in a damaged or injured conditionwhen deliveredby the

" When in the opinion in 149 Ky . 229, we used the words
some inherentviceof theanimal,' we meant thesame thing

INJURY TO BUSINESS .- The owner of property abutting
on an alley is not entitledto compensation for injury to his
businesscaused by vacating the alley. Henderson v . City of
Lexington,132 Ky .390;33 R. 703;111 S. W. 318; 22 L. R. A.
(N.S.) 20 .
INLAND BILL OF EXCHANGE . " An inland bill of
exchange is a billwhich is, or on its face purports to be, both

INN 1754 INNOCENT HOLDER
drawn and payable within thisState. Any otherbillis a foreign
bill. Unlessthe contraryappearson the faceof the bill, the
holder may treat it as an inland bill." Negotiable Inst. Law ,
Ky . Stats. (1915) Sec.3720b, Subsec. 129.
INN .- Inn and tavernare the same, and may existwithout
license; licensedmay retailspirits; unlicensedsubjectto fineif
itbe done . Com .v . Shortridge, 3 J. J. M. 638.
Furnishing food to guests as an essentialcharacteristicof
a hotelor inn. See note on thissubjectin 17 L. R. A. (N. S.)
1259 .
INN -KEEPERS . An inn-keeper is one who makes it his
business to entertaintravelersor passengersand provide lodg-
ing and necessariesfor them and theirhorses and attendants;
itisnot necessarythat he should have a sign indicatingthathe
is an inn-keeper, nor isit necessarythathe have provisionsfor
horses. Kisten v .Hildebrand, 9 B. M. 74.
One keeping a boarding-house or a licensedcoffee-house may
occasionallyentertain travelerswithout incurring the liabili-
tiesof an inn-keeper. Kistenv.Hilderbrand, 9 B. M. 75.
Persons who keep a boarding-house or lodging-house, or even
one who keeps a house for lodging strangersfor a season as vis-
itorsto watering places, unlesshe be the keeper of a tavern,has
not the privilegeof retainingthe goods of his guests until his
charges for lodging,etc.,are paid. Southwood v .Myers ,3 Bush
682 .
Sleeping car companies are not held to the responsibilities
of inn-keepers. Myers v. Pullman Co., 149 Ky . 778 , 149 S. W.
1002 .
The common law lienof an inn-keeper to secure his pay for
keeping guests does not secure an account due him for board
under a specialcontract. Reed v. Teneyck, 103 Ky . 65, 44
S. W. 356, 19 R. 1690.
See, also, TAVERN KEEPER .
INNOCENT HOLDER .- In an actionagainstA. as endorser
of certainpromissorynotes, where the defenseinterposedwas
thatby fraud or mistakethe words " withoutrecourse" were

INNOCENT PARTIES 1755 INNOCENT PURCH .
omittedfrom the endorsement,evidence examined and held that
B. was an innocent holder for a valuable consideration, and
had a rightto rely upon the open endorsement of A. Gaertner
v.Kraft, 164 Ky .712, 176 S. W. 207.
INNOCENT PARTIES .— The rule is elementary that where
one of two innocent partiesmust sufferbecause of a mistake, if
ithas resultedfrom the negligenceof one of them, he must
bear the consequences. Bevins v . Coates, 96 S. W. 585, 29
R. 978 .
Where one of the two innocentpartiesmust suffer,he should
sufferwhose negligenceput it in the power of another to com-
mit the fraud. Bank of Commerce v .Haldeman , 109 Ky ., 297 ;
Diamond DistilleriesCo. v . Gott, 137 Ky ., 585. This doctrine
was followedin the caseof J. R. Watkins Medical Co. v.Brand ,
143 Ky ., 468; McConnon & Co. v.Evans, 152 Ky .491, 153 S.
W. 773 .
INNOCENT PURCHASER .-See , also, BONA FIDE PUR-
CHASER ; PURCHASER IN GOOD FAITH .
An assignee for creditorsis not an innocent purchaser for
value. Walker v . Walker's Assignee, 41 S. W. 315, 19 R. 626.
Purchasers and creditorsof heirs or deviseesof a grantor in
a deed which was not acknowledged or proved and lodged for
recordas prescribedby law are protectedby the act of 1858,
which extendstheprovisionsof Sec.9,Chap .24,Rev. Stats.,so
as to embrace " innocent purchasers and creditorsof the heirs
ordeviseesof any grantor in such deeds as to the land embraced
therein." Dozier v .Barnett,13 Bush , 457 .
The allegation in an answer that the defendant is an " inno-
centpurchaser" ismerelyan avermentof a conclusionof law.
Wing v.Hayden ,10 Bush, 277.
Attorney who had been employed to defend a former suit
brought to set asidea deed, and who afterwards purchased one
of the slavesconveyed, isnot an innocentpurchaser, and must
surrender or account for the property. Russell v . Russell, 4
Dana ,48 .
Where a specialwarranty isexactedby the granteeunder
such circumstancesas imply knowledgethat the propertywas

INNOCENT PURCH . 1756 INNUENDO
held in trust, the grantee is not an innocent purchaser entitled
to hold againstthe beneficiaryof the grantor. C. & L. R. Co. v .
Bowler , 9 Bush ,495.
A purchaser who by reasonable inquiry could have known
of the existenceof a previous vendor's lienwillnot be regarded
as an innocent purchaser without notice. Honore v . Bakewell,
6 В. М. 73 .
INNUENDO .- (By Hinting). That partof thepleadingin
an action for libelwhich goes to explain a connectionbetween
what is said in the allegedlibel, and certainpersons or things
not named or explicitlystated therein; whereby it is made to
appear that the actual statement is libelous. Cochran's Law
Lexicon .
" The officeof an innuendoisto explain,not to extend,what
has gone before, and it can not enlarge the meaning of words
unless it be connected with some matter of fact expressly
averred." To the same effectis Newell on Slander and Libel,
page 619; Craigv.Pyles, 101 Ky . 594; Watson v.Hampton ,2
Bibb ., 319 ; Peters v . Garth, 20 Ky . Law Rep ., 1934 ; Feast v .
Auer , 28 Ky . Law . Rep ., 794 ; Brown v. Piner, 6 Bush , 518 ;
Wooten v . Martin, 140 Ky ., 781 ; Moore v . Johnson, 147 Ky .
584 , 144 S. W. 765 .
The officeof an innuendo is to apply that which has been
said, but itcannot supply the want of a colloquium nor enlarge
or change the legal effectof the utterancesas alone properly
interpretedor understood. Caldwell v.Abbey , Hard . 530 ; Port-
ter v .Hughey ,2 Bibb , 232 ; Watson v .Hampton , 2 Bibb , 319 ;
Martin v .Melton,4 Bibb ,99 ;Beswick v . Chappel , 8 B. Μ .488 ;
Brown v .Piner, 6 Bush , 518.
An innuendo cannot extend the meaning of words beyond
theirnaturalimport. It is only explanatoryof some matter
already expressed. It may show the application, but cannot
add to or enlarge or change the senseof the words . Watson v .
Hampton , 2 Bibb . 319 ; Moore v . Johnson , 147 Ky . 584 ; Spears
v. McCoy 155 Ky . 1, 159 S. W. 610 ; Sturdivant v . Duke , 155
Ky .100,159 S. W. 621; Feast v.Auer ,90 S. W. 564, 28 R. 794 ,
4 L. R. A. (N.S.) 560.

INNUENDO 1757 INOPS CONSILII
While it is the officeof the innuendo to explain the words
spokenand to annex to them theirpropermeaning, itmay not
introducenew matter or enlargethe meaning of the words , nor
can it supply the place of a colloquium. Curtis v. Iseman , 137
Ky . 796, 127 S. W. 150.
The " innuendo" stands in the place of aforesaid, and can-
not be used to extend the meaning of words beyond their own
import, nor to make that certainwhich was before uncertain.
Caldwell v .Abbey , Hard . 530.
Under Sec. 123, Civil Code , it is sufficientto allegein an
actionfor libelthat the publicationwas intended to apply to
plaintiff, without statingany extrinsicfactsfor the purpose of
showing its applicationto him . LouisvillePress Co. v . Ten-
nelly, 105 Ky .365,49 S. W. 15, 20 R. 1231.
The averment that such words were intended to charge, and
were understood by those who heard them as charging, that
plaintiffwas an indecentand unchaste character, who indulged
in vilepractices, does not renderthem actionable, as an innu-
endo can not extend the meaning of words beyond their nat-
ural import. Petersv . Garth, 50 S. W. 682,20 R. 1934.
The innuendo can not give to the words used a different
meaning from that which they reasonably bear. Winsette v .
Hunt , 53 S. W. 522,21 R. 922.
Words not actionableper se cannot be made so by innuen-
does. Barr v. Gaines,3 Dana ,258; McNamara v. Shannon, 8
Bush,557, and Curtisv.Iseman, 137 Ky . 796; Holt v.Ashby,
150 Ky .612, 150 S. W. 810.
The natural and reasonablemeaning of words can not be
enlargedby an innuendo. Hall v. Huffman, 159 Ky . 72, 166
S. W. 770 .
Effect of innuendo on defense of truth to civil action for
libelor slander. See note on this subject in 31 L. R. A.
(N.S.) 140.
INOPS CONSILII .-Destitute of counsel. In the construc-
tion of willsgreaterlatitudeis given, because the testatoris
supposedto have been inopsconsilii. Cochran'sLaw Lexicon.

INQUEST OF LUN . 1758 INSANE DELUSION
INQUEST OF LUNACY .- An inquestof lunacyisa judicial
determination of the statusof the person charged with lunacy.
He may be committed to an asylum and a committee may be
appointed to take charge of his property. The inquest is con-
clusiveof the conditionof the lunatic'smind at the time, and is
thereforea judicialproceeding. Hickman County v . Jackson
153 Ky . 551, 156 S. W. 391.
Provision for inquests on idiots, lunatics and persons of
unsound mind . Ky . Stats., Sec. 2156.
INQUEST OF OFFICE FOUND .- See OFFICE FOUND .
INQUIRING INTO AND REPORTING ON CREDIT.-
SEE CREDIT REPORTING .
INQUISITORIAL POWER . If the inquisitorialpower
possessedby the InsuranceCommissionermay be exercisedin
examining the condition and business of a single insurance
company or itsagents to ascertainwhether ithas violatedthe
law, or becausethe protectionof the policyholdersrequires
such examination, it may alsobe exercisedby him, on like
grounds , in making an examination of an associationof insur-
ance companies, or an associationof the agents of such compa-
nies. Bell, Ins. Commissioner of Ky . v . LouisvilleBoard of
Fire Underwriters, 146 Ky . 841, 143 S. W. 388.
INSANE AVERSION .— It was error to instruct the jury
to find against the willif they believedthat testatorwas actu-
ated by an insane aversion toward some of his children, the
mere fact that he was not on friendlyterms with contestants
not being sufficientto authorizethe instruction. Powers ' Exr .
v.Powers ,52 S. W. 845,21 R. 597 .
INSANE DELUSION .- " An insane delusion is an idea or
beliefwhich springsspontaneously from a diseasedor perverted
mind without reason or without foundation in fact; it isdis-
tinguishablefrom a beliefwhich is founded upon prejudice or
aversion no matter how unreasonable or unfounded the prej-
udice or aversionmay be . If it is the product of a reasoning
mind no matter how slightthe evidence upon which it is based

INSANE DELUSION 1759 INSANE DELUSION
itcannot be classedas an insane delusion. In everyday life
men of the strongestminds , being in possessionof the same
facts, reach differentconclusions. Coffey v.Miller, 160 Ky .415,
169 S. W. 852 .
" Insane delusionshould be distinguishedfrom prejudice, or
error, as well as from eccentricity. It differsessentiallyfrom
some rationalbelief, not wellfounded, however perverselythe
testatormay have clung to it. An illfounded belief, not actu-
ally insane, does not destroy testamentary capacity. And
where one indulges in an aversion, however harsh, which is the
conclusionof a reasoning mind , on evidence, no matter how
slightor inaccurate, his will cannot be on that account over-
turned." Coffey v.Miller, 160 Ky . 415, 169 S. W. 852.
" It is important to bear in mind what an insane delusion
is, and what kind of an insane delusion willjustifythe refusal
of probate to so solemn and important an instrument as a will.
Prejudices, dislikesand antipathies, however ill-founded, or
however strongly entertained, can not be classed as insane
delusions; nor is every delusion an insane delusion. Whenever
one's mind is tricked and deceived into a false opinion or
belief, it has been played upon ; it is deluded. But an insane
delusion isthe spontaneous production of a diseasedmind lead-
ing to a beliefin the existenceof something which eitherdoes
not existor does not existin the manner believed." Purdy's
Admr . v . Evans , 156 Ky . 342, 160 S. W. 1071.
Unless the testator'sconduct proved the existence of an
insanedelusion, itwould be no reason for settingasidethe will.
Injustice, unfairness, prejudiceor anger without a reasonable
cause, does not disqualifya person from making a will.
Purdy'sAdmr .v.Evans,156 Ky .342,160 S. W. 1071.
aA delusion exhibitedby a testator's aversion toward
daughter isnot sufficientto invalidatehis will, where itwas not
a spontaneous production of his mind , but had some basis in
fact, however slightthe basis, or however imperfectthe proc-
essof his reasoningor however illogicalor unjusthisconclu-
sion that she was greatly at fault as his conduct toward her
would indicatehis beliefto have been. Purdy's Admr . v . Ev-
ans,156 Ky .342,160 S.W. 1071.

INSANE DELUSION 1760 INSANITY
A belieffounded upon evidence, however unsatisfactorythat
evidence may be to some other mind ,which measures it to test
itssufficiency, is not an insane delusion. Belief in spiritualism
isnot " insanedelusion." Raison v .Raison, Extx .,148 Ky .116,
146 S. W. 400 .
The fact that testatorbelievedin witchcraftdoes not justify
an assumption that he was insane and incompetent to make a
will; and an instructionon the question of mental capacity
which makes that fact more prominent than the other facts
relied on is erroneous . Schild v . Rompf , 9 R. 120 .
Insane delusionsdefined. See note on this subject in 37 L.
R. A. ,261.
For approvedforms of instructionson insanedelusions, see
INSTRUCTIONS TO JURIES, (Ky .) Secs.633, 634, 635.
INSANITY . Generally. The mental defect or disease
necessaryto entitleone to avoid the contractsof an insane per-
son need not be so great as to dethrone his reason or amount to
an entire want of reason . It is sufficientif he is insane to such
an extent as to be incapableof comprehending and understand-
ing the nature,meaning and effectof his act, the subjectof the
contract and the probable consequences thereof. He must be
insane to this extent at least; mere weakness of intellectnot
being enough . Bevins v . Lowe , 159 Ky . 439, 167 S. W. 422.
In law insanityisa genericterm, and embraces every case of
defect of reason or weakness of mind which leaves the person
withoutmental capacityto distinguishrightfrom wrong, or
without the will-power , knowing right from wrong , to control
a tendency to wrong -doing. To allsuch cases Sec. 268, Code ,
applies. Richey v. Com .,6 R. 515.
The fact that a person has been properly adjudged to be of
unsound mind is conclusive evidence that such was his condi-
tion at the time of the inquest; but it is only prima facie evi-
dence of his conditionat the time of a subsequent saleand con-
veyance; and being a mere presumption, itmay be repelledby
oral testimony. Johnson's Committee v . Mitchell, 146 Ky . 382,
142 S. W. 675 .
A railroadcompany isliablein compensatorydamages to a
passenger who isassaulted, abused and insultedby a conductor,

INSANITY INSANITY1761
while acting within the scope of his employment and engaged
in attending to the company's business, even if he be insane at
the time.C. & O. Ry . Co. v .Francisco, 149 Ky . 307, 148 S. W.
46 .
It is only in cases where the lunatichas been adjudged of
unsound mind , and is in the custody of the State, that the
surety is released. Com .v. Allen, 157 Ky . 6, 162 S. W. 116.
Where insanityis clearlyestablishedand a party adjudged
tobe a lunatic,or his incapacityarisesfrom mania ,the presump-
tion of law is, that this conditionof mind continues; and the
party relyingupon a writing executed by another whose insan-
ity is establishedprior to itsexecutionmust show that it was
executedduring a lucidintervalor thatthe insanityhad ceased
to exist. Carpenter v . Carpenter, 8 Bush 286 .
Insane Person Liable for Torts . " While certain eminent
law writershave criticisedthe doctrine, it may be stated that
by the great weight of authoritythe law iswell settled, that an
insaneperson, to the extent of compensation, isjust as respon-
siblefor his tortsas a sane person; and thisrule appliesto all
torts, except, perhaps, those in which malice, and ,therefore, in-
tention, actual or imputed , is a necessaryingredient, likelibel,
slander and maliciousprosecution. Some of the courts have
based the doctrineupon the principlethat, where one of two
innocent persons must bear a loss, he must bear it whose act
caused it. The additionalreason has been given that public
policy requiresthe enforcement of the liabilitythat the rela-
tivesof a lunaticmay be under inducement to restrainhim , and
thattort-feasorsmay not simulateor pretendinsanitytodefend
theirwrongful acts,causingdamage to others. Young v .Young ,
141 Ky . 76 : Cooley on Torts, p . 99 ; Irvine v . Gibson, 117 Ky .
306; Boswellon Insanity, Sec. 355,22 Cyc. 1211; Williamsv.
Hayes , 143 N. Y. 442 ; McIntyre v. Sholty, 124 Ill. App . 605 ;
Bacon's Abridgement , p . 7 ; I. Hale P. C. 15 ; Robarts Reports,
p .134."
" If an insanepersonisliablein compensatorydamages for
an injuryinflictedby him,we perceiveno good reasonwhy the
master should not be held liable for the tort of an insane serv-
ant while acting within the scope of his employment, and

INSANITY INSANITY1762
engaged in attending to the master's business." C. & O. Ry .
Co. v . Francisco, 149 Ky . 307, 148 S. W. 46.
HereditaryInsanity. Defendant can not prove hereditary
insanityin his family by family traditionas tending to show
insanity of defendant. Wright v. Com ., 72 S. W. 340, 24
R. 1838 .
Moral Insanity. "According to matured philosophy, and
the corroborating authority of elementary writers, such as
Prichard and Esquirol and Ray and Taylor, and of many mod-
ern adjudications, both Britishand American, theremay be
moral as well as intellectualinsanity, and essentiallycontradis-
tinguished from it. When , as often happens from congenital
malorganizationor supervenientdisturbanceof the normal con-
dition of a sound mind in a sound body , the senses present
falseimages which, accreditednecessarilyby the deluded vic-
tim as intuitivecertainties,no reasoningorproofcan rectifythe
illusionof a mind in such abnormal condition, and consequently,
as no punitary sanction can prevent the effectsof such insane
delusion, there is no legal responsibility. This is called intel-
lectualinsanity." St. Louis Mutual Life Ins. Co. v . Graves,
6 Bush 275 .
" But while the senses are apparently sound and true, the
affectionsmay be perverted,or the moral sentimentsunhinged
in such a degree as tosubjugatethe willtosome morbid appetite
or ungovernablepassion,and thusprecipitatesagainstthe will
insanebut consciouswrong. This is contradistinctivelycalled
moral insanity. Such are the forms of monomania entitled
kleptomania, pyromania, nymphomania , homicidalmania, etc.,
now well-defined and recognizedas irresponsibleinsanity."
St. Louis Mutual Life Ins.Co. v . Graves, 6 Bush 276.
The courtsdo not recognizemoral insanityas an excusefor
crime. The insanitythat willexcuse violationsof the law must
be the resultof mental disease. Banks v. Com . 145 Ky .800, 141
S. W. 380 .
Excuse for Crime . "The insanitythat excuses is such as
deprives the person committing the act of his reason or will in
thatparticulartransaction. The irresistibleimpulserecognized

INSANITY INSANITY1763
by the law isthat only resultingfrom mental disease- from the
derangement of the mind caused by a diseaseof the mind . It
is not material how recently the derangement may have oc-
curred. A person acts under an insane, irresistibleimpulse
when ,by reason of the duress of mental disease,he has lostthe
power to choosebetween rightand wrong ,to avoid doing the act
in question,his freeagency being at the time destroyed. 1 Bish.
Cr . Law , Sec.387 ; Parsons v. State, 81 Ala . 577, 2 South .854,
60 Am . Rep .193 ; Portwood v . Com .,supra; Graham v. Com . 16
B. Mon. 587 ; Brown v . Com . 14 Bush 398 ; Moore v . Com . 92
Ky .637 (13 R.,738 ) (18 S. W. ,833.) ;McCarty v.Com .114 Ку .
626, 71 S. W. 656, 24 R. 1427.
Transient insanity produced by the voluntary act of the
accused would not excuse, but at the utmost would only
extenuate the homicide from murder to manslaughter. Blim v.
Com ., 7 Bush , 321.
A doubt of sanity is essentiallydifferentfrom a doubt of
insanity. The former should always avail, the latter never.
Smith v . Com . 1 Duv . 225 .
A mere doubt of sanityhas never entered as an element into
the rationaldoubt which should produce acquittal. Kriel v.
Com . 5 Bush 365
In every case where insanity is a defense, the accused is
allowed the widest latitude in bringing before the jury the
peculiaritiesof the particularaffectionhe issufferingwith ;but
when he has done this, he must submit to the inexorabletest
that it is only mental unsoundness or mental diseasethat will

Insanity of a defendant at the time of the commission of a
crime isa complete defense.
Where there is evidence produced in a criminal cause that

should instructthe jury that if they believedfrom the evidence
that the defendant at the time of the commission of the crime,
was not sane,and couldnot,becauseof mental incapacity,know

INSANITY INSANITY1764
or not,theyshouldacquit. McFall v.Com .of Ky .8 Ky . Opin.
236 .
The truetestof responsibility,where the defenseisinsanity,
iswhether the accused had sufficientreason to know right from
wrong ,and whether or not he had sufficientpower to controlor

On a plea of insanity,defendant isnot entitledto an instruc-

General Form of Instructionson Insanity. (a) Although
thejury may believefrom alltheevidencebeyond a reasonable
doubt that the defendant, C. D. (here statethe acts constitut-
ing offense), yet if they further believefrom all the evidence
thatat thetimehe sodid, thedefendantwas of unsound mind,
thentheyshouldacquithim,and shouldsay in theirverdictthat
he isnot guiltyon the ground of insanity.

power to govern his actions, by reason of some insane impulse
which he could not resistor control.
These instructionshave been approvedinAbbott v.Com .107
Ky .624,55 S. W. 196,21 R. 1372;Banks v.Com .,145 Ky .800,

In a prosecution for rape on a female under 16, where
