must appear thatby reasonof his insanityhe eitherdid not
know rightfrom wrong at the time he commited the act,or had
notsufficientwillpower tocontroland governhisactions. Mont-
gomery v . Com .88 Ky . 509,11 R. 40 .
It was error to instructthe jury that, in order to acquit
accusedof murder on the ground of insanity, they must believe
that at the time of the shootinghe " was laboringunder such a
defect of reason as not to know the nature and quality of the
acthe was doing,or, if he did know it, that he did not know it
was wrong ." Jollyv.Com .61 S. W. 49,22 R. 1622.
See instructioncopiedinopinion,and commented on,in Hall
v. Com .155 Ky . 541.
The question whether defendant knew right from wrong
with referenceto thespecificoffensecharged,shouldnot be sub-
mittedtojury instructions. Thompson v. Com .155 Ky .333.
To render a plea of insanityavailablethe jury must be sat-
isfiedfrom the evidence that the accused was laboring under
such a defect of reason as not to know the nature and quality
of the act he was doing, or,ifhe did know it, thathe did not
know itwas wrong. An instructionto thiseffect, when taken
in connectionwith otherinstructionsgivingaccusedthe full
benefitof the law applicableto the defenseof insanity, isnot
erroneous. Brown v . Com .,14 Bush ,398 .
No enlightened jurist now doubts the existence of such
a type of moral as contradistinguishedfrom intellectual
insanity, as homicidalmania, or morbid and uncontrollable
appetiteforman -killing; and pyromania, or a likepassionfor
house-burning; and kleptomania, or an irresistibleinclination
to steal. Smith v . Com . 1 Duv . 225 .
Sanity is always presumed by the law. This presumption
can only be overcome by a preponderanceof evidencein the
defendant'sbehalf. A mere doubt of sanity,however rational,
iswholly insufficientto rebutthe legalpresumption. Graham
v.Com .16 B. M. 591;Krielv.Com .5 Bush 365.
Where accused reliedon insanity at the time of the com-
missionof the crime, and therewas evidencethatduring the
time of the commission of the offense he was addicted to the
use of drugs impairing if not destroyinghis mind , the inquest

INSANITY OF INS . 1768 INSOLVENCY
of lunacy, held at the same term of court at which the trial
occurred, was admissible. Smedley v . Com ., 139 Ky . 767, 127
S. W. 485 .
An instructionthatthe law presumesevery man sane until
the contrary is shown by the evidence, and before a defendant
can be excused on the ground of insanity the jury must
believefrom the evidencethat he was at the time of the killing
without sufficientreason to know what he was doing or had not
sufficientreason to know right from wrong, or that as the
resultof mental unsoundness he had not then sufficientwill-power
to govern his actionsby reason of some insane impulse which
he could not resistor control, isproper. Banks v. Com . 145
Ky . 800, 141 S. W. 380.

INSECURITY CLAUSE .—-Effect of " danger," " safety," or
" insecurity" clausein mortgage. See noteson thissubjectin
23 L. R. A. 780 ; 19 L. R. A. (N. S.) 915.
INSIMUL COMPUTASSENT . They had accounted
together; an obsolete action of account. Cochran's Law
Lexicon .
To authorizea recovery on an insimul computassent itis not
necessarythat each item of plaintiff'saccount should be proved,
provided it is proved that the partiesaccounted together and
defendant acknowledged that a sum certainwas due the plain-
tiff. Crowdus v.Hutchings, 7 J. J. M. 44.
INSINUATING .- Words insinuatinga desire to fight with
deadly weapons as they tend to provoke a combat , may
amount to a misdemeanor at the Common Law . Com . v . Tibbs,
1 Dana 524 .
INSOLVENCY .-The term " insolvency" as used in Ky .
Stats., Sec. 597, providingthat if any presidentof a bank
shallreceiveor assent to the receivingof depositswith knowl-
erge thatthe bank isinsolvent,he shallbe guiltyof a felony,
means thatallof the bank'spropertyand assetsare not suffi-
cientto satisfyitsdebts,and not thatitmay not have sufficient
funds in itsvaults to satisfyall of itsdepositors, or any con

INSOLVENT DEBT . 1769 INSPECT - IN .
siderablenumber of them , on the same day , or in case of a run .
Parrish v . Com . 136 Ky .77, 123 S. W. 339.
An allegationthat the bank of which accused was president
was insolventwhen he receivedthe deposit in questionwas an
allegationof factand was not objectionableas a conclusion.
Parrish v . Com . 136 Ky .77, 123 S. W. 339.
Where , in a prosecutionof a bank presidentfor illegally
receivingdepositswith knowledge of the bank'sinsolvency,there
was no effortmade by the Commonwealth to prove that the
bank was insolventmerely becauseit did not have sufficient
cash on hand to pay itsdepositors, and evidencewas offered
demonstrating that when the bank receivedthe deposit all its
assets, prudently administered, were only sufficientto pay a
verysmalldividendtoitsdepositors,an instructionthata bank
is " insolvent" within the meaning of the law creating such
offense, when itspropertyand assetsare such that itcannot
meet itsdemands in the ordinary course of business, should be
construed as requiring that the evidence should show that all
the bank's assetswere insufficientto pay itsdebts, and was not,
therefore, erroneousas authorizinga findingof insolvencyin
case the bank had insufficientmonetary funds to pay deposi-
torson demand . Parrishv.Com .,136 Ky .77,123 S. W. 339.
Insolvency within the meaning of the Act of 1856 means
inabilityto pay one'sdebts. It is not enough to show thatthe
debtor did not have property subjectto executionin the county
of his residencesufficientto pay allof hisliabilities. Barr v.
Elder, 10 Ky . Opin . 390.
INSOLVENT DEBTOR'S OATH .- Form of, Ky . Stats.,
Sec . 2180 .
INSPECT - INSPECTION .- In an action against a muni-
cipalcorporationfor injuriesreceivedby reason of an obstruc-
tionin the street, an instructionimposingon the corporation
the duty of exercisingordinary care in the inspectionof its
streetsin addition to the duty of exercisingordinary care in
maintaining itsstreetsin a reasonably safe conditionfor ordi-
nary travel, iserroneous. The duty of inspection, ifsuch it

INSPECT - IN . INSPECT - IN .1770
may be termed, is included in the duty of exercisingordinary
care in the maintaining of itsstreetsin a reasonably safe con-
ditionfor ordinarytravel; and the methods, frequencyand
extent of inspectionare matters to be consideredby the jury in
determining the question of the exerciseor failureto exercise
ordinary care in maintaining the streetin reasonablysafe con-
ditionfor ordinarytravel. City of Louisvillev. Zoeller, 155
Ky . 192, 160 S. W. 500.
" The court by the firstinstructiontold the jury that it
was the duty of the defendant 'to use ordinary care, to furnish,
and inspect,and keep, and maintain' the hand-hold reasonably
safe; the word inspect should have been omitted from this
instruction. We so held in a suit againsta municipal corpora-
tion(See City of Louisvillev.Zoeller, 155 Ky . 192) and what
we said on the subject of undue prominence in that case
applies equally here. The usual instructionwithout the word
inspecthas been oftenapproved." Cincinnati, N. O. & T. P.
Ry . Co. v . Nolan , 161 Ky . 205, 170 S. W. 650.
A bona fide stockholderin a National bank is entitledto
inspectthe listof stockholdersunder the statuteand take such
memoranda therefrom as he may wish without regard to his
motive for making the inspection. Murray v . Walker , 156 Ky .
536, 161 S. W. 512.
A city is not bound to inspectthe poles of a public service
corporationand thisrule appliesthough the city used the pole
for years before the company took charge of it. American Dis-
trictTel. Co. v . Oldham , 148 Ky . 320, 146 S. W. 764.
It is not the duty of the servant to inspectthe place where
he works in the absence of a contractor specialassumption of
that duty , and his failureto do so is not contributorynegli-
gence. Bon JellicoCoal Co. v .Murphy ,161 Ку . 450, 171 S. W.
160 .
Duty of electriclightcompany as to inspectionof wires in
houses. Smith's Admx . v . Middlesboro Elec. Co., 164 Ky . 46,
174 S. W. 773 .
Under the oil inspectionstatutes (Ky . Stats., Secs. 2206-
2211),an oilinspectorwho did not inspectthe oilof a dealer,
but merely lefthis branding and marking implements with the

employes of the dealer, who pretended to perform the labor for
the inspector, but not in his presence, could not recover of the
dealerfeesfor inspection. Frielv .Standard Oil Co., 123 S. W.
1187 .
A tribunalof fact receivesevidence through three channels:
inspection, documents and witnesses. Evidence gained by
inspectioncoversfactswhich the tribunalcognizeswith itsown
senses; sees, hears, smells, tastes, or otherwise perceives for
itself. 17 Cyc .290 ; South Covington & CincinnatiSt. Ry . Co.
v.Finan's Admx ., 153 Ky .340, 155 S. W. 742.
INSTALLMENTS . An installment not sued for in an
actionmay be recovered in a subsequent action. City of Cov-
ington v. South Covington & C. St. Ry . Co., 147 Ky . 326, 144
S. W. 17 .
Where the contractis severableand the price is payable by
installments,which are payableas certainportionsof the work
isfinished, ifthe buildingisdestroyedwithoutfaultby either
party the contractorcan recoverfor any installmentwhich has
been fully earned. Peck -Hammond & Co. v . Miller, 164 Ky .
206,175 S. W. 347 .
INSTITUTED . In the statuteproviding that when by the
law of any otherstateor countryan actionupon a judgment
or decree rendered in such a state or country cannot be
"maintained" there, by reason of the lapse of time, an
actionupon the same can not be " maintained" in thisstate;
the word " maintained" is synonymous with institutedor
commenced, and it is immaterialwhether the actionis tried
withinthe periodof limitationprescribed, providedit is com-
menced withinthatperiod. Reynoldsv. Powers, 96 Ky . 481,
16 R. 448 .
INSTITUTION OF EDUCATION . A gymnasticassociation,
where regulargymnasticexercisesare taught,and a teacherin
physicalcultureis constantlyemployed, is an institutionof
education,withinConstitution,Sec.170,exemptingsuch institu-
tionsfrom taxation. German GymnasticAssn.v.Cityof Louis-
ville, 117 Ky .958, 80 S. W. 201, 25 R. 2105, 65 L. R. A. 120.

INSTITUTION OF ED . 1772 INSTRUCTIONS
Ky . Constitution, Sec. 170, providingthat there shallbe
exempt from taxation " institutions" of education not used
or employed for gain by any person or corporation, and the
income of which is devoted solelyto the cause of education, is
not limited to buildings and grounds of educational associa-
tions, but includestrust funds bequeathed to be invested and
the income used solelyfor the education of poor children to
be selectedby the trustee. Com . v . Gray , 115 Ky . 665, 74
S. W. 702 , 25 R. 52 .
" As said by the court in the course of the opinion in Trus-
tees Kentucky Female Orphan School v . City of Louisville,
supra: 'Upon the whole, it would seem that when the stat-
ute exempts the " institution" from taxation, and no qualify-
ing words are used showing or tending to show that only the
property"used" by the institution, or " connected" with the
institution, is to be exempt , then the associatedentity- the
corporate being with its estate as an entirety, is embraced
by the word " institution." , " Com .v .Young Men's Christian
Association, 116 Ку . 723, 76 S. W. 522, 25 R. 940 .
The State University and the State Normal Schools are
among the educationalinstitutionsfor which , under the pro-
visoof Ky . Constitution, Sec. 184, the Legislaturemay make
appropriationswithout submitting the question to the voters.
James v. State University, 131 Ky . 156, 114 S. W. 767.
INSTRUCTIONS .- The requirement of Subsec. 5 of Sec .
317 of the Civil Code of Practice, that requests for instruc-
tions by either party must be made in writing, does not
apply to a requestfor a peremptory instruction, since an
instructionof that character does not prosent to the jury any
point of law, but takes the case from the jury's considera-
tion. Bell'sAdmr . v . LouisvilleRy . Co., 148 Ky . 189, 146 S.
W. 383 .
Where affidavitsfor continuance are admitted as the dep-
ositionsof the absent witnesses it is not improper for the
courtto instructthe jury orallyas to the weight they should
give to the affidavits. This instruction is substantiallya
rulingas to the competency of the evidence, and while the
generalrule is that the jury must be instructedin writing,

INSTRUCTIONS INSTRUMENT1773
there is no law that the rulings of the court as to com-
petencyof evidenceshallbe in writing. Aiken v. Com ., 68
S. W. 849 , 24 R. 523 .
An admonition by the court to the jury as to the effect
and purpose of a contradictorystatement made by a witness,
" that it must not be considered for any other purpose
except to impeach the witness," need not be in writing, but
may be made orally. Metcalfe v . Com ., 86 S. W. 534, 27 R.
704 .
Verbal admonitionsof the court made during the prog-
ressof the trial, in referenceto questionsof evidence, are not
instructionswithin the meaning of the Code provision
requiringallinstructionsto be in writing. The Code refersto
instructionsthat are given at the close of the evidence.
Wendling v . Com . 143 Ky . 587, 137 S. W. 205 .
The modern tendency is to depart from the former prac-
ticeof giving general or abstractinstructions, and to charge
inas specificand concretea form as possible. L. & N. R. Co.
v . King's Admr . 131 Ky . 347, 115 S. W. 196.
The purpose of instructionsis to presentto the jury the
issuesin the most intelligibleform . L. & N. R. Co. v . King's
Admr ., 131 Ky . 347, 115 S. W. 196.
Under Subsec . 5 of Sec . 317 of the Civil Code of Prac-
tice, an instructionasked by either party to an action must
be reduced to writing, so that the instructionas asked shall
be in writing. An oral request of the court to instructthe
jury upon a designatedpoint is an insufficientrequest; and
the failureof the court to comply therewith, willnot be
reviewedby the Court of Appeals. Bell'sAdmr .v. Louisville
Ry . Co., 148 Ky . 189, 146 S. W. 383.
For statement of principlesgoverning instructionsto
juries, and approved forms in civiland criminalcases, see
INSTRUCTIONS TO JURIES, (Ky ).

MERELY . CivilCode,Sec.7,providesthatno actionshallbe
brought upon an instrument transferableby delivery merely
which is alleged to be lost, without a previous tender by

be known to plaintiff, of an indemnifying bond , etc. Held ,
that " instruments transferableby delivery merely " means
billsof exchange or negotiablepaper which the holder takes
free of defenses good between the originalparties, and does
not embrace a note secured by mortgage , which under the law
in force at the time was taken by a holder subject to all

W. 168 .
TRANSFERABLE BY DELIVERY
An action by the indorsee of a lost note against the payee
is an " action on an instrument transferableby delivery
only," within Civil Code , Sec. 7 , providing that in an action
on a lostinstrument, transferableby delivery only, no judg-
ment shall be given defendant until an indemnity bond has
been given him. Hoyland v. Natl. Bank of Middlesborough,
137 Ky . 682, 126 S. W. 356.
Sec. 7, Civil Code , for the tender of an indemnifying bond
beforeactionis brought upon an instrumenttransferableby
deliverymerely, appliesonly to such instrumentsas bills
of exchange or negotiablepaper, which the holdertakesfree
of defensesgood between the originalparties. Foster'sAdmr .
v .Metcalfe, 144 Ky . 385, 138 S. W. 314.
INSUBORDINATION . See DISOBEDIENCE.
INSUFFICIENT NUMBER OF HANDS .- The master is
liableto a servantinjuredby failureof the master to fur-
nish a sufficientnumber of hands, if the master could and the

INSURABLE INTEREST (LifeInsurance).- A personcan
not enforcea policyof insuranceon the lifeof anotherwith-
out having an insurable interestin the lifeof such person.

INSURABLE INTER . 1775 INSURABLE INTER .
New York Life Ins. Co. v. Brown's Admr ., 139 Ky . 711, 66
S. W. 613 , 23 R. 2070 .
In the absenceof statutoryprohibition, a personmay take
out insuranceon his own lifeand designatewhom he pleases
as the beneficiary. Hess' Admr . v. Segenfelter, 127 Ky . 348,
105 S. W. 476, 32 R. 225 , 14 L. R. A. (N.S.) 1172, 128 Am .
St. Rep . 343 .
A person may not himself procure, nor obtain by assign-
ment , insurance upon a lifein which he has not an insurable
interest, growing out of kinship, dependency , or the relation
of debtor and creditor; nor may a person insure his own life
for the benefit of any other, if that other induces him to
procure the insurance and pays the premium thereon, or if
the insurance was obtained with a view to evade the laws
against speculativeinsurance. Id .
A beneficiarymust have interestin lifeof insured. Ky .
Stats., Sec. 678.
The relationshipof uncle and nephew or niece is not of
itselfsufficientto constitutean insurable interestupon the
part of either in the life of the other where there is no
reasonableground of expectationof support to be furnished
by the assured to the other. Equitable Life Assurance
Societyv . O'Connor's Admr ., 162 Ky . 262, 172 S. W. 496.
One obtaining a policy of insurance on the lifeof another,
or who induces another to take out a policy for his benefit,
must have an insurable interest. Rupp v . Western Life
Indemnity Co., 138 Ky . 18, 127 S. W. 490.
One may take out insurance on his own life, pay the
premiums,and name as beneficiaryanothernot relatedto him,
or having no insurableinterestin his life. Id .
One having no insurable interestcan not take out life
insurance even with the consent of insured, and even if he
does the law will not enforce the contract because against
publicpolicy; and a policytaken out by insuredfor such a
beneficiaryand continuedin forceby the beneficiary'shus-
band afterit has been repudiated, abandoned, and dropped
by insured is void. Western & Southern Life Ins. Co. v.
Grimes ' Admr ., 138 Ky . 338, 128 S. W. 65.

INSURABLE INTER . 1776 INSURABLE INTER .
Where the relationshipof beneficiariesin an insurance
policy to the insured is, as in the case of husband and wife,
parent and child, sisterand brother, so close as to preclude
the probabilitythat mercenary motives would induce the sac-
rificeof lifeto gain the insurance, the elementof pecuniary
interestis not essentialto the validityof the policy. Hess '
Admr . v . Segenfelter, 127 Ky . 348, 105 S. W. 476, 32 R. 225,
14 L. R. A. (N.S.) 1172,128 Am . St. Rep . 343.
First cousins, who are not dependent on or creditorsof an
insured, have not an insurable interestin his life. Hess '
Admr . v . Segenfelter, 127 Ky . 348 , 105 S. W. 476, 32 R. 225,
14 L. R. A. (N.S.) 1172, 128 Am . St. Rep . 343.
A son has an insurable interest in the life of his father.
Buckler v. Supreme Council Catholic Knights of America ,
143 Ky . 618, 136 S. W. 1006.
A member of a mutual benefit associationcan not desig-
nate his niece as beneficiary, though the charter of the asso-
ciationdoes not prohibit it, for the reason that a niece does
not have an insurable interest in her uncle's life. Hull v .
Grand Lodge A. O. U. W. , 105 S. W. 479, 32 R. 212.
The relationshipbetween parent and child is itselfsuffi-
cient to give either an insurable interestin the life of the
other. Woods v . Woods ' Admr ., 130 Ky . 162, 113 S. W. 79,
19 L. R. A. (N.S.) 233.
Where a mother contractedwith her sons to pay premiums
on her life policy and take the proceeds at her death, an

had no insurableinterest, that he should furnish one-third
of the premiums and have one-third of the proceedsof the
policy, did not affectthe validityof the policyor affectthe
sons ' interest therein . Id .
A mother has an insurable interest in the life of her son .
Neal's Admr . v. Shirley'sAdmr ., 137 Ky . 818, 127 S. W.
471 .
The relationshipbetweenbrothersissufficienttogiveeitheran
insurableinterestin the lifeof the other. Hahn v . Supreme
Lodge of the Pathfinder, 136 Ky . 823,125 S. W. 259.

INSURABLE INTER . 1777 INSURABLE INTER .
Blood relationshipin and of itselfconstitutesan insurable
interest. Hahn v. Supreme Lodge of the Pathfinder, 136 Ky .
823 , 125 S. W. 259 .
On a judicialsaleof a paid-up lifepolicy, the courtdoes
not on its own motion inquire whether the purchaser has an
insurable interestin the life of the insured, and an order
confirming the sale, where no exceptions are filed, does not
establishthe fact that the purchaser is not a trustee for the
beneficiariesor that he has capacityto take the property
absolutely. Irons v . U. S. Life Ins. Co., 128 Ky . 640, 108
S.W. 904,33 R. 46,129 Am .St.Rep .318.
To entitle one to insurable interest in the life of another
it is sufficientthat in the ordinary course of events loss or

whose favor the policy is written from the death of the per-
son whose lifeis insured. Adams ' Admr . v. Reed , 38 S. W.
420, 18 R. 858, 35 L. R. A. 692. (36 S. W. 568 reversed.)
A mother -in-law, on the death of her son-in-law's wife,
paid the premiums on and had assigned to her by agreement
of the company a policy on the lifeof her son-in-law, with
whom she lived, she doing the housework , each furnishing
one-half of the supplies, they dividingthe profitsrealizedfrom
keeping boarders. Held , the mother -in-law had an insurable
interest in the life of her son -in-law . Id .
A surety has an insurableinterestin the lifeof the prin-
cipal to, the extent of the suretyship. Embry's Admrs . v .
Harris, 52 S. W. 958, 21 R. 714 .
A benefitassociation, " organizedfor the purpose of assist-
ing widows , orphans, or other dependents of deceased mem-
bers by providing for the payment by each member of a fixed
sum to be held until the death of a member , then to be paid
to the person or persons entitledthereto," etc., issued a policy
which providedthat itmight be assigned" to any party hav-
ing an insurableinterestin the lifeof said member , with the
assent theretoof the beneficiaryherein," and with the assent
of the proper officersof the association. Held, thata simple
creditor of the member did not have an insurable interest
inhislifewithinthemeaningof theprovision.Nat.Exchange
Bank v. Bright, 36 S. W. 10, 38 S. W. 135, 18 R. 588.

INSURABLE INTER . 1778 INSURABLE INTER .
A creditorto whom the debtor assignsa policyof insurance
on his lifeacquiresno interestthereinbeyond his debt, as he
has no insurable interestbeyond that. Barbour's Admr . v .
Larne's Assignee, 51 S. W. 5, 21 R. 94.
One not a creditor nor related to insured has no insurable
interestin his life. Smith v. Agnew , 137 Ky . 83, 122 S. W.
231 .
INSURABLE INTEREST (FireInsurance).- An insurable
interestneed not amount to a right of property or of posses-
sion. Whenever a legal connection can be shown to exist
between injury to the thing insured and the lossto the party
insuring, it is sufficient. So if one has a right which may
be enforced against the property, and which is so connected
with it that itsinjury or destructionwill necessarilydamnify
him , he has an insurableinteresttherein. Hartford Fire Ins.
Co. v . McClain , 85 S. W. 699, 27 R. 461.
And whoever has such titlethat if the property were lost
without insurance the losswould fallon him , has an insurable
interest. The interestof a mortgagor is within the rule. So
if one insured has any interestthat would be injured if the
peril insured against should happen , his contract of insurance
is a valid one . Id .
A person who has an interestin property, legal or equit-
able, or who stands in such a relationtheretothat itsdestruc-
tion would entailpecuniary loss upon him or those he repre-
sents, has an insurable interest. Phænix Ins. Co. v . Adams ,
8 R. 532 .
A trustee of an implied as well as of an express trust
may effectan insurance in his own name for the benefitof the
cestui que trust. Such contracts, though the names of the
beneficiariesare not disclosed, are not repugnant to the clause
in the policiesconcerning concealments or misrepresentations
of any material fact concerning the risk or of the interestof
the insured. Hartford Fire Ins. Co. v . McClain , 85 S. W.
699 , 27 R. 461 .
Assignee for benefit of creditorshas an insurable interest
in the trust property, although he has not qualifiedor exe

INSURABLE INTER . 1779 INSURANCE
cuted bond as required by statute. Phœnix Ins. Co. v . Adams ,
8 R. 532 .
The grantee in a fraudulent conveyance has an insurable
interest in the property, the conveyance being valid as
between the parties. Home Ins. Co. v. Allen, 93 Ky . 270, 14
R. 161. See 13 R. 95 .
Where one has erected a building on the lands of another,
in the absence of an agreement to that effect, he has no right
to remove it; and his right even to occupy such building
having ceased, he had no insurableinteresttherein. Niagara
Fire Ins. Co. v . Layne , 162 Ky . 665, 172 S. W. 1090.
Builders who undertake a building and whose compensa-
tion depends on its completion have an insurableinterestin
the building. Pro . Ins. Co. v. Hall, 15 В. М. 431.
For additionalauthoritiesas to what particularpersons
have insurableinterests,seeDigestKentucky Reports (Morton
& Co.), titleINSURANCE .
INSURANCE .- Nature of Contract or Policy. It is well
settledthat a contractof insurance is sui generis. While the
insured, by an observance of its conditions, may hold the
insurer to his contract, the latterhas not the power or right
to compel the insured to maintain the contract relationwith
it longer than he chooses. Whether the insured will continue
or not is optional with him . There being no obligationto
pay for the premiums , they did not constitutea debt. Noble
v . Southern States Mutual Life Ins. Co., 157 Ky . 46, 162 S.
W. 528 .
" There is a sharp conflictof authority as to whether such
contractsof insurance are to be treated as an entiretyor as
separable(see 19 Cyc. 674-676); but the rule in thisState
isthat they are regardedas separable(Speaglev. Dwelling
House Ins. Co., 97 Ky . 646), and this ruling seems to be in
accordwith the weight of authority." German Mutual Fire
Ins. Co. v . Weikel, 153 Ky . 288, 155 S. W. 373.
A life insurance policy is a contract to be kept in force
from year to year at the willof the assured, and the failure
to pay the premium is merely an electionon the part of the
assured not to continue the contract for another year ; it is

INSURANCE INSURANCE1780
not an act of forfeiture,but of abandonment . Koehler v .Phoe-
nix M. L. Ins. Co., 4 R. 903.
Insurance is not a contract of continuing performance.
The only performance which could devolve on the under-
writer is to pay the stipulatedamount in the event of the loss
insured against, fulfillmentof which is not a continuing act,
but a single act of a continuing contract, and the considera-
tion, though payable in annual installments, is yet an entirety
also. N. Y. Life Ins. Co. v . Clopton, 7 Bush , 186.
A lifepolicy for the benefitof the family of the person
procuring it, is in the nature of a will, and as far as possible
should be so construed. Duvall v . Goodson , 79 Ky . 224, 1
R. 319 .
Insurance is not commerce .
89 S. W. 168, 28 R. 217.
Com . v . Gregory, 121 Ky . 256,
Becoming surety in the bond of an employee, executed to
the employer, guaranteeing the fidelityof the employee, is
a species of insurance, and the representationsof the
employer to secure such insurance are not warranties, and are
not to be so construed. American Bonding Co. v . Ballard
County Bank's Assignee, 165 Ky . 63, 176 S. W. 368.

must be liberallyconstrued in favor of the insured. A. B.
Co. of Baltimorev. B. C. Bk.'sAssignee, 165 Ky . 64, 176 S.
W. 368 .
Where a policy of insurance is fairly susceptibleof two
constructions, it is the rule to adopt that constructionthat
will sustain rather than defeat the policy. Mutual Ben . L.
Ins. Co. v. FirstNat. Bank , 69 S. W. 1, 24 R. 580; Conti-
nental Ins. Co. v . Daniel, 78 S. W. 866, 25 R. 1501 ; North
River Ins. Co. v. Dyche , 163 Ky . 271, 173 S. W. 784 .
It is a well established rule in the law of insurance that
if, in lookingat allthe provisionsof an insurancepolicy, it
is fairlyand reasonably susceptibleof two constructions, one
favorable to the insurance company , and the other favorable
to the insured, the latterconstruction, if consistentwith the
objectsfor which the policywas given, must be adopted; and
this for the reason that the instrument which the court is

INSURANCE INSURANCE1781
invitedto interpretwas drawn by the attorneys, officersor

strongly against the insurance company whose representatives
prepared it. National Life & Accident Ins. Co. v . O'Brien's
Extx ., 155 Ky . 498 , 159 S. W. 1134.
In a case of doubt, the language of an insurance policy
will be construed in favor of the insured, and a doubtful
clause in one part of the policy will not be held to limit an
express provision in another part of it. PacificMutual Life
Ins. Co. v . McCabe , 157 Ky . 270, 162 S. W. 1136.
Oral Contract . An oral contract of insurance is enforceable.
National Fire Ins. Co. v . Rowe , 49 S. W. 422, 20 R. 1473.
An oral contract made by the authorized agent of a fire
insurancecompany to renew an existingpolicyof insurance
is valid, although made before the expiration of the existing

S. W. 13, 21 R. 1090, 92 A. S. R. 362 ; CaliforniaIns. Co. v .
Settle, 162 Ky . 82, 172 S. W. 119.
To show an oral contract of insurance a definite and cer-
tain agreement to insure must be proven . Fireman's Fund v .
Searcy, 157 Ky . 749, 163 S. W. 1103; CaliforniaIns. Co. v.
Settle, 162 Ky . 82 , 172 S. W. 119.
A contract of insurance is not within the statute of frauds .

83 S. W. 577, 26 R. 1187.
A parol contract of insurance is valid and enforceable, but

a written contractof insurance. There must be an agreement
as to the risk insured against, the amount of insurance, the

cation be made to an agent representing several companies,
the particularcompany agreed on to carry the riskmust be

113, 140 S. W. 49.
A contract of insurance is not within the statute of frauds .
It is not necessary that it should be in writing, it may be

pany that is to issue the policy. American Central Ins. Co.

Oral contractsfor insurancemay be enforced, but a specific

izens National Life Ins. Co. v . Murphy , 154 Ky . 88, 156 S.

One who signsan applicationfor insurancein a co-oper-
ative fire insurance company without paying the premium

Fire Policy- Indemnity Only. A contractof insurance

Concurrent Insurance . Concurrent insurance is such as

nal policy, against the same casualty, and covering the same
or part of the same time, on such terms that, in event of loss,
the insurerswillbear the same proportionally, accordingto
the terms of theirpolicies. It is not essentialthat the latter
policyshallinsure the property to the same extent, and only
to the same extent, as does the originalpolicy. The fact that

Affreightment. Leathersv. Com . Ins. Co., 2 Bush, 298,
without special consideration inadvertently illustratedby

What companies are deemed to be insurance companies

INSULT .- The allegationof the petitionthat the agents of

INSURRECTION .-Insurrection, insofaras it may affect

INTANGIBLE PERSONAL PROPERTY.There isa marked

INTELLECT .- A man's intellectishis bestproperty, and
every one has a natural and inviolableright to appropriate to
his own use, the productionsof his own industry and the con-
ceptions of his own genius. What is such an originaland
practicalconceptions, as to be entitledto the denomination of
" an invention," may sometimes be an abstruse metaphysical
question . But there can be no doubt that a distinct and
organized mode of operation, essentiallydifferingfrom any
pre-existing contrivance, in adaptation or design, and which

INTELLECTUAL INSANITY .-See , also, INSANITY.
" According to matured philosophy, and the corroborating

sarilyby the deluded victim as intuitivecertainties, no reason-
ing or proof can rectifythe illusionof a mind in such

AINTER VIVOS .- Inter vivos, between living persons.
gift inter vivos is a gift by one living person to another.
Cochran's Law Lexicon . See GIFTS .
INTERCHANGE TRACK.- An interchangetrack is a track
upon which loaded cars of freightare placed for removal by a
connecting carrier. France v . C. & O. Ry . Co., 156 Ky . 126,
160 S. W. 757 .
INTERCOURSE .- In a prosecution for rape the word ,
" intercourse," as used by the prosecutrix held sufficientto
meet the requirementssetforthby the court in the instructions.
Taliaferrov . Com ., 151 Ky . 10, 150 S. W. 977.
INTEREST . " Legal interestshall be at the rate of six
dollarsupon one hundred dollarsfor one year, and at the same
rate for a greater or lesssum , and for a longer or shorter
time." Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec. 2218.
An agreement to assume a mortgage debt and interest
means the assumption of legalinterest. Gardner v . Continental
Ins. Co., 75 S. W. 283, 25 R. 426 .
Whenever one has the money of another in his possession
and uses it as his own he is chargeable with interestfor its
use. Kenton Ins. Co. v.FirstNatl.Bank , 93 Ky .129 ,14 R. 32.
Where one is entitled to the interest on stocks or bonds
during his life, the interestthat has accrued thereon at the
time of his death goes to his representativeand not to the
remainderman. Chapman v. Chapman , 87 Ky . 140, 9 R. 978.
Interest, costs, and sheriff'scommissionsbecome principal,
and carry interestin a replevinbond. Hatcher v. Kelly, 1
Bibb , 282.
Note for payment on a particularday " with interest"
runs from the day of the date. Winn v . Young , 1 J. J. M. 52.
Upon the affirmanceof a supersedeasjudgment for the sale
of land to pay a debt the surety in the supersedeas bond is
liable for interest on the value of the land from the date
originallyfixedfor the sale. Hargis v . Mayes , 50 S. W. 844,
20 R. 1965 .
A note payable in two years after date, " with interestat
the rateof sixper cent.per annum from until paid ,"

INTEREST INTEREST1795
bears interestfrom date, and not merely from date of maturity.
Millerv . Cavanaugh , 99 Ky . 377 , 35 S. W. 920, 18 R. 183.
Interest is not recoverable on loss and damage claims.
Reading v .Railroad Co., 173 S. W. 451.
Interestwillbe allowedon money due under a writtencon-
tractand not paid when due . City of Covington v . S. C. &
C. St. Ry . Co., 147 Ky . 326, 144 S. W. 17.
Provision in charter of corporation allowing it to charge
greater interestthan is allowed by the general law is uncon-
stitutional. Contract to pay ten per cent. and in addition
" a premium " for the loan was a contract for more than ten
per cent. Mack v. Workingmen's Bldg.Asso., 5 R. 519.
" Interestis the premium allowed by law for the use of
money , while usury is the taking of more for the use of money
than the law allows." Gaar v .LouisvilleBanking Co., 11 Bush ,
180, 189 .
In order to ascertain the exact amount due on a note which
has been repeatedly renewed , so as to purge it from usury,
the sum originallyloaned should be taken as the original
principal, and interestshould be calculatedupon that sum
at the rateof six per cent. per annum , until the firstrenewal
or payment ; such payment should be applied firstto the
dischargeof interestaccrued, and any excessshould be applied
on the principal. When the note is renewed , accumulated
interest, which had not been paid, should be added to the
remainingprincipal,which sum istoconstitutea new principal,
upon which interestis to be calculatedand payments applied
as above indicated. Bramblett v . Dep . Bank of Carlisle, 92
S. W. 283, 28 R. 1228, 6 L. R. A. (N.S.) 612.
Where a personis claimingmoney as his own and isper
mitted to collectitand retainitas his own ,and isonly required
to hold it subject to the court's order in case it turns out
thathe is not entitledto it, the presumptionarisesthat he
retainedand used the money as his own , and he is chargeable
with interest. Albers v.Norton Co.,147 Ky .751,145 S. W. 757.
Where damages are soughtfor injuryto personalproperty
as well as damages for the deprivationof itsuse, interestis
not allowable, as the recoveryfor the use takesthe place of

INTEREST INTEREST1796
interest. Schulte v . L. & N. R. Co., 128 Ky . 627, 108 S. W.
941 , 33 R. 31 .
The legalmanner to calculateintereston a note is down to
the firstpayment , and , if the payment be equal to or larger
than the interest, apply the payment firstto the discharge of
the interestand apply the remainder to a discharge of the
principal, as a creditorhas the legal right to have the interest
discharged before the principal shall be reduced. Morton v .
Fullenweder , 1 Ky . Opin . 504.
Under the well known rule of law , before a claim can bear
interestit must be liquidated and due at a time certain.
HendersonCottonMfg .Co.v. LowellMachineShops, 36 Ky .
668 ;Magruder v .Ericson, 1.46Ky .89 ,141 S. W. 1195.
The rule is that interestruns as a matter of right on a
liquidateddemand , and in the case of an unliquidated claim,
the allowance of interestrests in the discretionof the jury
or of the court trying the case. L. & N. R. Co. v . Com ., 29
R. 669 ; Magruder v .Ericson, 146 Ky . 89 , 142 S. W. 1195.
Under a bank charter authorizingthe bank to loan money
in accordance with the general interestlaw then in force, the
bank can charge only the rate allowed by a subsequent amend-
ment to the general law . Bank of Russellvillev . Coke , 45
S. W. 867, 20 R. 291.
Sec. 864, Ky . Stats., requiring borrowing members of
building and loan associationsto pay premiums in addition to
legalinterest, violatesSec. 59, Subsec. 21, Ky . Constitution,
forbiddingthe passage of speciallaws " to regulatethe rate of
interest." Simpson v. Ky . CitizensB. & L. Assn., 101 Ky .
496, 41 S. W. 570, 42 S. W. 834, 19 R. 1176.
The proper method of computing interestwhere there are
partialpayments upon a note is to compute the interestfrom
the time the interestbegins to run untilthe firstpayment , then
subtract the payment and compute the interest on the
remainder, and so on to the end . But if the interestexceeds
the payment , then it must be computed until such time when
the payments exceed the interest then due . Hawkins v .
Merchants , etc., L. & B. Assn ., 89 S. W. 197, 28 R. 243.

INTEREST INTEREST1797
A writtencontractforthepayment of money carriesinterest
from the time the money isdue. Supreme Lodge K. of H. v.
Lapp ,74 S. W. 656, 25 R. 74.
Where a note bears interestpayable semi-annually, each
semi-annual installmentof interestat its maturity becomes a
debt due , and bears interestfrom its maturity the same as
any otherinterest-bearing debt untilpaid,and may be recovered
by an independentaction. Hall v. Scott, 90 Ky . 340, 11 R.
819; Shanks v. Stephens, 6 R. 516, 4 R. 838 .
Differentwritingsrelatingto the same subject, executedat
the same time and to effectuatethe same object, are to be
treated as one and construed together. Where a note, due
twelve months afterdate, provides that it shallbear " interest
from date, payable semi-annually," and a mortgage executed
tosecurethe notedescribesitas " bearinginterestuntilpaid,
said interestto be paid semi-annually," the contract is con-
strued to be for the payment of interestsemi-annually until
paid. Shanks v .Stephens,6 R. 515,4 R. 838.
A water company sued to enjointhe sherifffrom sellingits
property for taxes allegedby it not to be due , the court hold-
ing that the taxes were due , as a condition upon which it
would grant the injunctionorderedplaintiffto do equityby
paying the taxes into court, and upon its refusalappointed a
receiverto take charge of and operate the water works until
" from the net revenueshe realizessufficientto pay the amount
aforesaidinto court," with costs. Held , while a judgment for
taxes, likeany otherjudgment, draws interest, thisjudgment
does not draw interest. LouisvilleWater Co. v . Clark, 16
R. 585, 15 R. 94.
Under the charter of a railroadcompany , which authorized
countiesto subscribefor stock, and provided that the county
should allow to the holders of stock " intereston the same from
the time of paying for said stock up to the time of making
the firstdividend, and issueto the holder stock therefor," the
railroadcompany by declaringa stockdividendof one-fourth
of one per cent. did not acquirethe rightto stop intereston
stocksubscribedand held by the county. Hardin County v.
L. & N. R. Co., 92 Ky . 412, 4 R. 401 .

INTEREST INTEREST1798
Accounts . Interestis allowable as a matter of right and
law upon an account from the time when by contract it is
payable, or when itbecomes a liquidatedone, unlessthe parties
agree otherwise. An account is considered as liquidatedafter
a demand of payment , with knowledge upon the part of the
debtor of what is claimed, and without objectionby him ; or
after ithas been rendered to him without objectionto it upon
his part within a reasonabletime. Henderson Cotton Mfg . Co.
v . Lowell Machine Shops , 86 Ky . 668, 9 R. 831.
Interest on Taxes . Interest on taxes is in the nature of
a penalty and ought not to be allowed unless the law has been
strictlycompliedwith. Dauber v. City of Louisville, 5 R. 865;
Shanks v . Stephens, 6 R. 516. Interestis not allowed unless
the statuteso provides. Ky . C. R. v . Pendleton Co., 8 R. 517 ;
L. & N. R. Co. v.Hopkins Co., 87 Ky . 605, 10 R. 806; L. &
N. R. Co. v . Com ., 89 Ky . 531, 11 R. 734.
Interest is not allowed upon taxes by way of damages .
Ormsby v. Louisville, 79 Ky . 197, 2 R. 66, 297; Shanks v.
Stephens, 6 R. 515.
Against Receiver. Interestwill be allowed against a
receiverwhere he has used the fund himself or commingled
itwith his own funds (23 Am . & Eng . Ency . of Law , 1100;
Hinckley v. R. R. Co., 100 U. S. 153), and where he had been
guilty of negligence or misconduct, the court, if the circum-
stances warrant it, may deny him any compensation for his
services(22 Am .& Eng .Ency .of Law , 1105,and casescited).
Higgins v.Shields,151 Ky .227,151 S. W. 391.
Oral or Written LiquidatedClaim. While in the earlier
opinionsthe allowanceof interestwas made to depend upon
the fact that the claim was liquidatedand supported by a
writtencontract, the law isnow wellsettledthata liquidated
claim, whether oralor written, carrieswith it, as a matter of
law, interestfrom the time itwas due, in the absenceof any
agreementto the contrary. Lang v. Bach, 142 Ky . 224, 134
S. W. 188 .
INTEREST (Estate).- Where the husband and wife join
in a mortgage or deed which purports to convey all their

INTEREST INTEREST1799
estateand interest, the homestead passes without the use of
specificwords to that effect. Owens v . Spratt, 1 R. 265 ;
Vaughn v. Owsley , 3 R. 249 ; Gaddie v. Hodges , 5 R. 241 ;
Mullins v . Clark, 13 R. 29. And this is so whether the fee
be in the wife or husband . Sutton v . Puckett, 2 R. 316 , 319.
INTEREST IN LAND .- See TITLE TO OR INTEREST IN LAND .
INTEREST IN THE PROPERTY .- Where a father con-
veyedrealestateto anotherin trustforhisdaughter, reserving
" fullpower to revoke each or any or some or all of the uses
hereby created and to cause them to shift to other person or
persons, includinghimself," the titlepassed to the grantee,
subjectto the power reservedby the grantor to change the use ;
and the grantor having made a general assignment for the
benefitof his creditorswithout revoking the grant or changing
the use, the rightto do so did not pass to the assignee. Such
a power of revocationis not an interestin the property that
can be subjectedby the grantor'screditors. Hillv. Cornwall,
95 Ky .512, 16 R. 97 .
INTEREST ON DAMAGES .- M . recovereda judgment
againstthe L. & N. R. Co. for $22,000 on September 7, 1906.
The railroadcompany appealed to the Court of Appeals . The
judgment was affirmed with ten per cent. damages on May
13, 1908. On May 21, 1908, the railroadcompany appealed to
the United States Supreme Court, executing a bond that it
would prosecuteitswrit of error to effectand pay alldamages
and cost if it should failto make good itsplea. The Supreme
Court on May 21, 1910, affirmedthe judgment of the Court
ofAppeals ,but did not award damages . Held ,that the interest
on the judgment for $22,000 must be counted at six per cent.
from itsrenditionto the time itwas paid, and no interestis
to be counted on the ten per cent.damages . L. & N. R. Co. v .
Melton , 146 Ky . 242, 142 S. W. 382.
INTEREST PAID .- Where an assignednote,bearinginterest
at six per cent., has upon it the endorsement of a creditby
" interestpaid" to a certaindate priorto the assignment, the
testimony of the obligor that interestwas calculatedat eight

INTEREST TO BE 1800 INTERESTED PERS .
per cent. is not sufficientto establishthe plea of usury against
a bona fide purchaser for value; there being no testimony as
to the amount paid and the calculationhaving been made by
anotherthan the obligor, in his absence. Newman's Admr . v.
Blades, 54 S. W. 849, 21 R. 1353.
INTEREST TO BE PAID SEMI -ANNUALLY .- The words
" interestto be paid semi-annually" were added to the note in
this case, but under such circumstancesas not to entitlethe
surety to recoverback the amount of the note and interestpaid
by him , without any knowledge of the fact that the note had
been alteredby the principalafterthe suretyhad signed it.
Blakey v . Johnson, 13 Bush , 197.
INTERESTED PERSONS . See, also, PERSONS INTERESTED.
A policyholderin a mutual lifeinsurance company is not
disqualifiedto testifyfor the company , under Secs. 605 and
606, Subsec. 2. The disqualifyinginterestmust be direct and
certain, one that would charge the witness with a liabilityor
exempt him from one, but a mere uncertain, remote, or con-
tingentinterestwould not disqualifyone from beinga witness.
N. Y. Life Ins. Co. v.Johnson, 72 S. W. 762, 24 R. 1867.
The disqualifyinginterestmust be direct and certain, an
uncertain or remote interestwillnot disqualify. Doty v . Doty ,
26 R. 63;New York LifeIns.Co. v.Johnson,24 R. 1867.
Where a father before his death had conveyed all his land
to one of hissons, the othersonswere not disqualified, on the
ground of interest, as witnessesfor the son to whom the land
had been conveyed. Blackburn v . Hall, 97 S. W. 399, 30 R.
134 .
Where the interestsof partiesare jointand not several, one
of them can not testifyfor the othersas to transactionswith
decedent. Barnett'sAdmr .v .Adams , 82 S. W. 406,26 R. 622.
Under CivilCode, Sec. 606, Subsec. 2, providingthat no
person shalltestifyfor himselfconcerningany transaction
with one who isdead when the testimony is offeredto be given,
an attorney, whose fee is wholly contingentupon the establish-
ment of his client'sclaim, is not a competent witness for his
client as to a transaction with one who is dead when the

INTERFERENCE INTERIM1801
testimonyisoffered. Smick'sAdmr . v.Beswick, 113 Ky . 439,
68 S. W. 439, 24 R. 276.
Not only parties to the action but all persons who are
directlyinterestedin the recovery are disqualifiedto testify
againsta decedent's estate. Smick v . Beswick, 113 Ky . 439,
24 R. 276 ;Apperson v .Exchange Bank , 10 R. 943.
Under Sec. 606, Subsec.2, CivilCode , a stockholdercan not
testifyto transactionsbetween the corporation and a person
who has since died. Storey v . First Natl. Bank , 72 S. W. 318,
24 R. 1799 .
In an action involving the question as to whether money
paid by a testatorto his daughter after making his will was
intendedas an ademption of a legacy, the executorand his
suretyare competent witnessesto prove the declarationsof the
testator, as they are not interestedin the estate. Swinebroad
v.Bright, 73 S. W. 1031, 24 R. 2253.
In such actions and in contest of wills all devisees are
competent to prove the declarationsof the testator. Id .
The executor and his surety, in an action againsthim for a
legacy, are not incompetent, under CivilCode, Sec. 606, to
testifyto declarationsof testatorto show that a payment by
him was intended in satisfactionof the legacy; they not being
interestedin the result, the controversy being really between
such legateeand the residuary legatee. Not so, however , the
legateeor her husband or the residuarylegatee. Swinebroad
v. Bright, 116 Ky . 514, 76 S. W. 365, 25 R. 742. See, also,
Digests, titleWITNESSES .
INTERFERENCE .- Where thereisa saleof standing timber
on May 19, 1910, to be removed within twelve months, a
remark by the sellerto one of the purchasers' driversduring
the month of January, 1911, and at a time when defendants
claimed that the roads were so muddy and impassable that
they couldnot haul to advantage,to the effectthathe "would
not haul through the mud " is not a sufficientinterferenceon
the part of the sellerto excuse the purchasersfrom the
necessityof removing the timber within the time specifiedby
the contract. Harrell v .Danks , 151 Ky .71, 151 S. W. 13.
INTERIM . In the meantime ; meanwhile .

INTERLINEATION 1802 INTERNAL INJURIES
INTERLINEATION . An interlineationalteringthe true
date of the note made without the consent of the obligor,
renders the instrument not the act and deed of obligor. Miles
v .Major , 2 J. J. M. 154.
The presumption is that the interlineationswere not
incorporated after the filingof the originalaffidavit. Smith
v .Belmont & Nelson Iron Co., 11 Bush , 391.
INTEREST REIPUBLICAE UT SIT FINIS LITIUM .-It
is for the interest of the State that there should be an end of
litigation. Cochran's Law Lexicon.
Where there have been five mistrials and two verdicts for
plaintiffthe judgment will not be disturbed except for gross
error. The maxim interestreipublicaeut sitfinislitiumshould
govern. Adams Express Co. v . Hines, 1 R. 266.
Thismaxim isappliedinMoran v.Woodyard ,8 B. Mon. 538.
An estoppelby a former judgment is based upon public
policy. It demands that when a fact has been judiciallyand
finallydetermined between the same parties, it shall be at
rest. Interest reipublicae ut sit finis litium. Pepper v .
Donnelly, 87 Ky . 262.
INTERLOCUTORY JUDGMENTS .— “Finaljudgments are
such as at once put an end to the actionby declaring that the
plaintiffhas eitherentitledhimself, or has not, to recover the
remedy he sues for," and " interlocutoryjudgments are such
as are given in the middle of a cause, upon some plea, pro-
ceedingor default, which is only intermediate, and does not
finallydetermine or complete the suit." Mercer v . Glass'
Exr ., 89 Ky .201. See title, FINAL ORDERS, in thisbook.
INTERLOPING RAMBLER . An action for forcibleentry
will not liein favor of one who gains possessionmerely as an
interlopingrambler,or as a mere scramblingpossession. Sand-
ford v . Kemper , 6 Ky . Opin . 239.
INTERNAL INJURIES . In an action by an employe for
injuries, the complainant, afteralleginginjuriesto the head,
the hips, knee and shoulder, and the small of his back and his
kidneys,allegedthathe sufferedotherinternalinjuriesnot yet

INTERNATIONAL INTERPLEADER1803
fullyascertainedand determined." Held, that evidencewas
not admissibleof injuriesto a testicle, where such injury was
the principalone he sufferedand it was immediately known ,
and the sufferingcaused by the injury was protracted, as
" internalinjuries" are those sustainedin the body or trunk,
and not to other independent or separate organs. C., N. O. &
T. P. Ry . Co. v . Bennette, 134 Ky . 19, 119 S. W. 181.
Where plaintiffalleged that she received certain specified
internalinjuries, and sustained a shock to her nervous sys-
tem, from a collision, but did not allegeinjury to her eyes,
evidenceas to the conditionof her eyes after the injury was
admissible, as bearing upon the question of the internal in-
juriesreceivedand the shock to the nervous system, which
of necessitymust be manifested by outward symptoms . Louis-
villeRy . Co. v . Ellerhorst, 129 Ky . 142, 110 S. W. 823, 33 R.
605 .
INTERNATIONAL .- The word " international" is a gen-
eric term, pertaining to relationsbetween nations, and when
applied to business or to transactionsof private character it
imports dealings of some sort in matter or with people of
differentnations, or which have some realtionto them . It
is in common use, and in its nature, it is descriptive, and
ordinarily characterizesthe business to which it pertains,
ratherthan itsoriginor proprietorship; and, so treated, the
use of it cannot be exclusivelyappropriated by any party.
TravelersIns. Ma . Co. v . TravelersIns. Co., 142 Ky . 531, 134
S. W. 877 .
INTERPLEADER . " Upon affidavitof a defendantbefore
answer in an actionupon contract, or for the recoveryof
personalproperty, that a person who is not a party to the
action, withoutcollusionwith him, makes a claim to the sub-
jectof the action, and thatthe affiantisready to pay or dis-
pose thereofas the courtmay direct, the courtmay make an
order for the safekeepingof the subjectof the action, or
foritspayment or depositin court, or for itsdeliveryto such
person as the courtmay direct, and an order requiringsuch
allegedclaimantto appear in a reasonabletime and main

INTERPOLATION INTERPRETERS1804
tain or relinquishhis claim against the defendant, and , in the
meantime, stay the proceeding." CivilCode, Sec. 30. This
sectionadopts the substantialparts of an ancient billof inter-
pleader. Newman's Pleadingand Practice, Sec. 186.
See, further, Newman's Pleading and Practice, Secs. 454,
et seq .
It is essentialin every billof interpleaderthat the plain-
tiffshould show that each of the defendants claim a right, and
such a right as they may interplead for; otherwise both of
the defendants may demur , the one because the bill shows
no claim or right against him , and the other because the bill
showing no claim of right in the co-defendant, shows no cause
of interpleader. Starlingv.Brown , 7 Bush, 165.
Decree of interpleader is only necessary to enable the
complainant to know to whom of severalpersons claiming the
same thing in differentrightshe may with safety make pay-
ment . French v .Howard , 3 Bibb 302.
INTERPOLATION .- In the construction of wills it is not
proper to interpolateor supply an ellipsis, unless it be ren-
dered necessary by an apparent inconsistencyin the context,
or unless there be something in the sentence to show that
there isan ellipsis, which may be properly supplied by the in-
sertionof words found elsewhere in the sentence. Barclay v .
Dupuy , 6 В. М. 93 .
INTERPRETATIO TALIS FIENDA . Interpretatio talis
fienda est ut res magis valeat quam pereat: such an interpre-
tation is to be adopted, that the thing may rather stand than
fall. Cochran's Law Lexicon .
INTERPRETATIO TALIS (IN ) AMBIGUIS SEMPER.-
Interpretatiotalis(in) ambiguis semper fiendaest, ut evi-
tetur inconvenienset absurdum : in doubtful matters, such an
interpretationis to be adopted that inconsistencyand absurd-
ity may be avoided. Cochran's Law Lexicon.
INTERPRETERS .- Though the Ky . Stats. contain no
other provisionrespectingthe appointment of interpretersthan
Sec. 181, providingfor theirappointmentin bastardy cases,

INTERROGATORIES 1805 INTERSTATE COM .
and Sec. 1034, providing for their appointment in courts of
continuoussession, the courts, in the absence of statutory
authority, have the inherent power to allow the use of inter-
preters where justicerequires it. Nioum v . Com ., 128 Ky .
685, 108 S. W. 945, 33 R. 62.
INTERROGATORIES .— “ In equitable actions, a party
may annex to his pleading written interrogatoriesto the ad-
verse party, concerning any material allegationthereof; and
answers thereto, on oath, may be read by either party, as a
depositionbetween the party interrogatingand the party an-
swering." CivilCode , Sec. 140.
INTERSTATE COMMERCE .- See, also, EMPLOYERS' LIA-
BILITY ACT .
Generally. The question whether the business in which
a foreigncorporationis engaged within the State is interstate
commerce isone of fact to be determined by the proper tribu-
nal. Com . v . Read Phosphate Co., 113 Ky . 32, 67 S. W. 45 ,
23 R. 2284 .
The InterstateCommerce Act has no applicationto a con-
tract for transportationof goods between two points in the
same State. C. & O. Ry . Co. v . Maysville Brick Co., 132 Ky .
643 , 116 S. W. 1183 .
" InterstateCommerce ," within the exclusivejurisdictionof
Congress, involves, as an essentialand indispensableelement,
the transportationof propertyor intelligencefrom one State
to another, embracing not only the property or intelligenceso
sent but the physical agencies, as the railroad, express, and
telegraphcompaniesengaged in the commerce, and theirem-
ployes, as well as all persons who are directlyconnected as
principalsor agentsin carryingon the business, exceptthatas
applied to intelligenceit does not include all persons who
write letters, but is confined to those persons, such as the
postal authoritiesand employes of telegraph companies,
through whose agency or instrumentalitythe intelligencede-
sired to be sent is conveyed, and those who send or exchange
intelligenceas an essentialpart of theirbusinessand thatdi

INTERSTATE COM . 1806 INTERSTATE COM .
rectly resultsin the transportationof property. U. S. Fidel-
ity& Guar .Co. v .Com .,139 Ky .27,129 S. W. 314.
Recovery under the Federal Employers LiabilityAct al-
lowed only when carrier is engaged in interstatecommerce ,
and employe is employed in such commerce ; but the co-em-
ploye causing injury need not be engaged in such commerce .
Pedersen v . Delaware , etc., R. Co., 229 U. S. 146 .
A person engaged in duty of maintaining tracks, bridges
and engines or cars in proper condition, when in use in inter-
state commerce , is engaged in such commerce . Id .
Interstatetransportationis not ended by the arrivalof the
train at the terminal. The breaking up of the train and
moving the cars to the appropriate tracks for making up new
trains for further destinationor for unloading is as much a
part of interstatetransportationas the movement across the
Stateline. St.Louisetc.Ry . Co. v. Seale, 229 U. S. 156.
A brakeman on a train composed of some cars going from
one State into another is engaged in interstatecommerce , and
ifhe is guilty of contributorynegligencehis recovery must be
reduced in the proportion of his negligence to the combined
negligenceof the plaintiffand defendant. Nashville, C. & St.
L. Ry . Co. v .Banks , 156 Ky . 609, 161 S. W. 554.
Baggage is property within the terms of the Carmack
Amendment ; and the transportationof persons from one state
to another is interstatecommerce , as fully as the like trans-
portation of property, and as much within the regulative
power of Congress. " White Slave Traffic Act ." Hoke v .
United States, 227 U. S. 308 ; L. & N. R. Co. v . Miller, 156
Ky . 677, 162 S. W. 73 .
Sec. 201, Ky . Cons., which providedthatno railroadcom-
pany shall acquire by purchase " any parallelor competing
lineor structure, or operate the same ," is not a regulation of
interstatecommerce, nor does itsenforcementinfringeupon
the power of Congress to regulate commerce between the
States. L. & N. R. Co. v . Com ., 97 Ky . 675, 31 S. W. 476 ,
17 R. 427 .
A shipment of goods, which traversesanotherstate, though
the pointsof the originand destinationare in the same state,

INTERSTATE COM . 1807 INTERSTATE COM .
is interstatecommerce. Hanley v. RailroadCo., 187 U. S.
617 ;Railroad Co. v.Asher , 171 S. W. 1114.
The statuteauthorizingpoolingby farmers of theirprod-
uctsdoes not violatethe Federal Anti-trustAct of July 2,
1890 (Act July 2,1890, Chap . 647,26 Stat. 209 [U. S. Comp .
St. 1901, p. 3200]), known as the " Sherman Law ;" they
have no relationto interstatecommerce, but being confined
in their operation to products grown and pooled in the State
and to salestherein. Com . v. Hodges , 137 Ky . 233, 125 S. W.
689 .
Where , after the arrival of stock in the carrier's " break-
up " yards at the point of destination, the shipper demanded
that the stock be deliveredin the car to a connecting carrier,
with whose road the initialcarrierhad physicalconnection,
for shipment to anotherpointwithinthe State, such reship-

INTERSTATE COMMERCE ACT .— The original Inter-
stateCommerce Act of February 4, 1887, 24 Stat., 379, с. 104,
was amended in many respectsby the Act of June 29, 1906,
34 Stat. 584, c. 3591, which is generally referred to as the
Carmack Amendment . Cincinnati, N. O. & T. P. Ry . Co. v .
Rankin,153 Ky .730, 156 S. W. 400.
The InterstateCommerce Act does not deprive the State
courtsof their common -law jurisdiction. L. & N. R. Co. v .
Ohio Valley Tie Co., 161 Ky .212, 170 S. W. 633.
INTERSTATE COMMERCE COMMISSION .- The Inter-
stateCommerce Commission is not a court and cannot try ac-
tionsfor damages to interstateshipments, but has jurisdiction
tohear complaints in regard to ratesand rebates. L. & N. R.
Co.v . Scott,133 Ky .724, 118 S. W. 990. (Affirmed, 219 U. S.
209.)
The InterstateCommerce Commission has exclusivejuris-
diction to grant relieffrom unreasonable interstatefreight
rates and to establish reasonable ones .
An order of the Interstate Commerce Commission as to the
reasonablenessof an interstatefreightrateis conclusive, and
a rate or classificationof a commodity once establishedby it
may be taken advantageof by anyone affectedthereby. L. &
N. R. Co.v.Ohio ValleyTie Co.,161 Ky .212,170 S. W. 633.
INTERSTATE SHIPMENT .- The fact that coal was billed
from a point in Tennessee to a point in Kentucky does not
make the shipment an interstateone, it appearing that the
coal was mined in Kentucky and loaded on cars at a switch
at the mines and there taken charge of by the carrier. L. &
N. R. Co. v .Vancleave,63 S. W. 22,23 R. 479.

over the line of a carrierleaving and returning to the State.
between such points, is an interstateshipment. L. & N. R.
Co. v.Allen, 152 Ky .145, 153 S. W. 198.
INTERSTATE TRANSACTIONS .- The execution in this
State, by a residentthereof and by an agent, for and on be-
half of a non -resident, of a writing purporting to sellcertain
articlestherein designated and containing stipulationsgov-
erning salesby one party to the other, throughout the ensuing
year, evidences sales made in this State and not interstate
transactions. American Seeding Machine Co. v . Com ., 152
Ky . 589, 153 S. W. 972.
INTERURBAN ELECTRIC RAILWAY .- An electric rail-

and allintermediatepointsis a trunk railway within Ky . Con .,

cept to the highest bidder therefor, but that the sectionshall
not apply to a trunk railway. Dieboldv. Ky . TractionCo..
117 Ky . 146, 77 S. W. 674, 25 R. 1275, 63 L. R. A. 637. See,
also, Com .v.L. & E. R. Co., 141 Ky . 583.
Interurban electricrailroadcompanies are under the same
dutiesand responsibilities, so far as practicable, and have the
same powers, rights and privilegesas railroad corporations.
Ky . Stats., Sec.842a . See RAILROADS .
INTERVENING CAUSES . “ It is well settled that the
mere fact that there have been interveningcauses between the
defendant'snegligence and the plaintiff'sinjuriesis not suffi-
cientin law to relievethe former from liability; that is to say
the plaintiff'sinjuriesmay yet be naturaland proximate in
law, although between the defendant's negligenceand the in-
juriesother causes, conditionsor agencies may have operated,
and when thisis the case, the defendant is liable. So the de-
fendant is clearly responsiblewhere the intervening causes,
actsor conditionswere set in motion by his earliernegligence.
or naturally induced by such wrongful act or omission, or

INTERVENING INTERVENTION1815
even, it is generallyheld, if the interveningacts or conditions
were of a nature the happening of which was reasonably to
have been anticipated, though they may have been actsof the
plaintiffhimself. An act or omission may yet be negligent
and of a nature to charge the defendant with liability, al-
though no injurieswould have been sustainedbut for some in-
tervening cause ." 21 Am . & Eng .Ency . of Law (2nd
ed.), 490; Lou . Home Tel. Co. v. Gasper, 123 Ky . 128; L. &
N. R. Co. v . Eckmon , 137 Ky . 332 ; Watson v . Ky . & Ind .
Bridge Co., 137 Ky . 619 ; C. & O. Ry . Co. v. Young's Admr .,
146 Ky . 317, 142 S. W. 709.
INTERVENTION .-The actby which a thirdperson, not
originallya party to the suit, but claimingan interestin the
subject-matter, comes into the case, in order to protecthis
rightor interposehis claim. He is called the intervener.
Cochran's Law Lexicon .
" In an action or proceeding for the recovery of real or
personalproperty, or for the subjectionthereofto a demand of
the plaintiffunder an attachmentor other lien, any person
claiming a right to, or interestin, the property or itspro-
ceeds, may , before payment of the proceeds to the plaintiff,
file, in the action, hisverifiedpetition, statinghis claim and
controvertingthat of the plaintiff; whereupon the courtmay
orderhim to be made a defendant; and upon that being done ,
his petitionshallbe treatedas his answer. But if he be a non-
residenthe must give securityfor costs." CivilCode , Sec. 29.
For fulldiscussionof subject, " defendantsby intervention,"
seeNewman's Pleading and Practice, Sec. 187.
Under Sec. 29, CivilCode, providingthat in an actionto
recoverreal or personal property, or to enforce a lienthereon,
" any personclaiminga rightto or interestin the propertyor
itsproceeds" may intervene, one who seeksto setasidea deed
to land cannot intervene in an action brought by the grantee
to enforce a chattelmortgage executed to secure the rent ac-
cruing upon a lease of the land executed by him , though the
prayerof the interveningpetitionbe thatthe rentbe adjudged
tothe intervener. Vanmeter v.FidelityTrust & SafetyVault
Co.,53 S. W. 10,21 R. 744 .

INTIMIDATING . “ If two or more persons shallunlaw-
fully confederate or band themselves together and go forth

INTOXICATION .-See , also, DRUNKENNESS .
Intoxicationis a term which, in itseveryday application,
is given a very broad meaning . To some men it means being
under the influence of the intoxicant to such an extent as to
render the person helpless; while othersspeak of one as intoxi-
cated when slightlyunder the influence of the intoxicant.
Webster definesit as " stateof being drunk , inebriety, drunk-
enness. National Council, etc.v . Wilson, 147 Ky . 293, 143 S.
W. 1000 .
"
" The word intoxicationisso broad in itssignificancethat,
as stated in National Council of Knights and Ladies of Se-
curity v . Wilson, Guardian , 147 Ky . 293 , what one would de-
termine to be a stateof intoxicationwould be regarded by an-
otheras dulysober." ColumbiaLifeIns.Co.v. Tousey,152
Ky . 447, 153 S. W. 767 .
Under an accidentpolicy excepting " injuries, fatal or oth-
erwise, receivedwhile or in consequence of having been under
the influenceof, or affectedby, or resultingdirectlyor indi-
rectlyfrom intoxicants," a recoverywillnot be denied on the

INTOXICATION INTOXICATING1817
ground that assured was injured " while" under the influence
of or affectedby intoxicants, unless he was drunk at the time
he was injured; but a recovery will be denied on the ground
thathe was injured " in consequence of" being under the in-
fluenceof or affectedby intoxicantsif he was so far under the
influenceof intoxicantsthat his injury was received in con-
sequence of such influence, though it did not amount to
drunkenness. Campbell v . Fidelity& Cas. Co., 109 Ky . 661,
60 S. W. 492 ,22 R. 1295.
" Intoxication" is not a technical word needing expert
testimonyto explain or define it. Therefore a person who has
drunk a beveragesaidto be intoxicatingmay testifywhether
or not it intoxicatedhim , and also as to its intoxicatingef-
fectsupon otherpersons. Gourleyv. Com ., 140 Ky . 221, 131
S. W. 34 .
If a personby reasonof intoxicationfailsto exercisesuch
carefor hissafetyas might ordinarilybe expectedof a sober
personof ordinaryprudence, and by reasonof such failureis
injured, he cannot recover. Southern Ry . Co. in Ky . v . San-
ders, 145 Ky .679,141 S. W. 77 .
As Ky .Stats., Sec.2557, in terms prohibitsthesaleof malt
liquorsin localoptionterritory, it is enough to inquire
whether the liquor sold is malt liquor; if it is, the guilt is
fixed. Whether itintoxicatessome peopleor not, or isonly a
mild intoxicant,or whether defendantbelievedthatitwas an
innocentsoftdrink, are immaterial. Flanders v. Com ., 140
Ky . 38 , 130 S. W. 809 .
Where whisky, brandy, wine, or beer is soldin prohibition
territory, itwillbe presumed thattheseliquidsare intoxicants
withinthe meaning of the statute; but where other concoctions
or mixtures, the intoxicatingeffectof which is not known or
established, are sold, then the factthatthey are intoxicantsis
materialto the guilt of the defendant; and this fact must be
allegedand provedbeforehe can be found guilty. Flandersv.
Com.,140 Ky .659,131 S. W. 495.

INVENTION . A man's intellectis his best property, and
everyonehas a naturaland inviolablerightto appropriateto
hisown use, the productionof his own industryand the con

ceptionsof his own genius. What issuch an originaland

ganizedmode of operation, essentiallydifferingfrom any pre-

INVITATION TO ALIGHT .- The announcement of the

INVITATION TO CROSS .- At a railroadcrossing, where
there was a main track, the employes cut a freight train
on the side track in order to allow teams on the highway
to pass. Plaintiffattempted to cross from the side next
to the side track, when an engine, which plaintiffcould not
see on account of the freight train, ran without warning
over the crossing, breaking the tongue out of his wagon ,
and plaintiffwas either struck or thrown against some ob-
ject and injured. Held that the cutting of the cars on the
side track was an invitationto plaintiffto cross, and it was
negligence to run the engine over the crossing at that time
without warning . I. C. R. Co. v . Morris, 90 S. W. 979, 28
R. 956 .

" Irregularityof the orders made during the trial" and

IRREPARABLE INJURY .- It is a well-settled rule that
equity will interfereby injunction where irreparable injury
isthreatened, but it is equally well settledthat the threatened
injury must be substantialin its character in order to war-
rant the court to grant an injunction. Equity will not inter-
pose where the complainant's injury is merely nominal or
theoretical. Devou v . Pence 106 S. W. 874, 32 R. 697.
An irreparableinjury is one for which the complainant
can not be adequately compensated in damages , or where
there is no pecuniary standard for the measurement of the
damages . The inadequacy of damages as a compensation may
be due to the nature of the injury itselfor to the nature
ofthe rightor propertyinjured, or may be due to the insol-
vency or want of responsibilityof the person committing it;
but where compensationin money would be adequate, the
injurycan not be saidto be irreparable. Id.

In a will the term " issue" may be employed either as a
word of purchase, or of limitation, accordingto which sense
willbest effectuatethe testator'sintention. It is usually con-
strued as a word of limitation, and not of purchase, unless
there be controllingwords , clearlyshowing that the contrary
meaning was intended by its use. Bonnycastle v . Lilly, 153
Ky . 834, 156 S. W. 874.
" In Wistar v. Scott, 105 Pa . St., 200, 51 Am . Rep ., 198,
there is a full discussionof the term 'issue,' and a recog-
nitionof the rule that in a will its prima facie construction
is'heirs of the body ,' or linealdescendants, indefinitely, and
that it is held to be a word of limitation and not a word
of purchase." Bonnycastle v. Lilly, 153 Ky . 834, 156 S. W.
874 .
" Issue" is a word of purchase, as well as a word of lim-
itation, and it may be construed to operate in the one way ,
or the other, according to the intentionwith which it is used,
and in executory devises of personal estate, slight circum-
stances have been considered as sufficient evidence of an
intentionto use it as a word of purchase, not of limitation.
Moore's Trustees v . Howe's Heirs, 4 T. B. Monroe , 205 .
Under Sec. 4841, Ky . Stats., providing that the " issue"

which the deviseewould otherwisehave taken, a bastard child
of a daughterof a testator, who died beforethe testator, took

" the word 'issue' as applied to the descent of real estate
shall be construed to include all the lawful linealdescendants of

all persons who might lawfullyinheritthe estate. Cherry
v . Mitchell, 55 S. W. 689, 21 R. 1547.

forcea lienon the land. Park v. Humpich , 47 S. W. 768,
20 R. 879 .

ISSUES OF LAW AND FACT .- " Issues arise on the
pleadings when a fact, or conclusion of law, is maintained

" An issueof fact arises: 1. Upon a traversein an answer
or other pleading of a material allegationin a petition or

" Issues of law shallbe triedby the court. Issues of fact,
in prosecution for offensesof which the punishment is lim-
ited to a fine of sixteendollars, shallbe tried by the court.
All other issues of fact shall be tried by a jury." Crim .
Code , Sec. 180.
" Legal pleadings are a species of logic, in which the
partiesare compelled to state their respectivepremises, and
eithertacitlyor expresslyto admit the factsas stated, until
they arrive at some contestedfact or facts, which one affirms
and the other denies. This forms an issue, to which the jury
responds; and in most cases of this descriptiona jury was
necessary at the date of the constitution, and remains so
still." Harrison v. Chiles, 3 Litt. 201.
Where a plaintifftenders the issue and it is accepted as
tendered, the plaintiffshould abide the result, although it

City of Louisville, 161 Ky . 596, 171 S. W. 190.
" An issue of law arises on a demurrer to the indictment ."

Where there are several issues and the record states the

Hatcher v . Fowler , 1 Bibb , 337 ; Fowler v ..Garrett, 3 J. J.

Jury sworn to " try the issuejoined" can not decideupon

JAILER . It is provided in Civil Code , Sec. 732, that in

brought, or to which the processreferredto is directed.
A jaileris a " peace officer" under Crim. Code, Sec. 26,
which reads as follows: " A warrant of arrestmay be issued

ticesof the peace; and may be executed by the following
officers, who are calledpeace officersin this Code, viz:
sheriffs, constables, coroners, jailers, marshals and police-
men ."
"The jailerisan officerof the Commonwealth, with well-
definedduties and responsibilities, and within the scope of

JEOFAILS .- (Corrupted from j'ai failli, Fr., I have

Statutes of amendments and jeofailswere those passed from

Informal but not immaterial issuesare cured by the statute

JEOPARDY - Generally. "No personshall, for the same
offense, be twice put in jeopardy of his lifeor limb." Ky .
Constitution (1891), Sec. 13.
The fundamental law protects one from being twice put
in jeopardy. Indeed, the rule is imbedded in the very ele-
ments of the common law, not only as to criminal, but even
as tocivilcases;nemo debetbisvexari,pro una eteadem causa.
Chesapeake& Ohio R. Co. v. Com ., 88 Ky . 371.

JEOPARDY JEOPARDY1831
At the time of the adoption of the Constitution, as now ,
the principleevidentlyintended to be covered by the words,
" shallnot for the same offensebe twice put in jeopardy of
lifeor limb," found expression in the following maxims of
the common law : " Nemo debet bis vexari pro una et eadem
causa," applicableto civilcases- no man shallbe twice vexed
for one and the same cause; and in criminal law by the
maxims : " Nemo bis punitur pro eodem delicto," " Nemo
debit bis puniri pro uno delicto," no one can be twice pun-
ished for the same crime or misdemeanor . Williams v . Com .,
78 Ky . 97.
The statuteprovidingthat a person convicteda second
time for felonyshallbe confinedin the penitentiarynot less
than double the time of the first conviction is not a violation
of the constitutionalprovisionthat no one for the same offense
shallbe twice put in jeopardy. Chenowith v . Com ., 11 R.
561 .
"A person is in legaljeopardywhen he isput upon trial
before a court of competent jurisdiction, upon indictment or
information which is sufficient in form and substance to sus-
tain a conviction, and a jury has been charged with his deliv-
erance. And a jury is said to be thus charged when they
have been empaneled and sworn ." Cooley's Constitutional
Limitations, p . 327. See also Williams v . Com ., 78 Ky . 93 ;
Thomas v . Com ., 150 Ky . 374 ; Bennett v . Com ., 150 Ky . 604,
150 S. W. 806 .
A defendantis placedin jeopardywhen afterbeing reg-
ularlyindictedhe is triedbeforea jury regularlyempaneled
and sworn , though their verdict of not guilty was rendered
under a peremptory instructionfrom the court on the erron-
eous assumption that the court was without jurisdiction.
Com . v . Ball, 126 Ky . 542, 104 S. W. 325, 31 R. 887.
For a plea of former jeopardy to avail, it must appear
that in each prosecution, the accused, the sovereignty whose
law has been violated, and the offensesnot only as to the
actbut alsoas to the crime, are identical. McIntyre v. Com .,
154 Ky . 149 ,156 S. W. 1058.

JEOPARDY JEOPARDY1832
Where one is on trialfor murder before a jury sworn to
try the case, he is then in jeopardy, and every interference
on the part of the State after the jury has charge of the
prisoner, preventing a verdict, declaring his guilt or inno-
cence, unless upon facts clearly establishinga case of neces-
sity, or showing his consent, must operate as an acquittal, and
bar another prosecution for the same offense. Obrian v.
Com ., 9 Bush , 334.
Criminal Cases Alone Included. The constitutionalpro-
vision that the accused shall not be twice put in jeopardy
for the same offense does not apply to mere civil actions
for the recoveryof penalties. The constitutionalinhibition
applies only to criminal proceedings- such offensesas are
designated by our statute as feloniesor misdemeanors . See
1 Bishop on Cr . Law , Sec. 656 ; Com . v. Prall, 146 Ky . 109,
142 S. W. 202 .
Offense Included Within Another . It is true that in
casesof offenses, includedwithinone another, if a personhas
been put in jeopardy for the one which includes all the rest,
he has been in such jeopardy as to each of the others, and
this is often true where he has been put in jeopardy for
even the lowest of the offenses, such jeopardy will bar a
prosecution for the others. (1st. Bishop Crim . Law , Sec.
1056.) But this rule does not apply where the two or more
offensesarising out of the same transactionare not included
one within the other. Com . v . Browning , 146 Ky . 770, 143
S. W. 407 .
An acquittalor convictionof the offenseof burglary is
a bar to a prosecutionfor a larcenyforming part of the same
transaction. Triplettv. Com ., 84 Ky . 193, 8 R. 67.
" If an offense consist of differentdegrees, a conviction,
or acquittal, by judgment upon a verdict, shallbe a bar to
another prosecutionfor the offensein any of itsdegrees."
Crim . Code , Sec. 177.
New TrialGranted. A plea of former jeopardy can not
be sustainedwhere , after conviction, the verdict was set aside
and a new trialgranted on the defendant's motion. Jones

v. Com ., 124 Ky . 26, 97 S. W. 1118, 30 R. 288 ; Com . v .
Arnold, 83 Ky . 1.
Plea of Former Jeopardy . The entry on the record of
the plea of former acquittalor convictionmay be substantially
in the following form : " The defendant pleads that he has
been acquitted (or convicted, as the case may be) of the of-
fensechargedin theindictment,by thejudgmentof
court (naming it) renderedon the day of-
(naming the time)". CriminalCode, Sec.164.
Criminal Code, Sec. 164, prescribesthe form of the plea of
formerjeopardy, and thisform must be followed. The mere
entry of " a plea of former trialand conviction," without any
other referenceto or mention of the plea, willnot present the
defense. Shirleyv . Com . 143 Ky . 183, 136 S. W. 227.
A plea of former convictionin the following form is suffic-
ient: " The defendant pleads that ithas been convictedof the
offensechargedin the indictmentby thejudgment of the Shel-
by CircuitCourt renderedon the6th day of February,1905."
Standard Oil Co. v . Com . 91 S. W. 1127, 28 R. 1369.
A plea that a demurrer was sustained to a former indict-
ment forthesame offense,and the indictmentdismissed, isnot
good as a plea of former acquittalunless it is further pleaded
