acceptance by the injured party of a certain sum from one

JOINT TORT 1847 JOINT TORT
of two joint tort feasors in part satisfactionof his cause of
action does not release the other. Louisville & Evansville Mail
Co. v . Barnes ' Admr ., 117 Ky . 860 ; City of Covington v .
Westbay , 156 Ky . 839, 162 S. W. 91.
Contributionor Indemnity. The machinery of the courts
will not be put in motion to relieveone wrongdoer from the
consequencesof his wrongful act against another wrongdoer ,
equally guilty. No citizenhas a right to invoke the aid of
the courts for redress when it is necessary for him in
statinghis complaint to say that he and another by reason of
a breach of public duty upon the part of each of them have
caused injury or death to another citizen; and it matters not
thattheirseveralbreacheswere separateand distinctfrom each
other, and that neither participatedin or was connected with
the breach of the other, if the two breaches concurred to bring
about the result. The right of contribution between tort
feasors, when allowed at all, grows out of equity, although
enforcedat law. City of Louisvillev. LouisvilleRy . Co., 156
Ky . 141, 160 S. W. 771.
Where two tortiousacts, separate and distinctfrom each,
were committed by two independent agencies, but concurred
in bringingabout the injury, which would not have occurred
but for the commission of eitherof them , one of the wrongdoers
who has been mulcted in damages can not recover from the
other in contribution. City of Louisvillev . LouisvilleRy . Co.,
156 Ky . 141, 160 S. W. 771. See, also, Owensboro City Ry .
Co. v .L.,H. & St.L. Ry .Co., 165 Ky . 691.
One whose negligentact, concurringwith a separateand
distinctnegligentact of another, was the proximatecause of
an injury, who has been held liable in damages , can not
recoverby way of contributionfrom the other tort feasor.
City of Louisvillev. LouisvilleRy . Co., 156 Ky . 141, 160
S. W. 771 .
As between one liableby contractand one responsiblefor
a tort committed in connection with the contract, there is no
right of contribution, as there is no privity between them .
Branninv.Loving,82 Ky .370,6 R. 328.
There can be no contribution between joint wrongdoers
where the person who seeksto compel contributionknew , or

JOINT TORT JOINT TORT1848
must be presumed to have known , thatthe act was unlawful.
Sutton v .Morris, 102 Ky .611, 44 S. W. 127, 19 R. 1654.
The classof casesin which wrongdoers are held as not being
in pari delictois where the one asking for indemnity was not
an actual participantin the wrong , but by reason of the negli-
gent act of some one actingunder his authoritythe liability
arises. Cincinnati, etc., Ry . Co. v . L. & N. R. Co., 97 Ky .
128 ,30 S. W. 408,17 R. 21 .
There can be no indemnity among tort feasors. But this
ruledoes not apply with personsseekingindemnitywho did
not join in the unlawful act, although he may thereby be
exposed to liability, or to one who did not know , and was not
presumed to know that his act was unlawful ; it must appear
that the partiesare in pari delictoas to each other before
plaintiff'srecoverywill be barred. Robertson v. City of
Paducah , 146 Ky . 188, 142 S. W. 370.
" As under the rule already laid down , the party wronged
may , at his election, compel any one of the partieschargeable
with the act, or any number lessthan the whole to compensate
him for the injury, it becomes a considerationof the highest
importance to the person, or persons thus singled out and
compelled to bear the loss, whether the others who were equally
liablemay be compelled to contributefor his relief. On this
subject there is a general rule, and there are also some very
important exceptions. The general rulemay be found expressed
in the maxim that no man can make his own misconduct the
ground for an action in his own favor. If he suffersbecause
of his own wrongdoing , the law willnot relievehim . The law
can not recognizeequitiesas springingfrom a wrong in favor
of one committing it. But there are some exceptionsto the
general rule which rest upon reasons at least as forcibleas
those which support the rule itself. There are cases where ,
although the law holds all the parties as wrongdoers to the
injuredparty, yet, as between themselvessome of them may
not be wrongdoers at all, and theirequity to require the
othersto respondforallthedamages may be complete. There
are many such cases where the wrongs are unintentional, or
where the party,by reason of some relation,is made chargeable

JOINT TORT JOINT TORT1849
with the conduct of others." Robertson v . City of Paducah ,
146 Ky . 188, 142 S. W. 370, quoting from Cooley on Torts,
Vol. 1, p . 254.
An excellentstatement of the same doctrine may be found
in Geneva v . Brush ElectricCo., 50 Hun . 581, 3 N. Y. Supp .
595, wherein it is said: " The cases in which recovery over
ispermitted in favor of one who has been compelled to respond
to the party injured, are exceptionsto the generalrule, and
are based upon principlesof equity. Such exceptions obtain
intwo classesofcases: (1) Where thepartyclaimingindemnity
has not been guilty of any fault, except technically, or con-
structively, as where an innocent master was held to respond
forthe tortof his servantactingwithin the scope of his employ-
ment ; or, (2 ) Where both partieshave been in fault, but not
inthe same fault,towardsthe party injured,and the faultof
the party from whom indemnity is claimed was the primary
and efficientcause of the injury." Robertson v. City of
Paducah , 146 Ky . 188, 142 S. W. 370.
While it is the general rule that there can be no indemnity
as among tort feasors, where they were not in fault to the
same degree,and the faultof the one from whom the indemnity
issought was the primary and efficientcause of the injury for
which the other has been held liable, the right of indemnity
exists. Where the undisputed evidence shows that one tort
feasor has been held liable in damages by reason of the
primarily wrongful act of another, in which wrongful act he
was not an activeparticipant, he is entitledto a peremptory
instructionas against the originalwrongdoer . City of George-
town v . Cantrill, 158 Ky . 378, 164 S. W. 929.
There can be no indemnity as between joint tort feasors
unless the one seeking indemnity did not join in the unlawful
act,though exposedto liabilitytherefor,and he has been made
to sufferthereforin damages. City of Georgetown v. Groff,
136 Ky . 662, 124 S. W. 888.
Where a railroad company pays passengers for injuries
receivedin a collisionat a crossingbetween itstrain and the
trainof another railroadcompany , and sues the lattercompany
for indemnity, it must in addition to alleging defendant's

JOINT TORT 1850 JOINT TRESPASS
negligencein causing the collisionallegefactsshowing a right
of recovery by the passengers against the plaintiff. It is not
sufficientto allegeplaintiffwas compelled to pay the damages
wholly through the defendant's negligence. Failing to allege
factsshowing that itwas liableas between itand itspassengers,
itwillbe regarded as one who has compensated the passengers
for a wrong done them by another and then seeks to be
substitutedto theirrights, which can not be done . Cincinnati,
etc., Ry . Co. v . L. & N. R. Co., 97 Ky . 128, 30 S. W. 408, 17
R. 21 .
Even though the carrierwhich has been compelled to respond
in damages to its passengers was in fact liable, stillit can
not look to the other carrier for indemnity or contribution
unless it shows that it was not an actual participantin the
commissionof the injury, the two companies being regarded
as in pari delictowithout regard to the relativedegrees of
their neglect. Id .
In an actionto recoverdamages for injuriesto land by mine
slack cast in a stream, where the evidence shows that there
were severalmines from which slack could have come , the fact
that the plaintiffcan not establishthat the injury was due
solely to slack which came from defendant's mine will not
defeat a recovery. North JellicoCoal Co. v . Trosper, 165
Ky . 418, 177 S. W. 241.
JOINT TRESPASS .—“ Black's Law Dictionary defines
'joint trespassers' as 'two or more persons who unite in com-
mitting a trespass.' And in the case of Ferguson v . Terry,
1 B. Mon. 96, it was held 'that none were liablefor trespass
committed by others unless they gave authority, command , or
assentto it.'" Bonte v . Postel, 109 Ky . 72 .
Without a common intent and co-operation there can be no
jointliabilityin the commission of severaltrespasses, and
Sec. 12 of the Kentucky Statutesdoes not change the common
law of proceeding, or authorizea joint actionfor several
trespasses, but only authorizes several verdicts and several
judgments against each of the several joint trespassersin a
jointaction. See Ferguson v. Terry, 1 B. Mon. 96; Henry v.
Sennott, 3 B. Mon. 311. Bonte v . Postel, 109 Ky . 75.

JOINTLY BOUND 1851 JOINTURE
JOINTLY BOUND .- A contract by which the parties
thereto agree to be jointlybound to a certainbank on allnotes
endorsed by eitherof said parties, isnot a contractof endorse-
ment ; but isenforcibleas a primary liability. FirstNat.Bank
of Louisvillev. Doherty , 156 Ky . 386, 161 S. W. 211.
JOINTLY AND SEVERALLY.- Where the obligors in a
note " jointlyand severallypromise" to pay, any one of them
may , for the purpose of pleading the statute of limitations
applicabletosureties,show that he was merely a surety,without
showing that the obligeehad knowledge of that fact. Craddock
v. Lee, 61 S. W. 22, 22 R. 1651.
" Where an instrument containing the words , 'I promise to
pay,' is signedby two or more persons, they are deemed to
be jointlyand severallyliablethereon." NegotiableInst.
Law , Ky . Stats. (1915),Sec.3720b, Subsec.17.
JOINTURE . A jointureis a competentlivelihoodor free-
holdforthewife,of landsand tenements,to takeeffectin profit
or possession. presently after the death of the husband for
thelifeof thewifeat least. Griderv.Eubanks, 12 Bush,513.
Any conveyance or devise of real or personal estatewhich
isintended by the grantor or devisorto be in lieu or satisfac-
tionof dower , must be deemed a conveyance or devise by way
of jointure within the meaning of the statute. Tevis v .
McCreary , 3 Met . 153.
Jointure will not bar dower , unless expressed or intended
to be in satisfactionof dower ; the law on this subject has
not been changed by the Revised Statutes. Yancy v . Smith ,
2 Met . 409 .
A jointuremust take effectimmediatelyupon the death of
thehusband; itmust be for the lifeof the wife at least, and
not for the lifeof another, or for any term of years or other
smallerestate; itmust be made to thewife,and not to another
in trust for her and it must be in satisfaction of the whole
dower, and not of any portionof it, and shouldso appear in
thedeed. Crump v. Crump , 107 S. W. 778, 32 R. 1041.
When jointureisa bar todower. Ky .Stats., Sec.2136.

JOINTURE JOURNAL1852
A provisionin a deed of trustwillnot be consideredan
equitablejointurewhere itcan not be impliedfrom the deed
itselfthat the provision was intended to be in lieu of the
wife'spotentialrightto dower, and especiallywhen the pro-
vision made was less than her contingent right to dower .
McDowell v . Prather, 8 Bush , 61.
A contractwhereby the intendedwife agreesto accepta
provisionor annuity in lieu of dower, in the event of her
survivingher husband , is generally a valid equitablejointure,
which if not rendered unavailable without her consent, will
bar her right of dower . Garrard v . Garrard, 7 Bush , 440 .
So much of Sec. 2136 of the Statutes as provides that the
wife may voluntarilyrelinquisha conveyance or devise by way
of jointuremade afterher marriage, was impliedlyrepealed
by Sec. 2128 of the Statutes conferringupon married women
the power to make contractswith their husbands, and now a
married woman , who accepts jointure in lieu of dower and
distribution,can not voluntarilyelectto relinquishthe jointure;
but she may , if the contract of jointure was not freely and
voluntarilyentered into, or if she did not receivea sufficient
considerationfor the execution of the contract, maintain a suit
to have the contract set aside. Redwine's Exr . v . Redwine ,
160 Ky .282,169 S. W. 864.
A post-nuptial contractfor the conveyance or devise of real
or personal estatereceivedby way of jointure, as described in
Sec.2136 of theStatutes,may alsobe avoidedby the widow if
her contractto acceptjointurewas not based on a sufficient
considerationor if it was not freelyand voluntarilyentered
into. Redwine's Exr .v.Redwine's 160 Ky . 282, 169 S. W. 864.
A conveyance or devise " by way of jointure," within the
meaning of Sec. 6 , Art . 4, Chap . 52, Gen. Stats., is a con-
veyance or devise to the wife intended by the husband to be
in lieuof dower . This intent must appear eitherexpressly or
by implication; the bar does not arise by operation of law .
Pepper v . Thomas , 85 Ky . 539, 9 R. 122.
JOURNAL .- A journal held to be intended more as an
index, than as a full and complete record of the proceedings
of a council. Meacham Cont . Co. v . Kleiderer, 146 Ky . 441.

JUDGE JUDGE1853
JUDGE . See, also, COURT ; BIAS.
It isprovided in CivilCode , Sec. 732 , that in the construc-
tion of the Code ,exceptingCriminal Courts and judges thereof,
the words " Circuit Court " embrace courts having similar
jurisdiction, in whole or in part, to that of CircuitCourts;
and the words " circuitjudge " embrace judges of either of
said courts .
It is provided in Civil Code , Sec. 732 that in the con-
struction of the Code the word " court " refers to the court
in which an action or proceeding is pending, or may be
brought; and the words " judge," " justice," " clerk," refer
to officers of such court .
It is provided in Civil Code , Sec. 732, that in the con-
struction of the Code the words " Justices Courts " or
" Quarterly Courts" embrace courts having similar jurisdic-
tion, in whole or in part, to that of justices' courts or
Quarterly Courts; and the words " justice" or " presiding
judge," and words of likeimport, embrace judges of either
of said courts .
A court in itsbroadestsenseisa creatureof the law charged
with the administrationof justice, and is composed of a judge,
a clerk, and certain other officerscharged with the duty of
executing the court's processes and carrying into effectits
orders. But in a more restrictedsense, and the one in which
the words are customarily used, a court refers to the judge
thereofalone. Bosworth v .Marshall,165 Ky .32,176 S. W. 348.
Under Sec. 1136 of the Ky . Stats., the jury and not the
judgemust fixthe indeterminatesentence. Satterfieldv. Com .
165 Ky .216, 176 S. W. 980.
A judge can not constitutea court and render valid judg-
ments at a place other than that prescribedby law. Therefore
a justicecan not hold his court and render a valid judgment
outside of the district in which he was elected. Venhoff v .
Morgan , 11 R. 276.
A practicingattorneywho , by agreement of parties,presided
during the argument of counsel, the regularjudge being absent,
was a " specialjudge," and to him an applicationto have

JUDGE JUDGE1854
objectionablestatementsof counsel withdrawn could have been
made . L. & N. R. Co. v . Webb , 11 R. 369.
It is not necessary that a specialjudge shallbe residentof
the districtin which he presides. Com . v . Carnes, 98 S. W.
1045 , 30 R. 506 .
" It is a universalrule of law that a judge, acting within
the scope of his jurisdiction, is not liablein an action for
damages for any opinion he may deliveras such, or any rule
or action he may take for the proper conduct of the business
of his court. This doctrine applies to judges of all grades,
and to allpersons who act in a judicialcapacity while acting
within the limitsof theirjurisdiction, even if he should act
illegallyor erroneously. The only casein which he isliable
iswhere he actsfrom an impure,maliciousor corruptmotive."
Willisv. Linn, 148 Ky . 841 ; Ackerson v. Semple , 163 Ky . 395,
173 S. W. 1153 .
A specialjudge appointed under the act of 1910 may make
such orders or perform such acts, in vacation, for the comple-
tion of his records or the enforcement of his judgments , that
a regular judge may make in vacation; and this power will
continue until the next term, or until he shall have been
supersededby the return of the regularjudge (if he has
failedto serve by reason of his absence) or by the designation
of a differentspecialjudge by the Governor . Dial v . Com .,
143 Ky . 118 ; 136 S. W. 139 .
When a specialjudge has given time beyond the term to
filea bill of exceptionshe may , at a specialterm calledby
him , approve and filethe bill; or he may do this at the next
regular term when he has, at the previous term, so given
time . Id .
A specialjudge appointed to try a case because of the dis-
qualificationof the regular circuitjudge has authority to
hear and determine all questionsinvolved so long as the reg-
ular judge is disqualifiedfrom acting, and the specialjudge
is not superseded by the appointment of another specialjudge.
Sublett v. Gardner , 144 Ky . 190, 137 S. W. 864.

JUDGE IN HIS 1855 JUDGMENT
JUDGE IN HIS OWN CASE . It never was the law that
one could be a judge in his own case. Orr v . Kevil. 124 Ky .
720, 30 R. 761 ; 100 S. W. 314.
JUDGES OF CITY COURTS .- Judges of City Courts are
"magistrates" under Crim. Code, Sec. 26, which reads as
follows: " A warrant of arrestmay be issuedby the follow-
ing officers, who are calledmagistratesin this Code, viz.,
judgesof the County Courts, judgesof City or PoliceCourts,
mayors, chairmen of the trusteesof towns and justicesof the
peace; and may be executedby the followingofficers,who are
calledpeace officersin this Code, viz., sheriffs, constables,
coroners, jailers, marshalsand policemen."
JUDGES OF COUNTY COURTS . Judges of County
Courtsare " magistrates," under Crim. Code, Sec. 26 which
readsas follows: "A warrant of arrestmay be issuedby the
followingofficers, who are calledmagistratesin thisCode, viz.,
judges of the County Courts, judges of City or Police Courts,
mayors , chairmen of the trusteesof towns and justicesof the
peace; and may be executed by the following officers, who
arecalledpeaceofficersin thisCode, viz., sheriffs, constables,
coroners, jailers, marshals and policemen." See, also, title
COUNTY COURT .
JUDGMENT .- See RES JUDICATA ; DULY GIVEN OR MADE .
Generally. "A judgment is a finaldeterminationof a
rightof a party in an actionor proceeding." CivilCode , Sec.
368 .
The judgment of the court isthe pronouncement of the judge
on the issue submitted to him ; the record being merely his-
toricaland evidentiary. Montgomery v. Viers, 130 Ky . 694,
114 S. W. 251 .
In every judgment theremust be an actor and a reus. Tay-
lorv . Com ., 3 J. J. M. 401.
A judgment rendered by a judge in his own case and in his
own favor isabsolutelyvoid. Chism v .Evans , 9 R. 765.
A judgment is an entiretyand can not be reversed as to
one party without reversing as to all in whose favor it is

JUDGMENT JUDGMENT1.856
rendered. Murphy v.O'Reilly,78 Ky .263;Sullivanv.Frank-
fort B. & L. Asso ., 13 R. 48.
A judgment againstthe " Clay City Lumber Company " is
void,the company not being incorporated. Soper v. Clay City
Lumber Co., 53 S. W. 267, 21 R. 933 .
Judgment can not be entered on an issuenot made by the
pleadings. Irelandv. Bowman & Cockrell, 130 Ky . 153, 113
S. W. 56,114 S. W. 338.
Judgments for money are not liensupon the judgment
debtor's property. Low v . Skaggs , 105 S. W. 439, 31 R. 1292.
A note at the foot of a judgment is a part of the judgment .
Jones v . Overstreet, 4 Mon. 547.
The judgment isthe conclusionof law from the facts, which
the court will draw and pronounce, though not prayed in the
pleadings. Kerley v. Hume , 3 Mon. 183.
A judgment that plaintiffsare entitledto recover is not a
judgment in personam . Zeiglerv .Means ,8 Ky . Opin . 221.
Although a judgment may be prima facieevidence of a fact,
an allegationof the recovery of the judgment is not a suffi-
cient allegationof the fact. Turner v . Hamlin , 152 Ky . 469,
153 S. W. 778 .
A writ of habere facias was equivalentto a judgment for
the land where the petitionfor the recovery of the land was
taken for confessed and damages were assessedby a jury.
Dodd v .Hays , 3 R. 536 .
In Raymond v.Smith, 1 Metc.65,71 Am . Dec. 458, itwas
held that a loosepaper among the filesof a suit, purporting
to be thejudgment of the courtin thatcase,but not recorded,
nor signed by the judge, nor indorsed in any manner to show
that it had been filed, nor in the handwritingof the judge,
was not a judgment of the court. See, also, FINAL JUDGMENT .
Entry- Signature of Judge. It is well settledthat it is
essentialto the validityof a judgment that it shallbe entered
upon the orderbook of the court, and signedby a judge, and
that an unsigned judgment is no judgment at all. Farris v .
Matthews, 149 Ky . 455, 149 S. W. 896; Commonwealth v.
Chambers, 1 J. J. Marsh. 108; Raymond v. Smith, 1 Met. 65;

JUDGMENT JUDGMENT1857
Johnson v . Commonwealth , 80 Ky . 377 ; Ewell v. Jackson, 129
Ky . 214 ; InterstatePetroleum Co. v . Farris, 159 Ky . 820, 169
S. W. 535 .
" Whatever appears upon its face to be intended as the
entry of a judgment will be regarded as sufficientlyformal,
if it shows : (1) The reliefgranted; and (2 ) that the grant
was made by the court in whose record the entry is written.
In specifyingthe reliefgranted, the partiesof and for whom
itisgiven must, of course, be sufficientlyidentified." Mont-
gomery v . Viers, 130 Ky . 694, 114 S. W. 251.
The judge having signed a minute on the minute book,
" judgment to be enteredby the clerk," and the judgment
being drawn and put in the papers but never entered by the
clerkon the order book or signedby the judge, thereis no
judgment of the court, and the statusof the partiescontinues
as before. Hellardv.RockcastleMining, Lumber & Oil Co.,
153 Ky .259, 154 S. W. 401.
It isindispensableto the validityof a judgment that itshall
be enteredin a book providedfor that purpose, and signed
afterbeing so enteredby the presidingjudge or justiceof
the court. Anderson's Committee v. Anderson's Admr ., 161
Ky . 18, 170 S. W. 213.
An order or a judgment of a court of record never has any
validityuntil it is signed by the authoritydesignated by law
for that purpose. Fox v .Lantrip, 162 Ky . 178, 172 S. W. 133.
Under Ky . Stats., Sec. 1088, making justices' courts of
record, it is indispensable to the validity of a justice's
judgment that it be enteredin the book providedfor that
purpose and signed after being so entered by the presiding
judge or justiceof the court. Robertsonv. Donelan, 138 Ку .
149, 127 S. W. 754.
No Judgment Until Motion For New Trial is Decided.
-There isno judgment in factupon a verdictuntilthemotion
fora new trialisdecided. City of Louisvillev. Muldoon, 43
S.W. 867, 19 R. 1386.
A motion for new trialsuspendsthe judgment untilthe
motion is overruled, and untilsuch time there is no judgment ,

JUDGMENT JUDGMENT1858
within the meaning of Criminal Code , Sec. 348, which pro-
vides that the appeal must be prayed during the term at which
the judgment is rendered, and shallbe granted if the record
is lodged in the clerk'sofficeof the Court of Appeals within
sixty days afterthe judgment . Com . v . Tarvin, 114 Ky . 877,
72 S. W. 13 , 24 R. 1663 .
Order in Habeas Corpus Proceedings. The order or
judgment made in a habeas corpus proceeding is not a judg-
ment of a court, or finaldetermination by a court under our
practice. Bedford v . Hamilton , 153 Ky . 429, 155 S. W. 1128.
Whatever may be the rule in other jurisdictions, under the
express provisionsof our Criminal Code , Sec. 428, " The pro-
ceedingsand judgment upon the writ of habeas corpus shall
not conclude or be evidence in any civilsuit involving the
rightsdecided by the order or judgment on the writ of habeas
corpus." The order or judgment made in such a proceeding
isnot a judgment of a court,or finaldeterminationby a court
under our practice. Weddington v . Sloan, 15 В. М. 153 ;
Broadwell v . Commonwealth , 98 Ky . 15 ; Bedford v . Hamilton ,
153 Ky .429, 155 S. W. 1128.
Opinion of Judge. The expressedopinionof the judge
is not a judgment and thereforenot final, and an appeal
taken from such an opinion willbe dismissed. Smith v . Wil-
son & Co., 11 Ky . Opin . 946.
Real Question Not Decided. A judgment is erroneous
where the realquestionis not decided, and upon appeal the
case will be remanded with directionsto try the real issues
involved. Scott v . Daniels, 160 Ky . 365, 169 S. W. 830.
Erroneous Form . Where a judgment, though erroneous
in form, does in meaning and effectdetermine the rightsof the
partiesin thesame way thata judgment in properform would
have done, the case will not be reversed on account of such
error. May v . Vaughn , 91 S. W. 273, 28 R. 1088.
Decree is Judgment . A decree is a judgment, and the
provisionsof the Code concerning judgments apply to decrees.
Hughes v . Shreve, 3 Met . 547.

JUDGMENT JUDGMENT1859
Must Follow Verdict. A judgment must followand con-
form to the verdict, not only as to the amount of the recovery,
but also as to the nature and measure of relief and as to the
parties; and , it can not go beyond the verdict in settlingthe
rightsof the partiesor admeasuring the recovery, or declaring
or foreclosingliens, exceptthat in caseswhere the evidence
would have authorized the court to direct a verdict, it may ,
in rendering judgment ,go further than the verdictin adjusting
the equitiesof the parties. Dunn v . Blue Grass Realty Co.,
163 Ky . 384 , 173 S. W. 1122 .
Judgment Not a Lien. In thisState a judgment is not
a lienupon the property of the defendant, nor can a lien be
createdby the recitalin the judgment that it is a lien. Hol-
comb v . Alterus, 13 R. 971.
Settlement of Trustee is Judgment . A settlementmade
under the statuteby the trusteeof the jury fund for a Circuit
Court approvedby the courtis a judicialproceeding- a judg-
ment . Com . v. Cain, 80 Ky . 318, 4 R. 38.
Binds Only Partiesand Privies. A judgment of a court
doesnot bind one who isnot beforethe court, and only binds
parties to the suit, in which the judgment is rendered, and
theirprivies. Everidge v .Martin ,164 Ky .497,175 S. W. 1004.
Bond to Perform Judgment Embraces Costs. Bond to
perform judgment embraces the cost of the action. Galloway
v.Bethume , 6 Bush , 113 .
Judgment by Default. A defaultadmits only what is
well pleaded; consequentlya judgment by default can not be
sustainedif plaintiff'spetitiondoes not state a good cause of
action, or lacks those averments which are necessary to show
his rightto recover. 23 Сус .740 ; Doud , Sons & Co. v . Duluth
Milling Co., 55 Minn . 53 ; Schuler v . Mueller, 193 Ill. 402 ;
Barnes v . Bigore, 92 Cal. 631 ; Martin v . Ky . Land & Invest-
ment Co., 146 Ky . 525 ; Stamper v . Forman -Earle Co., 158 Ky .
324 ; Davie's Exr . v . City of Louisville, 159 Ky . 252, 166 S.
W. 969 .
Void or Voidable- Jurisdiction. “The proceedings of
judicial tribunals having no jurisdictionover the subject

JUDGMENT JUDGMENT1860
matter are absolutelyvoid, but where the case is within the
jurisdictionof the court and there iserror in the proceedingsor
judgment , the judgment is voidableonly and the remedy is by
appeal." Dorsey v.Kendall, 8 Bush , 294 ; Carpenter v .Moore-
lock, 151 Ky . 506, 152 S. W. 575.
A court can not render a validjudgment unless ithas juris-
diction of the parties as well as the subject matter of the
action. Carpenter v . Moorelock, 151 Ky . 506, 152 S. W. 575.
When a court is without jurisdictionto act in a given
stateof case, itsorders and judgments are void. They do not
deprive the judgment defendant of any rightshe had , nor do
they give to the judgment plaintiffanything. Carpenter v .
Moorelock, 151 Ky . 506, 152 S. W. 575.
Both foreign judgments and judgments of courts of sister
stateshaving general jurisdictionmay be collaterallyattacked
forwant of jurisdiction,regardlessof the recitalsinthe record.
Wood v . Wood , 78 Ky . 624.
If a decree is void it is a nullityas to both partiesand all
other persons; but if erroneous only it has full effecttill
reversedor set aside. Hynes v . Oldham , 3 Mon. 267.
Must Conform to Pleadings. No reliefshouldbe granted
except that asked for by the pleadings. Martin v . Turner,
115 S. W. 833 .
Plaintiff, suing for a balancedue for laborperformed, can
not recover money expended by him in making a trip for the
purpose of effectinga settlementof hisclaim, where his peti-
tiondid not set up such expense or allegethat defendant agreed
to pay them . Sealy v. Williston, 117 S. W. 959.
Judgment by Confession. "Any person may personally
appear in a court of competent jurisdictionand ,with the assent
of a person having a cause of actionagainsthim , confessjudg-
ment therefor; whereupon judgment shall be entered accord-
ingly." Civil Code , Sec. 381. How offermade . Civil Code ,
Sec . 640 .
Judgment by confessionadmits the law as well as the facts
to be againstthe party confessing,but such confession, although
recorded, willnot authorizethe issuingof an executionwithout

JUDGMENT JUDGMENT1861
an entry of the judgment of the court upon the confession.
Bonta v . Clay, 1 Litt. 27 .
Criminal Case- Voidable Judgment . A judgment of con-
victionin a criminal case, when merely erroneous or voidable,
like that in a civilcase, is, until vacated or reversed, con-
clusiveof what it decides, neither being subject to collateral
attack. Board of Prison Commissioners v . De Moss , 157 Ky .
289 , 163 S. W. 183 .
Pleading Judgment . “In pleadinga judgment or deter-
mination of a court or officer, it is not necessary to statethe
factsconferringjurisdiction; but it shallbe sufficientto state
that the judgment or determinationwas duly given or made ."
Civil Code , Sec.122 .
Agreed Judgment . An agreed judgment expresslyem-
bracing every thing between the parties must be treated as
an adjudicationof allmatters which were or might have been
pleaded as a defense to the action. Beattie v . Curtis, 4 R. 352.
By Consent. A judgment by consentisequalto a release
of errors, and can not be reversed. Boner v . Smith , 7 Mon.
380 .
Judgment Non Obstante. " Judgment shallbe given for
the party whom the pleadings entitlethereto, though theremay
have been a verdictagainsthim ." CivilCode , Sec. 386.
In civilcases the motion in arrest of judgment has been
superseded and replaced by the motion for a judgment not-
withstanding the verdict, and this must be made before the
judgment is entered. Wheeler v. Preston, 107 S. W. 274, 32
R. 791 .
It is the rule that where a party asks for a peremptory
instructionwhich should have been given, he is not thereafter
entitledto a judgment notwithstanding the verdict, but only
to a new trial for the error of the court in refusing the
peremptory. Mast, Crowell& Kirkpatrickv. Lehmn , 100 Ky .
464 ; LouisvilleRy . Co. v. Hibbett, 139 Kv . 43 ; Connecticut
Fire Ins. Co. v. Moore , 154 Ky .18, 156 S. W. 867.

JUDGMENT JUDGMENT1862
Judgment shallbe given for the party whom the pleadings
entitlethereto, though there may have been a verdict against
him . Code , Sec. 386. Evans v. Stone, 80 Ky . 78, 3 R. 751.
Unless the plea of contributory negligence is controverted
of record or repliedto, defendant is entitledto judgment on
the pleadings, though plaintiffmay have obtained a verdict.
Schulte v . L. & N. R. Co., 128 Ky . 627, 108 S. W. 941, 33
R. 31 .
A judgment " non obstanteveredicto" should not be rendered
by the court in a case where the parties understood and
attempted to join an issueto be triedhowever defectivein form
the pleadings may be, and a verdict for the one or the other
in such case will be held to cure such defectivepleadings,
unlessthe facts, when consideredas properlypleaded, do not
entitlethe party obtainingthe verdictto the reliefgiven. Hill
v .Ragland ,114 Ky .209,70 S. W. 634,24 R. 1053.
Where plaintiffin an action upon an accident insurance
policy failedto plead or prove that the death of insured was
caused by accidentalmeans , or to show that the manner of
death was unknown , so as to render operativethe presumption
that death from unexplained cause was the resultof accident,
defendant was entitledto a judgment upon the pleadings.
Farnsley's Admr . v. Philadelphia Life Ins. Co., 156 Ky . 699,
161 S. W. 1111 .
In an action for damages caused by defendant's negligence,
where a plea of contributory negligence is not controverted,
defendant is entitledto judgment notwithstanding a verdict
for plaintiff. L. & N. R. Co. v . Mayfield, 35 S. W. 924, 18
R. 224 .
Sec. 386, Civil Code applies only where a material allega-
tion concerning the cause of controversy has been sufficiently
and properly made and stands either confessed or not suffi-
cientlydenied. Mullikin v . Mullikin, 15 R. 609.
A defendant has a right to withdraw his motion for a new
trialand move the court for a judgment non obstanteveredicto.
Howser v . Watson ,3 R. 382 .
Where , upon the statementsin the pleadings, plaintiffis
entitledto judgment the court should, on his motion, render

JUDGMENT JUDGMENT1863
judgment for him though the verdictof the jury was against
him . Lindsey v . Rutherford, 17 B. M. 249.
A judgment non obstanteveredictomust be based upon what
thepleadingsshow , and not from deductions from the evidence.
Stevenson v .Moore , 118 S. W. 951.
For approved forms of judgments and orders in civiland
criminal cases, see Kentucky Form Book , Forms Nos . 1986-
2107 .
JUDGMENT- (Opinion).- Where a witness had shown
that he had ample opportunity for observationof factsupon
which to base an opinion as to whether a man was under the
influenceof liquoron a certainoccasion, itwas not improper to
ask him to give his " judgment ," ifhe did not know . Campbell
v.Fidelity& Cas .Co.,60 S. W. 492,22 R. 1295.
In an actionfor personalinjuries, itwas erroneousto leave
itto the jury to say what ,in their" judgment ," plaintiffmight
in future sufferbecause of the alleged negligence. That was
to be regulatedby the evidence, and not the judgment of the
jurors. B. & O. S. W. R. Co. v . Sheridan, 101 S. W. 928, 31
R. 109 .
JUDGMENT FOR THE PAYMENT OF MONEY .- Under
CivilCode, Sec. 764, providingthat " upon the affirmanceof
or dismissalof an appeal from a judgment for the payment of
money , the collectionof which in whole or in part has been
superseded, ten per cent. damages on the amount superseded
shallbe awarded againstthe appellant," the Court of Appeals
has universallyheld that the ten per cent. damages willbe
awarded only in caseswhere the judgment isfor the payment
ofmoney which may be enforcedby executionor similarprocess.
Bell'sTrusteev.Cityof Lexington,124 Ky .463,99 S. W. 344,
30 R. 609 .
This Section does not apply where the only pecuniary
reliefaffordedby the judgment appealed from was an award
of the costsof the action. Oberdorferv. White, 118 Ky . 291,
80 S. W. 1099, 26 R. 182.
An order for the payment of money to the receiverand its
immediatewithdrawalby appelleeis a judgment for the pay

JUDGMENT JUDGMENT1864
ment of money . J. M. Robinson -Norton Co. v . Corsicana Cotton
Factory, 126 Ky .75, 102 S. W. 869, 31 R. 527 .
The affirmanceof a judgment directinga saleby a master
commissionerisnot withinSec.761, CivilCode,providingthat
upon the affirmanceor dismissalof a judgment for the pay-
ment of money or delivery of personal property the appellee
may filea copy of the mandate below, and " thereupon such
writs of execution may be issued as if the mandate or order
had been entered in the court." McKay v . Mayes , 33 S. W.
838, 17 R. 1124. See same case,56 S. W. 992.
An appeal to the CircuitCourt from an order of the County
Court, rejectinga claim, is a proceeding to establishthe claim,
and is not in the nature of a mandamus to require a levy to
pay it. A judgment in the CircuitCourt for the claimant
is " a judgment for the recovery of money ," and if for less
than $100 thiscourthas no jurisdiction. LincolnCounty Court
v.Hansford , 6 R. 734.
JUDGMENT REVERSED AND CAUSE REMANDED.-
Where , on the reversalof a case in the AppellateCourt, the
opinion directedthat " the CircuitCourt should have set aside
the judgment and proceedingsthereunder, and have awarded
the new trial. Judgment reversed, and cause remanded for
proceedingsconsistentherewith," the effectof it was not to
concludethe rightsof eitherparty, but to remitthem to the
court in which the action was pending for a retrialof the
mattersin issue. Fugate v. Gill, 99 S. W. 602, 30 R. 731.
JUDGMENT SHALL BE GIVEN .— The requirement of
the Code that" judgment shallbe given for the party whom
the pleadingsentitlethereto, though theremay have been a
verdictagainsthim," must be harmonizedwith the rulethat
where thereis any defector omissionin a pleading, whether
in substanceor in form, which would have been fatalon
demurrer,yet iftheissuejoinedbe such as necessarilyrequired
on the trialproofof the factsso defectivelystatedor omitted,
and without which it is not to be presumed that either the
judge would have directedthe jury to giveor the jury would
have giventhe verdict, such defector omissioniscuredby

JUDGMENT JUDICIAL ACTS1865
the verdict. C. & O. R. R. Co. v . Thierman , 96 Ky . 507,16 R.
611, 15 R. 655.
JUDGMENT SUFFERED.- Where , with the consent of
defendant, who was insolvent, suitwas brought and summons
servedon him in a countyin which neitherhe nor theplaintiff
resided, forthe purposeof obtaininga speedy judgment with-
out the knowledge of other creditors, the judgment obtained
thereinwas a " judgment suffered" in contemplationof insolv-
ency and with design to prefer within the meaning of Sec.
1906, Ky . Stats. Laughlinv. FirstNat. Bank, 103 Ky . 742,
47 S. W. 623,20 R. 354.
JUDGT . An entry in the clerk'sminutesgivingthe style
of the caseand the casenumber, followedby the abbreviation
"Judgt.," is a sufficientbasisfor the entry of a judgment
nunc pro tunc. Monarch v . Brey ,51 S. W. 191, 21 R. 279.
JUDICANDUM EST LEGIBUS NON EXEMPLIS .- We
should judge by the laws, not precedents. Cochran's Law
Lexicon .
JUDICES NON TENENTUR .-Judices non tenentur, ex-
primere causam sententiae suae. Judges are not bound to
explainthe reasonof theirjudgments. Cochran'sLaw Lexicon.
JUDICIA POSTERIORA . Judicia posteriorasunt in lege
fortiora. The laterdecisionsare the strongerin law . Cochran's
Law Lexicon ,
JUDICIAL ACTS AND POWERS .- See MINISTERIAL ACTS .
The power " to hear and determine," is a judicial, not a
ministerialpower . Payne's Will, 4 T. B. Monroe ,424.
The fact that the act of May 20, 1897 (Sec. 1241a, Ky .
Stats.), providesfor the appointmentby the circuitjudge of
guards to protectproperty againstmobs does not render it
unconstitutionalas conferringupon thecircuitjudge otherthan
judicialpowers, that officerbeing a conservatorof peace
throughout the Commonwealth . Cahillv. Perrine, 105 Ky . 531,
49 S. W. 344, 20 R. 1454.

JUDICIAL ACTS 1866 JUDICIAL ACTS
The exerciseof the taxing powers being confined to the
legislativedepartment of the government , the court will not
interferewith the exerciseof legislativediscretionin imposing
taxes, regardlessof amount , if the tax imposed is uniform and
not contrary to constitutionalprovisions. Hager v. Walker , 128
Ky . 1, 107 S. W. 254, 32 R. 748, 15 L. R. A. (N.S.) 195, 129
Am . St. Rep .238 .
The legislativefreedom from controlby the judiciaryin the
impositionof taxes does not extend to taxes imposed on trades,
occupations, or professions; the court, as to such taxes, being
entitled to determine whether the statute was in violation of
the Constitution, though its purpose was the raising of rev-
Id .enue .
The provisiongivingcourtsjurisdictionto reviewthe action
of the racing commission would not relieve the act from the
objection, if there were any , that it conferred arbitrarypower
on the commission, as the courts can not exercise any but
judicialfunctions, and can not reviewon appeal purelymin-
isterialdiscretion, and such power can not be conferred upon
them by legislationnor can the commission as ministerialofficers
exercisejudicialpowers. StateRacing Commissionv. Latonia
Agricultural Assn ., 136 Ky . 173, 123 S. W. 681, 25 L. R. A.
(N.S.). 905.
In passing upon the validityof an act of the Legislaturein
apportioningrepresentativedistricts, under the provisionsof
the Constitution, itwillnot do to say that the questioninvolved
is politicaland not judicial. It is for the courts to measure
the acts of the General Assembly by the standard of the Con-
stitution, and ifthey are clearlyin contraventionof itsterms
it becomes the duty of the judiciaryto so declare. Ragland
v .Anderson ,100 S. W. 865,30 R. 1199.
The Legislaturemay declare what shallbe good cause for a
divorce, but grantingdivorcesupon causespresentedinvolves
therightsofproperty,and ispurelyjudicial. Gainesv.Gaines,
9 Β . Μ . 306 .
The proceedingsfor salesof infants' estatesunder the act
of 1813 are judicial. Singletonv. Cogar, 7 Dana ,479.

JUDICIAL ACTS 1867 JUDICIAL ACTS
The trial court's discretion as to the admission of belated
testimonyisjudicialand not arbitrary. Sun InsuranceOffice
v. Stegar, 129 Ky . 808, 112 S. W. 922.
A justicein deciding that he has the rightto issuean execu-
tionupon a replevinbond,beforeitsmaturity, actsjudicially.
Baker v .Morgan , 5 R. 323.
A justicemay act as a judicialor as a ministerialofficer.
For his wrongful ministerialacts he is liableas other min-
isterialofficers, but for his judicialacts he is not responsible
for resultingdamage, unlesshe is governed by maliciousor
corrupt motives, or acts beyond his authority. Baker v . Mor-
gan, 5 R. 323.
A proceedingto revokea physician'slicenseisnot judicial
but administrative, and ifthe board actsarbitrarily,and there
is no other adequate remedy , the courts will interveneto pro-
tectprivateright. Forman v.StateBoard of Health, 157 Ky .
123 , 162 S. W. 796 .
To enjoin what shallbe done and what leftundone , and to
secureobedienceto the injunctionby prescribingappropriate
penalties, belongsexclusivelyto legislation. To ascertaina
violationof such injunctionand inflictthe penalty,belongsto
the judicialfunctions. Marshall v. McDaniel , 12 Bush , 381.
The McChord Act (Ky . Stats., Sec. 820a), which author-
izesthe Kentucky RailroadCommission, upon complaintthat
railroadratesare extortionate, to fixreasonablerates, ifafter
a hearing, itfindsthe ratesextortionate, such orderto be " in
fullforceand effect" afterten days afternotice,does not confer
judicialpowers upon the Commission. L. & N. R. Co. v.
Garrett, 231 U. S. 298, 34 Sup . Ct . 48 .
A justiceactsjudiciallyin determiningwhether or not he
will approve and accept a traversebond in a case of forcible
detainer, and thereforemandamus does not lieto compel him
toapprovea bond which he has in good faithrejectedas insuffi-
cient. McDonald v.Jenkins,93 Ky .249,14 R. 157.
Sheriffwhose duty itisto decideon the qualificationof
voterswhen the two judgesdisagreeactsjudicially, and will
notbe liablefora mere errorof judgment. Morgan v.Dudley,
18 В. М. 712 .

JUDICIAL ACTS 1868 JUDICIAL ACTS
The County Court acts judiciallyin establishing, altering,
or discontinuingroads, and must act upon the report of the
viewers, and such other facts as may be adduced , not upon
itspersonalknowledge; if iterr in judgment, or if the facts
do not justifyits conduct, the Court of Appeals will reverse.
Hawkins v.Robinson, 5 J. J. M. 10.
The County Court in assessingthe property of delinquent
taxpayers under Sec. 22, Art . 6, Chap . 92 , Gen. Stats., and
imposing the penalty provided by the statutewhere the tax-
payer is willfullydelinquent, actsjudicially, and an appeal
liesto the CircuitCourt. Com .v . L. & N. R. Co., 89 Ky . 134,
12 R. 49 .
The rule in thisState is that, although a policejudge acts
corruptly and maliciously, he is not liablein damages as for
false imprisonment, where he acts judiciallyand within his
jurisdiction. McBurnie v. Sullivan, 152 Ky . 686, 153 S. W.
945, and in the matters of refusing proffered bail and in
denying the prisoner'smotion for guard upon his offerto pay
therefor, the police judge acted judicially, and is, therefore,
not liablein a civilactionfor damages . Clark v .Hampton , 163
Ky . 698, 174 S. W. 490.
It isdifficultto draw the linebetween executiveand judicial
powers, and to define with accuracy how far executiveofficers
in the discharge of executive or ministerialduties may bind
the other departments of government by the exerciseof quasi
judicialfunctions. But where the inquiry to be made involves
questions of law as well as of fact, when it affectsa legal
right, the power to be exercisedand the duty to be discharged
are essentiallyjudicial, and can not be constitutionallydele-
gated to or imposed upon executive officers. Com . v . Jones,
10 Bush , 749.
When the board of drainage commissioners makes the assess-
ments upon the lands, in accordance with the ratio of assess-
ments approved by the County Court, it is not exercisinga
judicialfunction, but it is an exerciseof the taxing power.
Williams v. Wedding , 165 Ky . 363, 176 S. W. 1176.
Under SessionActs, 1912, Chap .132, the board of drainage
commissionersare not performing judicialfunctions, when per

JUDICIAL CODE 1869 JUDICIAL PROCEED .
forming theirduties, as their acts are administrativeand not
judicial, and the act is not void, as being in conflictwith Sec.
109, of the Constitution. Williams v . Wedding , 165 Ky . 363,
176 S. W. 1176 .
JUDICIAL CODE .-The Federal Judicial Code is Act of
CongressMar. 3, 1911. 36 Stat. 1162, U. S. Compiled Stats.,
Supp . 1911,p .237.
JUDICIAL DAY .- Sunday isnot a judicialday in our law
on which our courts are authorized to sit and adjudicate.
Arthur v . Mosby , 2 Bibb, 589. See, also, JUDICIAL DAYS .
Sunday is not a judicialday , stillwhen in a criminal case
the jury is required by the Criminal Code to be kept together
until a verdict is found , and they find it and report it on
Sunday , and the court renders judgment next day , both the
verdict and judgment are valid. Meece v . Com ., 78 Ky . 586,
1 R. 337 .
Sunday is not a judicialday , nor is it a day upon which
any work , labor, or callingcan be legallypursued, unless of
necessityor charity. Ormsby v. Louisville, 79 Ky . 197, 2 R.
298. See, also, JUDICIAL DAYS .
JUDICIAL NOTICE .— The mattersof which judicialnotice
may be taken are those which must have happened according
to the constantand invariablecourse of nature, or are of such
general or public notorietythat every one may be fairlypre-
sumed to be acquaintedwith them. Com . v. Gabhart, 160 Ky .
32, 169 S. W. 514.
The matters of which courts take judicialnotice are set out
in Ky . Stats., Secs. 1624, 1625, 2419.
For additional authoritiessee Digest, Kentucky Reports
(Morton & Co.), title, EVIDENCE .
JUDICIAL OFFICER.- A master commissioner is not a
judicialofficer. Morgan v . Clements, 153 Ky . 33.
JUDICIAL PROCEEDING .- An inquest of lunacy is a
judicialdeterminationof the statusof the person charged
with lunacy. He may be committedto an asylum and a com

JUDICIAL PROCESS 1870 JUDICIUM A NON
mitteemay be appointedto take chargeof his property. The
inquest is conclusiveof the conditionof the lunatic'smind at
the time, and is therefore a judicial proceeding. Hickman
County v .Jackson,153 Ky .551, 156 S. W. 391.
JUDICIAL PROCESS .- See PROCESS .
JUDICIAL SALES .-Ky . Stats., Sec. 14a, providesthat in
addition to other noticesall judicialsalesshallbe advertised
in some newspaper publishedin the county,unlessthe appraised
value of the property islessthan $100. Held , that such section
was applicable to the sale of a land note assigned as collateral
securityfor a loan and ordered sold by a foreclosuredecree.
Bank of Cerulean Springs v . Gardner , 134 Ky . 632, 121 S. W.
608 .
A saleof land by a trusteein bankruptcy is a judicialsale,
and the sale is valid though the land be adverselyheld at the
time. Brown v. White , 153 Ky . 452, 156 S. W. 96.
A sale and conveyance by a districtcourt in bankruptcy
proceedings is a judicialsale and thereforevalid, though the
land is held adversely. Carlislev . Cassady, 46 S. W. 490, 20
R. 562 .
A saleby commissioneris,in law,a saleby thecourt,and is
not concludeduntiljudiciallyratifiedand confirmed. It differs
from a saleunder execution, over which , if fair and legal, the
court has no jurisdiction. Forman v . Hunt , 3 Dana , 622 ;
Campbellv. Johnson, 4 Dana , 186; Busey v.Hardin, 2 B. M.
411 ; Dale v . Shirley, 5 B. M. 494 ; Egard v . Chearnly, 1 Bush ,
13 .
JUDICIS EST JUDICARE . Judicisest judicaresecund-
um allegataet probata. It is the duty of a judge to decide
according to factsallegedand proved. Cochran's Law Lexicon.
JUDICIS EST JUS DICERE NON DARE .- It is for a
judge to declarenot to make law . Cochran's Law Lexicon.
JUDICIUM A NON SUO . Judicium a non suo judice
datum,nulliusestmomenti. A judgment renderedby one not a
properjudge isof no weight. Cochran'sLaw Lexicon.

JUMPED JURIDICAL DAYS1871
JUMPED . As the petition alleged that as plaintiffwas
leaving the defendant's steam ferryboat she " fell" from the
gang plank or floatleading to the shore, and was injured, the
court erred in instructingthe jury that if the plaintiff" fell,
stepped, or jumped " from the gang plank, and was thereby
injuredas alleged, the law was forher. The plaintiffshould
have been restrictedto the allegationsof her petition. C. & C.
Bridge Co. v .Brennan , 16 R. 126.
JUNIOR .- Junior is no part of a man's name . Johnson v .
Ellison, 4 Mon. 527.
JUNIOR EQUITY .- Where a junior equity, without notice
of the older equity, acquiresthe legal title, the senior equity
must yield. In the absence of the legal title, the senior will
have the preference. Carlislev . Jumper , 81 Ky .282, 5 R. 190 ;
Taylor v .Gilbert, 4 R. 830 ; Butler v .Monks , 4 R. 996.
JUNK DEALER . A man who keeps a general store is not
a junk dealer, although he may occasionallytake in trade a
pieceof secondhand furniture. Com . v. Hazel, 155 Ky . 30,
159 S. W. 673,and casescited.
JURA EODEM MODO .— Jura eodem modo destituunturquo
constituuntur. Laws are abrogated in the same manner in which
they are made . Cochran's Law Lexicon.
JURATORES SUNT JUDICES FACTI . Jurors are the
judges of fact. Cochran's Law Lexicon.
JURIDICAL.- Relating to the administrationof justice.
Cochran's Law Lexicon .
JURIDICAL DAYS . Juridicaldays, those on which courts
are held and justiceis administered. Cochran's Law Lexicon.
The words " juridicaldays " mean days that courts are open
or in session (Webster's Dictionary), and these words were
evidentlyinsertedin the Act for the purpose of exempting
from itsoperationthe periodof summer and other adjourn-
ments when the courts are not open or in session. Com . v .
Ewald Iron Co., 153 Ky . 116, 154 S. W. 931.

JURISDICTION JURISDICTION1872
JURISDICTION . Jurisdictionis the right to hear and
determine. Staples v. Shiver, 122 S. W. 826 .
Jurisdictionis the power to proceed by authorized service.
Kentucky Coal Lands Co. v. Mineral Development Co., 219
Fed .45 .
" Jurisdictionis authority to decide the case either way ."
The Fair v. SpecialtyCompany , 228 U. S. 25.
A court can not make jurisdictionfor itself; it must be
conferred by law. The mere recitalby the court in its
judgment thatithas the partybeforeitcan not make the fact
so,ifitisnot so,and such falserecitalcan not createa juris-
diction which the law has withheld. Francis v . Lilly, 124
Ky . 230, 98 S. W. 996, 30 R. 391.
Every judicialofficerhas the rightto passupon thequestion
of his own jurisdiction. Ackerson v. Semple, 163 Ky . 395,
173 S. W. 1153 .
Jurisdictionisthe " power and authorityconstitutionallycon-
ferred upon a court or judge to pronounce the sentenceof the
law or to award the remedies provided by law, upon a state
of factsproven or admitted, referredto the tribunalfordeci-
sion, and authorized by law to be the subject of investigation
or action by that tribunal in favor of or against persons who
present themselves, or who are brought before the court in some
manner sanctioned by law as proper and sufficient." Ingram
v.Fuson , 118 Ky .882, 884, 82 S. W. 606, 26 R. 862.
To give jurisdiction, eitherthe thing to be acted on, or the
person of the defendants, or some one of them , must be
within the circuitof the court. Brown v .McKee , 1 J. J. M. 474.
Fictitious allegationswill not give jurisdictionwhen it
appears that there can be no decree upon those allegations.
Kendrick v . Wheatley , 3 Dana , 34.
Person merely in presence of court does not authorize a
judgment to be entered against him unless he be brought into
court by legalprocess. Jones v . Kenny , Hard . 96.
Where a jurisdictionhas once properlyattached, itcan be
ousted only by the express words of the statute. Drake v.
Bedinger , Snd . 188.

JURISDICTION JUROR - JURY1873
Jurisdictionex vi termini carrieswith it the rightto effec-
tuate every judgment , sentence, or obligationbelonging to the
record, according to the forms of law . Peyton v . Mosely , 3
Mon. 79 .
The consent of partiescan not confer jurisdictionupon
the court to revisea judgment from which no appeal has been
granted. Dean v .Dean ,6 R. 652.
In courtsof generaljurisdictionthe presumptionis in the
absence of something to show to the contrary that the court
had jurisdictionto enter the judgment it did enter, and the
jurisdictionalfactsneed not affirmativelyappear; but in courts
of limited jurisdiction, of which the County Court is one, all
the facts necessary to confer jurisdictionmust affirmatively
appear, and that the court acted within itsjurisdictionwill
not be presumed . This distinctionis well recognized, and has
often been pointed out by this court. Brown Real Estate
Company v . Rogers, Committee , 132 Ky . 790. Potter v .
Matney , 165 Ky . 268, 176 S. W. 987.
It is a well known rule that where jurisdictionfor one
purpose properly attaches the court will grant full relief
among the partieson allpertinentquestionsthat may arisein
the case, even though some of the questions may be merely
incidentalto the action. Hamilton's Admr . v . Riney, 140 Ky .
476 ; Taylor v .Harris' Admr ., 164 Ky . 654, 176 S. W. 168.
JUROR - JURY .- Itisprovidedin Ky .Constitution(1891),
248 that " In civiland misdemeanor cases, in courts inferiorto
the CircuitCourts,a jury shallconsistof six persons."
" A petitjury in the Circuit Court shall consistof twelve
persons, and in alltrialsheld in courts inferiorto the Circuit
Court,or by any county, policeor cityjudge,or justiceof the
peace, a jury shallconsistof six persons; but the partiesto
any action or prosecution, except for felony, may agree to a
trialby a lessnumber of persons than is provided for in this
section." Ky . Stats. (1915),Sec.2252.
If, in a panel of eighteenjurorstenderedthe partiesfor
challenge, the court has calledfor the triallistmore than three
bystanders, it is not a valid jury, although in the twelve

JUROR - JURY JUROR - JURY1874
selectedto try the case thereare not more than threebystanders.
L. & N. R. Co. v.King , 161 Ky . 324, 170 S. W. 938.
A jury at common law consistedof twelve men , and a
unanimous verdictwas required. L. & N. R. Co. v . Lang , 160
Ky . 702 , 170 S. W. 2.
The " regular panel" referred to in Secs. 2243 and 2246,
Ky . Stats., consistsof twenty-four persons. Stone v . Saunders,
106 Ky . 904,51 S. W. 788, 21 R. 534.
Under Sec.194, CriminalCode, the judge has no rightto
summon jurors in an adjoining county until" a fair effort, in
good faith," has been made to obtaincompetentjurorsin the
county where the offense was committed. Brown v . Com .,
49 S. W. 545, 20 R. 1552 .
In cases tried in the State courts under the Federal
Employers ' LiabilityAct , three-fourths or more of the jury
may return a verdict. L. & N. R. Co. v . Winkler, 162 Ky . 843,
173 S. W. 151 .
It is no objectionto the verdictthat the jury was composed
of thirteeninsteadof twelve jurors, where defendant was
present in court and did not object. Berry v . Kenny , 5
В. М. 122 .

The Constitutionof thisState guarantees the right of jury

The " right to jury trial" is the right to the submission to

JUS .- Law , right, equity, authority. All law (jus) isdis-
tributedintotwo parts- Jus Gentium, the law of nations, and
Jus Civile, the law , i. e., the whole body of law peculiarto any
state. The Jus Civile of the Romans was divided into two
parts- Jus Civilein the narrower sense, and Jus Pontificum,
or the law of religion. This oppositionwas expressed by the
words Jus and Fas . The terms Jus Scriptum and Jus non
Scriptum, i. e., the writtenand unwrittenlaw, which cor-
responded roughly with our " statute" and " case" law, com-
prehended the whole of the Jus Civile. Cochran's Law Lexicon.

jointtenant). Cochran'sLaw Lexicon.

JUS DISPONENDI . The right of disposing of property.
Cochran's Law Lexicon .
The jus habendi, isthe rightto be put in actualpossession
of the property, and the jus disponendi, is the right to call
upon the trusteeto execute conveyances of the legal estateas
the cestuique trustdirects. Com .v . LouisvillePublic Library,
151 Ky .420 ,152 S. W. 262.
The jus disponendihas but littleplace in modern juris-
prudence. The advance of civilizationand the consequent
extension of governmental activitiesalong lines having their
objective in better living conditions, saner social conditions,
and a higher standard of human characterhas resulted in a
gradual lesseningof the dominion of the individualover private
property and a corresponding strengtheningof the regulative
power of theStatein respectthereof,so thattoday allprivate
propertyisheld subjectto the unchallengedrightand power
of the Stateto impose upon the use and enjoyment thereof
such reasonable regulationsas are deemed expedient for the
publicwelfare. Harrisv.City of Louisville, 165 Ky . 569.
There is also quoted from the North Carolina case the
followinglanguage : " Besides an ordinance of thiskind forbids
the owner of property to sellor to lease it to whomsoever he
seesfit, as well as forbidsthosewho may be desirousof buying
or renting property, from doing so where they can make the
best bargain. Yet this right of disposing of property, the
jus disponendi, has always been held one of the inalienable
rightsincidentto the ownership of property, which no statute
will be construed as having the power to take away ." Harris
v . City of Louisville, 165 Ку .568.
JUS DUPLICATUM . " Possession is favored in law . The
possessionof thingspersonalor real, ripensby lengthof time
into a perfect and indefeasibletitle. The lowest and most
imperfectclaim to land, by mere naked possessionor actual
occupation, without" any shadow or pretenceof right," may
be ripened by continuance, into apparent right of possession;
next into an actual right of possession, and lastly, into a

JUST COMPENSATION .- Just compensation to the owner

propertyfor publicuse withoutjustcompensation. Id.

It is provided in Civil Code , Sec. 732, that in the con-

Justicesof the Peace are " magistrates," under Crim . Code ,
Sec. 26, which reads as follows: " A warrant of arrestmay
be issued by the followingofficers, who are calledmagistrates
in thisCode, viz.:judges of the County Courts, judges of

A justiceof the peace may administer an oath; cause the
arrestof persons who commit criminal or penal offensesin his
presence, or issue criminal or penal process for persons
violatingthe criminalor penal laws, at any place in his county,
and may hear and try any person charged with the guilt of

trialmust be had in his own court, and within his own district,

where in some specialproceeding, the statute authorizeshim
to perform judicialduties outside of his district. Wheeler v .

The court of which the Constitution and statutes create the
justiceof the peace, a judge, is the court createdfor the

the amount due each laborer for work done by him , can not

tickets" under the act of 1888. The lien can exist only in
favor of those who " perform labor or who furnish labor,
materials, or teams for the constructionor improvement of a
railroad," and the purchaser of the " labor tickets" fallsunder
none of these classes. Frailey v . Winchester & BeattyvilleR.
Co., 96 Ky .570 ,16 R. 645.

LACHES . Laches is negligenceby which another has been
led into changing his condition with respect to the property
or rightin question, so that itwould be inequitableto allow
the negligentparty to be preferred upon his legal right to the
one whom his negligencehas misled. It fallsbut littleshort
of estoppel, and isapplied upon the same principle. It was the
creatureof the courtsof equity, to takethe placein equitable
actionsin those instanceswhere , in actionsat law, the statutes
of limitationwould have applied as a bar. Hughes v . Wallace,
118 S. W. 324 .
Laches, in legalsignificance, isnot mere delay,but delay that
works a disadvantage to another. McKenney v . Page , 146 Ky .
682, 143 S. W. 382; Cultonv.Asher, 149 Ky . 659, 149 S. W.
946 .
Laches, in legalsignificance, is not mere delay, but delay
that works a disadvantage to another. So long as the parties
are in the same condition, itmatterslittlewhetherone presses
a rightpromptly or slowly, withinlimitsallowedby law; but
when, knowing his rights, he takesno stepsto enforcethem
untiltheconditionof theotherpartyhas,in good faith, become
so changed that he can not be restoredto his former state, if
the rightsbe then enforced, delay becomes inequitableand
operatesas an estoppelagainstthe assertionof the right.
Cultonv.Asher,149 Ky .659,149 S. W. 946.
" The doctrinethatrequiresa persondefraudedto promptly
and activelyasserthis right, and prohibitshim from main-
taininghisactionincasehe failedtodo so,isthedoctrineof

LACHES LACHES1904
laches, rather than limitation. If one failsto bring his suit
until after the expiration of the period prescribed by the
statutesof limitations,he isnecessarilybarred from maintain-
ing his action; but he may , nevertheless, in certain cases in
equity, be barred by laches, if he delaysfor a period much
shorter than the statutory period of limitation, to bring his
case promptly , or to prosecute it diligently. The reason for
the applicationof the doctrine of laches to cases of rescission
for fraud is, that the failureof the party defrauded to act
promptly is conclusiveevidence of his ratificationof the con-
tract; the doctrineof lachesthus becoming a part of the
equitableremedy , in such cases." Culton v . Asher , 149 Ky .
659, 149 S. W. 946.
Laches is a question of fact, to be determined by the cir-
cumstances of each case. Equity willrefuse to enforce a stale
claim, though it is not barred by the statute of limitations,
where changed conditionswould place defendant in a disad-
vantageous position, and delay will not be excused, either by
aversionof plaintiffto litigationor by the fact that defendant
isa fiduciaryor trustee,where defendant has repeatedlyrefused
to recognizeplaintiff'srights. Glock's Admr . v . Weikel, 149
Ky . 170, 147 S. W. 897.
" No reliefwillbe grantedto him, who , withoutexcuse,has
failed in his stipulationsor has trifledwith the contract by
unreasonably delaying the performance of his part. This case,
we conceive, comes within the influencesof these principles,
and furnishesan example where thisextraordinaryreliefought
to be, and was properlyrefusedby the courtbelow." Madox
v.McQuean , 3 Mar. 400 (1221); Glock'sAdmr .v. Weikel,149
Ky . 170, 147 S. W. 897.
" Very long and unreasonabledelay in assertinga right
to redeem , amounting to laches, which is not attributableto
ignoranceand which is not explainedor sufficientlyexcused,
willjustifya courtof equityin refusingitsaid to the party
seeking to redeem . Of course there can be no imputation of
lachesagainsta party who was meanwhile ignorantof his
rights, and the court willlistento any plausibleexcuse for the

LACHES LACHES1905
delay." 27 Сус. 1820; McKenney v. Page, 146 Ky . 682, 143
S. W. 382 .
A person who is injured by fraud must be prompt in
seeking redress, and he must prosecute his suit with diligence.
Laches and negligence are always discountenanced. Nothing
can call a court of equity into activitybut conscience, good
faith, and reasonable diligence, and when these are wanting
the court is passive, and does nothing. Culton v . Asher , 149
Ky . 659,149 S. W. 946.
If a person failsto bring his suituntil after the expiration
of the periodprescribedby the statutesof limitations, he is
necessarilybarred from maintaining his action; but he may ,
nevertheless, in certain actionsin equity, be barred by laches,
if he delays for a period much shorter than the statutory
period of limitation, to bring his casepromptly , or to prosecute
itdiligently. Culton v .Asher , 149 Ky . 659, 149 S. W. 946.
It may be true that laches can not give validityto a void
act, but when no property right is involved and the question
ispurelypoliticaland administrative, individualsand parties
that have seen the act in operation for years, without objec-
tion or protest, willnot be heard at a lateday to question its
ability. They must act in seasonabletime and not delay until
the conditionsthey have acquiesced in have been firmly estab-
lishedas a part of the system of government . Adams v . Bos-
worth, 126 Ky . 61, 102 S. W. 861, 31 R. 518 , 10 L. R. A. (N.
S.) 1184.
The courts, independently of statutesof limitation, some-
times refuse reliefto partieswho have sleptupon theirrights,
or have been negligentin assertingthem ; but this is because
theirlaches would prevent the court from granting the relief
sought without imposing a peculiar hardship upon another,
or others, not in fault. In such case the laches of the party
refused reliefis made to operate as an equitableestoppelto a
recovery, in order to prevent injusticebeing done the opposite
party. Radford'sAdmrs .v. Harris, 144 Ky . 809, 139 S. W.
963 .

How relationof landlord and tenant may be created. See
note to Ky . Stats., Sec. 2291, alsoKentucky Digests under title

Reservationof rent in some form and allegianceto the title,
are the distinguishingcharacteristicsof a contract by which
the relationof landlord and tenant is created. Goldsberry v .
Bishop , 2 Duv . 144.
The reservation of rent is not essential to create the relation
of landlordand tenant. Where the owner of land givesanother
the rightto enterupon hispremisesfor a specifiedpurpose, in
subordination to the titleof the owner , for a valuable con-
sideration, or even without the payment of any consideration,
the relation of landlord and tenant is created . Alexander v .
Gardner , 96 S. W. 818, 29 R. 958.
The relation of landlord and tenant does not exist where
the grantor parts with allinterestin the property. Miller v .
Hart , 122 Ky . 499, 91 S. W. 698, 29 R. 73.
All the duties and obligationsof a tenant, or quasi tenant
to his landlord, devolve upon his subtenant and upon every one
in successionto whom the possessionis transferred. Elms v .
Randall, 4 Dana , 519.
Where a tenant from year to year whose term expired in
January , 1909, took a leasefrom a strangerin the fallof 1908
for the year 1909, and under thislease occupied the premises
in the same manner that he had been occupying them under
the landlord, he continued in 1909 to be the tenant of the
landlord under whom he had entered in 1908, as he did not
obtain the consent of the landlord to lease from a stranger
or renounce his tenancy by removing from the leased premises.
Ky . Coal Lands Co. v . Baker , 155 Ky . 344, 159 S. W. 943.
Where a tenant had remained in possession of land for
severalyears under annual leases and in 1909 the landlord

occupying the premises in 1909 under his leasefrom a stranger
for that year. Ky . Coal Lands Co. v . Baker , 155 Ky . 344,
159 S. W. 943 .
A contract by A., the owner of land, to employ B. to

LARCENY . Generally. Larcenywas properlydefinedin
the instructionas a wrongful and fraudulent taking and carry-
ing away by trespass by any person of the mere personal
goods of another from any place, with the feloniousintent to
convert them to his own use and make them his own property
without the owner's consent. Alexander v . Com ., 14 R. 290.
It isnot larceny where property istaken in good faithunder
a claim of rightto it.Kirk v. Com ., 14 R. 1089 ; Ross v . Com .,
20 S. W. 214, 14 R. 259 ; Utz v. Com ., 3 R. 88. Nor where it

LARCENY LARCENY1913
is taken without feloniousintent. Fletcher v . Com ., 83 S. W.
588 , 26 R. 227.
" The materialingredientsto constitutethe crime of larceny
are thatthe goods must be taken animo furandi, and against

Neither the preparation nor attempt to steal constitutes

Where one employed to play for a dance is not paid for

" Larceny isthe taking and carrying away of the mere per-
sonal goods of another with intent to steal them . The first
requisiteof larcenyis takingpossessionof the goods by the
thief; and a taking and carrying away of the goods is an
essentialelement of the crime." Adams v . Com ., 153 Ky . 90,
154 S. W. 381 .
In larceny, the identificationof the propertyand itsvalue
is essentialto determine not only the offense, but the grade of
the crime. Nichols v . Com ., 3 Ky . Opin . 258.
In an action for slander by a daughter against her father,
where it was doubtful whether the father meant to charge his

person takes the property of another with intent to convert

committed where the taking is done by a person innocently
and without any evil intent, and that unless the words were
intendedto chargethe plaintiffwith the crime of larceny, and
would be naturallyso understoodby the personhearingthem,
they should find for defendant. Beams v . Beams , 138 Ky .
818,129 S. W. 298 .
In itsordinaryuse the word " stole" importsa crime, and
to say thata person " stole" an articleisto charge him with

tiondoes not import the commissionof larcenyor any crime.

Chandeliersattached to the freeholdare subjectsof larceny;

Copper boxes connectedwith a stillby a pipe screwed into

Recognizancetaken by a singlejustice,showing thedefend-
ant tohave been guiltyof a "larceny" (therebeingtwo kinds

Larceny at common law can not be committed,saysWharton ,
of such animals as are ferae naturae and unclaimed, as deer,

Pigeons, unlessdomesticated, are not the subject of larceny.

Upon the trialof defendant for grand larceny an instruction
directingthe jury to find him guilty if they believedthat he
took and carried away property of the aggregate value of
$1,000, or property of the value of $20, was erroneous, because
the jury were warranted in finding him guilty if the articles
taken were of the value of $20, although the property taken
at no single instance was of as much value as $20. To
constitutegrand larceny the value of the things taken must
be at least $20, and the taking must be done at one time.
Weaver v . Com ., 86 S. W. 551, 27 R. 743.
If the taking was at one time, then the value of all the
articlestaken at that time could be added together in esti-
mating the degree of the offense. Id .

Or ifthe articleswere taken as the resultof a singlepurpose
or impulse, though the asportationwas at intervalsto better
suit his convenience, the degree of the offense will not be
lessenedby the fact that he could not, or did not, carry away
all the articlesat one load. But if appellanttook at one time
certain articlesof less aggregate value than $20, and later
determined to and did take others, also of lessvalue than $20,
but altogetherbeing of the valueof $20 or more, nevertheless
the two larceniescan not be added togetherso as to sustainthe
charge of grand larceny. Petty stealingby being often repeated
is not raised to the crime of a felony. The nature of the
transaction must determine whether the offense was one or
whether it was a series of offenses . Id .
Meaning of " larceny" in bond for fidelityof employe . See

Intent. To constitutethe crime of larceny, the taking of
the property must be with the intentto wrongfully deprive the

convert it to the use and ownership of the accused, and it is
a defense to an accusation of guilt of such crime, that the

Felonious intentis required to render one guilty of larceny
and one who without feloniousintent to deprive the owner of
his property takes possessionof it and after taking possession
forms the designto deprivethe owner of the property, isnot

In order to constituterobbery or larceny itis necessarythat
the animus furandi, or feloniousintent, should exist. Triplett
v. Com ., 91 S. W. 281, 28 R. 974.
One who receivedan overpayment made by mistake, and
afterward converted the excess to his own use, is not guilty
of larceny unless he knew of the overpayment at the time
it was made and then intended to convert the money to his
own use. Cooper v. Com ., 110 Ky . 123, 60 S. W. 938, 22
R. 1627, 52 L. R. A. 136.

Possession for ParticularPurpose . If the owner of goods
parts with the possessionfor a particularpurpose, and the
person who receivesthe possessionavowedly for that purpose
has a fraudulent intentionto make use of the possessionas a
means of converting the goods to his own use, and does so
convert them it is larceny. But if the owner intends to part
with the property and deliversthe possessionabsolutelyand
the purchaser receivesthe goods for the purpose of doing with
them what he pleases, it is not larceny, although fraudulent
means may have been used to induce him to part with them."
Commonwealth v . Williamson, 96 Ky . 1; 1 Wharton's Criminal
Law , Sec. 886 ; Blackburn v . Commonwealth , 89 S. W. 160,
28 R. 96 .
In Roberson's Criminal Law , Vol. 1, p . 564, it is said: " If,
therefore, the owner parts with the possessionof his property
for a particular purpose and the person who receives the
possessionavowedly for that purpose has a fraudulent inten-
tion to make use of the possessionas a means of convertingthe
propertyto hisown use,and doesso convertit, itislarceny."
Morton v . Com ., 159 Ky . 231, 166 S. W. 974 ; Elliottv . Com .,
12 Bush ,176 .
" But if the owner intends to part with the property and

Bush ,176.
Where one who pledges property which he has borrowed
does so with a fair and reasonableexpectationof returning it,

28 R. 96 .
Where one induces the owner of land to convey it to him

verteditsproceedsto his own use, he was guiltyof grand

his part to steal, take and carry away the proceedsof the
land and he did, in fact, convert them to his own use.
Morton v .Com ., 159 Ky .231, 166 S. W. 974.
Where the owner of property parts with the possession
thereoffor a particularpurpose, and the person receivingthe
possessionavowedly forthatpurposehas at the time a fraudu-
lent intent to make use of the possessionas the means of
converting the property to his own use, and does convert it,
he is guilty of larceny, but where the owner parts, not only

Grand Larceny. To constitutegrand larceny the value
of the things taken must be at least$20, and the taking must

The essentialelement of grand larceny is " the wrongful

New Asportationin Another County . If a horseisstolen
inone county and carriedto another, the thiefmay be prose-
cuted in eithercounty for larceny, as there is a new asportation
in each county to which the stolenproperty iscarried. Johnson
v .Com ., 154 Ky . 742, 159 S. W. 560.
If two persons steala horse in Tennessee and bring it to

otheraidinghim,and beingin the countywhere thehorsewas

Ferrillv. Com ., 1 Duv . 153.
Breaking Not Necessary. It isnot necessaryto allegeor
prove a felonious breaking to secure a conviction upon a

ChickenStealing. By Acts,1904,Chap.29,itisprovided
that" If any person shallstealchickens, turkeys, ducks, or

other fowls, of the value of $2 or more , he shall be confined

Or Robbery. On a chargeof robberythe evidenceshowed
that in an altercation between them defendant took hold of the
prosecutingwitness'scoat, and in the latter'seffortto fleehe
slipped his coat, leaving it with defendant; that defendant
afterward returned the coat, but appropriated to his own use
$60 which was in the coat; that defendant knew the money was
in the coat when he took it. Held ,that a convictionfor larceny
insteadof robbery was proper. Alexander v . Com ., 14 R. 290 .
A mare was forced out of the immediate possessionof the
owner and escaped to the commons , and was afterward taken
away by intimidation. Held , that this was larceny and not
robbery. Whitt v. Com ., 1 Ky . Opin . 185 .
Where defendant, by stealth, placed her hand in the pocket
of the prosecutingwitness and took therefrom a small sum of
money , and afterward, in a conversationbetween them con-
cerning the taking of the money , she drew a pistolon him , it
was error to give an instructionwhich permitted a conviction
for robbery where the evidence showed that if there was any
violenceor putting in fear it was afterthe taking of the money .
Defendant shouldhave been convictedof petitlarceny only.
Dawson v . Com ., 74 S. W. 701, 25 R. 5.
Or Embezzlement . Where one left in charge of a mill
sold wool that had been delivered to the owner of the mill
to be carded,and converted the proceeds to his own use, he was
guilty of larceny. But if he had been authorized by the
owner of the mill to sell the wool, he would have been
guilty of embezzlement in failingto account for the proceeds;
or if the wool had been deliveredto him by the owners to be
carded,afterhe was made superintendent, he would have been
guiltytherebyof a breach of trustin sellingthe wool and
convertingthe proceeds. Green v .Com ., 12 R. 750.
Or False Pretenses. Where the owner of property parts
with the possessionthereoffor a particularpurpose, and the
person receivingthe possessionavowedlyfor thatpurpose has

at the time a fraudulent intent to make use of the possession
as the means of converting the property to his own use, and
does so convert it, he is guilty of larceny, but where the
owner parts, not only with the possessionof the property,
but with the titlealso, the offenseis not larceny, but is false
pretenses. Com .v . Schang , 131 Ky . 405, 115 S. W. 218.
Degrees of Offense. Larceny and knowingly receiving
stolenmoney are not degrees of the same offense. Stone v .
Com ., 67 S. W. 841, 24 R. 10.
Where accused was held in the examining court to answer

Larceny from Common Carrier. Ky . Stats., Sec. 1201b,
provides: " That any person who shallopen any box , barrel or
otherpackage containingpersonalpropertyof any kind while
in the possessionof a common carrier for transportationor
delivery, for the purpose of appropriating the contents thereof
to his own use, or to the use of another, or who shall

tion or delivery, shall be guilty of a felony and shall be

defendant may be convicted of taking coal lying on open flat

Indictment . " In an indictment for the larceny or embezzle-
ment of money , or United Statescurrency, or bank notes, it
issufficientto allegethe larcenyor embezzlementof the same
without specifyingthe coin, number, denominationor kind
thereof." Crim . Code , Sec. 135 .
An indictmentfor grand larcenymust charge a felonious
taking. Allen v . Com ., 144 Ky . 222, 137 S. W. 1060.

ant'suse and deprivingB. " permanentlyof itsuse," sufficiently

Instructions. It is not necessary to define the word
" feloniously" in a larceny instruction. Hamlet v . Com ., 9
R. 418 .
It is not necessary to use the word " felonious" in the
instructions. Allen v . Com ., 144 Ky . 222 , 137 S. W. 1060.
Under an indictmentfor horsestealing,therebeing testimony

take it, he should be acquitted, fairlypresentedthe issue.

Accused was not entitledto have the jury speciallyinstructed

been stolen, it being sufficientto instructthem that they must

LARGE CONSUMERS .— Where a contract between a city
and a water company provided that the cost of furnishing
water to manufacturers and large consumers was to be estimated
according to the size of the establishment, etc., the term
" large consumers " does not include persons taking water for
ordinaryfamilyuse, theserviceto be renderedto such persons
being covered by another provisionof the contractcontaininga
schedulebasedupon houseshaving a certainnumber of rooms,
and whether occupied by one or more families. Berends v .
Bellevue Water , etc., Co., 119 Ky . 8, 82 S. W. 983, 26 R. 912.
