LARGE NUMBER .-The expressionin a petition, “ a
large number of voters," means any number that the drafts-
man may have upon his mind , viz., ten, a hundred , etc., but
does not mean a majority. Woolley v .LouisvilleSouthern R. R.
Co., 93 Ky . 223, 15 R. 13.
LASCIVIOUS .- The words " lewd " and " lascivious" each
import, not only great moral delinquency, but the actual
unlawful indulgenceof lustfulpassions, and such indulgence
by one of the partiesto a contractto marry, withoutthe pro-
curementor faultof theother, would presenta sufficientlegal
excuseforthe refusalof the party not in faultto executethe
contract. Squiresv.Hancock,5 Ky .Opin.767.

LAST CLEAR CHANCE .- See NEGLIGENCE .
LAST GENERAL ELECTION .- The term " last general

per cent.of the votescastat the lastpreceding general election,
means the lastpreceding general electionat which officersfor
the State or county embracing the districtin question were
elected, and not the last congressionaielection. Davis v .
Henderson , 144 Ky .13 ,104 S. W. 1009,31 R. 1252.
LAST SICKNESS .- What is " last sickness" permitting
nuncupativewill. See note on thissubjectin 13 L. R. A.

LAST WILL . It is providedin Ky . Stats., Secs. 446, 463,
that in the construction of statutes " The word 'will ' shall
be construed to mean codicilsas well as wills, and the words
'last will,' as meaning last will 'and testament.' The word
'issue' as applied to the descentof realestateshallbe construed
to include all the lawful lineal descendants of the ancestor."
LATA CULPA DOLO AEQUIPARATUR .- Gross negligence
isequivalentto fraud. Cochran'sLaw Lexicon.
LATENT AMBIGUITY .- See AMBIGUITY .
LATENT DEFECTS .— “ If you believefrom the evidence
that the defendant, or itsagentsor officersin charge of its
machinery and in charge of itsenginedid not know that it
was in a dangerous and defectivecondition, and could not have
known thereofby the exerciseof ordinary care, then the law
is for the defendant and you should so find. If you shall
believe from the evidence that the defect which caused the
explosionand consequentinjuryto theplaintiffwas a latent
defect which could not have been discoveredby the defendant,
or itsagents or employes charged with the care of itsmachinery ,
by the exerciseof ordinary care, then the law isfor the defend-
ant and you shouldsofind." Thisinstructiongivenin Shake v.
Kentucky Wagon Works,137 Ky .742, 126 S. W. 1095.

LATENT EQUITY 1923 LATERAL SUPPORT
Instructionsas to latentdefectsin action for injuries
causedby explosionof boiler. Wiltshire'sAdmr . v. Kister,
156 Ky .171 .
LATENT EQUITY .- Where a will directs the sale of real
estate if necessary for the payment of debts or legacies, a
purchaser at such saleshould be protected where he acted in
good faith, withoutnoticeactualor constructive, of the latent
fact that there was no necessityfor the sale; but where the
estate was large, and such time had elapsed as to raise a
presumption of payment of debts, a sale for a grossly inade-
quatepricewillnotbe upheld. Rutherfordv.Clark,4 Bush,29.
An innocent purchaser ought not to be deprived of a legal
advantage, whether rightfully or wrongfully obtained, in
favor of a latent equity. Patrick v . Marshall, 2 Bibb , 45 ;
Powellv.Eve ,2 Bibb,317; Duvallv.Guthrie,3 Bibb,532.
LATERAL ROAD . The statute does not authorize the
County Court to closeor discontinuea lateralroad, when it
is apparent that the two roads have not and never may have,
in one direction, any common terminusor placeof connection,
so that one of them may reasonably subserve the purpose of
the other. Shuck v .L. & R. T. P. Co., 9 Bush , 168. See, also,
PARALLEL .
LATERAL SUPPORT . If the owner of adjacent land
remove the earth and thus withdraw the natural support of
hisneighbor'ssoil,he isliablefor damages and may be enjoined.
This doctrine is confined to those cases in which the owner of
the land has not,by building or otherwise, increasedthe lateral
pressure upon the adjacent soil. Oneil v.Harkins , 8 Bush , 652.
An ordinary fence is not such a structureas will, by reason
of additionalweight it may add to the soil, deprive the owner
of the rightto recoverfor lossor injury to his realty. Oneil v .
Harkins, 8 Bush , 652.
Whether a privy vault and frame building over it increased
the weight or pressure, is a fact for the jury. 8 Bush , 652.
The owner of land adjacent to the lands of another iş liable
for damages for removing the earth upon his own land so

LATERAL SUPPORT 1924 LATERAL SUPPORT
as to withdraw the natural support of his neighbor's soil.
Under the Constitutionof 1891, the rule above announced
applies to a railroadcompany in the use of itsright of way ;
the railroadcompany occupyingthe same relationas any other
adjacent proprietorwhen the deed under which it holds does
not confer any larger right than usually follows from the
sale and purchase of land under ordinary deeds. L. & N. R.
Co. v . Culbertson, 158 Ky .561,165 S. W. 681.
A railroadcompany thatobtaineda rightof way " on, over
and through which to construct, maintain and operate a single
or doubletrackrailway" was liableto an adjacentland-owner
forthedamage he sustainedby theslippingof hisland caused
by the company making a deep excavation on the property
line between its right of way and his land. The owner of
land adjacentto the lands of another has no rightto remove the
earth and thus withdraw the natural support of his neighbor's
soil, and if he does he is liable, without referenceto whether
the removal of the lateralsupport was accompanied by acts of
negligenceor not. C. & O. Ry . Co. v . May , 157 Ky . 708, 163
S. W. 1112 .
While the owner of land has no right to remove or disturb
his own soil so as to withdraw the natural support of his
neighbor's land, yet if one changes the natural condition of
his soilby erectingbuildings on it, he does not thereby take
away from his neighbor the right to the use and enjoyment of
his land to the fullextent that he might have enjoyed it, had
no such change been made . City of Covington v . Geyler, 93
Ky . 275, 14 R. 145, 12 R. 466 .
In citiesof the firstclassan owner excavating to a depth
of ten feet or over must protect adjoining wall. Acts 1906 ,
Chap . 120.
Where a land-owner by digging on his own land has deprived
the land of his neighbor of itsnatural support, he is, whether
negligent or not, liablein damages to his neighbor not only
for the actual injury to the soil, but for injuriesto buildings.
But when the buildings erected by the neighbor on his land
have by theirdownward pressurecaused the natural support to

LATERAL SUPPORT 1925 LAUNDRY
give way , he can not recover, eitherfor injury to his land or
the buildings on it. L. & N. R. Co. v . Bonhayo , 94 Ку . 67,
14 R. 737 .
The general rule that the owner can not remove his own
soilso as to interferewith the support of his neighbor's soil,
does not apply where the neighbor has burdened his land with
artificialweight, as by a building. In such case the owner of
the improved land can only hold the adjoiningowner liable
for a negligent use of his property, or where it is reasonably
certainthat injury willresultto him by reason of the intended
use and thereis a failureto appraisehim of it. Clemens v.
Speed, 93 Ky . 284,14 R. 625 ; L. & N. R. Co. v . Bonhayo . 94
Ky .67, 14 R. 737 .
One can not acquirean easement or prescriptiverightto
the lateralsupport of his neighbor'sbuilding when the build-
ing of such owner isaltogetheron his own land. Clemens v.
Speed, 93 Ky . 284, 14 R. 625.
Liabilityfor removal of lateralsupport. See note on this
subjectin 68 L. R. A. 673.
LAUNDRY .- One who conducts various places " where
clothesare washed " is liablefor the tax prescribed for con-
ducting a laundry at each separate place where the laundry
businessisso conducted. Com .v.PearlLaundry Co., 105 Ky .
259, 49 S. W. 26, 20 R. 1172.
One who launders towels as part of a " towel supply"
business, for which he pays a licensetax, can not be required
alsoto pay the licensetax required for conducting a laundry.
Com v. Pearl Laundry Co., 105 Ky . 259, 49 S. W. 26,
20 R. 1172 .
" In Muir v. Samuals, 110 Ky . 605, the questioncame up
whether or not a laundry was a manufacturingestablishment
in the meaning of the lienlaws of the state, and the court,
in holding that it was not, said: 'The only businessof a
laundry is to transform soiled into clean linen. It is true
that this is done largelyby means of machinery and requires
the use of an engine and boilersand other appliancesordinarily
used in manufacturing establishments, but after all nothing

LAUNDRY LAW1926
new isproduced.'" Standard TailoringCo. v. City of Louis-
ville, 152 Ky . 504, 153 S. W. 764.
LAW . The statutes of a State have no extra-territorial
force. They are not law in another State in the ordinary
sense of the term . Mutual Life Ins. Co. v . Prewitt, 127 Ky .
399, 105 S. W. 463, 31 R. 1319.
Under a statuteauthorizingthe revocationof a licenseto an
insurancecompany which has " failedto comply with the law ,"
the quoted words refer to provisionsof the statutes. Mutual
Life Ins. Co. of N. Y. v . Prewitt, 127 Ky . 399, 105 S. W. 463 ,
31 R. 1319,32 R. 298,537.
Ky . Constitution, Sec. 51, providing that " no law enacted
by the General Assembly shallrelateto more than one subject,
and that shallbe expressedin the title," was intended to apply
alone to laws enacted by the General Assembly , and not to
municipal ordinances. Kentucky Light & Power Co. v . James
H. Williams & Co.,124 S. W. 840.
" Law " isa ruleof civilconduct, prescribedby competent
authority. Davis v . Ballard, 1 J. J. Mar. 577. Blackstone's
definitionhas been departed from , so far as he makes law
depend on the supreme power in a state. Id .
An ordinance giving a water company power to enforce
needful rules and regulationsnot inconsistentwith the law ,
means not only as the law was when the ordinance was passed,
but as the law might become . City of Owensboro v . Water-
works Co., 191 U. S. 217.
Where testatordevised property to his childrenas the " law
of Kentucky ," " fixes" at the time of his death, the wife having
died, the property went to the childrenabsolutely. Hayman v .
Morgan , 148 Ky . 230, 146 S. W. 722 .
The law looks at substance, not form . Ky . & Louisville
Mutual Ins. Co. v. Com ., 153 Ky . 824, 156 S. W. 897.
The doctrine is a very familiar one- it pervades every
department of our law- that what the law will compel parties
to do they may do voluntarily, and the transactionwill be
sustainedas validin whatevercircumstancesthe questionmay
arise. Campbell v . Galbreath, 12 Bush , 463 .

LAW LAW1927
" It is a principle, ripened into a maxim , that the court
respondsto the law,and the jury to the facts." Harrisonv.
Chiles, 3 Litt. 201.
When it is stated that the verdict is contrary to " law,"
referenceishad to the law as given by the court, and not as
itmight or shouldhave been given. St. Paul Fire & Marine
Ins. Co. v .Kendle , 163 Ky . 146, 173 S. W. 373.
That " the verdict and decisionare contrary to law " does
not mean that the verdict was not sustained by evidence.
Hornback v .Merrill, 8 R. 875 .
Where it is assigned as a ground for new trialthat the
verdict is contrary to " the law," the complaint relatesto
" the law " as given by the court in itsinstructions. Bertram
v.Ebert, 9 R. 198 ; Burns v . McGibben , 9 R. 441 ; Gausman v .
Paff, 10 R. 240 .
The act done by permission is as valid as that done by
command , having allthe sanctionswhich the legislativepower
can give it, and right acquired under one speciesof legislation
is as much to be protected as if acquired under the other.
Slack v . M. & L. R. R., 13 В. М. 23 .
The distinctionbetween the question whether an Act of
Congress is in violation of the Federal Constitution, and
whether an Act of the Legislatureis in conflictwith the State
Constitution, is, that in the former casethe power to enact
the law must be shown to have been delegated; in the latter
itmust be shown that its passage is prohibited. Griswold v .
Hepburn , 2 Duv . 24.
An Act of the Legislature, althoughtechnicallyprivatein
itscharacter, is notice to all the citizensin Kentucky . The
English rule with regard to privateactsof Parliament does not
prevailin thisState,where allthe actsof the General Assembly ,
privateas well as public, are published at the public expense.
Hensley v . Holly, 5 Ky . Opin . 493 .
When the statute of our State declares that the personal
estateof a non -residentshall be distributedaccording to the
laws of the State of which he was an inhabitant at the time
of his death,itmeans the law in forcein thatState, whether

LAW IN FORCE 1928 LAW OF NATIONS
it be common law or statute law , or by whatever name it
may be called. Lee v.Belknap ,163 Ky .418,173 S. W. 1129 .
Although thelocallaws ofKentucky can not,propriovigore,
operateextra territoriam, except so far as they may bind
the citizensof Kentucky , as citizens, wherever they may be ;
nevertheless, they may operate on all persons, on so much of
the Ohio River as iswithin the jurisdictionallimitsof Kentucky ,
if they be consistentwith the guarantee of free and common
navigation and commerce . Church v . Chambers , 3 Dana , 279 .
See titlesCONTRARY TO LAW , LAW OF THE CASE , LAW OF
THE LAND , in thisbook.
LAW IN FORCE PRIOR TO THE ADOPTION .- Where an
amendment to a city charter, authorizingthe incurring of a
certainindebtednessand the issuingof the bonds of the city to
pay same,was enactedpriorto theadoptionof theConstitution
of 1891, and allthe stepsrequiredby said Act to authorizethe
issuing of said bonds had been taken before the adoption of
said Constitutionof 1891, the city may issue the bonds after
the Constitution of 1891 became effective, and , the bonds
having been so authorized under a "law in force prior to the
adoption" of the Constitution, they are validand obligatory
on the city. City of Ludlow v .Board of Education,16 R. 805 .
LAW -MERCHANT .- The generalbody of commercialusages
which have become an establishedpart of the law of the land,
and which relate chiefly to the transactions of merchants,
mariners and those engaged in trade. Cochran's Law Lexicon.
The firstindorsershallbe consideredas sayingto the sub-
sequentindorsers, " I willstand behind you for the whole."
Parties to mercantile instruments are presumed to understand
the law-merchant and to intend to be bound by its rules.
Hixon v .Reed , 2 Litt.176 .
LAW OF NATIONS .- The Constitution, and not the laws
of nations, governs the relationsbetween the government and
citizensof the United Statesat alltimes, in war or in peace.
Norris v . Doniphan , 4 Met . 394. Corbin v . Marsh , 2 Duv . 193.

LAW OF PLACE 1929 LAW OF THE CASE
LAW OF PLACE .-The law of a place where a contract is
tobe performedgoverns, subjectto the rulethat ifitisvoid
where made it is void everywhere. Western Union Tel. Co. v .
Eubanks & Russell, 100 Ky . 591 ; 38 S. W. 1068, 18 R. 995,
36 L. R. A. 711 .
LAW OF THE CASE . Generally. What has been decided
on one appeal can not be re-examined on a second appeal
broughtin thesame suit. L. & N. R. Co.v.Survant,44 S.W.
88, 19 R. 1576; Schmetzerv. L. & N. R. Co., 44 S. W. 395,
19 R. 1713 ; Com .v . L. & N. R. Co., 46 S. W. 206, 20 R. 351 ;
L. & N. R. Co.v.Ricketts,52 S. W. 939,21 R. 662;Lancashire
Ins. Co. v. Monroe & Co., 52 S. W. 1058, 21 R. 782 ; Brashears
v.LetcherCounty Court,54 S.W. 840,21 R. 1250;Freeman v.
Mills,59 S. W. 3,22 R. 859 .
An opinion of the Court of Appeals is the law of the case
on a subsequentappeal. Union Cent. LifeIns. Co. v. Galvin,
81 S.W. 239,26 R. 343;Chenaultv.Quisenberry,81 S.W. 690,
26 R. 462; South Cov. & Cin. St. Ry . Co. v. Schilling, 89
S. W. 220, 28 R. 309; Marcum v. Davidson, 93 S. W. 1035,
29 R. 518; WesternBank v. Coldeway, 94 S. W. 1,29 R. 651;
Hocker v .L. & N. R. Co.,96 S. W. 526,29 R. 842.
The decisionof the Court of Appeals on appeal is the law
of the case on a subsequenttrial, and the principlessettled
therebyare not open for re-argument. Sanders & Walker v.
Herndon , 128 Ky . 437, 108 S. W. 908, 32 R. 1362.
Where instructionshave been approved by the Appellate
Court as the law of the case, only such instructionsshould be
given on a re-trialwhere the evidence on the trialis the same
as on the former trial. L. & N. R. Co. v.Payne, 133 Ky .539,
118 S. W. 352 .
The opinion of the Court of Appeals is the law of the case
on a subsequenttrial. City of Middlesboroughv. Coal & Iron
Bank,110 S. W. 355,33 R. 469;Loughridgev.Ball,118 S. W.
321; Sinclair'sAdmr .v.I.C. R. Co.,129 Ky . 828, 112 S. W.
910;Foster-Milburn Co.v.Chinn,137 Ky .834,127 S. W. 476;
Quisenberryv. Chenault, 143 Ky . 312,136 S. W. 625.

LAW OF THE CASE 1930 LAW OF THE CASE
The opinion upon a former appeal is the law of the case
upon a second appeal. Samuels & Co. v . T. M. Gilmore & Co.,
142 Ky . 166,134 S. W. 169.
This rule is not changed by the fact that on the re-trial
cumulative evidencewas offeredas to pointsdecided before. Id .
The opinionof the Court of Appeals upon a firstappeal, is
the law of the case thereafter; and , where the factsshown in
the secondtrialare substantiallythe same as on the firsttrial,
the ruling upon the second appeal will be the same as on the
firstappeal. Helm v . Cincinnati, N. O. & T. P. Ry . Co., 156
Ky . 240, 160 S. W. 945.
A holding by the Court of Appeals that a verdict was
flagrantlyagainst the evidence, and that notwithstanding this
fact there was sufficientevidence to take the case to the jury,
is the law of the case, so long as the evidence is the same ; and
where such conditionexistedon the second trialit was the duty
of the trialcourt, upon motion seasonablymade , to set aside
the verdict. Cincinnati, N. O. & T. P. Ry .Co. v.Martin, 154
Ky . 348, 157 S. W. 710.
The rule that the law as declared on the firstappeal is the
controllingprinciple of the case on the second appeal is
recognized, whether such rule be regarded as an application
of the doctrine of res judicata or of stare decisis, or simply
what is called in Van Fleet on Former Adjudication a rule
" of expediency, which is not to be lightlydisregarded."
Howard v . Com ., 114 Ky . 385, 70 S. W. 1055, 24 R. 1225.
It iswell settledthat a decisionby the Court of Appeals on
a former appeal is the law of the case, and is binding not only
on the trialcourt but thiscourt as well. This rule appliesboth
in civiland criminal cases. Keifer v . L. & N. R. Co., 143
Ky . 383;Foster-Milburn Co. v. Chinn, 137 Ky . 834; Southern
Ry .Co. of Ky .v. Winchester'sAdmx ., 143 Ky . 38; Arnold v.
Commonwealth, 23 R. 182; Gambrel v. Commonwealth, 142
Ky .839;Slaughterv.Com .,152 Ку .128,153 S. W. 46.
The opinionof the Court of Appeals upon a former appeal
isthe law of the case,and governsa subsequentactionbetween
the same parties,and upon the same causeof action. Wren v.
Cooksey, 155 Ky .620,159 S. W. 1167.

LAW OF THE CASE 1931 LAW OF THE CASE
The legalprinciplesdeterminedupon a firstappeal of a
case are not merely precedents for the guidance of this court
on a second appeal. The law as firstdetermined is the law of
the case, and must control, not only the lower court upon a
returnof thecase,but alsothiscourtin any subsequentappeal.
Mutual BenefitLife Ins. Co. v. O'Brien, 149 Ky . 514, 149
S. W. 870 .
The opinion delivered on former appeal is the law of the
case. N. Y. Life Ins. Co. v .Evans , 146 Ky . 600, 143 S. W. 37.
The opinion on the firstappeal is the law of the case and
under the same evidence the jury should be instructed as
therein directed. Straight Creek Coal Co. v . Huddleston's
Admr ., 147 Ky .94,143 S. W. 775.
The opinionon thefirstappealisthelaw of thecasethough
the evidence is fuller on the second trial. I. C. R. Co. v .
France's Admr ., 123 S. W. 336.
Cumulative evidence does not alter this rule. Samuels &
Co. v .Gilmore & Co., 142 Ky . 166, 134 S. W. 169.
All Questions Concluded , Whether Passed On or Not .
It is elementary that on a second appeal the opinion in the
first appeal must be treated as the law of the case; and all
questionswhich were then presentedand properlybeforethe
court are as conclusivelysettled, though not referredto in the
opinion, as if they are specificallymentioned and considered.
Hopkins v .Adam Roth Grocery Co., 105 Ky . 357 ; Harcourt &
Co. v . Redmon , 149 Ky . 612, 149 S. W. 938.
The opinion on the firstappeal is the law of the case, and
all questions which were presented to the court then by the
record are concluded by the opinion, whether passed on in the
opinion or not. Nat.LifeIns.Co. v.Anderson,102 S. W. 323,
31 R. 415 .
It is the general rule that on a second appeal the opinion
on the firstappealwillbe treatedas the law of the case, and
all the questionswhich were then properlybeforethe court
were conclusivelysettled, as if each was specificallyreferred
to in theopinion. Bauer CooperageCo.v.Ewell & Smith, 149
Ky .838,149 S. W. 1137.

LAW OF THE CASE 1932 LAW OF THE CASE
Right or Wrong . The law asdeterminedon appeal,whether
right or wrong , binds the Court of Appeals on a subsequent
appeal in the same case. Hopkins v . Adam Roth Grocery Co.,
105 Ky . 357, 49 S. W. 18, 20 R. 1227.
The opinion of the court on a former appeal, whether right
or wrong , is the law of the case on a subsequent appeal.
L. & N. R. Co. v . Penrod , 66 S. W. 1013, 1042, 24 R. 50 .
Questions Which Might Have Been Raised. Where the
opinion on appeal becomes final, all questionson that appeal
or that might have been raised must be regarded as settled,
and where instructionsthat were given on the trialwere before
the court and not criticised, they are tacitlyapproved and
become the law of the case, which the trialcourt can neither
modify nor depart from in succeeding trials. Cox's Admr . v .
L. & N. R. Co., 137 Ky . 388, 125 S. W. 1056.
Evidence SubstantiallyDifferent. The opinion delivered
on a former appeal is the law of the case and controls its
determinationon a subsequent appeal, unless the proof on the
second trialwas substantiallydifferentfrom that on the first
trial, and a slightvariationin the testimony is not sufficient.
Gossett v. Ky . Wagon Mfg . Co., 153 Ky . 101, 154 S. W. 897 .
Ruling as to Peremptory Instruction. A ruling on a
former appealthata peremptoryinstructionshouldhave been
given for the defendant isthe law of the case on another trial
and on a subsequent appeal,unlessthe proof on the second trial
issubstantiallydifferentfrom thaton the firsttrial. Beaver's
Admr .v .Proctor Coal Co., 159 Ky . 578,167 S. W. 885 .
New Issues- New Facts . If new issues are made or new
facts appear on a re-trial, the court may give such suitable
instructionsconcerning them as are necessary and proper.
L. & N. R. Co. v . Pearcy, 140 Ky . 677, 131 S. W. 1036.
Other Cases - Like Questions . A decision of the Court of
Appeals in any given caseis the law, not only of that case,
but of all other cases subsequently arising in the inferior
courts of the State which involve a like question of facts.
So , where the law as to any question or stateof case has been

repeatedlyand finallysettledby the AppellateCourt, it is
but a waste of the time of that court and an obstruction to the
rights of other litigants, to compel it to entertain further

753, 169 S. W. 475. For additionalauthoritieson Law of the
Case, see Digest Kentucky Reports (Morton & Co.), title
APPEAL AND ERROR , XVI -K .

LAW OF THE LAND .- The Federal Constitution is the
supreme law of the land; a State statutein conflictwith it is
void, and State statutesgoing within the domain of the powers
of government over which the Federal Constitution extends
must be construed with reference to it. Com . v . International
Harvester Co., 131 Ky . 551, 115 S. W. 703, 133 Am . St.
Rep . 256 .
It is competent, as we have seen, for the Legislature to
prescribe what shall be the mode of trialfor a misdemeanor
createdby statute, which was not indictableat common law,
and forwhich no infamouspunishmentisprovided. (Proffatt
on Jury Trial, Sec. 97.) The Legislaturehaving denounced
the penalty, and prescribeda method for itsrecovery, in which
a hearingisgrantedthe accused,the trialby jury ispreserved
inviolate,and a judgment beforedispossessionprescribed. This
is all that the constitutional" law of the land " requires in
such cases. Com .v .Prall, 146 Ky . 109, 142 S. W. 202.
See, also, DUE PROCESS OF LAW .
LAW OF THE RIVER .— The rulesissuedby the Secretary
of War for regulatingthe navigation of riversare promulgated
under the authority of the Federal government to regulate
commerce among the several States. They constitutepart of
the law of the river and are admissible in evidence . Johnson v .
Westerfield'sAdmr ., 143 Ky . 10, 135 S. W. 425.
LAW OF THE ROAD . " Any driver of a vehiclemeeting
any other vehicleon the road or bridge shall reasonablydrive
to the right, if the width thereofwillpermit, so that they may

LAWS OF NATURE 1934 LAWFUL
pass each other without interference. And when a vehicle is
overtaken by another vehicle, the driver of which desires to
pass the other, the driveron the former, upon being informed
of such desire, shallbear to the right and the driver of the
latterto the leftuntilthe latterhave passed. Any driver
failingto do so shallbe fined ten dollars." (Acts 1914, p .
371.) Ky .Stats., (1915), Sec.4347 .
LAWS OF NATURE .— An employe of mature years must
understand and appreciatethe every-day laws of nature. He
must know , unless he be shown to be of weak mind , that fire
will burn , boilingwater will scald, that a knife will cut, and
that a revolvingcylinderwith sharp teeth willinjure the hand
if the hand is permitted to come in contactwith it. Hutchison
v . Cohankus Mfg . Co., 112 S. W. 899. See, also, WHITEWASH .
LAWS OF SISTER STATE . What the laws of a sister
State are, are matterse of fact, triableas other facts, and the
court can not instructthe jury what those laws are. Tarlton
v .Briscoe,4 Bibb ,75.
Proof of the laws of a sisterState by the parol testimony
of respectablelawyers, who incorporate in their depositions
sworn copiesfrom the printed volume of thoselaws,issufficient.
Our statutesare only cumulative. Biesenthallv. Williams, 1
Duv . 329 .
LAWS OF THE STATE .-The laws of a State are such
enactments as its Legislaturepromulgates and as expounded
by its courts, and a State statute has such meaning as the
judicialdepartment of the State construes it to have, though
without such judicialconstructionthe Federal courts might
from its language construe it differently. Com . v . Interna-
tionalHarvester Co., 131 Ky . 551, 115 S. W. 703, 133 Am . St.
Rep . 256 .
LAWFUL . Under Sec. 3848, Ky . Stats., providing that
" when the will under which he (the executor) acted shallbe
declared invalid, lawful acts done by such executor
shall remain valid and effectual," an act done or

when done before itisinvalidated. Trustees of Home for Poor

An act lawful in itselfcan not become actionablesolely
becauseitwas done maliciously. Chambers v.Baldwin, 91 Ky .

LAWFUL ACT .- When a lawful act is performed in the

incidentalconsequencesinjuriouslyresultingfrom itto another.
(38 Сус. рр . 418-423.) Gott v. Berea College, 156 Ky . 376,
161 S. W. 204 .
LAWFUL BELLIGERENT .- " When a civilwar rages in a
foreign nation, one part of which separatesitselfinto a new
and distinctgovernment , the courtsof the Union must view and
treat the newly -constitutedgovernment as it is viewed by the
legislativeand executivedepartments of the government of the
United States. The government of the United States having
recognizedthe existenceof civilwar in question, the actsof
thedefendants were justifiedunder the commission of the revolt-
ing territoryas a lawful belligerent, and were in no manner
unlawful or in violationof the act of Congress." Martin v .
Hortin , 1 Bush , 631.
LAWFUL COURSE .- Where the obligorsin a bond prom-
ised to pay plaintifffor any interestit had in certaintimber,
to be determinedby any "lawful course," the lawful course
was a suiton the bond. Camp v. CapitalMining, Lumber &

LAWFUL CURRENCY .—-An indictment for larceny, charg-
ing that defendantdid feloniouslysteal, take, and carry away
from the possessionof M., with the intentto convertto her
own use, money "lawfulcurrencyof the realm" (i.e., of the

United States), to the amount or value of $20 or more, suffi-
cientlydescribedthe money . Todd v . Com ., 93 S. W. 631, 29

LAWFUL FENCE . “ Every strongand sound fenceof rails,
or plank, or wire, or wire and plank, or iron, or of hedge, four
and one-half feethigh, and being so close that cattlecan not
creep through, or made of stoneor brick, four and one-half

thereof, the hedge or fence being so closethat cattlecan not

LAWFUL HEIRS . " I disinheritmy nephew , J. S., and my
heirs-at-law willinheritmy undivided estate, but he must have
none ." Held , that when the testatortreated the children of
his half brother, who it was allegedwere illegitimate, as legiti-

Where a will devised land to one and her " lawful heirs,"

other than the legalmeaning to the words " lawful heirs," the

Sterlingv . Richmond , 6 Ky . Opin . 586 .
A devise to one and the " lawful heirs of her body " vests
the deviseewith an absoluteestateunlessa contraryintention
plainly appears from the will. Testator devised land to his

wish that her husband should have no controlof the property,
and appointinga trusteeforhis daughterand " her heirs" in

LAWFUL MONEY . “ In current funds," recitedin a draft,

The redemption by the employer at bi-monthly pay -days of

LAWFULLY .- Where facts are not stated with sufficient

LAWFULLY DIVIDED .- Testatorby his willhaving given
his son, son-in-law , and grandson one dollar each, disposed of
his estateas follows: " It is my wish that all the property I
may possessat my death be lawfully divided between my wife
and my two youngest children." Held , that the words " law-
fullydivided" were used with referenceto the estateof the
devisees, not the mode of division, and that the property goes
to the wife and two youngest children, as if testatorhad died
intestatewithout other heirs. Millerv .Miller, 1 Duv . 9.
LAYING PREDICATE . To contradicta witness by show-
ing that he has made out of court statements inconsistentwith
his testimony, he must firstbe asked as to the statements, with
time,place, and persons present fixed; and the statementsmust
be material. Higginsv.Com .,142 Ky .647,134 S. W. 1135.
LAY OFF .- Where the board of public safetyhad no power
to suspend policemenexceptupon writtenchargespreferred
against them , and an opportunity by them to be heard, it
could not " lay off" the force four days each month , because
the appropriationsfor the policedepartment were insufficient.
Gorley v . City of Louisville, 65 S. W. 844, 23 R. 1782.
LEADING QUESTIONS.— " A questionthat suggeststo the
witness the answer which the examining party desires is a
leadingquestion. On the directexamination,leadingquestions
are not allowed, exceptunder specialcircumstancesmaking it
appear that the interestsof justicerequireit. On cross-
examination, the adverse party may put leading questions, but,
if he examine the witness on new matters, such examination
is subjectto the same rulesas the directexamination." Civil
Code, Sec.595.
Leading interrogatoriesare such as "Did you not seethis?"
or, " Did you not hear that?" Craddock v. Craddock, 3
Litt . 78 .
Leading interrogatoriesvitiatethe answers and render them
at leastentirelyincompetent. Craddock v. Craddock, 3 Litt.
78 ; Doran v.Shaw , 3 Mon. 413.

LEASE . “ Bouvier definesa lease to be 'a speciesof con-

ItA. lease is but a conveyance of an estate in realty.
diveststhe owner , for a given time, of a certainestatein the

A lease for a term from year to year so long as the lessee
wants the land, is a present demise, and the lessee'sremaining
in possessionis an extension of the lease without renewal or
other notice. Kentucky Lumber Co. v . Newell, 105 S. W. 972,
32 R. 396 .
In the absence of mistake or fraud, a lease reduced to
writing and describingthe property leased is conclusiveon the
issueof what property isincludedtherein,and parol evidence is
inadmissibleto add to its terms. Aufenkamp v. Storch, 138
Ky . 104, 127 S. W. 529.
A leaseof land for the term of one year,to begin on a future
date,isnot invalidunlessthe contractisin writing. Hayden v .
Clark, 108 S. W. 845, 32 R. 1341.

LEAVE HER HUSB . 1943 LEFT
A parol lease for a year, the term not to begin untilsome
days afterthe contractismade , is void. Moore v . Terrell, 111
S. W. 297,33 R. 822 .
A leaseof land for the term of one year from a future date
isnot enforceableunless the contractis in writing. Garnes v .
Frazier & Foster, 118 S. W. 998.
A stipulationin a lease providing for its forfeiturefor
non-payment of rent is valid. Wender Blue Gem Coal Co. v .
LouisvillePropertyCo.,137 Ky .339,125 S. W. 732.
LEAVE HER HUSBAND .- Under Sec. 2133, Ky . Stats.,
providing that if the wife voluntarily "leave her husband "
and live in adultery she shall forfeitall interestin his estate
unless they afterward become reconciledand live together as
husband and wife, the wife, by living in adultery in the
husband's home during his enforced absence, forfeitsher right
to have eitherhomestead or dower. McQuinn v. McQuinn ,
110 Ky . 328, 61 S. W. 358, 22 R. 1770 .
LEAVING NO CHILD OR CHILDREN .- Testator devised
to each of his childrena separatetractof land, providing
that if any of his (testator's) heirs died " leaving no child
or children," then the share of the one so dying was to go
to the survivors. The only daughter of a married daughter,
a devisee, died leaving a child, and then the originaldevisee
died. Held , that the testator'sgreat-grandchild was entitled
to the property devised to his grandmother . The words
" child or children" used in the clauseproviding for survivor-
ship were used as synonymous with "heirs." Miller v .
Carlisle, 90 Ky . 205, 12 R. 66.
LEAVING NO LAWFUL ISSUE .-The words " and leaving
no lawful issue, held to be a contingentdevise by implication
to the children. Moore's Trustees v . Howe's Heirs, 4 T. B.
Monroe , 205 .
LEFT . Under an indictment charging that a horse stolen
by defendant was "blemished of left eye," evidence that
the stolenhorse was blemished in the right eye was not a fatal
variance. Boyd v . Com ., 59 S. W. 518, 22 R. 1017.

LEFT NO HEIRS 1944 LEGACY
LEFT NO HEIRS OR KINDRED .- The allegationof the
petition in an action to foreclosea mortgage that the mort-
gagor, who had died, "leftno heirsor kindredof any kind
or degree" surviving her, and that the property descended
under the statuteto her husband , is sufficient, this not being
a mere conclusionof law, and it not being necessary to state
specificallythat she leftneitherpaternal nor maternal kindred.
Montz v . Schwabacher , 119 Ky . 256, 83 S. W. 569, 26 R. 1214.
LEG . In a personal injury action under a general allega-
tion that the plaintiffreceived" great and lastinginjuries
to all parts of plaintiff'sbody," evidenceof an injury to
his leg or ankle may be introduced. While it may be that
the trunk of a man's frame is the main part of his body , his
head and limbs are also parts of his body as the term is
generallyused. LouisvilleRy . Co. v. Veith, 157 Ky . 424, 163
S. W. 217 .
LEGACY . A gift of personalty by will, which, arisingas
it does from the mere bounty of the testator, is postponed to
the claims of creditors. Cochran's Law Lexicon .
It is provided in Ky . Stats., Secs. 446, 467, that in the
constructionof statutesthe words " legatee" and " devisee"
shall each be held to convey the same idea, and the words
" bequeath" and " devise" to mean the same thing; and the
words " bequest" and " legacy" shalleach be held to mean the
same thing, and to embrace and include either real or
personal estate, or both.
" A legacy isdefinedby Webster to be a 'giftby willof
personal property,' and this definitionis substantiallythe
same as that given by Black and Bouvier in their law
dictionaries. A gift presupposes ownership on the part of
the giver or donor, and a legacy isusually created by the use
of some appropriate word or phrase expressing the testator's
intentionor desireto investthe legateewith the titleto certain
specificpersonal property upon the death of the testator.
While any word or phrase that will convey thismeaning may
be used, there are certain words which, by common consent
and custom , are usually used to create a legacy. These are

LEGACY LEGACY1945
the words 'give,' 'devise,' and 'bequeath.' Though the word
'devise' is more frequentlyused in passingor disposingof
real estate by will, it is sometimes used by testatorsin
disposingof personalproperty." Harding'sAdmr . v. Hard-
ing, 132 Ky . 133, 116 S. W. 305.
Under Ky . Stats., Sec. 2066, providing that when any
propertyisdevisedsubjectto the payment by the deviseeto
another of a sum of money , the lattershallhave a lien on the
legacyfor thesum to be paid,and Sec.467,providingthatthe
word " legacy" includes either real or personal estate, the
creditorsof a testator, who directsby his willthat his executor
shallsellhis realand personalestateand from the proceeds
to pay his debts, etc., and distributethe remainder between
his children, have, on the recording of the will, a lien of
record on the land for their debts. Hurst v . Davidson, 116
Ky . 351, 76 S. W. 37, 25 R. 555.
In willsand other writings, and often in legal papers, as
wellas in opinionsof courtsand statutes,the words " devise,"
" devisee," " legacy," " legatee," " bequest," and "bequeath"
are used interchangeably and without regard to their strict
legalsignification. Recording this fact, it has been provided
in Ky . Stats., Sec. 467, that "the words legateeand devisee
shall each be held to convey the same idea; and the words
bequeath and devise to mean the same thing; and the words
bequest and legacy shalleach be held to mean the same
thing, and to embrace and includeeitherreal or personal
propertyor both." Roberts v. Chenoweth, 112 S. W. 625,
33 R. 1081 .
In doubtful cases the law inclines against construing
legaciesas entirelyspecific; hence a legacy of a certain sum
" out of" or to be paid " out of" a designated fund , or of
stock " out of " a larger amount of likestock, is in general a
demonstrativelegacypledginga particularfund as a collateral
security, and being as to that securitymerely directory, but
not depending for itsvalidityor value on the sufficiencyor
existenceof the fund thus speciallydedicatedfor securingit.
Smith v . Lampton , 8 Dana , 71.

LEGAL AGE 1946 LEGAL CRUELTY
A specificlegacy is the bequest of a particularthing
specifiedand distinguishedfrom all others of the same kind.
Broadwell v. Broadwell, 4 Met . 291 ; Lilly v . Curry , 6
Bush , 592.
If a legacy isnot equal in amount to testator'sindebtedness
to the legatee, no presumption arises that the legacy was
intendedas a satisfaction, eitherin whole or in part, of the
indebtedness. Nor does a presumption of satisfactionarise
from a devise of land, the subject-matter of the devise being
of a differentnature from the indebtedness. Lislev. Tribble,
92 Ky . 304,13 R. 595.
Where the testator, being in a condition to be both just
and generous, directs that his just debts be paid, and the
languageof the willdoes not import payment the legacy is
to be regarded as a bounty and not as a satisfactionof the
testator'sindebtednessto the legatee. Id . See, also, SPECIFIC
LEGACY .
LEGAL AGE .- See AGE ; INFANTS .
LEGAL ASSETS .- Equalityis equity, and the chancellor
will, in the administration of equitable assets, so distribute
them as to produce as nearly as possibleequality among all
the creditors. But in the administrationof legal assetscourts
of equity follow the rules of law and give the same priority
to the differentclassesof creditorswhich is enjoined by law .
Muldoon v . Crawford , MS . Op .
LEGAL CAUSE .- An instruction precluding a recovery
for defendants unless they had " just or legal cause" was
erroneous. " Just cause" or " legal cause" are not synony-
mous terms with probable cause. The question in such cases
to be tried is not whether there was legalcause, but probable
cause for suing out the attachments. Sehon, Blake & Steven-
son v. Whitt , 92 S. W. 280, 28 R. 1222.
LEGAL CONCLUSION .- See CONCLUSION .
LEGAL COSTS .- See COSTS .
LEGAL CRUELTY .- See CRUEL AND INHUMAN TREATMENT .

LEGAL DISTRIBU . 1947 LEGAL ESTATE
LEGAL DISTRIBUTION . A testatorprovided in his will
that his wife hold his property in trustfor the benefitof
their children and to use it so long as she remained
unmarried, but thatitwas not his intentionto authorizethe
chancellorto transfer this power to another trustee, but on
her failingto act as trustee, or removal by the chancellor, the
trust estateshould then end, and a legal distributionof the
estate be made under instructions in the will and the law
of descent. The wife died a few weeks after the husband ,
without having qualifiedas trusteeunder the will. Held , that
on her death the trust estateended, and the legaldistribution
of the property took place according to the laws of descent
to the testator'schildren, who took the property in fee simple,
so that any limitationover provided in the will became an
impossibility. Clayton v . Mallory, 136 Ky .830, 125.S. W. 262.
LEGAL DOMICILE.— See DOMICILE .
LEGAL EFFECT .- The Code has not changed the common-
law rule that facts may be stated according to their legal
effect. Fogarty v. Smith , 100 S. W. 829, 30 R. 1237.
The act of a deputy sheriffin levying an attachment was
the act of the sheriff, and an allegationin appellee'spetition
that the loss sued on was occasioned by the sheriff'sagent,
when the proof showed that the employment was by the
deputy sheriff, is within the rule that factsmay be pleaded
accordingto theirlegaleffect, and the act of the agent in
this case was that of the principal. Fields v . Vallance, 87
S. W. 770, 27 R. 992.
When a writing is sued on, it must be set out in the plead-
ing in itswords, or accordingto itslegaleffect; that is, in
a suit on a note it must be alleged that by the writing the
defendant promised to pay the plaintiffa certainsum of
money at a certain time, or the note must be copied in the
petition, so that the petitionon itsface willshow the contract
reliedon. Cooper v.McKee , 89 S. W. 203,28 R. 270.
LEGAL ESTATE .-The mortgage of a "homestead inter-
est" was no conveyance of the legalestate, but of the home-
stead exemption only. Gaines v . Casey, 10 Bush , 92.

LEGAL HEIRS 1948 LEGAL INTEREST
LEGAL HEIRS .- Under a will devising real estate to
testator'sson for life, and providing that at the death of
himselfand wife saidpropertyisto passto his " legalheirs,"
the wife of the son livingat the time of the execution of the
will and at the time of the testator'sdeath having subsequently
died and the son having married again, his second wife, who
survived him , is entitledto a lifeestatein the property, as
the devise over to the son's "legalheirs," who might include
children of the son thereafterborn, shows testator'sintention
to make provision for those persons having a natural claim
upon the son for support or assistance, and not merely for
personsknown to testator. Perry v. Perry, 60 S. W. 855,
22 R. 1513 .
The word "heirs," or the words " legal heirs," when used
with reference to personal estate are generally construed as
meaning distributees, or all those who under the law take
the personal estateas the next of kin, or by reason of their
relationto the deceased. Erol'sAdmr .v.Erol,9 Ky .Opin .739.
Who are legal heirs to whom insurance is payable. See
note on thissubject in 3 L. R. A. (N.S.) 904.
A deed by " F " to his son John , in the granting clause,
conveyed the land to John " for his lifetime, and at his
death, then to descend to the legal heirs of his body ." In
the closingparagraph the provisionwas that " thisconveyance
is to John his lifetime and at his death to his children ."
Held , that under this deed John took a life estate and his
children then living, as well as those thereafterborn, took
a vested interestin remainder. McCoy v . Ferguson, 164 Ky .
136, 175 S. W. 23. See, also, HEIRS ; LAWFUL HEIRS.
LEGAL INTEREST . It is provided in Civil Code , Sec.
732, that in the constructionof the Code the word " property"
includes any vested interest, legal or equitable, in real or
personal property.
LEGAL INTEREST (On Money ).- " Legal interestshall
be at the rate of six dollars upon one hundred dollars for
one year, and at the same ratefor a greateror lesssum , and

LEGAL JEOPARDY 1949 LEGAL OBLIGATION
for a longeror shortertime." Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec. 2218.
See, also, INTEREST.
Under the conventionalinterestlaw ten per cent. was
not " legalinterest," unlessthat interest, and no greater, was
contracted for in writing. Where more than ten per cent.
was contractedfor under that law only six per cent. can
now be recovered. Mack v. Workingmen's Bldg Asso., 5
R. 519 .
LEGAL JEOPARDY .- See JEOPARDY .
LEGAL JOINTURE .- See JOINTURE .
LEGAL LIABILITY .- Whenever there is a legal liability
the law raisesa promise. Instone v . Frankfort Bridge Co., 2
Bibb , 577.
LEGAL MANNER .- Where , in an action by a city on a
bond conditionedthat the principalwould conduct his liquor
businessin a legalmanner , the evidence shows that the princi-
pal's clerk illicitlysold liquor on a single Sunday in his
absence, but does not show that his saloon was kept open at
any time beforethis, but on the contrarythat it was kept
closed, a motion for a peremptory instructionshould have
been sustained. Jones v. City of Paducah , 131 Ky . 686, 115
S. W. 801 .
LEGAL OBLIGATION .— " It is true, that the law will not
notice every violation of the rules of morality; for legal
obligationis necessarilymore circumscribedthan moral duty .
Sometimes, the injury done by a breach of moral duty , is
so evanescent and perishable, or so minute and trivial, as not
to equal the inconvenienceof applying a legalremedy; and
such cases come within the maxim , de minimis non curat lex;
and sometimes the injury may be of such a character, that
the person prejudiced by it might have guarded against it,
with the exerciseof that prudence which allmen are required
to use in their own affairs; and then the case comes within
the maxim , vigilantibusnon dormientibusjura subserviunt.”
Mills v . Rogers, 2 Litt. 218. See, also, Blair v . Williams, 4
Litt . 34 .

LEGAL OFFSET 1950 LEGAL REMEDY
LEGAL OFFSET .- An affidavitis sufficient, whether it
allegesa " legal" or " just" offset. It is not required that
it be a literalcopy of the form prescribed. Gray v . Gray's
Admr ., 3 Ky . Opin . 557.
LEGAL PLEADINGS.- See PLEADINGS .
LEGAL PROCEEDINGS .- Though the undertaking to
restore the premises to the lessor in good repair at the
termination of the lease was qualifiedby the addition of the
words " unlesspreventedby unavoidablecasualty, legalpro-
ceedings or operation of law," the lease is not susceptible
of the construction that the lessee was not to turn over the
property in good repair in case the leasewas terminated by
the saleof the propertyunder foreclosureproceedings. L. &
N. R. Co. v. Schmidt, 112 Ky . 717, 66 S. W. 629, 23 R. 2097.
LEGAL PRESUMPTION IS REBUTTED .— An instruc-
tion that defendant railroad company having admitted that
plaintiff'smare was killedby its train, the law presumes
negligence, but thatdefendant'sservantshaving testifiedthat
the train was properly manned , and that they used reasonable
care and skillto avoid the accident, " the legal presumption
is rebutted," is erroneous as assuming that the statements of
the servants rebutted the presumption of negligence. Maddox
v. Newport News and M. V. Ry . Company , 37 S. W. 484,
18 R. 635 .
LEGAL PROVOCATION .- See PROVOCATION .
LEGAL PUBLICATION .— An affidavitthat legal publica-
tion of an order against absent defendants had been made ,
states but a conclusion. It should show the facts. Lewis v .
Hancock , Sneed , 151 .
LEGAL REMEDY . “ Hence it is that we are told, that
it is a general and indisputablerule, that, 'where there is a
legalright, thereis alsoa legalremedy, by suitor actionat
law, whenever that right is invaded,' and that 'the want of
rightand the want of remedy is the same thing. 1 Bl . Comm .

LEGAL REMEDY 1951 LEGAL REPRESENT .
23; 1 Bac . Ab . title,Action in General, letterB.'" Lapsley
v . Brashears, 4 Litt. 59 .
" It istrue, thatthe law willnot noticeevery violationof
the rules of morality; for legal obligationis necessarilymore
circumscribed than moral duty . Sometimes , the injury done
by a breachof moral duty, isso evanescentand perishable, or
so minute and trivial, as not to equal the inconvenienceof
applying a legal remedy ; and such cases come within the
maxim , de minimis non curat lex; and sometimes the injury
may be of such a character, that the personprejudicedby it
might have guarded against it, with the exercise of that
prudence which all men are required to use in their own
affairs; and then the case comes within the maxim , vigilantibus
non dormientibus jura subserviunt." Mills v . Rogers , 2
Litt . 218 .
LEGAL REPRESENTATIVES .- Assignees under a deed
of trustforthebenefitof creditorsare "legalrepresentatives"
within Rev. Stats. U. S., Sec. 5198, providing that in case a
greater rateof interesthas been paid to a nationalbank than is
allowed by the laws of the statein which the bank is located,
the person by whom ithas been paid, or hislegalrepresenta-
tive, may recovertwice the amount of interestthus paid.
Henderson Natl. Bank v . Alves, 91 Ky . 142, 12 R. 722 .
Ky . Stats., Sec. 655, provides: "When a policyiseffected
by a person on his own life, in favor of some person other
than himself, having an insurableinteresttherein, the lawful
beneficiarythereof, otherthan himselfor his legalrepresenta-
tives, shall be entitledto its proceeds against creditorsand
representativesof the person effectingthe same ." Held , that
as to a policyin an assessmentcompany the words "legal
representatives," in thissection, mean heirsand distributees,
and " representatives," as used therein, means the personal
representativesof the deceased insured person. Hall v .
Ayers ' Guardian, 105 S. W. 911,32 R. 288, quoted in Buckler
v . Supreme Council, 143 Ky . 622. See HEIRS .
A patent issuedprior to January, 1787, to the "legal
representative" vested the titlein the heir-at-law, notwith

LEGAL RESIDENCE 1952 LEGAL RIGHTS
standing the land had been devised otherwise before the
emanation of the patent. McConnell v. Brown , Litt. Sel .
Cas . 463 .
Motion given by the statuteto one surety for contribution
against the other and his " legal representative" lies against
the executor or administratoronly, and does not embrace the
heirs. Lansdale v . Cox , 7 Mon. 404.
aLEGAL RESIDENCE . " There is broad distinction
between a legal and actual residence. A legal residence
(domicile) can not, in the nature of things, co-exist in the
same personin two Statesor counties. He must have a legal
residence somewhere . He can not be cosmopolitan. The
successionto movable property, whether testamentary or in
caseof intestacy, exceptas regulatedby statute; the jurisdic-
tion of the probate of wills; the right to vote; the liabilityto
polltax, and to militaryduty, and other things, alldepend
upon the party'slegalresidenceor domicile. For thesepur-
poses he must have a legalresidence. The law will, from
facts and circumstances, fix a legal residencefor him , unless
he voluntarilyfixesit himself. His legal residence consists
of factand intention; both must concur; and when his legal
residenceis once fixed, it requiresboth fact and intention to
change it." Tipton v. Tipton, 87 Ky . 243; Boyd's Exr . v .
Com ., 149 Ky . 764, 149 S: W. 1022
A person can not have a legal residencein two States or
countries, althoughhe may have an actualresidencein many
placesand his actualresidencemay be in one place and his
legalresidencein another. Baker v. Baker, Eccles& Co., 162
Ky . 683, 173 S. W. 109 .
If a person has actually removed to another place with
the intentionof remaining there for an indefinitetime and as
a place of fixeddomicile, it is to be deemed his place of
domicile, notwithstandinghe may entertaina "floatinginten-
tion" to return at some future period. Baker v. Baker ,
Eccles& Co., 162 Ky . 683, 173 S. W. 109. See, also, titles
RESIDENCE ; DOMICILE .
LEGAL RIGHTS .- The judgment enteredpursuant to the
mandate of the AppellateCourt having been renderedby a

LEGAL AND PROPER 1953 LEGAL -TENDER
court having jurisdictionof the parties and the subject-
matter, is not void, and the appellantscan not be deprived
of the "legal" right it confers upon them , although it was
obtained by unfair means . Therefore money deposited in
court upon a rule against a garnishee is subject to the judg-
ment . Semonin v .Duerson , 13 R. 169.
" Wherever there is a legal right, there is a legal remedy ;"
" the want of right and the want of remedy , are the same
thing." Stanley v . Earl, 5 Litt. 282.
A person does not losehis legalrightby the use of the
methods prescribed by law for their enforcement. May v .
May , 110 S. W. 808, 33 R. 638.
" It is a generaland indisputablerule, that, 'where there
isa legalright, there is also a legalremedy , by suit or action
at law , whenever that right is invaded,' and that 'the want
of right and the want of remedy is the same thing.- 1 Bl .
Com . 23 ; 1 Bac . Ab ., title, Action in General, letterB. '"
Lapsley v . Brashears, 4 Litt. 59.
When the chancellor undertakes to supply a remedy ,
because the law has none, he should give one commensurate
with the exigenciesof the case. When the chancellorfinds a
party with a legal right, but without a remedy to enforce it,
he should furnish an adequate remedy . Prather v . Davis, 13
Bush , 372 .
LEGAL AND PROPER ENDEAVORS .- Contracts having
for their considerationan agreement to impede , hinder, or
defeat the administrationof the criminal or penal laws are
void; nor is it material that the undertaking was to use only
" legaland proper endeavors" to end the prosecution. The
law does not look to the means to be employed , but to the end
to be accomplished. Averbeck v .Hall, 14 Bush ,505 .
LEGAL TENDER . The gold and silvercoin and treasury
notes, which are by statutedeclared to be good and sufficient
for the payment of debts, public and private. Cochran's
Law Lexicon .
LEGAL -TENDER GREENBACK MONEY .- A note pay-
able " in legal-tender greenback money ,and should itdepreciate

LEGAL TENDER 1954 LEGAL TIE
and the exchange is above $1.40 then we are to make it good
to him at $1.40." Plaintiffin a suit on the note elected to
take greenbacks, and if the land (upon which there was a lien
to secure the note) had to be sold that the sale should be
made for that kind of money . Held , that a judgment to that
effectwas properly rendered. Ledford v . Smith , 6 Bush , 132 .
LEGAL TENDER NOTES .- A judgment is invalid when
itprovidesthat plaintiffshallrecoverprincipaland interest,
" in gold coin or itsequivalentin legaltender notes," but
failsto determine what constitutessuch notes. Burbanks , Jr.,
Admr . v . Burbanks , Sr., Admr ., 8 Ky . Opin . 113.
LEGAL TENDER TREASURY NOTES .- A tender of
" legal tender treasury notes" will not stop the running of
intereston a note or account. A contract for the payment
of money can not be discharged in the United States Treasury
notes. McWilliams v . Fecleamer, 3 Ky . Opin . 680.
LEGAL TIE.- " Pothier,whose characteras a profound and
able jurist, is known to every lawyer, is stillmore explicit
and more in point. He dividesobligationsinto those that are
both natural and civil, those that are merely civil, and those
that are merely natural. 'A civilobligation,' he says, 'is a
legaltie,vinculumjuris, which givesthepersonin whose favor
itiscontracted,a rightof judiciallyenforcingthe performance
of it. A natural obligationis that which obligesthe person
in honor and conscience. Obligations are commonly both
naturaland civil; thereare some, however, which are merely
civil, and which the debtormay be judiciallycompelled to
perform , without being under any obligationto do so, in point
of conscience. There are, also, obligationswhich are merely
natural, without being civil.' 'These obligations,' he continues
to observe, 'obligethe person contractingthem, in point of
honor and conscience; but the law does not allow any action
to compel the execution of them. These are only called
obligationsin an improper sense; for they are no legal tie,
vinculum juris; they do not impose upon the person contract-
ing them any real necessityto accomplish them as he can not

LEGAL TITLE 1955 LEGALLY NOTIFIED
be compelled to do so by the person in whose favor they are
contracted; and it is this necessitywhich constitutesthe
character of obligations, vinculum juris quo necessitate
adstringimur.' Evans' Pothier, part 2, Chap . 1." Blairv.
Williams , 4 Litt. 39.
LEGAL TITLE .-The legaltitledatesfrom patent, not from
survey ; but the inchoate titleof one who makes an entry and
survey and failsto return the plat and certificatewithin the
time allowed stillsubsistsand may be perfected by grant.
Flippin v . Hays , 3 Met . 217 .
LEGAL VOTER .-See , also, VOTER ; TAXPAYER .
An averment that one " was electedby the loyal voters"
is irrelevantand lays no foundation for proof that he was
duly elected. The laws do not define the meaning of the
phrase " loyal voters," but as ordinarilyused its meaning is
believed to be quite differentfrom that of the phrases " legal
voters," " qualifiedvoters." Leeman v . Hinton , 1 Duvall, 41.
For note on meaning of these words , see 43 L. R. A.
(N.S.) 294.
LEGALLY IN CUSTODY .- A bail bond is not valid unless
the defendant was "legallyin custody," charged with a public
offense, when the bond was executed. Com . v . Thompson , 98
Ky . 593 , 33 S. W. 1103,17 R. 1132 .
LEGALLY IN POSSESSION .- In an actionto quiettitleto
land,the plaintiffmust show he isin possession. An allegation
that plaintiffsare " legally" in possessionis but a conclusion
of law , and not equivalentto an averment of actualpossession.
Upon the issueof possessionthe burden of proof is on the
plaintiff, and in the absenceof proof of thatfacthis petition
should be dismissed without prejudice. Smith v . Gatliff,
9 R. 553 .
LEGALLY NOTIFIED .- Where an endorser on a note in
his answer does not deny that he receivednotice of the non-
payment of the note, but merely aversthat "He deniesthat
he was legallynotified," his answer is not good, it being

LEGALLY QUALIFIED 1956 LEGATEE
nothing more than the defendant's opinion. Dickey v. First
NationalBank of Franklin, 9 Ky . Opin.664.
LEGALLY QUALIFIED .- See ELIGIBLE .
LEGALLY SOLD BY CONSTABLE .— Reciting in writing
between constableand owner of lot that lot " was legallysold
by constable" is not such a confirmationof the sale as willbe
conclusiveupon the owner . Loan v . Chenault, 3 J. J. M. 296.
LEGALLY SWORN .- An indictment for burning a barn
was defective in that it did not show that the barn which was
burned was within the State. Under the statute, the Fire
Marshal and his assistantshave authorityonly to examine into
" firesoccurring within the State." If he made an examina-
tion which he was not authorizedto make , then the defendant
was not legallysworn within the meaning of Sec. 1174, Ку .
Stats., and he did not testifyon a subject on which he is
required by law to be sworn . It has been often held that an
indictment for false swearing must show the jurisdictionof
the officeradministeringthe oath, and that the indictment is
bad unless thisappears. Woolsey v . Com ., 4 R. 353 ; Kerfoot
v . Com ., 89 Ky . 174 ; Com . v . Taylor, 96 Ky . 394 ; Com . v .
Ransdall, 153 Ky . 334, 155 S. W. 1117.
LEGATEE . It is provided in Ky . Stats., Secs. 446, 467 ,
that, in the constructionof statutes, the words " legatee" and
" devisee," shalleach be held to convey the same idea, and
the words "bequeath" and " devise" to mean the same thing;
and the words " bequest" and "legacy" shall each be held
to mean the same thing, and to embrace and include either
realor personal estate, or both.
It was formerly held that a devisee was one to whom real
estate was devised; and that a legatee was one to whom
chattelswere bequeathed. Legatees were not embraced by the
statuteagainst fraudulent devises. Rogers v . Farrar, 6 Mon.
423. But now " legatee" and " devisee" mean the same thing.
Ky . Stats., Secs. 446, 447.
A legateeis a person to whom a legacy is bequeathed, and
this is true whether the person be named or merely described

in the will; and when a bequest is,made to the " heirs of my
deceased brother," the word "heirs" is not used to designate
a classor collectivebody , but as a designationof each of said
legatees. Neeson'sGdn .v. Young , 10 Ky . Opin. 420; Roberts
v.Chenowith,112 S. W. 627,33 R. 1081.

LEGISLATIVE INTENTION . It isprovidedin Ky . Stats.,
Sec. 459 , in the chapter on " Construction of Statutes" that
" No part of this revisionis retrospective, unless expressly so
declared. There shall be no distinction, in the construction
of statutes, between criminalor civiland penal enactments.
All statutesshall be construed with a view to carry out the
intention of the Legislature."
It is well settled in this State that where words used in a
statutedo not convey the meaning intended by the Legislature
and where from the context and a general survey of the
attending circumstances and a consideration of the object
sought to be accomplished, the true intentionis apparent, the
words may be modified so as to express the legislativeintent.
Bowman v .Hamlett , 159 Ky . 184, 166 S. W. 1008.
Indeed we might say that the meaning of the words
" manufacture" and "manufacturingestablishment" has been
adapted to meet the varyingcircumstancesarisingin the case
or class of cases in which it was necessary to define them , so
that the intent with which they were used might be
accomplished. The purpose of the lawmaking body in using
the words has always been allowed to have controllingweight
in the decisionof the meaning that should be attached to them ,

LEGISLATIVE INTEN . 1958 LEGISLATIVE POWER
as may be seen by an examination of the number of cases
cited in " Words and Phrases," Vol. 5 , title" Manufacture ."
Standard Tailoring Co. v . City of Louisville, 152 Ky . 504 ,
153 S. W. 764 .
The Legislativemeaning is always the object sought in
construing a statute. The firstand most important rule of
constructionis to resortto the language of the Act itself, and
if the language be unambiguous , to gather the legislative
purpose from the language employed . City of Covington v .
C., C. & R. Ry . Co., 144 Ky . 646, 139 S. W. 854.
LEGISLATIVE POWER OR ACTS .- The words " legislative
power ," used in Ky . Constitution, Sec. 29 , providing that the
legislativepower shallbe vested in a House of Representatives,
etc., is a comprehensive phrase, meaning all powers that
appertainto or are usuallyexercisedby a legislativebody.
Booth's Exr . v . Com ., 130 Ky . 88, 113 S. W. 61, 33 L. R. A.
(N.S.) 592.
The well-establishedprinciplecontrollingthe State Legisla-
ture is that it has authority to pass such laws as are not
prohibited by the Constitution. When there is no constitu-
tionallimitationupon itsauthority, the Legislaturemay act.
Banks v . Com ., 145 Ky . 800, 141 S. W. 380.
All legislativepower of the Commonwealth is by the Con-
stitutionvestedin thelegislativedepartmentof thegovernment,
subjectto no restraintsexcept such as are imposed by the
fundamental law, itsown wisdom and responsibilities. Slack v .
M. & L. R. Co., 13 B. M. 22 .
The fact that Sec. 3665, Ky . Stats., empowering the Circuit
Court to investigateand adjudge whether facts existed to
authorizean ordinance of the town of the sixth classenlarging
the boundariesof the town does not providefor a jury trial,
does not render it violativeof Sec. 7 , Ky . Constitution, the
Act of annexation being a LegislativeAct , which might have
been leftto the discretionof the locallegislativebody . Lewis
v.Town of Brandenburg,105 Ky .14, 47 S. W. 862, 48 S. W.
978 , 20 R. 1011 .
There is no publicpolicyof a State which prohibitsits
Legislaturefrom doing anything which its Constitutiondoes

LEGISLATIVE STAN . 1959 LEGISLATURE
not prohibit. Flowers v. Logan County, 138 Ky . 59, 127
S. W. 512, 137 Am . St. Rep . 347.
To enjoin what shall be done and what left undone , and
to secure obedience to the injunction by prescribing appro-
priate penalties, belongs exclusivelyto legislation. To ascer-
tain a violationof such injunction and inflictthe penalty,
belongs to the judicialfunctions. Marshall v . McDaniel , 12
Bush , 381.
The power to levy and cause taxes to be collectedis legis-
lative, and all power of the citygovernment of Covington
is vested in the council. Wheatly v . Covington, 11 Bush , 21.
In most royal governments the power to declare or recog-
nize war is executive, but in the United States it is legislative,
because the Constitutiondelegates to Congress the exclusive
" power to declarewar," and, of course, to recognizeor
declare insurrectionas existingwar . Leathers v. Commercial
Ins. Co., 2 Bush , 298.
It is fundamental law that whatever the Legislaturemight
have done originallyit can afterward do in the furtherance
of equity and justice, and for the purpose of preventing the
legislativewill from being frustrated. Durrett v . Davidson ,
122 Ky . 851, 93 S. W. 25, 29 R. 401, 8 L. R. A. (N.S.) 546.
LEGISLATIVE STANDARD OF DUTY OR CARE.-Ky.
Stats., Secs.2722 and 2733a, requirethe owner or operatorof
a coal mine to adopt certain methods for the safety of his
employes. One operating such a mine now assumes to his
employesthathe willdo so, and hisfailureisa breachof the
impliedundertaking,and isper se an actof negligencetoward
the employe, althoughbeforethe statuteitmay or may not
have been deemed negligence of itself. One effect of the
statute is the erection of a legislativestandard of duty or
care, in additionto that imposed previouslyby the common
law. Andricus' Admr . v. PinevilleCoal Co., 90 S. W. 233,
28 R. 704 .

The Legislatureprovides law for cases which may occur ;
have no judicialpower of decidingupon individualrights

LEGITIMACY .- " If a man having had a childby a woman
shall afterwards marry her, such child or its descendants, if
recognizedby him beforeor aftermarriage, shallbe deemed
legitimate." Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec. 1398.
" The issue of an illegalor void marriage shall be legiti-
mate, exceptthat the issueof an incestuousmarriage, found
such by the convictionor judgment of a court, in the life-
time of the parties, or of a marriagebetween a white person

and a negro or mulatto, shall not be legitimate; and where
one of the parties is an idiot or lunatic the issue shall be
legitimateas to both." Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec. 2098.
" Where the marriage is contractedin good faith, and with
the beliefof the partiesthat a former husband or wife then
living was dead the issue of such marriage, born or begotten
before notice of the mistake shall be the legitimateissue of
both parents." Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec. 2099.
" The customary marriage and the issue of same of negroes
and mulattoespriorto February 14, 1866,shallbe held legiti-
mate." Acts 1910, Chap . 70; Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec. 1399-b.
The law presumes one's legitimacy. Dunn v . Garnett, 129
Ky . 728, 112 S. W. 841.
Under Ky .Stats., Sec.1398,which providesthat" ifa man ,
having had a child by a woman , shall afterwards marry her,
such child or itsdescendants, if recognizedby him before or
after the marriage, shallbe deemed legitimate," itmust appear
that the man was father of the child, and that he recognized
the child as his, in order to render the child legitimate.
Stein'sAdmr . v. Stein, 106 S. W. 860, 32 R. 664.
If a man having had children by a woman afterwards
marry her, and recognizedthem as his children, they became
legitimateand under Sec. 1398, Ky . Stats., capable of inherit-
ing from him . Haggard v . Mason , 153 Ky . 113, 154 S. W.
907 .
The statute providing that " if a man having had a child
by a woman shall afterward marry her, such child or its
descendants, if recognized by him before or after marriage,
shallbe deemed legitimate," does not apply to the children
of a married man begotten and born of another woman than

Under Sec. 2098, Ky . Stats., providingthat " the issueof

841, 20 R. 2014.

The law presumes that every child in a Christian country

LEX JUDICAT DE REBUS .- Lex judicat de rebus neces-

LEX LOCI - The law of the placewhere a contractismade ,

Remedies upon contractsand their incidents are regulated
and pursued according to the law of the place where the

LEX LOCI CONTRACTUS .- The proper law of the con-
tract is presumed to be the law of the country where the
contract is made (lex loci contractus). This presumption
applieswith specialforce when the contractis to be performed
wholly in the country where it is made , or may be performed
anywhere , but it may apply to a contract partly or even
wholly to be performed in another country. Clarey v . Union
Cent . Life Ins. Co., 143 Ky . 540, 136 S. W. 1014, 33 L. R. A.
(N.S.) 881.
The laws in force when the contract was made form a part
of it. Collinsv . Collins, 79 Ky . 88, 1 R. 323.
The law of the State where the instrument is made payable
must be presumed to have entered into and become a part of
the contract, and the courts of thisState, recognizingthe law
of comity, will enforce such contractsfree from the equities
existingagainstthe indorser. Stevensv. Gregg, 89 Ky . 461,
11 R. 686, 10 R. 267.

LEX LOCI REI SITAE.- Each State may regulatefor itself
the manner in which real estate within its limits shall be

inutiliaperagenda. The law forces no one to do vain or
uselessthings. Cochran's Law Lexicon.

LEX NON COGIT AD IMPOSSIBILIA .- The law does not
require impossibilities. Cochran's Law Lexicon.
LEX NON CURAT DE MINIMIS .- The law cares not about
trifles. Cochran's Law Lexicon .
LEX NON FAVET DELICATORUM VOTIS .- The law
favors not the wishes of the dainty. In deciding whether an

venience persons of ordinary, not fastidious, habits. Cochran's
Law Lexicon .
LEX NON SCRIPTA.- The unwritten law .
LEX NUNQUAM COGIT VANA , SEU IMPOSSIBILIA.-
The law will not force any one to do a thing which will be

LEX PROSPICIT NON RESPICIT .- The law looks for-
ward , not backward ; i. e., statutes are not, as a rule,

LEX RESPICIT AEQUITATEM .-The law pays regard to

LEX SEMPER DABIT REMEDIUM .-The law will always

The law aidsthe vigilant; not thosewho sleep. This maxim

LIBEL . See,also,CRIMINAL LIBEL ;INNUENDO ;PUBLICATION ;

A libelis a malicious and false defamation expressed in
print or writing, tending to blacken the memory of one who
isdead or the reputationof one who is alive, and expose him

There is a marked distinction between slander and libel;

Riddle, 145 Ky . 459, 140 S. W. 661, 36 L. R. A. (N.S.) 974.

A publicationcharging that a man was a " henchman " of

Feltner, 158 Ky . 35, 164 S. W. 370.
or
In an actionfor libelthe truth is always a complete defense,

ill-will and be libelousper se. Herald Publishing Company v .

to deprive him of the favor and esteem of his friends or

The law iswell settledthat a written or printed publication

Words verballyspoken are not actionableper se ordinarily,

The distinctionbetween publicationsmade in good faith

" In actions for defamation, the material and actionable

some latitudeis allowed. The rule is that material words ,

Easement and License Distinguished. The difference
between a license and an easement is that a license is an author-
ity to enter on land, but confers no interestin the estate; it
is generally granted by parol and may be revoked by the
licensor at pleasure, and being a personal privilegeis not
assignable and usually amounts to an excuse for what would
otherwise be a trespass, whilst an easement confers an interest
in the land and invests the owner with privilegesthat he
can not be deprived of at the mere will or wish of the
proprietorof the servientestate. Rittenhousev. Swango, 97
S. W. 743 , 30 R. 145.
" 'A licenseis an authoritygiven to do some act or acts
on the land of another, withoutgivingany estatein the land
itself. It differs from an easement in that the latter is a
permanent interest, with a right at all times to enter and
enjoy it, while the continuance of the former depends on the
will of the person who has created it.' There is a distinction
between an 'easement' and a 'license,' although in some cases
it is difficultto see a substantial difference between them .
An easement is a privilegein land founded upon a deed or
other writing, or upon a prescription. Hazelton v . Putnam , 54
Am . Dec. 158. An easement is a permanent interest in
another's land, with the right to enjoy it fully and without
objection. An easement may attachto the land, and pass
with the dominant tenement, as an appurtenance thereto."
Asher v. Johnson , 118 Ky . 705 , 82 S. W. 300, 26 R. 586.
License distinguished from easement. See note on this
subject in 6 L. R. A. 159.
Tax and License Distinguished. A provisionin a com-
pany's charterexempting it" from the payment of any State,
county , or municipal tax or licensefor transactingbusinessin
the State" exempts it from the payment of all taxes and not
merely from paying licenseto carry on itsbusiness. The words

LotteryGrant. A lotterygrant is not a contract, within
the meaning of the Constitution, but is merely a license, or
gratuity. Dougulas v .Kentucky , 168 U. S. 488 .

ing any estate therein. East Tennessee Tel. Co. v . Paris
ElectricCo., 156 Ky . 762, 162 S. W. 530.
A parol licenseto haul logs over land is without considera-

503 , 146 S. W. 1106.
A licenseis defined to be : " An authority to do some act
or a seriesof acts on the land of another without passing an
estate in the land. It amounts to nothing more than an
excuse for the act which would otherwisebe a trespass. Being
a personal privilege, itcan be enjoyed only by the licensee. It
is not assignable, so that an undertenant can claim the benefit
of the licenseto the licensee." Alexander v . Gardner , 123 Ky .
555 , 96 S. W. 818.
PropertyRight or License. An ordinanceof a citygrant-
ing a telephonecompany the right to place and maintain upon
the streetsof the city the poles and wires of such company is
not a mere license, but is the granting of a property right.
City of Owensboro v. Cumberland Tel. & Tel. Co., 230 U. S.
58 , 33 Sup .Ct .989.

LICENSE FEES AND TAXES . Sec. 181, Constitution,
empowering municipal corporationsto impose " licensefees"
on occupations and professions, authorizes a license tax on
lawyersfor the purpose of raisingrevenue. Baker v. City of
Lexington, 53 S. W. 16, 21 R. 809.
Where a corporation has a franchise authorizing it to do
business in a city, the council can not charge it a licensefee
for the privilegeof doing the business it acquired the right
to do by the purchase of its franchise. Cumberland Tel. &
Tel. Co. v. City of Calhoun, 151 Ky . 241, 151 S. W. 659.
" The licensetax isone imposed on the privilegeof exercising
certain callings, professions, or vocations, that when collected
go intothe State treasury,and when applied to municipal taxa-
tion is termed license fees. A license based on franchises
includestheascertainmentof itsvalue, and the mode of deter-
mining thatvalue." Levi v. City of Louisville,97 Ky .401,30
S. W. 973 , 16 R. 872 , 28 L. R. A. 480 .
A city can not by ordinance legalizean illegalthing or
impose a licensefee that will give authority to do an illegal
thing, nor can it, under such an ordinance, punish a person
who refusesto take out the license. Jones v . City of Paducah ,
157 Ky . 781, 164 S. W. 101.
A franchisetax is not a licenseor occupation tax, but an
ad valorem or property tax. City of Newport v . South Coving-
ton & CincinnatiSt. Ry . Co., 156 Ky . 403, 161 S. W. 222.
The charterof thecityof Louisville(Ky .Stats.,Sec.2980),
authorizesthe city councilto levy a franchisetax or a license
tax upon corporationsand persons for the privilegeof doing
businessin the city. But under thisauthorityboth a franchise
tax and a license tax can not be exacted for the same time .
The councilmay impose either, but not both. Adams Express
Co. v . Boldrick, 141 Ky . 111, 132 S. W. 174.
Where a city imposes a license or franchise tax for the
privilegeof doing businessin the city, it can not levy a license
tax upon an agency or instrumentalityindispensableto the
conduct of such business, as this would be in effectimposing
a double tax for the privilegeof doing business. Id .
It isnot necessarythatan ordinanceimposinga licensetax
should state the purpose for which the tax was levied.

Cumberland Tel.& Tel. Co. v. City of Calhoun, 151 Ky . 241,
151 S. W. 659 .
The constructionby the Court of Appeals of Kentucky that
the tax upon person compounding or blending spiritsis a

followed by the Supreme Court of the United States. Brown-
Forman Co. v . Commonwealth , 217 U. S. 563.
Where a telegraph company constructed its lines along

considered as a contractualobligation; and the company can

Such a tax can not be considered a license tax within the

The imposition of licensetaxes based upon three theories
is recognizedas valid: First, a uniform tax upon all persons
engaged in the same business, without referenceto the amount
of businessdone; second, thelevyof a uniformtax on the
volume ofbusinessdone,withoutincreasingitin theproportion
that the businessincreases; and ,third, the divisionof a general
classinto separate classes, according to the volume of business
done,and the impositionof a differenttax upon each division
into which the classis divided. Gordon v . City of Louisville,
138 Ky . 442, 128 S. W. 327.
Where the charter of a municipality empowered it to levy

licensetax will not be implied from general powers conferred
by charter. Districtof Cliftonv. Cummins , 165 Ky .526.

LICENSE TO SELL LIQUORS . See LIQUOR LICENSE .

The mere use of a railroadtrack by the public does not

A licenseein entering upon the premises of another does

Where a railroadcompany , by negligentlyrunning itstrain,

A boy carrying dinner to his father, who worked in defend-

LICK . Though a place is generallyknown by the appella-

LIEN . A lienis a specialrightwhich one has in that of
which another has the general property; and , to the extent of
the lien, it is an abridgment of the dominion , which the latter
has in the thing. From itsnature, therefore, a lien can only
be createdby the consentof the party who has the general
property, or by the operationof some positiverule; or, in other

LIEN LIEN1990
words , it can only existwhere the person having the absolute
dominion of the thing has so far voluntarilyparted with his
right, or it has been taken from him without his consent.
Hayden v . Delay, Litt. Sel Cases, 279.
A lien is a rightto retainproperty until some charge upon
it is paid ; and may be legal or equitable. The seller'slien
upon goods for theirprice, is a legallien and is founded on
possession. A sale of goods, made on the express condition
that they were to be paid for on delivery,held, to create a lien
in the creditor, to the exclusionof other creditors, where the
goods were temporarily stopped in transitu and attached.
Davis & Co. v .Rice & Co., 4 Ky . Opin . 132.
Where preferred stock is issued under a stipulationin the

" mortgage or lien of any nature" do not include claims of

S. W. 752 .
A mere deposit of a title-bond for land with a surety
passes no interestin nor does it give a lien upon the land .

The mere deposit of titlepapers cannot create a lien or
operate as a mortgage , in thiscountry, as in England , to give
one creditor preference over another. Hughes v . Hughes ,
Admr ., 5 Ky . Opin . 374.
In the absence of a valid agreement to that effect, the fact
