A provisionin a contractgiving a constructioncompany all
the earnings of a railroad" during construction" and " until
accepted" held not to give it a lien on the road. Wright v .
Ky .& Great Eastern Ry . Co., 117 U. S. 72 , 96 .
One who furnishes money to a debtor to pay a mortgage
debt,has no lienon the propertymortgaged to securethedebt .
Ellison v . Davis, 159 Ky . 818, 169 S. W. 552.
A lien on property such as county bonds may be created
by parol. Walker v.Harris' Exors., 114 S. W. 775.

The lienpasses as an incidentto the assignment of the notes

edies for enforcing payment ; and the continued subsistenceof

ance. It iseither (1) Conventional, or expresslycreatedby

vexatiouslitigationupon staleclaims, and the obviousneces-

Where the parties to an agreement have themselves stipu-

In determining whether or not liquidated damages are
intended, it is proper not only to look to the language of the

entail. Hence the rule is that where the damages resulting
from a breach of the bond are necessarilyvery uncertain and
difficultof ascertainment, and the parties have provided a
sum certainto be paid in the event of a breach, and the sum
so named is unjust or oppressive, or out of all proportion to

sum so fixed is construed as liquidateddamages rather than

The doctrineof lispendens thatprevailedbeforethe enact-
ment of thisstatutedoesnot now obtain,and thirdpartiesand
strangers to a suit to enforce a lien upon property, who are
without actual notice, are not chargeable with notice of the
suit, unless the statutorynoticeisgiven. Donacher v . Tafferty,

" It requiresthis statutory notice to put in operation the
lispendens rule, and in the absenceof the noticethereis no
lispendens of which third partiesare required to take notice.
Perkins v . Ogilvie, 140 Ky . 412." Id.
Under Sec.2358a, of the Kentucky Statutes, an action seek-
ing to enforce a lien upon real property will not affectthe
titleof a purchaser without notice, except from the time there
has been filedin the officeof the clerk of the County Court
the notice required by the statute. Donacher v . Tafferty, 147
Ky . 337, 144 S. W. 13.
The doctrine of lispendens is, that real property, when it
has been put in litigationby a suit in equity in which if it
is specificallydescribed, will, if the suit is prosecutedwith
vigilance, be bound by the finaldecree, notwithstandingany
intermediatealienation; and one who intermeddles with prop-
erty in litigationdoes so at his peril, and is as conclusively
bound by the resultsof the litigation, whatever they may be,
as if he had been a party from the outset. It is essentialto
the existenceof a valid and effectivelis pendens that three
elements be present: First, the property must be of a character
to be subjectedto the rule; second, the court must have juris-
dictionboth of the person and the res; and third, the property
must be sufficientlydescribed in the pleadings. Boyd v .
Emmons ' Admr ., 103 Ky . 401, 45 S. W. 364, 20 R. 107.
The lispendens statutewas .not intended to restrictinquiry
to the record only. Neither was it intended to repeal the
Champerty Statutes. Daniel v. Holtclaw, 160 Ky . 522, 169
S. W. 1013 .
When a lispendens notice is sufficientin form and is filed
in the proper office, the party filingit will be protected,
although the clerkmay failto dischargehis duty in connection
with it. The statutedoes not impose upon the party filingthe

LIS PENDENS LIS PENDENS2013
noticetheduty of seeingthat itisproperlyindexed. Fletcher
v . Wireman , 152 Ky . 565, 153 S. W. 982.
To create a lis pendens there must be a suit assertinga
claim to or lien upon specificproperty, and it must be
prosecutedwith reasonablediligence. A lispendens is good
to protectpartiesto a suitagainstpurchasersfor value from
one of the partiesonly so long as the suit is diligentlyprose-
cuted. Woodward v .Johnson, 90 S. W. 1076, 28 R. 1091.
To obtain the benefitsof a lispendens, the party asserting
itmust have not only a suit in which reliefin rem is sought
againstspecificpropertysufficientlyidentifiedby the record,to
which the other claimant or title-holder must be a party in
fact, but he must prosecute his suit with reasonablediligence.
Kelley v. Culver, 116 Ky . 241, 75 S. W. 272,25 R. 443 .
The rule is that a party claiming the benefitarisingfrom a
lispendens must , in order to entitlehimself to it against a
bona fide purchaser, show that the suit had been prosecuted
with reasonable diligence. City of Louisvillev . Burke , 87
S. W. 269,27 R. 896 .
Ky . Stats., Sec. 2358a, which provides that bona fide pur-
chasers of land shallnot be affectedby suitsrelatingthereto
unlessnotice of the suitsis filedand recorded, was enacted to
protectlispendens purchasersby requiringa noticeof the
pendency of actionsto enforce liensto be filedand recorded
in the county clerk'sofficein much the same way that a deed
is required to be recorded. Perkins v . Ogilvie, 140 Ky . 412,
131 S. W. 200 .
A lispendens can only be assertedin a suit in rem , prose-
cuted with reasonable diligence, for relief against specific
property which is identifiedby the proceedings, to which pro-
ceedings the other claimant is an actual party. Morton v .
Jones, 136 Ky .797,125 S. W. 247.
A suit could not operate as a lispendens where the petition
did not attempt to describeany property sought to be subjected
thereby. Morton v .Jones,136 Ky .797 ,125 S. W. 247.
A lispendens createdby an amended petitiondoes not relate
back to the commencement of the action so as to affect inter-
vening rights. Morton v.Jones,136 Ky .797,125 S. W. 247.

Those charged with keeping a lookout, where a lookout is

The fireman willnot be excused because he may have occasion

between the duty of firingthe engine and looking out for
the safety of people to whom he is under duty to look out

conserves human life and safety. Id .
The lookoutthat is contemplated by the law means a lookout
that willenable the party keeping it to discoverin the exercise
of reasonablecare the presence of persons on the track in time
to give them warning of the danger and to prevent injury to
them by the applicationof the brake or the other means at

& N. R. Co., 128 Ky .627,108 S. W. 941, 33 R. 31.
LOSS OF TRADE . If a common carrierfailsto transport
coal offeredto it for transportationby the coal miner, it is
liableto such damages only as are the natural and proximate
consequences of the failure, which are the plaintiff'sexpenses
incurred while expecting the cars to be furnished and any
reasonableprofitsthey could have earned during the time the
miners were in their employment . And as to any coal mined
the measure of damages would be the differencebetween its
value at the mines and on the market . " Loss of trade on
account of defendant's refusal to furnish cars" should not be
considered. L. & N. R. Co. v . Queen City Coal Co., 13 R. 832 .
See CARRIERS .
LOSS OR DAMAGE . In a policy insuring the owner of
an automobile" againstlossor damage, ifamounting to $25.00
or more on any singleoccasionby theft, robbery, or pilferage
by any person or persons other than those in the employment ,
service, or householdof the insured" the company was liable
for the diminution in value of a machine so stolen and driven
to a distantState and abandoned , notwithstanding the further
provisionin the policythat"thiscompany shallbe liableonly
for the actualcostof repairingor, if necessary, replacingthe
parts damaged or destroyed." Federal Insurance Co. v . Hiter,
164 Ky . 743, 176 S. W. 210.
Under a specialcontract providing that $50 was the limit
of the carrier'sliabilityin case of " lossor damage " to a pack

LOST LOST CORNERS2037
age, itappearing that the package was not lostor damaged ,but
was stillin the possessionof the carrier'sagent, the shipper
is entitledto recover the value of the package, and is not lim-
ited to the amount fixed by the contract. Southern Express
Co. v . Gutman , 6 R. 654.
A company isexonerated from liabilityfor tobaccodestroyed
in the burning of the depot at which it was received, by the
contract in the billof lading that it " shallnot be liablefor
loss or damage by fireor other casualty while in transit or
while in depot or other landings at point of delivery," the
shipper having receivedand retained the billof lading until
aftertheloss,when he might have returneditbefore. L. & N.
R. Co. v .Brownlee , 14 Bush , 590.
LOST . In an embezzlement prosecution,by the firstinstruc-
tion, the court told the jury that they should findthe defend-
ant guilty, ifthey believedfrom the evidence beyond a reason
able doubt that he did " unlawfully, fraudulentlyand felon-
iously," " without the consent of the owner , convert to his own
use" the money referred to. Held , this did not present the
defense thatdefendant lostthe money . Cline v . Com ., 161 Ky .
678 , 171 S. W. 412 .
LOST CORNERS .- Lost corners are to be found by run-
ning the courses and distancesfrom the known corners to the
intersectionof the lines, allowing such variationsof the mag-
netic needle from the true meridian as are found to exist on
the ascertainedlines. Haggan v . Wood , Snd .274 .
If all four of the corners of a four-sided survey be found,
but the closinglineleftopen ,the course must be so varied as to
strikethe corners. Brown v .Hobson , 3 Mar. 382.
Certain principlessettledto aid in the recovery of lostlines
and corners are: 1. Distancesin general must yield to courses;
2. The beginning corner is of no higher dignity or importance
than any other corner; 3. It is lawful to begin at any corner
of the survey, or reversethe successionof lines; 4. That con-
structionis to prevailwhich ismost againstthe party claiming
under uncertain survey. Beckley v . Bryan , Snd . 92 ; Beckley
v . Bryan , Litt. Sel. Cas . 93 ; Thornberry v. Churchill, 4 Mon.

LOST PROPERTY 2038 LOST RECORDS
32 ; Pearson v . Baker , 4 Dana , 323. See BOUNDARY , 17-22,
Hard . 369, 1 Bibb, 466, 1 Mar. 183, 1 Mar. 470 , 2 Mar. 157 , 5
Dana , 543.
The rule for finding a lost corner, where there are two
known corners, the one preceding and the other following the
lostcorner, isto run the linefrom the preceding known corner,
accordingto the course and distancecalledfor,and then reverse
the call from the last known corner until the two lines inter-
sect, and locate the lost corner at the point of intersection.
Chambers v . Tharp , 93 S. W. 627, 29 R. 271.
It is a primary rule in establishinglost corners to go to
known corners of the survey and reversethe calls, and in this
way find or locatethe lostcorners. Limbert-Driskillv . Dixon
& Sexton, 143 Ky . 759, 137 S. W. 768.
LOST PROPERTY . When a man losesproperty there is an
implied requestfrom him to everybody elseto aid him in recov-
ering it, and any one who finds and restoresit may recover
of the owner , upon the implied assumpsit, at leastan indem-
nity for his time and expenses. Reeder v. Anderson , 4 Dana ,
193 .
LOST RECORDS .- Statutoryprovisionsas to supplying lost
records. Ky . Stats. (1915), Secs. 3991-4000.
" We held in Com . v . Keger , 1 Duv . 240 that there were
two methods of supplying lost pleadings; one, the statutory
method of a referenceto a commissioner, the other, the common
law method of a substitutionby the court. In Gregory v .
Meister, 141 Ky . 54, we said that when a pleading in a case
had been lost, which couldbe promptly and correctlysupplied
under the eye of the court, it was proper to permit it to be
done so rather than to continue the case for a reference to the
commissioner." May v . May , 150 Ky . 522, 150 S. W. 685.
The method of supplyinga lostrecordis given in Chap .
106, Ky . Stats. (See alsoCom . v.Keger, 1 Duv . 240; Mayo
v .Emery ,103 Ky . 637 ; Farmers Bank of Frankfort v . Farmers
Bank of Wickliffe,147 Ky .766,145 S. W. 746.
When the recordof an actionislostitmust be legallysup-
plied, and where a lostrecordhas not been legallysupplied,

LOST RECORDS 2039 LOST RECORDS
itwillbe presumed on appealfrom an orderrefusingto cor-
recta judgmentforclericalmisprision, thatthecourtruled
correctly. FarmersBank of Frankfortv. Farmers Bank of
Wickliffe, 147 Ky .766,145 S. W. 746.
To admit parol evidence of the contentsof a writing which
is in the papers of the party offeringthe evidence, it must be
shown that diligentsearch has been made for it, and that the
difficultyof findingit is sufficientto warrant the court in con-
cluding that the paper is lost. The Haven Malleable Casting
Co. v . W. E. Caldwell Co., 146 Ky . 135, 142 S W. 227.
The accidentallossof a bill, declaration, etc., after service
of process, will not necessarilyabate the suit; but a substitute
may be prepared, and the courtbeing satisfiedof the lossof
the original, and that the substituteis of the same import,
should permit it to be filedand to have the same effect. Sug-
gett v . Bank Ky ., 8 Dana , 202.
There are two modes of supplying lost records: 1. A com-
missioner may be appointed by the court whose records have
been lostor destroyed, who shalltake proof and report. 2. The
court on satisfactoryproof may substitutea paper for that lost
or destroyed. Com . v . Keger , 1 Duv . 241
Courts have inherent power to supply theirlostor defaced
records. Montgomery v . Viers, 130 Ky . 694, 114 S. W. 251.
A lostor destroyed ballotcan not be supplied, like a lost
deed or other lost instrument of writing, for the purpose of
counting the vote. Schollv.Bell, 125 Ky 750, 102 S. W. 248,
31 R. 335 .
Ordinarily, when negotiablepaper has been lostby the real
owner, he may tender a bond of indemnity, which, when
approved by the court, will authorizepayment to him by the
maker ; but where the applicationof this rule, on account of
the peculiar circumstances of the case, would multiply litiga-
tion, an injunctionwould be the proper remedy. Bainbridge
v. City of Louisville, 83 Ky . 285, 7 R. 269.
Lost papers may be supplied by a proceeding in the circuit
court, and the supplied papers may take the place of the
originaland for the purposesof appeal. Turner v. Hamlin,
152 Ky . 469, 153 S. W. 778 .

LOST THE MOTION .- Where a servant has actuallyoper-
ated and seen others operate an implement or machine often
enough to enable him by the exerciseof ordinary intelligence
and care to learn how to avoid being injured by it, or where
the mode of operating itis so simple that a person of ordinary
intelligenceor care can at once perceive the safe and proper
mode of operatingit, there is no duty restingupon the master
to instructhim . Jones v . L. & N. R. Co., 95 Ky . 576, 16 R.
132 ,13 R. 542.
Where a sectionhand on a railroadwhile engaged, under
the direction of the section boss, in operating a hand -car,
stooped down to throw aside some loose tools on the floor of
the car, and " lost the motion of the lever" and was thrown
offthe car and injured, the company was not liableon account
of the failureof the sectionboss to warn him of the danger ,
for while he had never before operated a hand -car, yet he had
seen othersdo so and must have known that it was dangerous
" to lose the motion of the lever." And even if the section
boss was negligentin permitting the toolsto be placed looseon
the floorof the car it does not appear that that negligencewas
the proximate cause of the injury to the plaintiff, there being
nothing to show that there was a reasonableprobabilityof his
being injured by the tools, and even if it had been necessary
for them to be put aside he might have had the car stopped
for that purpose. Id .
LOST TIME .- See LoSS OF TIME .
LOT . A strip of land appropriated to use by a railroad
as a rightof way is a " lot" within the meaning of the statute
governingstreetimprovements, and is liableto an assessment
for a streetimprovement . Figg v . L. & N. R. Co., 116 Ky .
135, 75 S. W. 269, 25 R. 350.
A stripof land appropriatedto use by a railroadas a per-
petual rightof way , and which is a lot within the statute
governing streetimprovements, is liablefor an assessment for
a streetimprovement . L. & N. R. Co. v. Barber Asphalt Pav-
ing Co., 116 Ky . 856, 76 S. W. 1097, 25 R. 1024 .

LOT OF HORSES 2041 LOTTERIES
A deviseby a testatrixof " the house and lotand appur-
tenances thereto belonging wherein I now reside," includes
three adjoining lotsused by the testatrixas a homestead, and
not merely the lot on which the house stands. Myers v . Nor-
man , 46 S. W. 214, 20 R. 343.
A street car franchise to operate a line of road in the middle
of a street is not a lot or a part of a lot of land " fronting or
abutting or bordering upon the street." City of Maysville v .
Maysville St.Ry . Co., 128 Ky . 679, 108 S. W. 960.
A decree describingthe property to be sold as " a lot with
a livery stable thereon," situated on a certain street, and
naming the person who conveyed it to defendant, is sufficient.
McCue v . Sharp , 45 S. W. 770, 20 R. 216.
A contractto convey land consistingof " one lot with black-
smith shop thereon" sufficientlydescribedthe property, where
the contractwas executed by the vendees taking possessionand
holding it with the acquiescenceof the vendor under his con-
tract. Ferguson v . Cabell, 141 Ky . 499, 133 S. W. 539.
LOT OF HORSES .- A pleading averred that a payment
was made in part out of the proceeds of a " lot of horses,
buggies and property, amounting to over $500 " claimed to be
exempt , but without any statement of fact showing that they
fellwithin the listof articlesspecificallyexempted by statute,
or that such specificarticlesso exempted were not on hand
and retainedby the debtor, other than breadstuff Held , defec-
tive on the ground that it does not show what provisionsand
provender the debtor had on hand . Oldham's Admx . v . Old-
ham's Admx ., 141 Ку . 526, 133 S. W. 232.
LOTTERIES .- Lotteriesare forbidden by Ky . Stat., Sec.
2573 .
" A lotteryis definedby Webster as a scheme by which one
or more prizes are distributedby chance among persons who
have paid or promised a considerationfor the chance to win
them . And , in 25 Cyc . 1633, it is said that 'A lotteryis a
speciesof gambling which may be definedas a scheme for the
distributionof prizesby chanceamong personswho have paid
or agreed to pay a valuableconsiderationfor the chance to
obtaina prize. " Com .v.Jenkins,159 Ky .80, 166 S. W. 794.

LOTTERIES LOTTERIES2042
A lotterygrant is not a contract, within the meaning of the
Constitution, but is merely a license, or gratuity. Douglas v .
Kentucky , 168 U. S. 488 .
A popularity contestinaugurated by a newspaper publisher
whereby persons paying for subscriptions or advertising,
receivedballotsin proportion to the amount paid; and partici-
pating merchants bought from such publisherand gave to their
customers ballotswith their purchases, the persons receiving
the highest number of votes to receivean automobile is not
within the purview of the statutedenouncing lotteries. Com .
v . Jenkins, 159 Ky 80, 166 S. W. 794.
The promotion of a lotteryand the aiding in such promo-
tion are but differentmodes of committing or participatingin
the commission of the same offense. The latterisbut a degree
of the former, and under an indictment for the major offense
a convictionmay be had for the minor. Millerv. Com ., 13
Bush , 731.
The words of the statute prohibiting the sale of lottery
ticketsare general, and include all ticketsand all lotteries.
Com . v . Bull, 13 Bush , 656.
The offenseof vending a lotteryticket,set out in the indict-
ment in the language of the statute, isdescribedwith sufficient
certainty. Com . v. Bull, 13 Bush , 656.
The firstsectionof the billof rightswas intended to pre-
vent the exerciseof some public function or an exclusiveprivi-
lege affectingthe interestsand rightsof the public generally,
when not in considerationof publicservice. Specialprivileges
may be granted to one or more citizenswhere the rights of
othersare not affected. In allcaseswhere privaterightsare
invadedor jeopardizedby legislativeenactments, the granting
of a privilegeto one by way of exemption from the operation
of a general law is unconstitutional. The " Act for the benefit
of W. C. D. Whipps ," which authorizeshim to run a lottery
forthe purposeof sellingcertainrealestatebelongingto him,
the proceeds of the drawing to be paid to his creditors, is not
within the inhibitionof the firstsectionof the billof rights.
Com .v . Whipps , 80 Ky . 269, 3 R. 798 .
One who advertises a lottery scheme conducted in another
State, or the sale of ticketsin such lottery, is not guilty of a

violationof Sec. 5, Art . 23, Chap . 29, Gen. Stats., unless, by
advertisement, he indicateswhere the ticketsmay be obtained
in this State. LouisvilleCourier-Journal Co. v . Com . 92 Ky .
22, 13 R. 341, 11 R. 809 .
Illegalcontract to pay money to bribe officialsto secure
immunity from prosecution for operating lottery. Smith v .
Richmond , 114 Ky . 303, 70 S. W. 846, 24 R. 1117.
LOVE AND AFFECTION .- Love and affection is a sufficient
considerationto support a conveyance from a father to his
son. Rittenhousev.Swango , 97 S. W. 743. 30 R. 145.
The relationshipbetween the partiesis sufficientto uphold
a deed made " in consideration of love and affection " from a
husband and his wife to the husband of her sister. Blackerby
v .Holton , 5 Dana , 521.
Love and affectionis a sufficientconsiderationto support
a deed from the wife to the husband . Todd v . Wickliffe, 18

LOW . " 'High ' and 'low ' are relativeterms. A thing is
said to be high or low when compared to other things. A
structure is said to be high or low according to the uses to
which it is to be put. So , in this case, the bridge is high or
low according to the heighth of cars to pass under it." Louis-
ville& Nashville R. Co. v . Tucker's Admr ., 65 S. W. 453,
23 R. 1929 .
LOW BRIDGE .- Where a brakeman was fatallyinjuredby
being struck by a bridge while he was on top of a foreign
refrigeratorcar, it was proper for his administratorto allege
negligence in defendant company in maintaining the bridge
too low, there being evidence that defendant used cars of its
own of the same height as the one on which the brakeman
was riding when injured. L. & N. R. Co. v . Tucker's Admr .,
65 S. W. 453 , 23 R. 1929.
It is negligenceon the part of a railroadcompany to main-
tain an overhead bridge so low that a brakeman standing on
top of a train can not pass under it in safety. L. & N. R. Co.
v.Cooley'sAdmr .,49 S.W. 339,20 R. 1372.
Where a railroadfreight brakeman was knocked from the
top of a box car and injured by an overhead tunnel gauge

LOW LIMB 2044 LOWEST AND BEST
situatedhalf a mile from the tunnel and unprotected by a
" telltale" or other device to notify the brakeman of the pres-
ence of the tunnel gauge, the trialcourt did not err in over
ruling defendant'smotion for a peremptory instruction. L. &
N. R. Co. v .Roe ,142 Ky . 456, 134 S. W. 437.
The maintenance by a railroadcompany of a bridge across
its track at such a height as to endanger the lives of brake-
men in the dischargeof theirduty on the top of itsfreightcars
is willfulnegligence. C., N. O. & T. P. Ry . Co. v . Sampson's
Admr ., 97 Ky . 65, 30 S. W. 12, 16 R. 819.
The fact that a brakeman had opportunity to know that the
bridge was so constructedas to involvedanger to brakemen in
the performance of theirduties in crossingthe bridge does not
preclude a recovery for his death. Hughes v . L. & N. R. Co.,
104 Ky .733,48 S. W. 671,20 R. 1029.
See, also, Finley v. LouisvilleRy . Co., 103 S. W. 343, 31 R
740 ; Louisville& I. R. Co. v . Hardin's Admr ., 154 Ky . 282 ,
157 S. W. 15 .
LOW LIMB.- A city is liable, under its duty to keep its
streetsin good condition, for injury to one who , without con-
tributorynegligence, is driving a wagon along a street, and is
thrown therefrom by coming in contact with a large limb of a
treeprojectingover the streetdangerously low . City of Louis-
villev. Michels, 114 Ky . 551, 71 S. W. 511, 24 R. 1375.
LOW -WATER MARK .- Low -water mark does not mean the
lowest stage of the water in seasons of great drought, but is
to be determined from the height of the water at ordinary
stagesof low water. Kentucky Lumber Co. v . King , 65 S. W.
156 , 23 R. 1422 .
LOWEST AND BEST BIDDER .- Ky . Stats., Sec. 3094 .
gives to the general councilof citiesof the second classexclu-
sive control over streetsand the improvements thereof; and
Sec.3096 prescribesthat the general councilmay ,by ordinance,
provide for improvements , etc. A city of the second class
enacted an ordinance which provided that in making improve-
ments the work should be awarded to the " lowest and best
bidder." Afterward it undertook to reconstructa street, and

advertised for bids both on brick pavement and bituminous
macadam . Held , that the fact that the bid of one proposing
to do the work with brick was the lowest in amount did not
require an award to him , that fact not being conclusivethat
he was the "lowestand bestbidder." Trapp v. City of New-
port, 115 Ky . 840, 74 S. W. 1109, 25 R. 224.

" To lowest bidder." See note on this subject in 38 L. R.

LOYAL VOTERS . An averment that one " was electedby

that he was duly elected. The laws do not define the mean-
ing of the phrase " loyal voters," but as ordinarilyused its

phrases"legalvoters," " qualifiedvoters." Leeman v.Hinton,

LUMBER .- Children injured by playing on pilesof lumber .

907 ; Instructionsto Juries, Sec. 466, and copious notes.
LUMBER NOT LESS THAN SIX FEET IN LENGTH.-
The terms " lumber not less than six feet in length" and
" trash," as used in a contract of sale of all lumber six feet
and more in length in certainbuildingsand pens, for the con-
siderationin part to wreck the buildingsand remove the trash,
do not, in theirordinarymeaning,includea spur trackon the

track,such terms cannotbe construedto includeit, and itdid
not pass to the purchaser under the contract. Woodruff v .

LUMP COAL .- Lump coal is that which remains after
the nut, slack and dirt have been separated from it by screen-
ings. Hardin v . Thompson , 57 S. W. 12.
LUMPING TRADE .-The fact that a note is not discounted
at a certainper cent., but by a "lumping trade," does not

negotiablecharacter. Nicholson v . Natl. Bank of New Castle,
92 Ky . 251, 13 R. 478 ; Rev. Sup . Ct ., 12 R. 216.
LUNACY .- Lunacy may be given in evidenceunder the
general issue, or itmay be pleaded. Pearl v .McDowell ,3 J. J.
Μ . 660 .
An inquest of lunacy is a judicial determination of the
status of the person charged with lunacy. He may be com-
mitted to an asylum and a committee may be appointed to take
charge of his property. The inquest is conclusiveof the con-
dition of the lunatic'smind at the time, and is therefore a
judicialproceeding. Hickman County v .Jackson,153 Ky . 551 ,
156 S. W. 391 .
Lunacy is not a ground for divorce, though it prevents the
wife from dischargingher conjugal duties. Pile v. Pile,94 Ky .
308 , 15 R. 88 .
LUNATIC . Itisprovidedin Ky .Stats.,Secs.446,450, that
in the construction of statutes " the words 'unsound mind '
shallbe construed to includeevery one who is an idiot,lunatic,

LURCH OF TRAIN . See, also, JERK .
Passenger thrown from rear platform of streetcar by lurch
held not entitledto recover unlesslurch was unusual, unneces-
sary, and of such violenceas would reasonablybe expected to
cause a passenger to be thrown off. S. C. & C. St. Ry . Co. v .
Trowbridge, 163 Ку . 79, 173 S. W. 371.
A passenger riding on the platform of a passenger car can
not recover for being thrown therefrom by the lurching of the

trainas itisoperated around a curve at ordinary speed. Craw-
fordv.L. & N. R. Co.,122 S. W. 220.
LYING IN WAIT.- " To make lying in wait for a man or
besettinghis house an assault, it must be with an intention
to beat him , or use personal violenceto him ; and thisintention
is not a matter of law, but a matter of fact to be determined
by the jury." Metcalfe v. Conner , Litt.Sel.Cases 371.
LYING JUST BELOW .— The words "lying just below the
mouth of Shelby Creek," constitutingin part the description

McCHORD ACT .- The McChord Act . (Ky . Stats., Sec.
820 -a), which authorizesthe Kentucky Railroad Commission ,
upon complaint that railroad rates are extortionate, to fix
reasonable rates, if after a hearing, it finds the rates extor-

MACHINE . A boilerinstalledfor heating a dwelling house
was material for repairing the house, and was also a fixture
and machine furnished for the improvement of the property

withinKy . Stats.,Sec.2463. Menne v.American RadiatorCo.,

MACHINE SHOPS .- The grantor,in considerationof a rail-
road company's agreement to locateitsdepot on certainland ,
conveyed the possessoryright to said land to the company for

poses," to hold the same for the use of said depot and other

ilegeof using itfor such purposesin conjunctionwith itsuse

MACHINERY .- Neither a flouringmill or the machinery in

islocateda mill, in which ismachinery , and no lienisreserved
on suchmachinery,the personreceivingsuch conveyanceisnot

What instrumentalitiesare covered by the term " machin-
ery." See note on thissubect in 57 L. R. A. 821.
MACHINERY IN MANUFACTORIES .- Exemption of

MACHINIST AND RAILROADER . The applicanthaving

MADE IN COURT .-The rule is firmly establishedthat the

MAGISTRATES . “ A warrant of arrestmay be issued by
the followingofficers, who are calledmagistratesin this Code ,
viz.:judgesof thecountycourts,judgesof cityor policecourts,

MAINTENANCE .- The word " maintenance," as used by

shelter, or tuition, clothing, food and shelter, depending upon
the circumstances surrounding the parties and the connection

MAINTENANCE (Of Suit).- Though champertyisthe only
form of maintenance forbidden by Ky . Stats., Sec. 209, the
ancient rule of the common law againstsimple maintenance is
yet in forcein Kentucky . Lynn v .Moss ,139 Ky .637, 62 S. W.
712 , 23 R. 214.
" Maintenance signifiesan unlawful taking in hand or
upholding of quarrels, or sides, to the disturbanceor hind-
rance of common right. It is divided into two classes. First:
Ruralis , or in the country, as where one assistsanother in his
pretensionsto certainlands, or stirsup quarrelsand suitsin
the country, in relationto matters wherein he is in no way
concerned . Secondly : Curialis, or in a court of justice, as
where one officiouslyintermeddlesin a suitdepending in any
such court, which no way belongs to him by assistingeither
party with money , or otherwise, in the prosecutionor defense
of any such suit." Brown v . Beauchamp , 5 T B. Monroe ,
414 .
" Of the second classof maintenance, there are three species.
First : Where one maintains another in his suit without any

MAINTENANCE MAINTENANCE2052
contracttohave part of thethingin suit,which generallygoes
under the common name of maintenance. Secondly : Where
one maintains one side, to have part of the thing in suit, which
is called champerty. Thirdly: Embracery, as where one
attemptsto corrupt, or influence, or instructa jury, or any
way to inclinethem to be more favorableto the one side, or
the other, by money, letters, promises, threats, or persuasions,
exceptonly by the strengthof evidenceand the arguments of
counselin open court,at thetrialof thecause;so alsoby labor-
ing a jurorto appear, and act accordingto conscience, or by
indirectmeans , as where persons procure themselves or others
to be sworn on a jury to serve the one side" Brown v .Beau-
champ , 5 T. B. Monroe , 414.
Champerty isthe unlawful maintenanceof a suit, in con-
siderationof some bargain to have part of the thing in dis-
pute, or some profitout of it. 1 Hawk .pl. co.p .545 ; Co. Litt.
368. Every champerty is but a speciesof maintenance, which
isthe genus. 2 Inst.207. Brown v .Beauchamp .5 T. B. Mon-
roe, 416.
" They tend to prevent the observanceof the golden rule and
sacred advice, 'agree with thine adversary quickly whilst thou
art in the way with him .' Maintenance is malum in se, and
againstthe common law , and statutoryprovisionsin aid of the
common law." Brown v . Beauchamp , 5 T. B. Monroe 416 .
Maintenance is a misdemeanor , and punishable by fine and
imprisonment. Brown v . Beauchamp , 5 Mon. 416 .
A contract to aid in defense of a suit in consideration of
money and a part of the land, in the event of success, is both
simple maintenance and champerty . Brown v . Beauchamp , 5
Mon. 416. See, also, CHAMPERTY .

MALICE . In Slander, see SLANDER ; in Malicious Prose-

Malice, in its legal sense, is the intentionaldoing of a
wrongful act toward another without legal justificationor

cause; and where the evidenceshowed malicein thissense, it
was propertoinstructthejury as tomurder. Ludwig v.Com .,

An act is maliciouslydone if done with a deliberateevil
intent; ill-willtoward the person injuredby the act is not

The court should, in definingthe word " malice," define its

The defendant was not prejudiced by an instructionwhich

" Express malice is such as isdiscoveredby externalcircum-

It is not error to failto definethe words " without previous

17 R. 965 .
A wrongful act done intentionallyand without just cause
denotes malice, i. e. furnishes evidence of malice. Nichols v.

An instructionthat "malice in the legal sense denotes a

Malice Implied. The common -law doctrinethat malice is
implied from the recklessuse of a deadly weapon does not
obtainin Kentucky, but the existenceof maliceis a question
for the jury. Ewing v . Com ., 129 Ky . 237, 111 S. W. 352,
33 R. 749 .
Where one recklesslykillsanother with a deadly weapon ,
malice may be implied. It is not necessarilyimplied. The
questionisfor the jury on allthe factsof the homicide. Lewis
v . Com ., 140 Ky . 652, 131 S. W. 517.
In a prosecution for malicious wounding it was not error
to refuse to instruct that " malice can not be inferred." While
malice, likeany other fact, must be proved, this may be done
from circumstantialevidence, from which the jury may infer
it. Walker v. Com ., 7 R. 44.
If one killsanother without cause the law implies malice;
but malice can not be implied from every deliberatecruel
act committed by one person against another, for if the killing
is in sudden heat and passion, the crime is manslaughter and
not murder . No malice can be implied where the killing,though
intended, was done in sudden heat of passion. Maxey v . Com .,
8 Ky . Opin . 251.
Where malice is an essentialingredientin a crime the jury
should be leftto find that it existsor does not exist, the same
as it is left to them to find the killingin a homicide case.
Hale & Head v.Grogan ,10 Ky . Opin . 297.
The law never implies malice from a given fact or facts,
but itsexistenceis in every instanceto be determined by the
jury. Hence , an instructionis erroneous which informs the
jury that under certain circumstancesthe law implies malice.
Under the law in this State it is error to define in an instruc-
tion to the jury the term " Implied Malice." Crittenden v .
Com ., 11 Ky . Opin . 193, 3 R. 56.
It is error to instructthe jury that they may infer malice
from certain acts. Farris v . Com ., 14 Bush , 362 ; Bush v .
Com .,78 Ky . 268.
It iserrorto instructthatmaliceisimpliedby thelaw from

MALICE AFORETHOUGHT .- The phrase " malice afore-
thought " means a predetermination to do the act of killing
without legal excuse, and it is immaterial how suddenly or
recently before the killing such determination was formed .
An instructionsubstantiallyin thisform has been approved in

v . Com .,129 Ky .237,111 S. W. 352,33 R. 749 ; Com .v . Combs ,

ously and maliciously" sufficient.

nection with definitionof "feloniously." Potterv. Com ., 142

MALICIOUS CUTTING . In a trialunder an indictment for

and battery, such an assault and battery is punished by

MALICIOUS PROSECUTION .- Generally. The gistof an
action for maliciousprosecutionis the abuse of the process of

malice and want of probable cause. The proper enforcement
of our criminallaws requiresthatpersons should not be deterred
from institutingproceedings to enforce them where they have
probable cause for theiraction. Schott v .Indiana National Life
Ins. Co., 160 Ky . 533, 169 S. W. 1023 ; Moser v . Fable, 164
Ky . 517, 175 S. W. 997.
The gist of the action is the procuring of the warrant
maliciously and without probable cause, and this must be

156 Ky . 156, 160 S. W. 917.
It is essentialto the sufficiencyof a petitionin an action

To maintainan actionformaliciousprosecution,theprosecu-

To sustain an action for malicious prosecution it must
affirmativelyappear as a part of the case of the party demand-
ing damages that the party sought to be charged was the
proximate and efficientcause of maliciouslyputting the law
in motion . It must be maliciouslydone and without probable
cause and malice, want of probable cause and a procuring of
the warrant or indictment must concur. McClarty v . Bickel,
155 Ky . 254 ; Schott v . Indiana National Life Ins. Co., 160
Ky .533 ; Dunn v .Deskins, 163 Ky .689,174 S. W. 501.
It is error to instructthe jury in an action for malicious

on the charge preferred; it is only necessary that he entertain

arrested is guilty of the offensefor which he was so arrested
and prosecuted. Schwartz v . Boswell, 156 Ky . 103, 160
S. W. 748 .
" The law requires only a reasonable, honest and strong
beliefupon thepart of the prosecutor,thatthe person arrested

isguiltyof the offensefor which he is arrestedand prosecuted.
Lancaster v .McKay , 103 Ky .617, 45 S. W. 887, and casescited
therein. 26 Cyc ., page 24 (note) and page 29. It would be
against public policy to adopt so harsh a rule as to require
that every lover of justice who desires to enforce the law,
must expect a convictionin every case before commencing the
prosecution." Schwartz v .Boswell,156 Ky .103, 160 S. W. 748.
To render a person liablefor maliciousprosecution, he must
have acted maliciouslyand without probable cause. Roberts v .
Thomas , 135 Ky .63,121 S. W. 961.
An action for maliciousprosecutioncan be maintained only
where the prosecutionwas malicious and the prosecutor acted

132 Ky . 228, 116 S. W. 325.
It takes both malice and the want of probable cause to

cause can not be inferredfrom malice. Hudson v . Nolen, 142

To sustain an action for malicious prosecution it must
affirmativelyappear as a part of the case of the party demand-
ing damages , that the party sought to be charged was the
proximate and efficientcause of maliciouslyputting the law
in motion. It must be maliciouslydone and without probable
cause. Malice, want of probable cause, and a procuring of the
warrant or indictment must concur. McClarty v . Bickel, 155
Ky . 254 , 159 S. W. 783 .
There are three descriptionsof damages , either of which
will support the action: (1) To the person by imprisonment ;
(2 ) To the reputation by scandal; (3) To the property by

Malice . Malice in malicious prosecution is the intentional

or unlawful purpose. The court should tellthe jury what

In defining malice in a case of malicious prosecution or

MANDATORY PROVISION . A mandatory provisionin a
statuteis one, the omissionto followwhich rendersthe pro-
ceedingsto which it relatesillegaland void, whilea directory
provisionis one, the observanceof which is not necessaryto
thevalidityof theproceeding; and whethera particularstatute
is mandatory or directory, does not depend upon itsform , but
upon the intentionof the Legislature, to be ascertainedfrom
a considerationof the entire act, its nature, its object, and
the consequencesthat would resultfrom construingit one way
or the other. Caldwell County v.First National Bank , 151 Ky .
720 , 152 S. W. 757 ; Brodie v . Hook , 135 Ky . 92.

directionsof a statuteare given merely with a view to the

may generallybe regarded as directory. Lancaster v . Hamon ,

cloths into suits for wear . So , too, a seamstress would be

MARKED CAPACITY .- In an action for death of a brake-

MARKET .- Plaintiffclaimed damages for the failureto
deliver lumber ; the defendant insistedthat at the place where
the delivery was to be made there was no market , and that
therefore the nearest market to the place of delivery must
govern in fixing the damages ; the evidence was that at the

sold. Held ,that thisconstitutesa market ; itwas not necessary

was the subject of contentionwas at the time on sale at the
place. Bennett v . Gregory, 9 R. 194.
MARKET VALUE . Where the court instructedthe jury
that the measure of damages for injury to a horse was the
difference between the market value of the horse in the condition
in which he was before said injury " and his market value after

ascertained," and were thereforenot objectionable. I. C. R.
Co. v .Radford ,64 S. W. 511, 23 R. 886.
In an action against a railroad company for killing and
injuring stock, it is better to use, in the instructionas to the
measure of damages , the words " reasonable market value,"
instead of the single word " value." C. & O. Ry . Co. v .
Grigsby , 131 Ky . 363, 115 S. W. 237.
When we speak of the market value of a building, it is

ing islocated. L. & N. R. Co. v.Home Ins. Co., 146 Ky . 281,

itat itsmarket value,no rightto electas to thevalueat which

" What the'realvalue'of a thingis,dependsupon probative
facts; it is a matter not to be fixed, as is 'just and reasonable
compensation,' but one that has already been establishedby

of pre-existing facts, and therefore may be known at any

132),had no necessaryconnectionwith any pre-existingfact,
and thereforewas insusceptibleof beingknown in advance of
its determinationby the jury." Com . v. International

MARRIAGE .- See NEGRO MARRIAGE ; CUSTOMARY MARRIAGE .

There is now no such thing as legalmarriage by cohabita-

Marriage in contemplation of law is not only a valuable
considerationto supporta settlement,but isa considerationof
the highestvalue. Sanders v .Miller, 79 Ky . 517, 3 R. 295.
Marriage isa factthatmay be provedby reputationas well
as by a record,or by the testimonyof thosewho saw the rites
performed . Caldwell v . Williams,118 S. W. 932.
The reputationof a marriage,coupledwith a man's holding
himself out as a husband and father of a family, establishes
the marriage. Id.

MARRIAGE MARRIAGE2104
A marriage brought about by force and duress is void .
Such marriage should, upon the petitionof the injured party ,
be annulled, though the reliefasked was a decree of divorce.
Bassett v . Bassett, 9 Bush , 697.
A marriage celebrated according to the forms of law is
prima facie valid; the onus is on the party seeking to annul
it, and he is not a competent witness, even under the testimony
act of 1872. Bassettv .Bassett, 9 Bush , 697.
A marriage by a man who has a living wife is null and
void absolutely, and in virtue of such marriage the second
wife acquiresno claim to dower . Sneed v . Ewing , 5 J. J. M.
490 ; Donnelly v . Donnelly, 8 B. M. 116.
A marriage procured by fraud is voidable only at the elec-
tionof the party defrauded. The party who commits the fraud
is bound , and remains bound until the party deceived has
made his or her election, and will thereafterbe bound or not ,
according to the electionmade . Tomppert v . Tomppert , 13
Bush , 326 .
The right to avoid a marriage is personal, and if not taken
advantage of by a party in his lifetimeit can not be exercised
after his death by his executors or devisees. Tomppert v .
Tomppert ,13 Bush ,326.
If a marriage is otherwiselegal,itis not invalidbecause the
female did not reside in the county where the license was
obtained. Gatewood v. Tunk, 3 Bibb, 246; Stevensonv. Gray ,
17 В. М. 209 .
A marriage celebrated, but not according to the statute
regulatingthe solemnizationof marriages, isneverthelessvalid,
the statute being directory to the functionaries, but not
addressedto the parties. Dumaresly v. Fishly, 3 Mar. 370 .
Person of unsound mind can not be married . Performance
of marriage ceremony over one of unsound mind and cohabita-
tion until death can not constitute a legal marriage nor give
any claimtodower or curtesy. Jenkinsv.Jenkins,2 Dana ,103.
Marriage celebratedin a sisterState must be tested by
the laws of that State. Dumaresly v . Fishly, 3 Mar. 369.
A marriage valid where celebratedisvalidin other countries
where thepartiesmay be domiciled,though itwould have been
4

MARRIAGE MARRIAGE2105
invalidby the law of the subsequent domicile if it had been
originally celebrated there. Polygamous and incestuous
marriages are exceptions to this rule. Sneed v. Ewing , 5
J. J. M. 490 ; Stevenson v . Gray , 17 B. M. 208 ; Dannelli v .
Dannelli, 4 Bush ,57.
In an action where the marriage of plaintiffand the legiti-
macy of her children are in issue, the fact that she and her
allegedhusband lived together as man and wife and enjoyed
the reputationof being married, that therewas a judgment
of divorce introducedby defendant himself, and the defendant
and others recognized the children as legitimate, is sufficient
in the absence of evidence to the contrary, to create the pre-
sumption of marriage and establishthe legitimacy of the
children. Skidmore v .Harris, 157 Ky . 757, 164 S. W. 98.
Where a man and woman livetogetheras husband and wife,
and she in a deed assumes his name , their marriage is pre-
sumed . Kentucky Stave Co. v .Page , 125 S. W. 170.
The presumption is that the relation of marriage, once
established, continuesuntil it has been shown that it was dis-
solved . Id .
MARRIAGE BROKERAGE CONTRACT .- An undertaking
by one for a moneyed or valuable consideration to bring about
a marriage between others is void. Johnson v . Hunt , 81 Ky .
321 , 5 R. 194 .
MARRIAGE CERTIFICATE . A paper purporting to be a
marriagecertificateissuedby a justiceof the peace of another
State does not prove itself. The fact that the justiceof the
peace was an officerof another State, as well as the fact that
he executedthe paper in hisofficialcapacity, must be proved.
Holtman v . Holtman , 114 S. W. 1198.
MARRIAGE CONTRACT .- A person having a living hus-
band from whom she has not been divorced can not make a
binding contractto marry anotherconditionedupon a divorce
being granted. The same capacity is required to make a
contractto marry upon a contingency that is requisiteto make
an absolute contract to marry at a future time. A contract

MARRIAGE MARSHALING2106
must be valid when entered into or it never can be valid .
Webb v . Forman , 8 Ky . Opin . 697. See, also, MARRIAGE .
MARRIAGE DE FACTO . A marriagede facto, though not
de jure, is valid for most purposes, as relatesto personal
matters or torts. Dumaresly v.Fishly, 3 Mar. 371.
MARRIAGE SETTLEMENT .- (a) Ante-nuptial,an arrange-
ment made before marriage, and in considerationof it, whereby ,
as a rule, a jointure is secured to the wife, and portions to
children, in the event of the husband's death, and ulterior
limitationsare made of the settledproperty in the event of
no childrentaking under it; (b ) post-nuptial, or aftermarriage ,
which lacks the considerationof marriage, and is therefore
voluntary . Cochran's Law Lexicon .
MARRIED WOMAN'S ACT .- Act of March 15,1894. Now
Ky . Stats., Secs. 2127-2148.
MARSHAL.- A marshal is a " peace officer" under Crim .
Code , Sec. 26, which reads as follows: "A warrant of arrest
may be issuedby the following officers, who are calledmagis-
tratesin thisCode, viz.: judgesof the County Courts, judges
of City or Police Courts, mayors , chairmen of the trusteesof
towns and justicesof the peace; and may be executed by the
following officers, who are called peace officersin this Code ,
viz.:sheriffs, constables, coroners, jailers, marshalsand police-
men ."
The use of the word " constable" in writing a marshal's
bond, being a mere misdescription, could not legallyenlarge
nor diminish the obligor'sresponsibility. Forsee v . Trustees of
Owenton , 3 Ky . Opin . 175 .
MARSHALING ASSETS AND SECURITIES .- Marshaling
of assetsis an equitableproceedingwhich depends upon the
principle that a person having two funds out of which to
satisfyhisclaimswhen anothercan resortto one of them only,
shouldbe compelledto satisfyhisclaim, ifhe can, out of that
fund to which the other can not resort, thus enabling the
latter to secure more complete satisfaction. Cochran's Law
Lexicon .

MARSHALING MARSHALING2107
Marshaling assetsfor payment of debts should be done in
the following order: (1) The general personal estate; (2)
Estate specificallyand expresslydevised to be sold; (3) Estate
descended; (4) Estate specificallydevised, though charged
generallywith the payment of debts. McCampbell v. McCamp-
bell, 5 Litt. 96.
To this arrangement there is an exception, and second and
third rules exchange places where the estate set apart is
charged generallyand not specifically. Id .
" It is well settledthat a creditorwho has a claim against
two debtors, one a principaland the other a surety, can not
be compelled by another creditorof the principal debtor to
exhausthisremedy againstthesuretybeforeproceedingagainst
the principal. For a like reason a second mortgagee having
a lien on a husband's property can not require a prior
mortgagee having a lienon the same property, and also on the
propertyof the wife which was pledgedmerely to securethe
husband's debt, firstto exhaust the wife's property before
proceeding to subjectthe husband's property." Gaines v .
Hill, 147 Ky . 445, 144 S. W. 92, and cases cited.
This rule applieswhere the debtor or the person who placed
a lien on the funds or the fund upon which the junior lien-
holder has not a lien is still the owner . Id .
As between mere equities, that which is prior in time is
regarded as best, and takes precedence over any other which
may be subsequently created. Id .
" The general rule is that, if one creditor, by virtue of a
lien or interest, can resortto two funds, and another to one
of them only, as, for example , where a mortgagee holds a
prior mortgage on two parcelsof land, and a subsequent
mortgage on but one of the parcelsis given to another, the
former must seek satisfactionout of that fund which the latter
can not touch." Shewmaker v .Yankey , 66 S. W. 2,23 R. 1759.
Equity will not marshal securitiesexcept as against a
common debtor,and willnever do so where itwillwork injustice
to an innocent third person. Hughes v . Shannon & Lowe , 13
R. 782 ; Hite v .Reynolds,163 Ky .502, 173 S. W. 1108.
If one creditor has a lien on two funds and another has a
lienof a younger dateon only one of them,thelatterwillhave

MARSHALING MARSHALING2108
a right in equity to have the fund on which he has no lien
firstapplied to the payment of the other creditor. Bank Ky .
v. Vance , 4 Litt. 175 ; Swigert v . Bank Ky ., 17 B. М. 285 ;
Glass v . Pullen, 6 Bush , 349 ; Griffinv . Gingell, 79 S. W. 284 ,
25 R. 2031 .
The equitablerule of compelling a holder of liens on two
piecesof property to resortfirstto that not occupied as a home-
stead will not be applied in a case where there has been an
exchange of land, as the debt existedprior to the homestead
right and it would work an injusticeto the person who has
given the land in exchange. Williams v. Samuels , 90 Ky . 59 ,
11 R. 863 .
A creditorhaving more than one contract securitywill be
allowed to prorate with general creditorsfor the full amount
of his claim, taking no account of his independent securities;
he may then apply these securitiesto such balances as may be
due him after exhausting the general estate, and if anything
remains of these securitiesafter satisfyinghis claim in full it
willbe applied for the benefitof the general creditors. Hibler
v.Davis, 13 Bush , 21.
Where one lien covers the whole of a fund , and another
only a part of it, equity will compel the satisfactionof the
firstout of that part of the fund which the second does not
cover. Schupp v . Thomasson , 9 R. 360.
Where one has a contractlien upon two funds he may look
to both for the full amount of his debt until it is satisfied .
Merrifieldv. Tyler, 9 R. 399.
Equity will not marshal securitieswhere it will work
injusticeto interestedthird parties. Hughes v Shannon , 13
R. 782 .
The equitablerule which requiresthat, as between a creditor
who has a right of recourse against two funds and a creditor
who has a recourseagainst only one of the funds, the former
shallfirstexhaust the fund on which the latterhas no claim,
appliesonly when the demands are againsta common debtor. Id .
Where a creditor of an insolvent husband has a lien on the
property of both the husband and the wife, the general
creditorscan not require the secured creditorto firstexhaust
the wife's estateupon which he has a lien. Id.

MARSHALING MARSHALING2109
Equity will not marshal securitieswhen in aiding one
encumbrancer it would injure another or trench upon the
rights or operate to the prejudice of the party entitledto a
double fund . Logan v .Anderson , 18 B. М. 119.
Taxes are not debts, and the rule isnot applicablethat where
one has a lien upon two funds and another has a lien upon
only one of them the chancellor will require the firstlien-
holder to exhaust his claim against the fund on which he has
an exclusivelien before resortingto the other. Husband's v .
City of Paducah , 12 Ky . Opin . 201 .
Where there are two funds and one creditorhas a lienupon
both, and another creditorupon only one of them , the former
isrequiredto satisfyhis claims out of the fund upon which the
other creditorhas no lien; but thisruleof marshallingsecurities
does not apply as between creditorand debtor, but only between
differentcreditors. Bronson v .Ransom , 11 Ky . Opin . 960.
The rule of equity that a creditorwho can resortto two
funds will not be allowed to disappoint another creditorwho
has a lienon only one of the funds can never be invoked so as
to deprive a debtor of his homestead. Ralls v . Prather, 52
S. W. 800, 21 R. 555 ; denying rehearing, 51 S. W. 318, 21
R. 322 .
Where a creditorwho had attached tobacco was adjudged to
be entitledto be substitutedto a mortgage lien held by a
creditorwho had a prior lien on the tobacco, it was error, in
distributingthe proceeds of the mortgaged land, to give him
any part thereof until the mortgagee's debt had been paid in
full. Worthington v . Brooks -Waterfield Co., 49 S. W. 450,
20 R. 1432 .
A creditorwho holds a note on a principaland surety, both
of whom assign for the benefit of their creditors, is not
required to relinquishhis demand to any extent upon either.
His pro rata on his entiredebt from each should be ascertained,
and aftercreditingthe amount derivedfrom the principal's
estatethewhole pro rataassetsof thesuretyshouldbe applied
untilthey equalthedebt. CitizensBank of Parisv.Patterson,
78 Ky . 291.
Where two personshave a lienor claimagainsta fund, and
one of them has also a lienor claim against another fund, the

MARSHALING MARTIAL LAW2110
latterought to firstexhaust his exclusivesecuritybefore
coming intothe common fund ; but the burden is on one invok-
ing this rule to show that there is another securitysubject to
the debt soughtto be postponed. Brown & Bro.v. Lopp, 89
S. W. 304, 28 R. 409 .
Where thereis a part of a judgment debtor'sproperty on
which attaching creditorshave not a lien by reason of their
attachment, one who holds a prior lien should be required to
exhaust firstall the property covered by his lien which is not
covered by the attachment. Jones v . Dulaney & Mitchell, 86
S. W. 547, 27 R. 702 .
Where property mortgaged by a brewing company was
insufficientto pay the mortgage and over-due taxes, and debts
owing to laborersand material men , which were entitled to
priority, and which were alsolienson unincumbered property ,
the mortgagees were entitledto have such unmortgaged property
firstapplied to such tax and labor liensbefore subjection of
the mortgaged property thereto. Goepper v . Phoenix Brewing
Co., 115 Ky . 708, 74 S. W. 726, 25 R. 84 .
A second mortgagee can require the firstmortgagee to first
exhaust other property on which he has an exclusive lien-
Miles v .Natl.Bank of Ky ., 140 Ky . 376 , 131 S. W. 26.
While an innocent third person may take a second mortgage
on property and thus defeat the equity of a junior mortgagee ,
the holder of a seniorequity, when he has noticeof the junior
equity, stands differently. To allow the holder of a senior
mortgage to take a second mortgage and thus defeat the junior
equity, of which he has notice, would be to allow him by his
acts to take from another a vested right. Id .
In an action to set aside a mortgage as a preference to a
certaincreditorthe questionof marshaling assetsand securities
can not be determined, where all parties concerned are not
before the court. Foley's Trustee v . Foley, 108 S. W. 270 ,
32 R. 1228 .
MARTIAL LAW .- The declaration of martial law in Ken-
tucky did not suspend the Constitutionof the United States, or
stiflethe laws of this State; and what that Constitutionand
thoselaws denounced as a crime, martiallaw did not legalize.
Com .v.Palmer,2 Bush ,571.

MASCULINE 2111 MASTER AND SER .
MASCULINE GENDER .— In construingthe CivilCode each
gender includesevery gender. See CivilCode , Sec. 732 .
It is provided in Ky . Stats., Secs. 446, 457, that in the
construction of statutes, " a word importing the masculine
gender only may extend and be applied to females as well
as males ."
It is provided in the Tenement House Act , applicable to
citiesof the firstclass, that words in the masculinegender
include the feminine and neuter. Acts 1912, p . 573 ; Ку .
Stats. (1915), Sec. 3037g.
MASS .- Mass is " the sacrifice in the sacrament of the
eucharist, or the consecrationand the oblation of the host.
It is, as we understand it, a public service- a public act of
worship- by which, accordingto the tenetsof the Roman
Catholic Church , the priestwho celebratesit helps the living
and obtains rest for the dead." Coleman v . O'Leary's Exr .,
114 Ky . 402, 70 S. W. 1068 , 24 R. 1248.
MASSES OF MEN .- The " masses of men " means the
principal or main body . Commonwealth v . American Snuff
Co., 125 Ky . 354, 101 S. W. 364, 30 R. 1373.
MAST . When mast became ripeand fellon the ground, it
was no longer a part of the timber, and the right to cut and
remove the timber did not carry with it the independent right
to go on the premises and carry away the fallen mast .
Vincent v. Haycraft, 158 Ky . 845, 166 S. W. 613.
MASTER AND SERVANT .-See , also, SERVANT ; EMPLOYE ;
VOLUNTEER .
" The relationof master and servant is created by contract,
either express or implied. It imposes reciprocalrights, duties
and obligations. As a part of the duties growing out of this
contractualrelationthe law imposes on the master the obliga-
tion of providing reasonablysafe premises and reasonably safe
appliancesfor the use of the servant; and ifdelinquentin these
respects,and the servantisinjuredthereby,the mastermay be
compelledto respondin damages forhisbreachof duty. This
relationcan not be created by one of the parties. The assent

MASTER AND SER . 2112 MASTER AND SER .
of both, eitherexpress or implied, is necessary. A person can
not impose on another the responsibleobligationrestingupon a
master without the participationof such other person. The
law, in the absence of some contractualarrangement or agree-
ment , express or implied, willnot createthe relationof master
and servant." L. & N. R. Co. v . Pendleton, 126 Ky . 605, 104
S. W. 382,31 R. 1025.
-
In Ballard & Ballard Company v. Lee's Admr ., 131 Ky .
412, the Court of Appeals says: " If the owner or person
having the work done not only directswhat shallbe done but
how itshallbe done, and controlsthe laborersemployed upon
it, or, if the person engaged to do the work is at all times
subjectto the willof hisemployer,or can not properlyrefuse
to obey his directionsas to the mode in which the work shall
be done and the persons to be employed , the relationof master
and servant, and not that of an independent contractor, exists ,
although the employer should never exercisesuch control and
the employe should be paid by the job instead of by the day
or week." Madisonville, H. & E. R. Co. v. Owen , 147 Ку-
1, 143 S. W. 421.
The test of the relationshipof master and servant is the
right to control; it is not the fact of actual interferencewith
the control,but the rightto interferethat makes the difference
between an independentcontractorand a servantor agent-
Williams v. NationalCash RegisterCo., 157 Ky . 836, 164
S. W. 112 .
The real test by which to determine whether a person is
actingas the servantof another, isto ascertainwhether, at
thetime when the injurywas inflicted,he was subjectto such
person'sordersand control, and was liableto be discharged
by him fordisobedienceof orders,or misconduct. Employers"
IndemnityCo.v.Kelly CoalCo.,149 Ky .712,149 S. W. 992.
Whether or not the relation of master and servant exists
in a given case, is a questionof fact, or of mixed law and fact ,
and is to be proved, as any other likequestion; and, any
evidencetending to prove or disprove the relationshipis
admissible, itsweight and sufficiencybeing leftto the jury ,
under instructionsof the court. Employers' Indemnity Co. -
Kelly Coal Co., 149 Ky .712 , 149 S. W. 992 .

MASTER AND SER . 2113 MASTER AND SER .
Where plaintiffwas employed as a bookkeeper, his salary
being fixedat one-fourth of the net profitson goods sold, this
created a contract of employment merely, and not a contract
of partnership. Clarkev.Rogers, 159 Ky .762,169 S. W. 485.
A fatherisliablefor injuriesresultingto anotherfrom the
negligence of his son while driving the father'scarriage and
horses with his approbation; the son is regarded as in the
employ of his father. Lashbrook v . Patten, 1 Duv . 317 .
Where a head miner , employed in mining coal at a fixed
price per ton, employs a "helper" or assistant, who is paid
by the mine owner out of the money due the head miner, the
relationof master and servant existsbetween the " helper"
and the mine owner . Borderland Coal Co. v . Small, Admr .,
160 Ky .738,170 S. W. 8.
A painter who is employed by the job to do some painting
isnot a servant of the employer, and the latteris not answer-
able for the painter'sfailureto watch over an infant, when
he goes away and tellsthe painter to lock up when he is
through. Standard Oil Co. v . Marlow , 150 Ky . 647, 150
S. W. 832 .
The ruleisthata personwho isnot authorizedto perform
as a servant the work in which he is injured, can not recover
of the master if he is injured, damages for his injury, because
the master not having authorizedhim to act owes him no duty .
Central Ky .Traction Co. v .Miller, 147 Ky . 110, 143 S. W. 750.
A plumber doing work by contract is the servant of the
owner, when doing with his authoritywork outsidehis contract,
such as digging a hole in the sidewalk. Board of Councilmen v .
Vanarsdall, 148 Ky . 507, 147 S. W. 1.
The mode of payment is a circumstance in solving the
questionwhether the relationof master and servant exists, but
itisnotdecisiveof thatquestion. Employers'IndemnityCo.v.
Kelly Coal Co.,149 Ky .712, 149 S. W. 992.
Where a mining company employed R. to mine coal at a
givenpriceper ton, and R. employed C. to assisthim, and the
mining company , as an accommodationto R., paid C.'swages
out of R.'s money , the mode of payment did not make C. an
employe of the mining company within the meaning of an

MASTER AND SER . 2114 MASTER AND SER .
employer'sliabilitypolicywhich insuredthe mining company
againstdamage causedby injuryto itsemployes. Employers'
Indemnity Co. v .Kelly Coal Co., 149 Ky .712, 149 S. W. 992 .
He is to be deemed the master who has the superior choice,
control, and direction of the servant, and whose will the
servant represents, not merely in the ultimate result of his
work , but in allitsdetails. Robinson v. Webb , 11 Bush , 477 ;
quotedin CentralC. & I. Co. v.Grider'sAdmr ., 115 Ку . 755,
74 S. W. 1060, 25 R. 165, in which lattercase it is said that
the legal understanding of " master and servant" is broader
than the common understanding of the words .
Where a servant was employed by a railroadcompany as a
car inspectorand repairer, and without the directionor author-
ity of the master, volunteered to assistthe switching crew in
the yard , with a view to his own advancement in becoming
proficientas a brakeman and switchman, he couldnot thereby
create the relation of master and servant in such unauthorized
employment, and the mere knowledge of the agent in charge
of the businesswho saw him thus employed did not have the
effectof creatingby implicationof law the relationof master
and servant, or convert him from a volunteer into a servant.
L. & N. R. Co. v . Pendleton, 126 Ky . 605, 104 S. W. 382 , 31
R. 1025 .
It does not follow that because a servant is given an inter-
mission from work during the noon hour he ceases to be a
servant, so as to relievethe master from liabilityfor injuries
to him by a defect in the premises. Mitchell-Tranter Co. v .
Ehmett,65 S. W. 835,23 R. 1788.
Where a contract of employment is for an indefinitetime,
eitherpartyhas a rightto terminateitat any time he seesfit,
without notice or cause. Miller v. N. W. Ritter Lumber Co.,
110 S. W. 869, 33 R. 698.
The relation of master and servant does not exist between
an employer and the servants of an independent contractor.
Mason & Hoge Co. v .Highland , 116 S. W. 320.
The duty of a father to educate and maintain his minor son
entitles him to the son's services and creates the relation of
master and servantbetween them. L. & N. R. Co. v. Willis,
83 Ky . 57, 6 R. 784.

MASTER COMMIS . 2115 MASTER IN CHAN .
The employe is bound to treat the employer with respect,
to be faithfuland reasonably diligent, and to obey all reason-
able orders, within the scope of his employment , which may
be given by the employer or his agent, for the breach of which
the employer may dischargehim . Otis & Co. v . Power , 1 Ky .
Opin . 313.
The only basis of responsibilityof one for the neglect of
another is the relationof master and servant, the former being
responsiblefor the acts of the latter; and to incur the responsi-
bilitythe master must not only have the power to selectthe
servant or agent,but to directthe mode of executing the work ,
and so controlhim in his acts in the course of the employment
as to preventinjuryto others. Robinson v. Webb , 11 Bush,
473 .
MASTER COMMISSIONER .- A master commissioner is
not a State officeror a deputy State officer. Goodloe v . Fox ,
96 Ky . 627, 16 R. 653.
The officeof master commissioner and receiver is usually
united in one person. Ky . Stats., Sec. 409.
" The permanent master in chancery, known to the English
courts, is unknown here. We , under Act of Virginia (1 Dig .
L. K. 226 ), have substitutedin his place a commissioner or
auditorpro hac vice. The objectof the law in providingfor
the appointment of the commissioner is to save time of the
court; and it is his duty , when appointed, to do those things,
and those only, which are required of him by the order of
court, and , if he transcends the authority given him by the
court,his actsare nugatory." Ison v . Com ., 110 Ky . 117.
MASTER IN CHANCERY.- " The permanent master in
chancery, known to the English courts, is unknown here. We ,
under Act of Virginia (1 Dig . L. K. 226 ), have substitutedin
hisplacea commissioneror auditorpro hac vice. The object
of the law in providing for the appointment of the commis-
sioner is to save time of the court; and it is his duty, when
appointed,todo thosethings,and thoseonly,which are required
of him by the order of court,and ,if he transcendsthe authority
givenhim by the court,hisactsare nugatory." Ison v. Com .,
110 Ky . 117.

MATERIAL ALLEG . 2116 MATERIAL ALTER .
MATERIAL ALLEGATION .--" A material allegationis one
which isnecessaryfor the statementor supportof a causeof
actionor defense." CivilCode , Sec. 127.
The Code provisionthat all material allegationswhich are
not denied by a responsiblepleading are taken as true, has no
applicationto immaterial or impertinent allegations. Diuguid
v .Roberts, 121 S. W. 464.
By Sec. 126 of the Code every material allegationof a
pleading, with certainexceptions, must be taken as true unless
speciallytraversed. This provisionof the Code refersonly to
affirmativeallegations. There is no requirement of the Code
that a traversein a rejoinderwhich makes up an issuemust
be responded to. L. & N. R. Co. v . Cox , 145 Ky . 667, 141
S. W. 389 .
A material allegationis one essentialto the claim or defense ,
and which could not be stricken from the pleading without
leavingit insufficient. Barret v . Godshaw , 12 Bush , 598.
MATERIAL ALTERATION .- Of Negotiable Instruments .
" Where a negotiable instrument is materially altered with-
out,theassentof allpartiesliablethereon,itisavoided, except
as againsta party who has himselfmade , authorizedor assented
to the alterationand subsequent endorsers. But when an
instrument has been materiallyalteredand is in the hands of a
holder in due course, not a party to the alteration, he may
enforcepayment thereofaccordingto itsoriginaltenor. Any
alterationwhich changes: (1) The date. (2) The sum payable,
eitherforprincipalor interest. (3) The time or placeof pay-
ment . (4) The number of or the relationsof the parties.
(5 ) The medium or currency in which payment is to be made .
Or which adds a place of payment where no place of payment
is specified, or any other change or addition which altersthe
effectof the instrument in any respect,isa materialalteration. "
Negotiable Inst. Law , Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec. 3720b, Subsecs-
124, 125. Tyler v. First NationalBank , 150 Ky . 515, 150
S. W. 665 .
Negotiable Instruments Law (Laws 1904, Chap . 102), Secs-
124 and 125, declaring the effectof material alterationsand

MATERIAL ALTER . 2117 MATERIAL ALTER .
defining what shall constitute such alterations, is merely
declaratoryof the law merchant . Diamond DistilleriesCo. v .
Gott, 137 Ky . 585, 126 S. W. 131.
The insertionof the word " fixed" after the figuresdesig-
nating the date of maturity of a note was not a materialaltera-
tion, where the note was never placed on the footing of a bill
of exchange. Tranter v. Hibberd ,56 S. W. 169, 21 R. 1710.

MEANING OF LAW . The law,in itsregardfor thepublic
good, goes so far, in some cases, as to hold that communis error
facitjus; but courtsshouldbe slow to setup a misconception
of the law as the law , and there is no need of it in this

opinioin construingit. The objectof constructionis to give

the law . In the language of the Supreme Court of the United
States, in the case of the United Statesv .Moore , 95 U. S., 763,
" a thing may be within the letterof a statute, and not within

within the letter," and the meaning and not the lettermust
control. Harrison v . Com ., 83 Ky . 171 .

" In Campbell v. Preece, 133 Ky . 572, afterquoting the

One who has mind enough to understand the nature and

MENTIONED .- Testatordied in 1900. His daughter and

that thiswillbe the same , or that she have fullcontrolof all

Bean , and soon thereafterdied. The infantchild, Caroline,
by her guardian,set up claim to testator'sland, on the ground
that she was not born at the time this will was written, and
was not provided for or mentioned in the will. Held , that
under Ky . Stats., Secs. 4842 and 4848 , the appellant, Caroline,

712 , 87 S. W. 1110, 27 R. 1081.

MERGER .- An order for trialof severalcases together
does not have the effectof merging the several actions into
one. Its only effectis that the suitsbe tried together. Each
case retainsitsdistinctivecharacteristicsand the judgment in
each caseisseveral. And if the proceedingsand judgment in

MERGER MERGER2152
eitherof the cases are erroneous, such error must be fatal to
that judgment , although the others may be free from error.
It is within the discretionof the trialjudge at what time to
submit each case to the jury. Where the defenses in the
several cases are different it is erroneous to order them to be
triedtogether. But error in ordering a trialof separatecauses
together is harmless if the final result reached was right.
Benge's Admr . v . Fouts, 163 Ky . 796, 174 S. W. 510.
While an account against several may be merged by the
creditorsaccepting in satisfactionthereof the note of one of
the debtors, yet where he accepts the note which purports
to be the note of the two debtors, when in fact it is void as to
one, that isno merger . Brannin v .Brawner ,8 R. 434 ,9 R. 343.
Where two persons jointly owned two adjoining lots, and
the building upon one lot, and subsequentlybought the build-
ing upon the adjoining lot, there was no such merger of two
separatelyowned propertiesinto a singleownership that would
put an end to an easement attached to the lastnamed building,
by which it had the right to use the wall of the firstnamed
building. Ringgold Lodge v.DeKalb Lodge, 157 Ky . 203,162
S. W. 1111 .
A merger will not resultwhere it is the expressed intention
of the one acquiring the greater estate that his acquisition
thereof shall not operate to extinguish the lesserestateheld
by him , or where in the absence of such expression the
circumstances surrounding the transactionshow that it was
not his purpose that the merger should result. Not only will
there be no merger where the intention is that there shall be
none, but where there isan outstanding or interveninginterest
or equity the merger will not be declared. Wiedemann v.
Crawford , 158 Ky .657 ,166 S. W. 185.
The recitalin a deed or any writing of a parol agreement
previouslymade is a covenant, and merges the parol contract.
Colyer v. Jackson, 3 Mon. 23. But a writing merely to
recognizethat a debt existsand containing a provision as to
the mode of liquidatingor adjusting the balance, whether
sealedor not, isnot a merger. Smith v.Morrison, 3 Mar. 82.

METHODIST EPISCOPAL CHURCH .- In the division of

MIGHT . In an actionfor assaulton a passenger by fellow-
