mortgage . Three Forks Lumber Co. v . Smith , 32 S. W. 167,
17 R. 566 .
A writing purporting to be a billof sale should be treated
as a chattel mortgage where it appears that such was the
intention. Boli v. Irwin, 51 S. W. 444, 21 R. 366.
A writing purporting to be a billof sale, but reservingto
the sellerthe right to take possessionof and sellthe property
in case the purchaser shallmake default as to payments , will
be enforced as a chattelmortgage . D. H. Baldwin & Co. v .
Owen , 51 S. W. 438, 21 R. 352.
A writing executed by the buyer of a monument purporting
to resellitto the seller, but providingthatif the buyer shall
cancel was extended by written agreement. The value of the
property was largelyin excessof the nominal consideration,
and before the grantee sued for possessionthe grantor tendered
to him allhe claimed, including ten per cent. interest. Held ,
that the deed willbe treatedas a mortgage , and the mortgagee
allowed to recover only the debt with legal interest. Allen v .
Brown , 62 S. W. 726, 23 R. 217.
A written contract between B. and P. whereby B. agreed
to advance to P. $8 on every one thousand feet of lumber of
a certain descriptiondeliveredon the bank of C. river, a lien

MORTGAGE MORTGAGE2198
being given to B. by the contractto secure the money advanced,
with the stipulationthat if any of the lumber should be
shipped to market for saleitwas to be shipped for the account
of B., did not constitutea mortgage , though recorded, but was
a contractfor a pledge which was valid, though a part of the
lumber was not then in existence, no lien attaching until
delivery to B., actual or constructive, upon the bank of the
river. Bogard v . Tyler'sAdmr ., 55 S. W. 709, 21 R. 1452.
A mortgage on all the lands the mortgagor holds " on the
Dry Fork of Otter Creek " isnot void for uncertainty. Albert-
son v . Prewitt,49 S. W. 196, 20 R. 1309.
The power conferred on a corporation to contract debts
impliesthe power to mortgage itsproperty. Bell.& Coggeshall
Co. v .Ky .Glass Works Co., 50 S. W. 2, 20 R. 1684 ; modifying
opinion, 48 S. W. 440, 20 R. 1089.
Although under the National Banking Act national banks
are not authorized to take mortgages upon real estate, they
may be substitutedto the rights of suretieswho have taken
such mortgages. Magoffin v . Boyle Nat . Bank , 69 S. W. 702 ,
24 R. 585 .
A mortgage executed to secure an account, without any
covenant therein to pay , is a mere incident to the demand ,
and can not stand upon the footing of a written obligation
to pay a debt. Prewitt v . Wortham , 79 Ку . 287.
Contracts of sale, intended originally as securities, are
to be treated as mortgages. Fenwick v. Macey , 1 Dana , 277.
Mortgages are regarded in equity as securitiesfor money ,
and as such are assignable. They pass to the personal repre-
sentative, and not to the heir, and by an assignment of the
principal debt the mortgage also pases. Barnes v . Lee , 1
Bibb , 526.
" We know that the terms surety and security are some-
times used as synonymous , and it may be admitted as true
that a bond or obligationwith surety is a security, for the
latter term includes it, as well as mortgages, pledges, or
whatever elsemay be given, conveyed, or depositedto secure the
payment of a debt or the performance of a contract. But it

MORTGAGE MORTGAGE2199
is manifest that the rights and obligationspertaining to the
latterkinds of securitiesare essentiallydifferentfrom those
which are acquired and incurred by the act of a party in
becoming the surety of another in an ordinary bond or
obligation. That description of security is clearly compre-
hended by the provisionof the statutereferredto, both by the
apt and appropriate language used, and the connection of the
sectionwith other provisionsof the same chapter relatingto
suretiesand co-obligors. But we can not conclude that the
Legislatureintended by the use of the word surety that the
limitationit provided should be so applied as to affectthe
rights of creditors holding mortgages or pledges made or
created according to laws governing such securitiesinstead
of personal security for their debts." Hobson v . Hobson's
Executor , 8 Bush , 667.
A mortgage given in good faith for a specifiedsum , as
security for future advancements , is valid to the extent of
the amount specified. Straefferv . Rodman , 146 Ky . 1, 141
S. W. 742 .
Mortgage Distinguishedfrom ConditionalSale. The dis-
tinctionbetween a mortgage and conditionalsaleis well known
to law, and to ascertainto which classa contractbelongs, the
court will look at the intention of the parties and other
extraneouscircumstances. Reed v. Lansdale, Hard . 6 ; Prince
v . Reardon , 1 Mar. 170 ; Prather v. Norflet, 1 Mar. 178 ;
Flowers v . Sproule, 2 Mar. 57.
When the debt forming the considerationof the contract
stillsubsistsor the money is advanced by way of loan, with a
personalliabilityon thepart of the borrowerto repay, and by
the terms of the agreement the property is to be reconveyed on
payment of the money , it will be regarded as a mortgage .
Reed v . Lansdale, Hard . 6 ; Honore v . Hutchings , 8 Bush , 695.
In determining whether a contract is a conditional sale
or a mortgage the form of the conveyance is unimportant .
Generally whenever a conveyance or other instrument trans-
ferring an estateis originallyintended as securityfor money ,
whether the intention appears from the same instrument or

MORTGAGE MORTGAGE2200
from any other,itisconsideredin equitya mortgage. Baldwin
v . Crow , 86 Ky . 679,9 R. 836.
A conveyance absoluteon itsface, executed by a mortgagor
to the mortgagee at the maturity and for the amount of the
debt, and an agreement executedat the same time by the
grantee allowing the grantor twelve months within which to
repurchasefor the amount of the debt and interest, and
providing that on his failureto do so he is to become the
grantee's tenant for a stated period at a specifiedrent, con-
stitutea conditionalsale and not a mortgage . Tygert v .
Potter,16 R. 809.
A note for a mule stipulated" that the mule is bound or
the titleof the mule remains in the vendor until he gets his
money ." Held, thatitwas a conditionalsaleand not a bail-
ment , if the possessionwas delivered. As between the parties,
vendor could recover the property upon the failureof vendee
to pay the price. Vaughn v.Hopson , 10 Bush , 338.
A conditionalsale, without change of possession, is void
against a purchaser without notice. Townsend v . Frazer,
54 S. W. 722, 21 R. 1183.
" The distinctionbetween a conditionalsale and a mortgage
as drawn by Greenleaf is that 'where the debt forming the
considerationof the conveyance stillsubsists, or the money is
advanced by way of a loan, with a personal liabilityon the
part of the borrower to repay it, and by the terms of the
agreement the land is to be reconveyed on payment of the
money , it will be regarded as a mortgage ; but, where the
relationof debtorand creditorisextinguished,or never existed,
there a similar agreement will be considered as merely a
conditionalsale.' " Tucker v. Witherbee, 130 Ky . 275, 113
S. W. 123, quoting from Honore v. Hutchings , 8 Bush , 687.
While partiescompetent to contract can agree upon terms
that will amount to a conditional sale, where a deed is
intended to secure a debt, and the relation of debtor and
creditorexistsbetween the parties, the legal inferenceis that
a mortgage and not a conditionalsale was intended; courts
being inclinedin doubtful cases to treat a transactionas a

MORTGAGE MORTGAGE2201
mortgage rather than a conditionalsale. Tucker v . Witherbee,
130 Ky . 269, 113 S. W. 123 .
Where the nature of the conveyance is doubtful, it will
be considereda mortgage and not a conditionalsale. Fulwiler
v.Roberts,80 S. W. 1148, 26 R. 297.
The doctrineof treatinga conditionalsale as a mortgage is
a creatureof equity and will not be applied when the parties
have unreasonably sleptupon their rights. Sheffieldv . Hurst ,
104 S. W. 350, 31 R. 890.
In all cases where it is doubtful whether the transaction is
a mortgage or conditional sale the chancellor will rather
construe it a mortgage . Thompson v . Campbell , 6 Mon. 121 ;
Secrest v. Turner , 2 J. J. M. 472 ; Edrington v . Harper , 3
J. J. M. 354; Bishop v. Rutledge, 7 J. J. M. 217; Bright v.
Wagle, 3 Dana , 253;Honore v.Hutchings, 8 Bush, 694.
A contractof salestipulatingthat the titleshallremain in
the selleruntil the price is paid is a mortgage , and is not
valid againstpurchasers for value or creditorsunless recorded.
Wender Blue Gem Coal Co. v. LouisvilleProperty Co., 137
Ky .339,125 S. W. 732.
Pledge or Mortgage. A pledge is a thing pawned , to
remain in possessionof the pawnee untilthe money is repaid,
in which the pawnee has only a specialproperty, the title
yet remaining in the pawnor . A mortgage gives a general
property to the mortgagee, to become absolute upon the
failureof mortgagor to perform the conditions. Hamilton v .
Wagner , 2 Mar. 334. But see (1877), Bartlettv. Borden, 13
Bush , 45 .
" The distinguishingdifferencebetween a mortgage , and a
pawn or pledge, is that, by the former the general property
passes; by the latterit does not, but the pawnee has only a
specialproperty. A mortgage isa conveyance of the titleupon
condition, and it becomes an absoluteinterestat law, if not
redeemed by a given time. A pledge or pawn is a deposit of
goods upon terms, either with or without a fixed period for
redemption. Deliveryof possessionmust accompany a pledge
or pawn ,and isessentialtoitsvalidity. Itneed not accompany

MORTGAGE MORTGAGE2202
a mortgage , in the general, but may , and sometimes does do
so." Lobban v. Garnett, 9 Dana , 390.
Mortgage is Mere Security for Debt. A mortgage is a
mere securityfor debt. The legal titleat law and in equity
is in the mortgagor during the life of the mortgage . His
right to use and occupy the premises carrieswith it the right
to the proceeds of such use untilhe is divestedof the titleor
the rents are subjected, as provided in Sec. 299 of the Code .
Woolley v . Holt, 14 Bush , 788 ; Taliaferrov . Gay , 78 Ky . 496.
A mortgage is a mere securityfor debt, and substantially,
both in law and in equity, the mortgagor is the real owner
of the property mortgaged . Mercantile Trust Co. v . South
Park Residence Co.,94 Ky . 271, 15 R. 70 ; Cumber v . Chandler,
5 R. 185 .
A mortgage is a mere security, and both at law and in
equity the mortgagor is the real owner of the property.
Guill'sAdmrs .v . Corinth Dep .Bank , 68 S. W. 870, 24 R. 482 .
At common law a mortgage was a saleof the property with
the right of redemption within a named time, and if the
redemption did not take place within the time fixed in the
instrument the mortgagor losthis property absolutely. Equity
relievedthe hardship of this rule by allowing the mortgagor
to redeem afterthe time named in the instrument; and there-
fore it was necessary, in order for the mortgagee to enforce
his rights under the deed, for him to foreclosein chancery.
The mortgagee now has only a lienfor his debt, and a deed of
mortgage has long since ceased to be a conveyance of title.
Rissberger v. City of Louisville, 118 S. W. 319.
Mortgagor Remains Owner . The old doctrinethat the
mortgagee is the owner of the estate has long since been
exploded. He merely holds the titleas security and for a
particularpurpose, and the mortgagor remains the owner and
is prima facie entitled to the possession until foreclosure.
Bartlettv .Borden , 13 Bush ,45 .
Sale and Mortgage Back . A mortgage stipulatingthat
the titleshould remain in the vendor of personal property,
and that the vendor might take possessionat any time, if any

MORTGAGE MORTGAGE2203
installmentof the purchase money was not paid, passes the
titleto the vendee, and only gives a lien on the property,
under the statutesof Kentucky . Singer Sewing Machine Co. v .
Dyer , 156 Ky . 156, 160 S. W. 917.
In such case a clause providing against the passing of the
titleto the buyer, is invalid; the titlepassed to the buyer ,
and the transactionwillbe treated as a sale, and a mortgage
back to the seller, with a lienfor the purchase price. Barney
& Smith Mfg . Co. v . Hart , Receiver, 8 R 227 ; Greer v .
Church , 13 Bush , 430 ; Baldwin v . Crow , 86 Ky . 682 ; Wicks
Bros. v . McConnell , 102 Ky . 435, 438 ; Aultman v . Meade , 121
Ky . 241 ; Wender Blue Gem Coal Co. v . LouisvilleProperty
Co., 137 Ky . 347 ; Singer Sewing Machine Co. v . Dyer , 156
Ky . 159; Montenegro-Riehm Music Co. v. Beuris, 160 Ky .
557 , 169 S. W. 986 .
Agreement that Title Shall Remain in Seller Until Price
isPaid. A contractforthe saleof personalpropertywhereby
it is agreed that the titleshall remain in the selleruntil the
price ispaid in full is only a mortgage , and must be recorded
as such to affect an innocent purchaser of the property.
Welch v . Nat . Cash Reg . Co., 103 Ky . 30, 44 S. W. 124, 19
R. 1664 .
Propertyto be Acquired, The generalruleis, thatat law
a grant or mortgage of property to be acquired in future is
void; and if it can be upheld in equity it is only valid as a
contractto assignwhen the property shallbe acquired, and not
an assignment of a present interest; and if enforcible in
equity at all, it can only be enforced as a right under the
contract and not as a trust attached to the property. Ross v .
Wilson , 7 Bush , 32 ; Vinson v . Hallowell. 10 Bush , 538 .
Duress . The rule is that a mortgage is not invalidated
by force or duress exercisedby one of the mortgagors upon
another, not participated in by the mortgagee. Pribble v .
Hall , 76 Ky . 61 ; Davis v . Jenkins, 93 Ky . 353 ; Tackitt v .
Tackitt, 127 S. W. 987 ; J. M. Robinson, Norton & Co. v .
Randall , 147 Ky . 45, 143 S. W. 769.
Indemnity. A mortgage, in the usualform, purportingto
secure the payment of a sum certain, may be shown to have

MORTGAGE CER . 2204 MORTIFICATION
been givenonlyfor thepurposeof indemnifyingthe mortgagee
againstloss. Allen v. Shepherd, 162 Ky . 756, 173 S. W. 135.
Acceptance. Acceptance of mortgage is essentialto its
validity. Bell v . Farmers Bank , 11 Bush , 41.
Delivery. Deliveryisessentialto thevalidityof a mortgage.
Sec. 11, Chap. 24, Revised Statutes, does not dispensewith
delivery. Bell v. Farmers Bank , 11 Bush , 41.
Payment of Debt - Effect. Payment of the debt is an
extinguishmentof themortgage. Hawkins v.King,2 Mar. 109.
MORTGAGE
PLEDGES .
CERTIFICATES OF DEPOSIT .- See
MORTGAGE OR LIEN OF ANY NATURE .— Where pre-
ferredstock is issuedunder a stipulationin the certificate
that " no mortgage or lienof any nature" shallbe placed upon
the property of the company without the unanimous consent
of the preferred stockholders, the words " mortgage or lien of
any nature" do not include claims of general creditors.
Fryer v . Wiedemann , 148 Ky . 379, 146 S. W. 752.
MORTGAGED .- Where a personleasesland for a definite
period, and has a clause in the lease that he may purchase
the property at any time before the expirationof the lease, the
interestacquired under it- the leaseand the option- may be
mortgaged under the statute, which provides " that any
interestin or claim to real estate may be disposed of by
deed or will." Bank of Louisvillev . Baumeister, 87 Ky . 6 ;
9 R. 845 .
MORTIFICATION AND HUMILIATION.- Where a passen-
ger is wrongfully put off the train at the wrong station,
whether the action be maliciousor wanton or not, he must be
compensatedby paying him such sum as willfairlyrepresent
his physicaland mental suffering, caused by the wrongful act.
Mortificationand humiliationare always regarded as elements
of damage in such cases, though there be no warrant for the
assessment of punitive damages . Tennessee Cent . R. Co. v.
Brasher , 97 S. W. 349,29 R. 1277.
Where it is alleged that a tenant was evicted against his
willand consentand in a high-handed manner, being thereby

MORTMAIN MOTION2205
put to great trouble, annoyance and distress, it was proper to
authorize a recovery for mortificationand humiliation. The
word distressis a generic term, and includesanguish or suffer-
ing, both of mind and body . Humiliation and mortification
are simply phases of mental anguish, and under the allegation
of distress, or mental anguish, any phase of mental anguish
could be proven, and a recovery had therefor. Perkins v .
Ogilvie, 148 Ky . 309, 146 S. W. 735.
MORTMAIN .- Such a stateof ownershipof land as makes
it inalienable(whence it is said to be in a dead hand ). This
ariseswhen land becomes the property of a corporation, which
has a continuous existence. To prevent this, Statutes of Mort-
main have been passed in England , and many of the United
States, restrictingor prohibitingthegiftof landsin perpetuity
to religiousand charitablecorporations.
The statuteof mortmain has never been in forcein Kentucky .
Moore v . Moore , 4 Dana , 356 ; Lathrop v . Commercial Bank
of Scioto,8 Dana , 121.
MOSES .- Deliveryof law to Moses referredto in opinion
in Commonwealth v.I.C. R. Co.,160 Ky .756.
MOSQUITO BITE .- The declaration of the insured to his
attendingphysicianthattheinjurysubsequentlycausingdeath
was the result of a mosquito bite, is competent. Omberg v .
U. S. Mut .Assn ., 101 Ky . 303, 40 S. W. 909, 19 R. 462 .
MOST APPROVED . It was misleading to instruct the
jury that itwas the duty of defendant to provide itslocomotive
with a spark-arrester " most approved by those who , from
experience and business, are most competent to judge and
determine," as thelaw onlyrequiresthebestand most effectual
appliance in general use. L. & N. R. Co. v . Samuel's Exrs .,
57 S. W. 235, 22 R. 303.
MOTION . “An applicationfor an order is a motion."
Civil Code , Sec . 623 .
" A motion or rule of court shallbe considered an action;
but when made in a pending suit or action an attorney'sfee
shall not be recovered as a part of the costsof the motion or
rule." Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec. 898.

MOTION MOTION2206
A motion, unless it be such an one as arisesincidentally
in the prosecutionof a suit, is to allintentsand purposesan
action. It differsfrom other actionsonly in being conducted
ore tenus. There must be in it as well as in other actions,
actor reus et judex. Richardson v . Talbot, 2 Bibb , 384.
The forfeitureof a peace bond on motion is not authorized
by law . The motions allowed by Sec. 444, Civil Code , do not
include motions on peace bonds. Such remedy can be had
only by an ordinary civilaction. Combs v . Com ., 71 S. W.
504 , 24 R. 1310 .
A motion is a suit, and non -residentsare bound to give
securityfor costsas plaintiffsin other suits, or they may be
abated. Canterberry v . Smith, 5 J. J. M. 225
Motions against sheriffsand their suretiesfor failure to
return executionsmay be tried without pleadings in writing.
Pogue v . Lusk , 6 J. J. M. 579.
It is not necessary in motions to observe the same technical
strictnesswhich is required in pleading. Logan v . Steele,
7 J. J. M. 42 .
The filingof a motion for a new trialin the clerk'soffice
within three days after verdict is not sufficient. The motion
must be made in court within that time. Klein v. Meyers,
68 S. W. 144 , 24 R. 183 .
Under Sec. 449 ,CivilCode , providing that in casesin which
judgment be obtained on motion , " the motion may be heard
and determined upon or without written pleadings," the
question whether the pleadings shall be in writing or not is
addressed to the discretionof the court. Borches v. Bellis,
62 S. W. 486 , 23 R. 37 .
The giving of a remedy by motion does not exclude the
common -law action. Hamilton. v . Com ., 3 Mon. 213.
Motions againstofficersfor failureto return executions, etc.,
must contain all the requisitesof a petition. Johnson v .
Bradley , 11. Bush , 667.
In motion by purchaser of land sold under execution for
a writof possessionas providedin Sec.9,Art.12, Chap.38,
General Statutes, the noticetakes the place of the petition, and

MOTION DOCKET 2207 MOTION IN ARREST
must set out a state of facts entitlingplaintiffto the relief
demanded . Bunnell v . Thompson , 12 Bush , 117 .
In motions against sheriff, etc., or in motions by sheriff
againsthis deputy and sureties, notice to a common intent is
sufficient. Bradley v. Tompkins, Hard . 135; Mars v. Buckler,
1 Bibb , 267 ; Kennedy v . Coleman , 2 Litt. 6 ; Martin v . Hardin
Justices, 6 J. J. M. 7.
The court decides both law and fact in the remedies by
motion . Johnson v . Thompson ,4 Bibb , 294 .
MOTION DOCKET . “ On the motion docket may be
entered any motion relating to proceedings in the court."
Civil Code , Sec. 352.
" The entry on the motion docket shall show the date of the
entry and the objectof the motion." CivilCode , Sec. 355.
MOTION FOR NEW TRIAL .-The entry of a motion for
a new trialsuspends the judgment , and the judgment does
not become final until the motion for new trial is overruled .
Leonard's Admr .v. Cowling, 87 S. W. 812, 27 R. 1059.
Prior to the amendment of Criminal Code , Sec. 281, by the
Act of 1910, a ruling of the Circuit Court in refusing a new
trialin a criminal case could not be reviewed by the Court of
Appeals , but, as amended , that sectionnow permits any error
of the Circuit Court in refusing a new trialto be reviewed
on appeal. (Acts 1910, page 269.) Gleason v. Com ., 145 Ку .
128 , 140 S. W. 63 ; Wilson v . Com ., 140 Ky .1, 130 S. W. 794 ;
Tucker v . Com ., 145 Ky . 84, 140 S. W. 73. See, also, NEW
TRIAL .
MOTION IN ARREST OF JUDGMENT .- " A motion in
arrestof judgment is an application, on the part of the defend-
ant, that no judgment be rendered upon a verdictagainsthim ,
or on a plea of guilty." Crim . Code , Sec. 275.
" The onlyground upon which a judgment shallbe arrested
is that the facts stated in the indictment do not constitute a
public offensewithin the jurisdictionof the court."
Code , Sec. 276.
Crim.

MOTION IN ARREST 2208 MOTION IN ARREST
In civilcases the motion in arrest of judgment has been
supersededand replacedby the motion for a judgment not-
withstanding the verdict, and this must be made before the
judgment is entered. Wheeler v. Preston, 107 S. W. 274,
32 R. 791 .
" A motion in arrest of judgment is an applicationon the
part of the defendant, that no judgment be rendered upon a
verdict againsthim , or on a plea of guilty." Criminal Code ,
Sec. 275. " The only ground upon which a judgment shallbe
arrested is that the facts stated in the indictment do not
constitutea public offensewithin the jurisdictionof the court."
Criminal Code , Sec. 276.
Although the alleged offensemay be defectivelystated in
the indictment so as to render it bad on demurrer , still, if,
considering the facts stated in the indictment to be true, a
public offensehas been committed by the defendant, within the
jurisdictionof the court, a motion in arrestof judgment will
not avail; the defectis waived by failureto demur . Duncan v .
Com ., 165 Ky . 247, 176 S. W. 984.
It is a well-settledrule of law that the motive of a litigant
can not affecthis legal rights, since his rights are to be
determined by the facts and the law , regardlessof his good
or bad motive in assertingthem . Broadway Coal Mining Co. v .
Robinson, 150 Ky . 707; I. C. R. Co. v. Outland'sAdmx ., 160
Ky . 714, 170 S. W. 48.
Motive and intent with which an act is done is a matter
of presumption depending on the nature of the act and cir-
cumstancesattendingits commission. Chrisman v. Bruce, 1
Duv . 68 .
Where theplaintiffissuijuristhe defendantcan not ques-
tion or deny his right to prosecute any lawful cause of action
which he may have. Whether plaintiffwas assertinghis cause
of action in compliance with the request of or under the dom-
inationof some one elseis immaterial. Diuguid v. Roberts,
121 S. W. 464 .
If one with a bad motive exercisehis rightin a lawful
way and injuryresults, thereisdamage withoutlegalinjury;

MOTIVE FOR CRIME 2209 MOTOR VEHICLES
but if with a bad motive he exercisehis rightsin an unlawful
manner, there is both damage and legalinjury. Avery v.
Meikle, 81 Ky .73 , 4 R. 759.
A judgment obtained by fraud may be void under some cir-
cumstances. But if a party has a legal right to have a judg-
ment of a particulartenor entered in an action of which the
court has rightfuljurisdiction, as well as jurisdictionof the
parties, itwillnot be inquiredinto, in a collateralproceeding,
what motive prompted the successfullitigant. A legal right
is to be exercised regardlessof motive. Disman v . Flippin's
Admx ., 116 S. W. 740 .
When the exerciseof authorityby a citycouncilis within
its power , the motives that influencedit will not be inquired
into, except in rare cases, where it is manifest that a flagrant
wrong has been perpetrated upon the public, and valuable
rightshave been surrenderedostensiblyfor the publicgood,
but really for the benefit of private individuals. Lutes v .
Fayette Home Telephone Co., 155 Ky . 555, 160 S. W. 179.
MOTIVE FOR CRIME . It is competent for the Common-
wealth to prove the existenceof a motive for the commission
of the crime, and this may be shown by the existence of
affidavits, warrantsof arrest, and otherpapers in the posses-
sion of the deceased. Ellisv . Com ., 146 Ky . 715, 143 S. W.
425 .
The word " motive," as used in the instructions, means
inducement , reason, cause or incentive to do the acts and
things charged in the indictment, if they or any of them were
done . Ellisv. Com ., 146 Ky . 715 , 143 S. W. 425.
To show motive, facts may be proved though they involve
the commission of another offense. Welch v. Com ., 108 S. W.
863 , 33 R. 51 .
While motive is an essentialelement of crime, it may be
inferred, and it is not necessary to show a bad motive to
secure or uphold a conviction. Rains v . Com , 92 S. W. 276,
29 R. 66 .
MOTOR VEHICLES . It isprovidedin the Motor Vehicle
Law of 1914 that : " Whenever the term motor vehicle shall

MOVEABLE PROP . 2210 MULATTO
be used in thisAct it shall include automobiles, locomobiles,
motorcycles, and allother vehiclespropelled otherwisethan by
muscular power , except common bicycles, traction engines,
road rollers, electricand steam railways." [Acts 1914, p .
179.] Ky Stats. 1915, Sec. 2739 .
MOVEABLE PROPERTY .- The civiliansdivided property
in moveable and immoveable . Land and chattelsreal, they
denominated" immoveable;" and personalchattels, they called
" moveable." Sneed v . Ewing , 5 J. J. Mar. 480 .
The words " movable property" in a will, when standing
alone or unqualified, should be interpretedto mean property
which may move or be moved ; they embrace money and bonds
for money , but not a debt merely as such. Wood v . George,
6 Dana , 343.
MOVEABLES .- A devise of all " moveables" passes all
chattelspersonal. A devise of all " immoveables" passes the
land and all chattelsreal. Sneed v . Ewing , 5 J. J. Mar.
480 .
MOVED AND JERKED .—-Defendant can not complain of
an instructiontellingthe jury that plaintiffwas entitledto
recover if the servants in charge of the train " moved and
jerked" same whileplaintiffwas attemptingto alight, as the
court by other instructionstold the jury that plaintiffcould
not recover unless defendant failed to hold the train long
enough to enable her to alightin safety, or moved it knowing
that plaintiffwas attempting to alight. L. & N. R. Co. v.
Harmon , 64 S. W. 640, 23 R. 871.
MULAGE . Master held not liablefor libelby his servant
in charging another servant with " mulage ." Case v . Steel
Coal Co., 162 Ky . 68, 171 S. W. 993 .
MULATTO.- Who is a negro, mulatto, or person of color
within statutes not specificallydefining same . See note on
thissubjectin 31 L. R. A. (N.S.) 180.
A mulatto was held to be a person having at least one-
fourthAfricanblood. Gentry v. McMinnis, 3 Dana, 385.

MULE 2211 MUNICIPAL CORP .
MULE . It is provided in Ky . Stats, Secs. 446, 456, that
in the construction of statutes " cattle" includes mule .
It is a matter of common knowledge that a mule is prone
to kick. Tolin v . Terrell, 133 Ky . 210, 117 S. W. 290.
It is contributorynegligence to go behind a mule , without
warning to the mule , to pick up lines for the purpose of
placing them across the mule . Tolin v. Terrell, 133 Ky . 210,
117 S. W. 290 .
In an action by a servant for injuriesfrom a kick by a
mule , the court properly instructedthe jury that if the mule
was dangerous and vicious, and was known by the defendant
to be such, or if by the use of ordinary care this might have
been known by itor itsservants, they should findfor the plain-
tiff; but if plaintiffknew before he was kicked by the mule that
it was dangerous and liableto kick and injure him , and after
thisvoluntarilywent on haulingwith the mule, he assumed
the risk, and they should find for the defendant. East Jellico
Coal Co. v .Stewart, 68 S. W. 624, 24 R. 420 .
MULTA NON VETAT .— Multa non vetat lex, quae tamen
tacitedamnavit . The law does not forbid many things which
yet it has silentlycondemned . Cochran's Law Lexicon.
MULTITUDO IMPERITORUM PERDIT CURIAM .- A mul-
titude of ignorant practitionersdestroys a court. Cochran's
Law Lexicon .
MULTIPLICITY OF SUITS .- There is a broad distinction
between invoking the jurisdictionof a court of equity to pre-
vent a multiplicityof suitsby differentplaintiffseachhaving a
separate,distinct,meritoriouscauseof action,and invokingthis
jurisdictionto preventa multiplicityof suitsby plaintiffswho
have not legallyenforceabledemands against the defendant.
In the one case the plaintiffsmight be required to asserttheir
claims in one suit, while in the otherthey would not. I. C.
R. Co. v . Baker , 155 Ky . 512, 159 S. W. 1169.
MUNICIPAL CORPORATIONS . A municipal corporation
has a double character. In one it acts strictlyin its govern-
mental capacity. In the other for the profitor convenience

MUNICIPAL CORP . 2212 MUNICIPAL CORP .
of itscitizens. Consideredin the latterlight, it occupiesthe
attitudeof a private corporationmerely, while in the former
itis an arm of the State government , or a part of its political
power . It is an imperium in imperio. The property necessary
to the exerciseof these duties which are strictlygovernmental
is exempt from taxation, but thisis not so of that which is
held by the municipality for the comfort of its citizens, indi-
vidually or collectively, or for money -making purposes merely .
Clark v . LouisvilleWater Co., 90 Ky . 522.
The word " municipality," as used in the Kentucky Con-
stitution, coversnot merely incorporatedvillages, towns and
cities, but counties and taxing districts. A board of educa-
tion is a municipality. Brown v . Board of Education , 108
Ky . 788 , 57 S. W. 613.
A city is a public instrumentality, and neither its charters
nor any Act regulating the use of property held by it for
governmental or public purposes, is a contract, within the
meaning of the Constitutionof the United States. Covington
v.Kentucky , 173 U. S. 231.
One contractingwith a city must , at his peril, inquire into
the authority of the city or its officersto make the contract,
and the fact that the city accepts the servicesor materials
and receivesthe benefitsof the contract, will not render it
liableon an implied contract to pay the same . Worrell Mfg .
Co. v .City of Ashland ,159 Ky . 656, 167 S. W. 922 .
It is well settledthat a cityis not liablefor injuriescaused
by an obstructionin itsstreetunless itknew , or by the exer-
ciseof ordinary care could have known , of the existenceof the
obstruction. Hazelrigg v . The Board of Council, 92 S. W. 584,
29 Ky . L. R. 207 ; City of Harrodsburg v. Sallee, 142 Ky .
830. But this principleis not applicableto the case at bar,
for the reason that the obstructionwas caused by the city
itself, and it therefore necessarilyhad actual notice of the
obstruction. Board of Councilmen of City of Frankfort v .
Buttimer , 146 Ky . 815, 143 S. W. 410.
That municipal corporations have a dual character, one
governmental, the other private; in one capacity exercising
governmentalfunctions, and in the other performing duties

MUNICIPAL CORP . 2213 MUNICIPAL CORP .
that appertain to the private affairsof the people of the city,
is a well recognized doctrine. But in whatever capacity
the city acts the limitationsimposed by the charter apply
and if the citymust act by ordinance concerning governmental
legislation, so it must act by ordinance when acting in its
private or business capacity. City of Louisvillev . Parsons,
150 Ky . 420 , 150 S. W. 498.
All persons contractingwith a municipal corporationmust ,
at theirperil, inquireintothe power of itsofficersto make
contracts, and municipal corporations are not liable upon
implied promises by reason of benefitsreceived. City of Louis-
villev . Parsons, 150 Ky . 420, 150 S. W. 498.
A city can only speak by the records and a parol contract
made by some of its officersis not binding on the city, the
councilhaving taken no action authorizingit. Creekmore
v . Central Construction Co., 157 Ky . 336, 163 S. W. 194.
Classes of citiesin Kentucky . Ky . Stats., Sec. 2740.
Citiesand towns are the creaturesof the Legislature, which
has the power to abolishtheircourts and to destroy their cor-
porate existenceby repeal of the charter, but not with the
effectof relievingthem from their liability. Boyd v . Cham-
bers, 78 Ky . 140.
An Act entitled" an Act for the benefitof Belle Point,"
providingthat a tax shallbe leviedon the property of all
white citizens" of the town " to defray the school expenses
of an additionalterm of fivemonths , and that all white chil-
dren of pupil age residingin the districtof which the town
isa part," but outsidethe town limits," may attend the school
free for the firstfive months , but after that time shall pay
such tuitionas the trusteesmay require, is unconstitutional,
in that it taxes the town to educate the entire district. Town
of Belle Point v. Pence, 13 R. 371.
While itisobviouslytrue that the words " citiesand towns,"
as used in the Constitutionand statutes, do not embrace a
districtor quasi municipality such as the districtof Clifton,
the latteris one of the " other municipalities" mentioned in
the Constitution and statutes. It is of like character to the

MUNICIPAL INDEBT . 2214 MUNICIPAL SEG .
Districtof the Highlands , of which it was said by this court
in City of Covington v . Districtof Highlands , 113 Ky . 612 :
" The districtis not a town , and , therefore, could not be
properlyclassifiedas such, as the Legislaturewas required
by the Constitutionto do ." Gleason v. Weber , 155 Ky . 431,
159 S. W. 976 .
MUNICIPAL INDEBTEDNESS .- See INDEBTEDNESS .
MUNICIPAL LAW .- Municipallaw may be properlydefined
to be a rule of civilconduct, prescribedby any power in a
State, having, according to itsConstitutionor form of govern-
ment , authority to act. Blackstone's definition has been
departed from, so far as he makes law depend on the supreme
power in a State. Davis v . Ballard, 1 J. J. Mar. 576.
MUNICIPAL PURPOSE .- Ky .Stats.,Sec.3038, givescities
of the second class power to acquire property for municipal
purposes,and Sec.3058, Subsec. 16,conferspower to purchase
or leaseany real or personal property for the use of the city.
Held , that the acquisitionof land for a public park was for a
municipalpurpose and authorized. City of Lexingtonv. Ky .
Chautauqua Assembly , 114 Ky . 781, 71 S. W. 943, 24 R. 1568.
The erectionof a schoolbuildingwas a "municipalpur-
pose" for which the city was authorizedto issuebonds . Frost
v. Central City, 134 Ky . 434, 120 S. W. 367.
The maintenance of a public school is a public duty of
the State, and not purely a municipal purpose. City of
Louisvillev. Com ., 134 Ky . 488, 121 S. W. 411.
An ordinanceof a town of the sixthclass, providingfor
the levy of a tax " for municipal purposes for the year 1905,"
sufficientlyspecifiesthe purposesfor which the levy ismade,
and is not, therefore, in violationof Sec. 180 of the Consti-
tution. The phrase merely means that a tax is to be levied
to carry on the town government . Town of Mt. Pleasant v.
Eversole, 96 S. W. 478, 29 R. 830.
MUNICIPAL SEGREGATION ORDINANCE . The munici-
pal segregationordinanceenacted by the municipallegisla

MUNICIPAL TAXES 2215 MURDER
tureand hereincopiedin fullis a proper and validexercise
of the police power and a reasonable and expedient measure
for the public welfare, directedto the prevention of outbreaks
of violenceresultingfrom racialantipathies,and to the preser-
vation of racialsolidarity. Harris v . City of Louisville, 165
Ky . 560.
MUNICIPAL TAXES .- Every common schoolin the State,
whether it be located in a populous city or in a sparsely
settledrural district, is a State institution, and taxes levied
for the benefitof schools are State taxes and not municipal,
county or districttaxes, although they may be levied and col-
lectedby municipal,countyor districtagencies. City of Louis-
villev . Board of Education, 154 Ky . 316, 157 S. W. 379.
Under an exemption from " municipal taxation," a school
tax leviedby the municipal authoritiesfor the benefitof the
public or common schoolsof the city is not a municipal tax
within the meaning of theseconstitutionaland statutorypro-
visions. City of Louisvillev. Board of Education, 154 Ку .
316 , 157 S. W. 379 .
MURDER . "If any person be guiltyof willfulmurder,
he shallbe punishedwith death or confinementin the peniten-
tiary for life, in the discretionof the jury." Ky . Stats.
(1915 ), Sec. 1149.
" Murder " is the unlawful, willful, and feloniouskilling
of another with maliceaforethought, not in the necessaryor
apparentlynecessaryself-defenseof the slayer. Com .v.Mos-
ser, 133 Ky . 609, 118 S. W. 915.
Murder is a common law offense, the punishment of which
is fixedby Ky . Stats., Sec. 1149. Conner v. Com ., 13 Bush,
714 .
Murder is punishableunder Ky . Stats., Sec. 1149. Volun-
tary manslaughteris punishableunder Ky . Stats., Sec. 1150.
Involuntarymanslaughterispunishableunder the common law
only by fineand imprisonmentin the county jail. Defendant
may be found guiltyof voluntaryor involuntarymanslaughter
under an indictment for murder . The offense of unintentional

MURDER MURDER2216
killingdefined by Ky . Stats., Sec. 1151, is not a degree of the
offense of murder .
In all instructionsas to murder it is proper for the court
to define that crime. O'Brien v . Com ., 89 Ky . 354, 11 R. 534.
It is not error to give the law as to murder and man-
slaughter in one instruction. Ball v. Com ., 125 Ky . 601, 101
S. W. 956, 31 R. 188.
The fact that the words " with malice aforethought"
occurred in the latterpart of an instructioninsteadof the first
part was not prejudicialto defendant. Substance rather than
form is regarded. O'Day v . Com ., 99 S. W. 937, 30 R. 848 .
The mere passive sanction of one present, by constraint,
at the commission of a homicide, does not render him guilty
of murder . Butler v . Com ., 2 Duvall, 435.
An unnecessary or a cruel killing is not always willful
murder . If from all the facts and circumstances there was an
apparent necessitythat the accused should take the lifeof his
antagonist in order to preserve himself from death or great
bodily harm , then about to be inflicted, he is justifiedon the
ground of self-defense; and a killingwith a deadly weapon
will be excusable when done in apparently necessary self-
defense, or may be manslaughter when done in sudden heat
and passion. Farris v . Com ., 14 Bush , 370.
A charge that defendant was guilty of murder if any one
with him shot and killeddeceased with malice aforethought,
provided he was present, and in his mind or will assented
thereto,and by words or conductencouragedit, was not objec-
tionable. Delaney v . Com ., 15 R. 797.
The common law rule that an intentto killis not necessary
to constitutethe offenseof murder or manslaughter has not
been changed by statutein Kentucky . Pence v . Com ., 51 S.
W. 801, 21 R. 500.
An indictment is not bad because it charges the defendant
with " murder " insteadof " willfulmurder," as it is desig-
nated in Ky . Stats., Sec. 1149. The statutedoes not create
the crime of murder or manslaughter, but only fixesthe pun-
ishment for the common law offense. The word " willful "

MURDER MURDER2217
in the statutemay be consideredtautological, as willfulnessis
an essentialconstituentof murder . Metcalfe v . Com ., 86 S.
W. 534 , 27 R. 704 .
Where a killing is done willfully, feloniously, and with
malice aforethought, the crime is murder . Combs v . Com .,
112 S. W. 658, 33 R. 1058.
At common law the recklesskillingof another with a deadly
weapon was murder ; malice being implied. Ewing v . Com .,
129 Ky . 237, 111 S. W. 352, 33 R. 749.
If the defendant willfully, feloniously, and with malice
aforethoughtshot and killeddeceased, he was guilty of murder .
Hunn v . Com ., 143 Ky . 143, 136 S. W. 144 .
If one willfully, feloniously, and with malice aforethought
killsanother, he is guilty of murder . McGeorge v . Com ., 145
Ky . 540, 140 S. W. 691.
If defendant shot at certain persons willfullyand with
malice aforethought, and killed a bystander, he is guilty of
murder . Shelton v. Com ., 145 Ky . 543 ,140 S. W. 670.
If he shot at them in a sudden affray, or sudden heat and
passion, he was guiltyof manslaughter. Id .
The use of the word " murder " in the accusatory part of
an indictment willnot make it a good indictment for murder
if it is lacking in the essentialelements which go to make
up that crime. Hocker v . Com ., 111 S. W. 676, 33 R. 944.
Although itsuse can not make an indictment good where
some of the essentialelements are lacking, its absence in a
case where all of the constituent elements of the crime are
present, will render an indictment defective. Id.
An indictment charging that " the said W. on the day , etc.,
did unlawfully, willfully, maliciously, feloniously, and of his
malice aforethought, killand murder his wife U. W. ," does
not contain a statement of the acts constitutingthe offense,
and is insufficient. White v . Com ., 9 Bush , 179.
Where the wound is apparently mortal and a surgicaloper-
ation is performed in a proper manner , under circumstances
which render it necessary, in the opinion of competent sur-
geons , and such operation is itselfthe immediate cause of the

MURDER MURDER2218
death, the person who inflictedthe wound is stillresponsible
for the death. But if the death resultsfrom grosslyerroneous
surgicalor medical treatment, the originalauthor will not be
responsible. Coffman v. Com ., 10 Bush, 500.
Killing must be unlawful as well as premeditated to con-
stitutemurder . Where the plea is one of self-defense it is
error to instructunqualifiedlythat malice aforethought means
a predetermination to kill, however suddenly formed in the
mind of the person killing. Bohannon v . Com ., 8 Bush , 482 .
If a person permits his passions to be inflamed by some-
thing which is not a legal provocation, and while under the
influenceof such passion and moved thereby he kills, he is
guilty of murder . Chambers v. Com ., 6 R. 448 ; Helm v . Com .,
156 Ky . 751, 162 S. W. 94.
An intentionalkillingdoes not necessarilyimply the crime
of murder . A killingin self-defense, for instance, would be
an intentionalkilling, and yet not wrongful in the eye of the
law. A predeterminationto do a wrongful act without law.
ful excuse is not, of necessity, that malice aforethought which
is an essential element in the crime of murder . It must be a
predetermination to kill, and that intentionmust occupy the
mind at the time of the killing. Frazier v . Com ., 10 Ky.
Opin . 136.
If one unlawfully and with malice aforethought, not in
his necessary self-defense, shoot at another and kill an inno-
cent bystander instead of the intended victim, his act would
be as much murder as if he intended the shot for the person
slain. C. & O. Ry . Co. v. Robinett, 151 Ky . 778, 152 S. W.
976 , and many cases cited.
Malice . An instructiondefiningmurder omits an essential
element of that offenseif it failsto tellthe jury that, in order
to finddefendant guiltyof murder , they must believefrom the
evidence, beyond a reasonable doubt, that the shooting of
deceased by defendant was done with malice aforethought.
Shelton v . Com ., 145 Ky . 543, 140 S. W. 670.
The courtsofKentucky from theirfoundationto the present
time have required the jury to believe from the evidence

MURDER MUST2219
beyond a reasonabledoubt, that the accusedwillfully, unlaw-
fully and with malice aforethoughtcommitted the crime
charged, beforeitwas authorizedto findhim guiltyof mur-
der. In the case of Shelton v. Com ., 145 Ку . 543, 140 S. W.
670, the lower court omitted these words from the instruction
on murder , and thiscourt, in reversingthe case, used the fol-
lowing language: " It is manifest that the jury were not
instructedon all the law of the case; and also manifest that
the instructionas to murder was erroneous, for it omitted an
importantelement essentialto the commissionof that crime,
viz., the words 'maliceaforethought.' To make the instruc-
tion conform to the law, there should have been insertedin it,
between the word 'willfully' and the word 'shot,' the words
'unlawfully and with malice aforethought.' " Watkins v.
Com ., 146 Ky . 449, 142 S. W. 1035.
Malice an essentialingredientin murder , and is to be pre-
sumed from the violence and wantonness of the assault and
character of the weapon used. Kriel v . Com ., 5 Bush , 373 .
Malice, expressor implied, must be proved in orderto con-
stitutethe offenseof murder . Shannahan v . Com ., 8 Bush ,
465 .
Instructionsas to murder should require the Commonwealth
to show the existenceof malice at the time of the shooting, and
an instruction " predicated upon " previous malice of the
accused was erroneous . Walker v . Com ., 5 R. 861 .
An indictment which avers that defendant " feloniously,
willfully, and with malice aforethought did kill and murder
F. with a knife" is not defectivebecause it failsto allegethe
manner in which the knife was used- whether a thrust, blow ,
or stab. Noble v. Com ., 11 R. 867. See, also, MALICE AFORE-
THOUGHT .
Many of the paragraphsunder thistitleare taken, by per-
mission, from Digest Kentucky Reports, (Morton & Co ) .
MUST . See, also, MAY .
The sheriffis, under ordinary circumstances, the proper
officerto collecta tax voted by a school district. The pro-
vision of the statutethat the trustees" may appoint a col-

lector" does not mean that they must . Frederick v . Ragland,
7 R. 743 .
" It will be noticed that the last clause of the sectionjust
quoted (Sec. 141. Criminal Code ) does not make it compul-
sory upon the circuitcourt to order a bench warrant, or commit
the defendant to custody and fixthe amount of bailto be given

cretionarywith the circuitcourt to take such action. We have

Com . v . Swanger , 108 Ky . 579 ; Com . v . Bray , 123 Ky . 339;

MUTATIS MUTANDIS .- With the necessary changes in
points of detail. Cochran's Law Lexicon
MUTILATING CEMETERY FENCE .- Where the owner of
a farm upon which therewas locatedan old graveyard, inclosed
with a loose rock fence, leased the farm to a tenant, who
removed a part of the fence without the knowledge or consent
of the owner , the latterwas not guilty of mutilatingthe fence
of a cemetery in violationof Ky . Stats, Sec. 1336. Masters
v . Com ., 145 Ky . 611, 140 S. W. 1021.
Where a neighbor asked the owner of a farm upon which
therewas locatedan old inclosedgraveyard, for permissionto
use some of the rock of the fence, and the owner of the farm
responded that so far as he was concerned he had no objec-
tion, whereupon the neighbor used the rock, the owner was
not guiltyof violatingKy . Stats., Sec. 1336, which makes it
an offensefor one to willfullymutilate the fence inclosinga
cemetery. Id .
MUTUAL . " Every insurancecompany doing businessin
thisStateshall, if itbe a mutual company, embody the word
'mutual ' in its title, which shall appear upon the first page

MUTUAL ACCOUNTS 2221 MUTUAL AFFRAY
of every policy and renewal receipt; and every company doing
business as a cash stock company shall, upon the face of its
policy, in some suitablemanner , express that such policy is
a stock policy. Nor shallany company transact the business
of insurance in this Commonwealth on both the stock and
mutual plans." Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec 686.
The Act of March , 1870, does not apply to companies or
associationsorganized before 1870 , without capital stock or
premium notes, the contractsbeing simply obligationsof indem-
nity againstlossby fire, the performance secured by a pledge
of the property of each member to the extent of his own
insurance, and the entrancefeesintendedonly as a fund for
paying the expenses of the companies. Such companies are
not stock or mutual companies within the meaning of the Act ,
and theirchartersare not thereby repealed. L. G. M. F. Ins.
Co. v . Com ., 9 Bush , 395.
MUTUAL ACCOUNTS .- When the creditor owes a claim
or demand to the debtor he can not, without the consent or
directionof the debtor, apply what he owes as a crediton the
note or demand he holds against the debtor, and if he makes
the applicationwithout the directionor consent of the debtor
it will not interruptthe running of the statuteof limitation.
The debtor has the right to control the dispositionthat shall
be made of his debt and to apply it as he pleases, and this
privilegecannot be taken out of his hands by the mere act
of another person. Samuel v . Samuel's Admr ., 151 Ky . 235,
151 S. W. 676 .
MUTUAL AFFRAY . On a prosecutionfor homicide, where
the evidence showed that deceased accosted defendant with an
insultingremark , and invitedhim to " come out and take his
medicine," meaning that the speaker was prepared to fight,
and was challengingdefendantto a combat; that defendant,
without sayinganything,went intohishouse, got a pistol,and
returned, when he and deceasedengaged in a shootingmatch
tilldeceased threw down his gun and picked up an ax, and
continuedto fightwith it, defendantretreatingand shooting

as he went the questionwhether the two engaged in a mutual
and willingaffraywas properly submitted to the jury. Hellard
v. Com ., 119 Ky . 445, 84 S. W. 329, 27 R. 115.
No indictmentwillliefor an assaultand batteryin an affray
where the fightingis mutually voluntary. Com . v . Miller, 5
Dana , 320 .
MUTUAL COMBAT .- Where the combat was mutual, and
the accused entered into it with a deadly weapon , giving him
an undue advantage, the killing was murder . Chambers v.
Com ., 6 R. 448. See, also, DUEL .
MUTUAL DEMANDS .- In an action involving the settle-
ment of mutual accounts the statutedoes not bar any of the

L. Dodge Lumber Co. v . Macquithy , 14 R. 142 .
MUTUAL DEPOSIT COMPANIES .- The business of the
mutual deposit companies has not been treated by the Court
of Appeals as so far illegalthat no action could be brought
on contracts made by them . The court has not applied to
them the rule that it will leave the partieswhere they placed
themselves, on the ground that the business was unlawful.
On the contrary, it has applied the principlethat the parties
enteredupon an impracticablebusinessby mistakeor by reason
of falserepresentations, and should be placed in statu quo.
Adams v . Curran , 110 S. W. 280, 33 R. 498.
MUTUAL MISTAKE .- A mutual mistake is a mistake
reciprocalto both parties where each alikelabored under the
same misconception in respect to the terms of the written
instrument. Coleman v . IllinoisLife Ins. Co. 82 S. W. 616,
26 R. 900 .
Where the partiesto a sale of stock acted on the supposi-
tionthatthe publishedstatementsof the affairsof the corpor-
ationwere true,in which casethe stockwould be worth par,
whereas under the true state of affairs- the statement as to
amount of merchandise on hand being grosslyfalse the stock

MUTUALITY MY HOME FARM2223
was worthless, the purchaser has a right to rescissionfor
mutual mistake. Neal v. Wright , 130 Ky .146, 112 S. W. 1115.
A mistake to authorize relief from express contracts must
be certain, material, and mutual . Hill v. Pettit, 66 S. W. 188,
23 R. 2001. See, also, MISTAKE .
MUTUALITY .-The questionof mutuality in a contract
isto be determined from the contractin itsentirety. A single
clause may be binding though not mutually binding or recip-
rocal. Montanus v . Buschmeyer , 158 Ky . 53, 164 S. W. 802.
Want of mutuality is no defense in the case of an executed
contract. VictoriaLimestoneCo. v.Hinton, 156 Ky . 674, 161
S. W. 1109 .
A provision in a contract for the sale of land that if the
vendee should failto pay the balanceof the purchase price
by a certaindate " then the agreement is to be void and of no
effect, as it regards said vendors, at their option," did not
render the contract unenforceable for want of mutuality, as
thisprovisiondoes no more than express what the law would
put into the contract by implication. " The mutuality of a
contract, as we have seen, is to be judged of at the time it
isentered into; so that it isno objectionto the plaintiff'sright
that the defendant may , by delay or other conduct on his part,
subsequent to the contract, have lost his right against the
plaintiff." Ochs v . Kramer , 107 S. W. 260, 32 R. 762 .
MUTUUM . A loan whereby the absolute property in the
thing lent passes to the borrower (it being for consumption ),
and he is bound to restore, not the same thing, but an equiva-
lent in things of the same kind. Cochran's Law Lexicon.
MY . Descriptionof propertyas " my farm," " my house
and lot," whether sufficientunder statute of frauds. Bates
v .Harris, 144 Ky . 400, 401, 138 S. W. 276.
MY HOME FARM .- While proof can not be heard to con-
tradicta probated will, proof may be heard to show what tes-
tator meant by the words " my home farm ." P'Simer v .
Steele, 106 S. W. 851, 32 R. 647 .

Whether a person has two names , i. e., a Christianand sur-
name , is a matter of fact and not of law ; and if he sues by
only one name the court will not presume that he has any
other. Brashears v.Stothard, Litt. Sel.Cas .209.

It is not material under what name the manufacturer or

NARRATIVE OF 2228 NATURAL FOOL

lading meant " naphtha " was incompetent. Standard Oil Co.
v. Tierney, 92 Ky . 367, 13 R. 626.
NARRATIVE OF PAST OCCURRENCES . A conversation
which isnot concomitant with the principalact, nor connected
with it so as to form a part of the res gestae, but a mere

NATIONAL . The usual abbreviation of the word

Bank , 103 Ky . 641, 45 S. W. 870, 20 R. 295 .
NATIONAL GUARD.- See KENTUCKY NATIONAL GUARD .
NATURAL CAUSES .- Testimony of a witness that mules
died from natural causes, held to mean that they died from
colic, and not from violentinjuries, wounds or bruises. Rob-
insonv .L. & N. R. Co., 160 Ky . 235,169 S. W. 831 .
NATURAL CONDITION.- An instructionfixing the meas-
ure of damages for injury to land as the differencebetween the
value of the land in itspresent conditionand the value thereof
in " its natural condition" is erroneous . North Jellico Coal
Co. v . Trosper, 165 Ky . 417, 177 S. W. 241.
NATURAL DRAIN . When a natural drain is to be con-
sidereda publicditch, under the Drainage of Lands Act. See

NATURAL GAS 2229 NATURAL LAWS
NATURAL GAS .- Natural gas, when in place, is a part of
the land and goes with the inheritance. Therefore, a contract
of leaseby the holder of the lifeestategiving his lesseethe
right to enter and open gas wells is void as to the remain-
dermen , and they may have wells so opened closed. Gerkins
v.Kentucky Salt Co., 100 Ky . 734, 39 S. W. 444, 19 R. 130.
There is no property in natural gas until taken; before
taken it is fugitivein its nature, and belongs in common to
the surface owners. LouisvilleGas Co. v . Kentucky Heating
Co., 132 Ky . 435, 111 S. W. 374, 33 R. 912.
The right of surface owners to take natural gas is subject
only to the limitationthat it must be for a lawful purpose
and in a reasonable manner . Id .
The measure of damages for injury to the right to take
natural gas is the differenceat the point where taken between
the value of the natural flow and that of the diminished flow
directlyattributableto the wrong . LouisvilleGas Co. v . Ken-
tucky Heating Co., 132 Ky . 435, 111 S. W. 374, 33 R. 912.
Punitive damages , as well as compensatory, may be had
for an injuryto the rightto takenaturalgas,where malicious
and with design to injure one in his business. Id .
An action will lie by the owner of a gas well against the
owner of other wellsin the same districtfor illegitimatewaste
or destructionof the gas, but not for exhaustionresultingfrom
legitimate use or sale of the gas. Calor Oil & Gas Co. v .
Franzell, 128 Ky . 715, 109 S. W. 328, 33 R. 98, 36 L. R. A.
(N.S.) 456 .
NATURAL HEIRS . For note on meaning of these words,
see 45 L. R. A. (N.S.) 1163.
NATURAL LAWS . " Even the most ignorantworkmen , if
not of impaired intelligence, are conclusivelypresumed to know
the effectand operationof naturallaws, and certainlywhen
the law presumes a knowledge of itsown shiftingdoctrines,
a familiaritywith the immutable laws of nature ought to be
conclusivelypresumed on the part of every creaturesubject
to thoselaws." InterstateCoal Co. v. Molner, 150 Ky . 326,

NATURAL MON . 2230 NATURAL OBLIGA .
150 S. W. 372, quotingWhite's "PersonalInjuriesin Mines,"
Sec. 153. See, also, titleLIME , in this book.
NATURAL MONUMENTS .- While ordinarilynatural mon-
uments and not distancescontrol, yet the natural monuments
must be located with reasorable certainty. Sackett v . Ky .
Coal Lands Co., 160 Ky . 793, 170 S. W. 164. See, also, BOUN-
DARIES .
NATURAL OBJECTS OF BOUNTY .-Instructions which
do not recognize that a testatorhas natural objects of his
bounty and owes them a duty, disregardnot only the principles
as to what constitutestestamentary capacity, but also dis-
regard paternal instinct. Rasdall v . Brush , 104 S. W. 749,
31 R. 1138 .
An instructionrelating to the " objects of his bounty "
should be modified by prefixingthe word " natural" before
such expression. Lancaster v . Lancaster, 87 S. W. 1137 , 27
R. 1127 .
See, also, OBJECTS OF HIS BOUNTY .
NATURAL OBLIGATION . " Pothier, whose characteras a
profound and able jurist, is known to every lawyer, is still
more explicitand more in point. He divides obligationsinto
those that are both natural and civil, those that are merely
civil, and those that are merely natural. 'A civil obliga-
tion,' he says, 'is a legal tie, vinculum juris, which gives the
person in whose favor it is contracted, a right of judicially
enforcing the performance of it. A natural obligationis that
which obliges the person in honor and conscience. Obliga-
tions are commonly both natural and civil; there are some,
however , which are merely civil, and which the debtor may
be judiciallycompelled to perform , without being under any
obligationto do so, in point of conscience. There are, also,
obligationswhich are merely natural, without being civil.'
'These obligations,' he continues to observe, 'oblige the person
contractingthem , in point of honor and conscience; but the
law does not allow any actionto compel the execution of them.
These are only calledobligationsin an improper sense; for

NATURAL PERSON 2231 NATURALIZATION
they are no legaltie, vinculumjuris; theydo not impose upon
the person contractingthem any real necessityto accomplish
them as he can not be compelled to do so by the person in
whose favor they are contracted; and it is thisnecessitywhich
constitutesthe character of obligations, vinculum juris quo
necessitateadstringimur. " Evans' Pothier, Part 2, Chap . 1,
Blair v . Williams, 4 Litt. 39 .
NATURAL PERSON .- See PERSON .
NATURAL SON .- A naturalson recognizedby his father,
who procured an Act of the Legislatureto make him capable
of inheritinghis estate as though he had been a legitimate
son, will inherit his father's estate, if he survives him , the
same as if he had been born in lawful wedlock . Drain v . Vio-
lett, 2 Bush , 157.
NATURAL WATER COURSE .- A ravine or gully, which
is simply an outletfor surface water at certainseasons of the
year, having no well-definedbed or channel with banks and
sides, can not be termed a natural water-course, and is not
thereforegoverned by the well-settledrulesapplying to natural
streams. Middlesborough Town Co. v . Helwig , 14 R. 430.
NATURAL WEAR AND TEAR EXCEPTED .- A lease of
a building provided that the lesseesshould keep the property
in the same repair as on the completion of certain improve-
ments , " natural wear and tear excepted," and to surrender
them in as good conditionas received, " naturalwear and
tear and naturaldecay, and injury or destruction," by any
cause not theirfault, excepted. Held, that while the lease
imposed no obligationon the lesseeto repairinjuriescaused
by naturalwear and decay, itdid not requirethe landlordto
repairthe roof,destroyedby naturalwear and decay. Thomas
v. Conrad, 114 Ky . 841, 71 S. W. 903, 74 S. W. 1084, 24 R.
1630 .
NATURALIZATION .- Naturalizationisa personalprivilege,
and the alienwife does not become a naturalizedcitizenby the
naturalizationof her husband . White v . White , 2. Met . 191.

NATURALLY AND 2232 NAVIGABLE STREAM
See naturalizationlaws copied on p . 32, Ky . Stats. (1915).
See, also, ALIENS .
NATURALLY AND REASONABLY . In an action on life
policies, an instructionthat if answers by assured in his appli-
cation were untrue, and if true answers had been given the
insurer, acting naturally and reasonably, would not have
entered intothe contract, should have been modified by adding,
after the words " naturally and reasonably," the words " in
accordance with the practiceusual among lifeinsurance com-
panies." Provident Savings Life Assur . Soc. v . Whayne's
Admr ., 131 Ky . 84, 93 S. W. 1049, 29 R. 160.
NAVIGABLE STREAM .- The common -law rule with ref.
erence to navigable or non -navigable waters has been adopted
in this State. By that rule only those waterways are deemed
navigable in which the tide ebbs and flows, and all other
waters, whether navigable in fact or non -navigable, are held
to be non -navigable. Wilson v . Watson , 141 Ky . 324, 132 S.
W. 563 .
In Kentucky all rivers are non -navigable so far as the
doctrine of riparian ownership is concerned. Robinson v .
Wells , 142 Ky . 400 , 135 S. W. 317 .
A stream acrosswhich one can stepin itsordinarystages,
ten or twelve feet at the top of the banks, about four feet
deep when the banks are full, with bushes growing on the
sidesand meeting in the middle of the stream, with water-gate
acrossit, incapableof use for floatingstavesat any stage unless
men walk along the banks with poles to push the staves from
the banks and around the shoals, is not a navigable stream,
and can not be used for the purpose of floatingout staves
against the wishes of the owners of the land through which the
streamflows, withoutcompensation. Asher v. McKnight , 129
Ky . 623 , 112 S. W. 647 .
A stream may be navigable for the purpose of floatingtim-
ber, though not navigable for boats. Ireland v. Bowman &
Cockrell, 130 Ky . 153, 113 S. W. 56, 114 S. W. 338.

NAVIGABLE STREAM 2233 NAVIGABLE STREAM
The existenceof tide-water is the testof navigabilityso far
as riparian rights may be involved. This is the common -law
rule, and has been so settledin this State. Berry v. Snyder ,
3 Bush , 269 ; Millerv . Hepburn , 8 Bush , 331.
A stream which even in periods of high water cannot be
used to any practicableextent for floatinglogs, because it runs
down in a few hours, is not navigable. Murray v . Preston,
50 S. W. 1095, 21 R. 72 .
A creek susceptibleat certainperiods of the year of valu-
able use for the purpose of floatinglogs to market , and which
has been continuously so used for more than twenty years,
must be considered to that extent a navigable and floatable
stream, subjectto the use of the public. Ford Lumber Co. v .
McQueen , 14 R. 521.
The Court of Appeals will take judicial knowledge that
the Kentucky River is a navigablestream,and thisfactmust be
recognizedby individuals. The public, for purposes of navi-
gation, is entitledto the free and undisturbed use of every
part of the stream from bank to bank , and of such accessibility
as to render it capable of navigation by boats or the floating
of logs, and for all purposes of travel and transportation.
Warner v . Ford Lumber & Mfg . Co., 123 Ky . 103, 93 S. W.
650 , 29 R. 527 .
The court judiciallyknows that the Ohio is a navigable
stream, and will be so regarded in construing a petitionfor
opening a road under the statute " for the convenience of
travelingto any navigableriver." Bennett v .Bryan , 1 R. 274 .
The court takes judicialnotice that the Tennessee River
is a navigable stream, and as such the rights of the public
extend to ordinary high water mark , and as the public'sright
in the land forming the bank of the river between high and
low water mark was paramount to the rights of the owner
of the adjacent fee, which called to extend to the river, an
improvement of a way for the public across their own prop-
erty ought not, and under the charter of the city can not, be
made at the expense of the property-holders, who are the
servientowners. Terrellv. City of Paducah , 92 S. W. 310, 28
R. 1237 .

NAVIGABLE STREAM 2234 NEAR
Sec. 182, Ky . Stats., requiringthe payment of a licensetax
by any person residingupon a boat on a navigable river " for
any purpose whatever," is not unconstitutionalas depriving
the citizenof the privilegeof living where he chooses, as the
right to occupy the highway rests on the will of the State.
Robertson v . Com ., 101 Ky . 285, 40 S. W. 920, 19 R. 442 .
The Act of 1808 , giving jurisdictionto the courts of either
county over the navigableriverswhich dividethem, applies
to criminaland civilproceedingsalone, not to any rightto
appropriate the land embracing the entire bed of the river.
Hart v .Rogers, 9 B. M. 420 .
NEAR . See, also, ABOUT SAID CROSSING; AT ; ON OR NEAR .
Under the facts alleged in the petitiondefendant would
be liablewhether plaintiffwas standing " at" or " near " the
crossing. L. & N. R. Co. v . Eaden , 122 Ky . 318, 93 S. W.
7, 29 R. 365, 6 L. R. A. (N.S.) 581.
In an actionagainsta railroadcompany for injuriesat a
private crossing, near a public crossing, where there was no
averment that signalsusually given on the approach of trains
to the public crossing could have been heard at the private
crossing,and the distancebetween the crossingswas not given,
but a bare statementthat they were " near" together, the
questionof defendant'snegligencein failingto give signals
at the publiccrossing, on which plaintiffcould rely, was not
raised. Davis' Admr . v. C. & O. Ry . Co., 116 Ky . 144, 75
S. W. 275 , 25 R. 342 .
An allegationin a petition that plaintiff'sintestatewas
killed" at or near " a private crossingshould be construed to
mean that she was killedat a place on the track other than
the crossing. Davis' Admr .v. C. & O. Ry . Co., 116 Ку . 144,
75 S. W. 275, 25 R. 342.
Where the decree of sale directed the officerto advertise
by posting a notice " on or near" the premises, his return
that he posted a notice " on or near" the property shows a
compliance with the decree. Slaughter v. Graham , 5 R. 324.
" About ten feetnorth" of a designatedgate is sufficiently
certainas a descriptionof the point it was desired to discon

NEARLY NEARNESS2235
tinue at; this literallysignifies" near to" a point ten feet
from the gate, and means lessthan a foot eitherover or under
ten, and legallymeans ten feetfrom the gate. Moore & Ken-
ney v . Stone, 2 Ky . Opin . 484.
Where an indictment for obstructing a highway alleges
that the obstructionwas near a certain town , evidence that
the obstruction was within the town constitutes a material
variance, for the reason that obstructionswithin the town and
those outsidethe town are governed by differentlaws. Ill.Cent.
R. Co. v . Com ., 104 Ky . 362, 47 S. W. 255, 20 R. 748, 990.
Where a callin a State patent to land is for a stake " near
Cumberland Gap ," the language can not be construed as
meaning in Cumberland Gap . Creech v . Johnson, 116 Ky .
441 , 76 S. W. 185, 25 R. 657.
Where a call in a patent from the State placed a certain
corner " near Cumberland Gap ," but the placing of the corner
in accordance with the call would have caused the patent to
embrace a town with the county court house and public
grounds, the word " near" was too indefiniteto justifya con-
structionof the patent in accordance therewith, so as to sus-
tain such a result . Id .
Where the callsof a patent were for 664 poles, 8,320 poles,
3,200 poles, and 3,150 poles, and one of the callsestablished
a corner" near Cumberland Gap ," consideringthe length of
the linesof the patent and the notorietyof Cumberland Gap ,
the locationof a corner within fivemiles of the gap might be
reasonablyspoken of as near Cumberland Gap . Id .
A callin a survey to a stake " near Cumberland Gap ," was
construedto mean at or in Cumberland Gap . Davis' Heirs v.
Hinckley , 141 Fed . 708.
" Stopping place," or " near dwelling," defined. See note
on thissubject in 26 L. R. A. 132.
NEARLY . A callto run " nearly a south-east course" must
be run due south-east. Moore v . Harris, Sneed 18.
NEARNESS .- The nearness with which a machine is run
to an animal does not constitutenegligence unless it is pur.

NEARNESS NECESSARIES2236
posely done to cause fright or unless the driver before going
near discovers, or should in the exerciseof ordinary care have
discovered, that running the machine closeto the animal would
frighten it. The duty of the driver of an automobile is the
same whether it is being run within three feet or thirty feet
of an animal. Shelton v .Hunter , 162 Ky . 531, 172 S. W. 950.
NECESSARIES .- Necessariesand family expenses gener-
ally.
It was error to require the surviving husband to pay the
cost of administering his deceased wife's estate and of an
action by her administrator for a settlement of the estate.
Long v .Beard , 48 S. W. 158, 20 R. 1036.
The surviving husband is liablefor the physician's bills
and burialexpenses of his deceased wife. Id.
Under Sec. 2130, Ky . Stats., providingthat the husband
shall be liable for " necessaries" furnished to the wife after
marriage, the husband's and not the wife'sestateis liablefor
the expense of medical attentionto the wife suitableto her
situationand to his condition in life, and he is not entitled,
therefore, to be reimbursed out of her estatefor such expenses
paid by him. Towery v.McGaw , 56 S. W. 727, 22 R. 155.
The deceased wife's estate, and not the husband's, is liable
for the deceased wife'sfuneral expenses. Id .
The surviving husband is liablefor the necessary expenses
of the deceased wife's sicknessand burial, and the executor
having paid them may retain the amount out of his share of
the estate. Brand's Exr .v . Brand , 60 S. W. 704, 22 R. 1366.
Under Sec.2, Chap .52,Art. 2,Gen. Stats., providingthat
the wife's real estate shall be liable for such of her debts
contractedafter marriage " on account of necessariesfor her-
self or any member of the family, her husband included,"
as shall be evidenced by writing, the wife's land may be
subjectedto the payment of a note executed by her for a mule
to enable her husband to make a crop for the support of
themselvesand theirinfant children. Allen v . Long , 41 S. W.
17, 19 R. 488 .

NECESSARIES NECESSARIES2237
A debt for the rent of a hotel leasedby the wife solelyfor
profit, under a contractsignedby herselfand her husband,
is not within the statute providing that a wife's real estate
can be soldto pay debtscreatedon accountof "necessaries"
forherselfor any member of her family. Crow v. Shacklett,
38 S. W. 692, 18 R. 908.
In an actionto subjecta married woman's estatefor necessi-
ties, it is necessary to allege in the petitionthat the credit
was originallygiven to her, for herselfor family. Waller v.
Johnson,4 Ky . Opin.309.
The purchase of a house is a " necessary" within the mean-
ing of the statute, if the purchase is reasonable, considering
the wife's estateand rank in society, and her general estate
may be subjectedto the payment of a note executed by her and
herhusband forthepurchasemoney . McKee v.Hays ,9 R. 288.
Conveyance to husband in trustfor " use, benefit,and behoof
of his wife," can not per se vest in her a separate estatefree
from his participationand control; consequentlyhis and her
jointbond for the price of a musical clock, not a " necessary,"
imposed on her no obligation. Guishaber v. Hairman , 2
Bush , 321.
Food , raiment, and shelter, or other comforts for the family,
iswhat iscontemplatedby the terms " necessariesfor herself
or any member of her family, her husband included," as used
in the statute. Ford v. Teal, 7 Bush , 157
A house rented for the residenceof the family, suited to
their circumstances, is a necessary within the meaning of the
statute. Bergen v . Forsythe,17 B. M. 556 .
Lumber necessary to repair fencing and building on the
wife's lands to prevent their fallinginto ruin and for the
proper use and enjoyment of the land and houses, is
" necessaries" for which the general estateof the wife is liable
ifthedebtthereforisevidencedby a writingproperlyexecuted.
Marshall v .Miller, 3 Met . 334.
The word " necessaries" as used in the statute should
receive a liberalconstruction, and be held to embrace such
things as the family, including the husband , considering its
socialposition, ought to have . Bergen v . Forsythe, 17 В. М.
555 ; Pell v.Cole,2 Met .253 ; Harris v . Dale,5 Bush ,63.

NECESSARIES NECESSARIES2238
" Necessaries" did not include a debt incurred in procuring
a substitutefor the husband when drafted. Ford v . Teal, 7
Bush , 157.
" Necessaries" do not include a debt incurred for the
husband's relieffrom imprisonment, impressment, or other
personal and sole liabilityunconnected with family sustenta-
tion.7 Bush , 157.
Supplies used in keeping a hotel as a business of profit
and not a mere means of support can not be regarded as
necessariesfurnished for the use of the family within the
meaning of the statute. Harris v . Dale, 5 Bush , 62.
The law compels the husband to furnish his wife things
necessaryto her sustenancein the positionof lifeshe deserves,
and any one furnishing such things to the wife can compel
the husband to pay for them . Righter v . Righter, 114
S. W. 787 .
In an action against a widow on a note executed by her
and her husband during coverture, plaintifffailed to show
that the note was for " necessaries," and asked merely for a
personaljudgment. By an amendment he allegedthe omitted
facts and asked for a sale of defendant's land to satisfythe
debt. Limitationhad not run when the originalpetitionwas
filed, but had when the amendment was filed. Held , that the
action is not barred, as the amendment did not set up a new
cause of action, but merely perfected that which had already
been instituted. McKee v.Hays , 9 R. 288.
If a married woman buys articles not necessaries for the

therein; (2 ) in the use of the words " authorizedagents" there-
in; and (3) intheuse of thephrase" whileexercisingordinary
care for his own safety." Stevenson v. I. C. R. Co., 157 Ky .
561, 163 S. W. 747 .
Where the word " negligence" was properlyused in instruc-
tions, the failureof the court to defineitwas not error, where
no requestto do so was made . South Cov .& Cin .St.Ry . Co. v .
Brown , 104 S. W. 703, 31 R. 1072.
As to a passengeron a trainan instructionwas approved
which defined" negligently" as failingto use the highest

drawn or payable, shallbe deemed negotiable, and treatedin

" An instrument to be negotiatedmust conform to the follow-

maker or drawer. (2) Must containan unconditionalpromise
or orderto pay a sum certainin money . (3) Must be payable
on demand or at a fixed or determinable future time. (4)
Must be payableto theorderof a specifiedpersonor tobearer;
and (5) Where the instrument is addressed to a drawee, he
must be named or otherwiseindicatedtherein with reasonable
certainty." NegotiableInst. Law, Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec.
3720b, Subsec.1. See,also,subsequentsubsectionsof thatlaw.

NegotiableInst.Law,Ky.Stats.(1915),Sec.3720b,Subsec.184.
"The negotiableinstrumentneednotfollowthelanguageof
thisAct, but any termsare sufficientwhich clearlyindicate

ableInst.Law ,Ky .Stats. (1915), Sec. 3720b , Subsec.10.
"A negotiablepromissorynotewithinthemeaningofthis
Act is an unconditionalpromisein writingmade by one
personto another, signedby the maker engagingto pay on

demand or at a fixedor determinable future time, a sum certain
in money to order or to bearer. Where a note is drawn to

Subsec . 184 .
" An instrument is negotiated when it is transferredfrom

In order to make a note or a billnegotiable, the words

An instrument in writing, signed by the drawer and con-

The NegotiableInstrumentsAct is, in the main, a mere

Under the statute which had been in force in Kentucky
up to the time the NegotiableInstrumentAct took effect,
promissorynoteswere assignable, but the assigneetook them
subjectto allthe defenseswhich the maker had to them inthe
hands of the assignorbeforenoticeof the assignment. That
Act,however, changed the rule, and under itpromissorynotes
which have been transferredto a holder in due course, that
is, a bona fidepurchaser without notice, are placed upon the
footingofa billofexchange,and arenot subjectto the equities
which would obtain between the maker and the payee.
Persons now who make such notes, and deliver them , take
the riskof theirbeing transferredto a bona fidepurchaser
without notice. The statutechanged the old rule, and must
likeotherstatutesbe enforcedaccordingto itsterms. Hard
cases may arise, but the courts are powerless to afforda

Rounds , 160 Ky . 358, 169 S. W. 848.
Checks are, by statute, as they were at common law, bills

NEW ACTION .- Where an indictment for violation of the
local option law was dismissed more than a year after the

a subsequentcivilactionbrought to recovera penaltyfor the
same offensewas not a continuanceof the originalproceeding,
but a new action, and barred by limitations. Com . v. Elkins,
116 Ky . 303, 76 S. W. 25, 25 R. 485.
In a foreclosuresuit, in the originalpetitionthe amount of
plaintiff'sdebt was set out, but the prayer asked judgment
only for the interestthereon and costsand that the land be
soldto satisfysaidjudgment,and forallproper and equitable
relief. By an amended petitionplaintiffasked the enforcement
of his lien to secure both his debt and interest. Held , the
amended petitiondid not then statea new cause of action, but
simply correctedthe prayer to conform to the reliefto which
the originalpetitionshowed plaintiffwas entitled, and a new
warning order was not required. Highland L. & B. Assn . v.
Audus , 110 S. W. 325,33 R. 214.
An amendment of the prayer of the petitionso as to make
itconform to the cause of action set forth in the petitionwas
not a new and distinct cause of action . Id .
If a promise to pay the note be made afterit isbarred, this
promise createsa new obligationand constitutesa new cause
of action, and the action must be upon this promise and not
upon theoriginaldebt. Carr v.Robinson, 8 Bush, 274; Trous-
dale v . Anderson , 9 Bush , 277.

and particularrepetitionof the matter contained in the orig-
inal petitionso as to indicatethat the plaintiffis suing for a

v .Bannister,79 Ky . 205,2 R. 72 .

or under the provisionsof Chap . 56 of the General Statutes,

ever it amends itscharter or filescorporationpapers which so

" The generalauthoritythroughoutthe Union upholds the
right of a corporationto extend itslifeby amendment . Sec.
559 of the statutes, expresslygrantingthisrightwhen read
in connectionwith Sec. 539 , is in linewith thisview. Though
the question under the present statutehas not been answered
