in the case of Home Building Associationv . Bruner , 134 Ky .
361; and indeed the statutesadmit of no other construction.
Such an extensionisnot the formation of any new or different
corporation. Its affairsare not wound up or liquidated; there
is no distributionof itsassets; there is no change in the per-
sonnel of the stockholdersor theirliability; there is no altera-
tionor break in the continued existenceof the corporationupon
which the organization tax has once been paid. Franklin
County Court v. DepositBank , 87 Ky . 370. The General

NEW COURTS 2291 NEW PROMISE
Assembly might provide for a new tax upon such an exten-
sionbut ithas not done so." Ohio Valley Tie Co. v . Bruner ,
148 Ky . 358, 146 S. W. 749.
A corporationowning a factoryin Louisvilleceasedopera.
tions in March and turned over itsproperty to a liquidating
agent; in July fiveof itsstockholdersformed a new corpora-
tion and procured others to go in with them ; in October the
new corporationbought the plant of the old corporation, stock
in the new corporationbeing exchanged for stock in the old.
Held , that under Sec. 170 of the State Constitutionand the
cityordinanceenactedpursuantto it, the new corporationis
not entitledto exemption from taxationfor fiveyears. Victor
Cotton Oil Co. v .City of Louisville, 149 Ky .149,148 S. W. 10.

petitionreducingthe amount demanded,and chargingthatthe
negligence was gross, does not raise a new issue, demanding

NEW MATTER . Every matterof factwhich the plaintiff
need not allegeand prove to make out his case must be alleged
by the defendant as new matter of defense. In an action to
recover specificpersonal property, where the allegationof title
isdenied,defendant may relyon limitation, or any factshowing
that plaintiffdid not have the titleat the commencement of
the action. But where he claimstoholdthepropertyas bailee,
or relieson any otherdefensethatadmitstitlein plaintiffand
possessioninhimself,he must pleadthe facts. Perry v.Riding,
9 R. 536 .
NEW PROMISE .- Under Limitation Statute . A debt
barredby thestatutemay be revivedby a new promise,express
or implied. Gray v. McDowell, 6 Bush, 480. But ithas been

NEW PROMISE NEW PROMISE2292
held that to take a case out of the statutean express acknowl.
edgment of the debt as a debt due at that time, coupled with
the original considerationor an express promise to pay it,
must be proved to have been made within the time prescribed
by the statute. Bell v . Rowland , Hard . 301 ; Harrison v.
Handley,1 Bibb,445;Gray v.Lawridge,2 Bibb,285;Rochester
v . Buford , 5 J. J. M. 33 ; Head v . Manners , 5 J. J. M. 260;
French v. Frazier, 7 J. J. M. 431 ; Trousdale v . Anderson , 9
Bush , 277.
The promise to take the case out of the statute must be
sufficientof itselfto sustain the action, and on it the action
must be brought. Hord v. Lee, 4 Mon. 37.
If from the express acknowledgment , it appear clearlythat
the party ought to pay, the law willimply a promise to pay,
and the case willbe taken out of the statuteby such acknowl-
edgment and promise withinfiveyears. Marcum's Admx .v.
Terry, 146 Ky .145, 142 S. W. 209.
In order for a debtor to revivea debt barred by the statute
of limitations, there must be an express promise to pay, or
an acknowledgment of the debt as a debt due at the time.
Davis v. Strange, 156 Ky . 420, 161 S. W. 217.
There can be no recovery on a promise to pay a debt barred
by limitationsunlessthe promiseisclear, absolute, and uncon-
ditional; and thereforeitiserrorto instructthe jury to find
for plaintiffif they believedefendant, after the debt was
barred, " acknowledged the same as a subsistingdemand."
Chism v .Barnes, 104 Ky . 310, 47 S. W. 232,875, 20 R. 569.
Implied promise may be created from the fact of a positive
and unqualifiedadmission of the debt; but if the acknowledg-
ment isaccompaniedby any circumstancesor expressionwhich
repelthe idea of an intentionto pay, no impliedpromiseis
created, and the debt isnot thereby revived. Warren v .Perry,
5 Bush ,449 ; Gray v .McDowell , 6 Bush , 480.
To cut off the running of the statutethere must be a clear
and express promise to pay the claim to which the statute
applies. Loose declarationsare insufficient. McGrew's Exr .
v . O'Donnell, 92 S. W. 301, 28 R. 1366.

NEW PROMISE NEW PROMISE2293
If new promise is made afterstatutorybar is complete,
actionmust be on the new promise, and the periodof limita-
tion will be determined by the nature of the evidence of the
new promise. Gilmore v . Green, 14 Bush , 772 .
To recoveron a promiseto pay a pre-existingdebt plain-
tiffmust allegefactsshowing a considerationfor such promise.
If the pre-existingdebt was enforciblewhen the promise was
made , the actionisupon the originalliability. But if no action
ismaintained on the originalliabilityto recover on a promise
to pay it, the plaintiffshould allegeevery fact essentialto a
recovery on the originaldebt and also on the subsequent prom-
ise to pay . An allegationof " an agreement and promise by
defendantto pay plaintiffa pre-existingdebt that never was
discharged" isinsufficient. Meyer v.Zotel, 96 Ky . 362, 16 R.
506 .
A part payment, accompaniedby receiptin full, does not
amount to a new promise. Richardson v . Chanslor's Trustee,
103 Ky . 425 , 45 S. W. 774, 20 R. 121.
A mere statementby defendantwhen a merchant'saccount
is presented to him (after severalto which he objected were
stricken out) that " it was all right" was held not to do .
Harrison v .Handley , 1 Bibb , 444.
Promise " to settlewith and pay the plaintiffin money or
property" isnot sufficientto revivea debt barred by the statute
of limitations. Fischer v .Hess ,9 B. М. 617.
If debtor acknowledges an account for store goods to be
" just" (except a designated item, which is then struck out) ,
but says he thinks he is charged a littletoo high for some of
the articles, the case will be taken out of the statuteby such
acknowledgment within fiveyears. Ditto v. Ditto, 4 Dana , 505.
The act of a city in levying a tax to pay offold debts does
not constitutea new promise to pay the debts. Hardin v. Town
of Highland Park , 144 Ky . 489, 139 S. W. 765.
A promise by a surety on a note, discharged from liability
to pay the obligation, is not enforceableunlessbased on a
new and sufficientconsideration. Steger v. Jackson, 139 Ky .
491, 102 S. W. 320, 31 R. 434.

NEW PROMISE NEW PROMISE2294
A new promise to pay a debt already existingand not barred
by the statute can not be made the foundation of an action. Its
only effectis to cut off and exclude from the period of limita-
tion the time that had elapsed when the new promise was
made . Gilmore v . Green , 14 Bush , 772.
Limitations. Application of general rule. See note on this
subjectin 25 L. R. A. (N.S.) 806.
When a contractin writing,not requiredto be so by statute,
ischanged by subsequentparol agreement of the partiesthereto,
if the change goes to a material provision of the contract, a
new contract results, which operates to discharge the original
contract, and to substitutein its stead a new contract by
parol, some of the terms of which are identifiedby the previous
contractin writing; and in such casethe fiveyear statuteof
limitationapplies. Homire v. Stratton& TersteggeCo., 157
Ky . 822,164 S. W. 67.
Where the consideration, new promise, and breach are
averred, and the allegationsas to the new promise are
sustained, though the originalcause of action was barred the
plaintiffis entitledto recoveron the new promise made within
two years from the institutionof the action. Adams v . O'Rear ,
80 Ky . 129, 3 R. 605.
A promise to pay a demand barred by limitationcreates
a new obligationand constitutesa new cause of action, and
thependency of an actionon the old promisedoes not prevent
the running of the statuteagainstthe new promise. Turner v.
Everett, 5 R. 325.
Promise Must Be Made to Creditor, Not to Stranger . New
promise or unqualifiedacknowledgment to a stranger will not
take the caseout of the statute, or constitutea good cause of
actionasupon a new promise. Trousdalev.Anderson,9 Bush ,
277 .
In order to revivea debt barred by the statuteof limitations,
the acknowledgmentof thedebt and the promiseto pay it,
must be made to the creditor, or to some one authorizedto act
forhim, and itisnotsufficientifmade to a stranger. Davis
v . Strange, Admr ., 156 Ky . 420, 161 S. W. 217 ; Hargis V.
Sewell,87 Ky . 63,9 R. 920.

New Promise After Discharge by Law or by Voluntary
Act of Creditor. Where a debtorhas been dischargedfrom
the debt by provisionof positivelaw,an expresspromiseafter-
ward to pay thedebtwillbe enforced,but,where the discharge
isthe fair, voluntaryact of the creditor, a subsequentexpress
promise to pay the debt will not be enforced. Samuel West-
heimer & Co. v .Flasheim, 99 S. W. 346, 30 R. 641.
After Discharge in Bankruptcy. A promiseto pay a debt
after the same has been discharged in bankruptcy willsupport
an action on the debt provided the promise is clear, distinct,
and unequivocal. Brooks v .Paine,77 S. W. 190, 25 R. 1125.
A new promise made by a bankrupt to pay a debt from
which he had been dischargedis founded upon a moral con-
sideration, and constitutesa valid enforceablecontract. Gra-
ham v .Hunt ,8 B. Mon. (Ky .) 7 ; Carson v .Osborn ,10 B. Mon.
(Ky .) 155 ; Egbert v. McMichael , 9 B. Mon. (Ky .) 44 ; Eckler
v . Galbraith,12 Bush (Ky .) 71 ; Ogden v .Redd , 13 Bush (Ky .)
581 ; Posey v.Mayer's Admr ., 11 Ky .Opin . 457.
A promise to pay a debt afterdischargein bankruptcy is
upon a valid considerationand may be enforced. If plaintiff
may recover on such a debt he is entitled to recover interest
as well as principal. Worsham's Admr . v. Miller, 8 Ky .
Opin . 19 .
An expressionby a debtor,afterhis dischargein bankruptcy,
of his intentionto pay certainold debts, does not amount to
a promise to pay, unlessmade to the originalcreditor, or to
some one therefor, or on presentationof the accountsto him
for payment . Wilson v . Morris, 4 Ky . Opin . 55.
New promise after discharge in bankruptcy . See note on
this subjectin 53 L. R. A. 362.
NEW ROAD .- When the locationof a road ismerely changed
for a part or all of its distance from one place to another,
so that it may be more convenient or accessiblefor public

case from the bed of a creek in which itrun to a point nearby ,
a short distance from the bank of the creek, we do not
think it is necessary that there should be any order of court

NEW TRIAL .- See ACCIDENT ; CONTRARY TO LAW ; SMALL-
NESS OF DAMAGES .
" A new trial is a re-examination in the same court of an
issue of fact after a verdictby a jury or a decisionby the
court." CivilCode, Sec.340;Humphreys v. Walton, 2 Bush,
580 .
" A new trial is the re-examination of an issue of fact in
the same court, by anotherjury, after a verdicthas been
given." Crim . Code , Sec. 269.
The greatobjectof grantingnew trialsisthe attainmentof
the justiceof the case. Lord Mansfield, 1 Burr , 54. Bagby v.
Lewis, 2 T. B. Monroe , 79 .
It is not the modern policy of the courts to seize upon
carelessexpressionsin instructionsand grant new trialsupon
such grounds; in factunder the very terms of our Code of
Practice, we are authorizedto reversejudgments only where
therehas beenan errorprejudicialtothesubstantialrightsof
appellant. Cincinnati,N. O. & T. P. Ry .Co.v.Reed,154 Ky.
380, 157 S. W. 721 .
The Legislaturehas the rightto prescribethe terms upon
which one who has been convictedof crime may have a new
trial; thereforeSec. 270, CriminalCode, whichprovidesthat
"thegrantingof a new trialplacesthe partiesin the same
positionas ifno trialhad beenhad," isnot unconstitutional.
Com .v .Arnold , 83 Ky . 1 , 6 R. 181.
Beforea new trialcanbegranted,notonlymustthegrounds
reliedon thereforbe establishedbut a validdefense to the
originalactionmust be pleadedand proved. (CivilCode,
Sec.521.) McCoy v.FieldGroceryCo.,142Ky. 812,135 S.
W. 319 .
Applications for new trialsare addressedto the sound discre-
tionofthecourt,tobeexercisedaccordingtotherulesand
usagesof law, and the courtshouldregardthe substantial

NEW TRIAL NEW TRIAL2297
justiceof the caseequallyremotefrom favoringnegligenceor
exactingunreasonableeffortor impracticablediligence. Haggin
v. Christian, 1 Mar. 581.
A motion for a new trialsuspends the judgment for every
purpose. Turner v.Booker, 2 Dana , 335; Wright v.Haddock ,
7 Dana , 254; Reynolds v. Horine, 13 B. M. 235. An appeal
can not be prosecuted until the motion is disposed of. Lou .
Chemical Works v . Com ., 8 Bush , 181.
A reversalof a judgment by the Court of Appeals for the
admission of improper evidence is not the granting of a new
trialwithin the meaning of the statuteprohibitingthe grant-
ing of more than two new trialsto the same party. Burton v .
Brashear, 3 Mar. 278 ; Ball v. Lively, 1 Dana , 62.
After three or more verdictsfor the same party the Court
of Appeals may re-examine the questionsof law arisingon the
trial, and for error reversethe judgment. The act of 1796
providing " that not more than two new trialsshallbe granted
to the same party," etc., does not apply to appeals. Ball v .
Lively, 1 Dana , 62.
Remittitur . Plaintiff can not avoid a new trial because of
excessivedamages by entering a remittiturunless it reduce the
verdictto the amount the jury were clearlyauthorizedto find.
Masterson v .Hogan , 17 B. М. 332.
Where the court has authorized the jury to find as much
as $ 160 on account of loss of time, when they should have
been limitedto $80 on that account under the pleadings, plain-
tiffmay prevent a new trialby remitting the excess. City
of Dayton v . Gardner , 40 S. W. 779, 19 R. 302. See, also,
Washington M. & M. Co. v . Barnett, 19 R. 959.
Where the verdict is excessive because of error in the instruc-
tions, plaintiffmay be required to reduce his judgment if the
amount of the excesscan be determinedfrom the proof, and
defendant can not then insiston a new trial. Johnson's Admr .
v .Johnson ,104 Ky .714,47 S. W. 883,20 R. 890.
It is error for the court, in an action for personal injuries,
to requireplaintiffto take judgment for a smaller amount than
the verdict, or take a new trial, since, if defendant is entitled

NEW TRIAL NEW TRIAL2298
to a new trial, it should be granted without condition, and ,
if not, plaintiffshould have judgment for the full amount of
the verdict. L. & N. R. Co. v . Earl, 94 Ky . 368, 15 R. 184.
The court, having granted defendant a new trial, had no
right thereafterto set aside the order granting the new trial
upon the plaintiff'sremittinga part of hisjudgment, thereby
deprivingdefendantof the rightto an assessmentby a jury.
Johnson v .Keel, 12 R. 292.
Where a verdictis so flagrantlyexcessiveas to indicatethat
itwas influencedby passionor prejudicethe wrong can not be
rightedby the remissionof any part of the verdict, or by any
act shortof givingthe party complaininga new trialbefore
an unbiased jury. Evansville Grain Co. v . Stout, 14 R. 927.
The rule is different when the excess is attributable to an error
in the instructions,if itcan be shown how much of the damage
is attributableto the error. The party claiming under the
verdict must make this showing . Merrick v . Holt, 7 R. 758,
8 R. 162 .
The jury having found a verdictjustifiedby both the evi-
dence and the instructions, and the excessivedamages being
due toa clericalerrorin theinstructions,the court,with plain-
tiff'sconsent, properly omitted so much of the verdict as was
necessary to cure the error in instructions, the amount of the
damages attributableto that error being manifest. Broadfoot
v . Rowe , 14 R. 895 .
A party to an action is entitledto a new trialonly when
his rightshave been prejudicedin the trial. If the rights
of neitherparty have been prejudiced in the trial, there is no
reason for granting a new trial; and the agreement of the
partiesto retry their case does not give them the right to do
SO. John B. Carter Co. v .Cox , 159 Ky .711, 169 S. W. 472 .
Where the items constitutingthe damages recovered are
separable, so that the courtmay eliminatethosenot properly
recoverablefrom thoserecoverable, the courtmay , on plain-
tiff'smotion, remitso much of the damages as representsthe
itemswhich are not properlyrecoverable. C. & O. Ry . Co.v.
Meyers , 150 Ky . 841, 151 S. W. 19.

NEWLY DISCOV . 2299 NEWLY DISCOV .
NEWLY DISCOVERED EVIDENCE .— Generally. Newly
discoveredevidence within the meaning of the law with refer-
ence to granting new trialsis not merely testimony given, or
that might be given, by living witnesses, but is any character
of evidence competent and allowable to illustrateand deter-
mine an issue of fact involved. L. & N. R. Co. v . Whitley
County Court, 100 Ky . 413, 38 S. W. 678, 18 R. 868.
" In Price v . Thompson , 84 Ky . 219, certainrules were laid
down governing applicationsfor a new trialthat have been
uniformly adhered to, one of them being, 'That the evidence
discovered will tend to prove facts which were not directlyin
issue on the trialnor were not then known or investigatedby
the proof'; another, 'That the new evidenceis not merely
cumulative'; and in Allen v.Perry ,6 Bush ,85, the further rule
that has been constantlyadhered to was announced that 'To
entitlethe party to a new trialthe discovered evidence must
be of such permanent and unerring characteras to preponderate
greatly or have a decisiveinfluenceupon the evidence to be
overturned by it'; and in Louisville& Nashville Railroad v .
Ueltschi, 126 Ky . 556, it was further said: 'New trialsare
reluctantlygranted; and , when disputed matters have been
fully litigated, the courts will not re-open a case for the
purpose of enablingthe defeatedparty to introducenew evi-
dence unless the reasons why it should be done are very
strong and it ismade to appear with reasonablecertaintythat
injusticeor wrong would resultunless the reliefwas granted
and another opportunity allowed to re-litigatethe question in
issue." National Concrete ConstructionCo. v . Duvall, 153 Ky .
394,155.S. W. 757.
In an action involvingthe locationof a boundary line, the
proceedings and report by the processionersreliedupon for a
new trialbeing not only ex parte but subsequent to the judg-
ment were not " newly discoveredevidence" within the mean-
ing of the Code . Beck v . Brown , 4 R. 437 .
The generalrulesof law governing applicationsfor new trials
on the ground of newly discovered evidence have been stated
by the Court of Appeals , as follows: " 1. That the names of

NEWLY DISCOV . 2300 NEWLY DISCOV .
the witnesseswho have been discovered must appear ; 2. that
the appellanthas been vigilantin preparing his case for trial;
3. that the new factswere discoveredafterthe trial, and would
be important; 4. that the evidencediscoveredwilllead tend to
prove factswhich were not directlyin issue on the trialor
were not then known , nor investigatedby the proof; 5. that
the new evidence is not merely cumulative." Price's Admr .
v . Thompson , 84 Ky . 224, 8 R. 201.
Decisive,Controlling, Convincing Character. Where newly
discoveredevidenceis unerringand convincing, satisfyingthe
mind of thejudge thatitwillprobablyhave a preponderating
influenceupon another trial, a new trialshould be granted.
Owsley v. Owsley, 117 Ky . 47, 77 S. W. 397, 25 R. 1186.
Newly discoveredevidence which is not of a decisiveor con-
trollingcharacter will not authorize a new trial. Johnson v .
Stivers, 95 Ky . 128, 15 R. 477 .
To entitlea party to a new trialon the ground of newly
discoveredevidence,that evidence must be of such a permanent
and unerring character as to preponderate greatly or have a
decisive influenceupon the evidence to be overturned by it,
and generally a new trialwill not be granted upon the sole
ground of a discoveryafterjudgment of paroltestimonycon-
cerning a point litigated. Cahill v. Mullins, 101 S. W. 336,
31 R. 72 .
To entitlea partyto a new trial,when thepointupon which
it is sought was in issuein the former trial, the newly discov-
ered evidencemust be of such a permanent and unerring char-
acteras topreponderategreatlyor have decisiveinfluenceupon
the evidence to be overturnedby it. Paducah Ice Co. v .H. E.
Hall & Co., 113 S. W. 104 .
Newly discoveredevidencenot of a conclusiveor prepon-
derating character does not authorize a new trial. Shely v.
Shely, 47 S. W. 1071, 20 R. 1021.
A new trialwillnot be granted in any stateof case where
the newly discovered evidence is merely cumulative, or unless
it be of such a permanent and unerring character as to have
decisiveinfluenceon the evidenceto be overturnedby it. Ellis
v . Com ., 146 Ky . 715, 143 S. W. 425.

Cumulative Evidence. New trialsare reluctantlygranted,
and a new trialwill not be granted on the ground of newly
discovered evidence if the new evidence is merely cumulative
or does not tend to prove factswhich were not directlyin
issueon the trial; nor unless the discoveredevidence willhave
a decisiveinfluenceupon the evidenceto be overturnedby it.
National Concrete Construction Co. v . Duvall, 153 Ky . 394,
155 S. W. 757 .
CorroboratingEvidence. A new trialwillnot be granted
on the ground of newly discoveredevidencewhere it serves
only to corroboratethe evidence establishingthe very factsto
which proof has alreadybeen introduced. Mulloy v. City of
Louisville,161 Ky .596,171 S. W. 190.
CriminalCases. The practiceas to grantingnew trials
for newly discoveredevidenceis the same in criminalas in
civilcases; and a new trialwillnot be grantedon thisground
unlessthe party askingthe new trialin additionto the other
requisitesfileshis affidavitsettingout that he did not know
and by the exercise of reasonable diligencecould not have
known of the existenceof the newly discoveredevidence until
after the trialhad concluded. Ellisv. Com ., 146 Ky . 715, 143
S. W. 425 .
The rule as to granting a new trialfor newly discovered
evidence is the same in criminal as in civil cases . A new
trialwill not be granted on account of newly discovered evi-
dence which is merely cumulative, or which only tends to
discreditor impeachan opposingwitness. Hays v.Com .,140
Ky .184,130 S. W. 987.
Evidence Discreditingor Impeaching Witness. A new
trialwill not be granted except in rare cases on account of
newly discovered evidence, which only tends to discreditor
impeachan opposingwitness. Pricev.Thompson ,84 Ky .219;
L. & N. R. Co.v.Ueltschi'sAdmr .,126 Ky .556;Hayes v.Com-
monwealth , 140 Ky . 184 ; Ellisv . Com ., 146 Ky . 715, 143 S.
W. 425 .
To Prevent Injusticeor Wrong . New trialsare reluctantly
granted. When disputed matters have been fully litigated

the courts will not reopen a case to enable the defeated party
to introduce new evidence, unless it be made to appear with

Parol Evidence . Generally a new trialwillnot be granted
upon the sole ground of a discovery after judgment of parol
testimony concerning a point litigated. Cahillv . Mullins, 101
S. W. 336, 31 R. 72 ,and casesthereincited.
NEWPORT SAND .- Newport is the name of a city and
Newport Sand means sand taken from the earth at, in, or
near that city. Any one engaged in the sand business in that
city would have a perfect right to sellhis sand as " Newport
Sand ," and no cause of complaint would thereby be afforded
any other person dealing in that sand . But where one so
advertisesitsbusinessas to perpetratea wrong and fraud
upon another by leading the public to believethat it is in
facttheplaintiff'ssand,as by naming a grade of itssand "No.
5 Newport," as anotherdealerhad done, equity will grant
reliefwithout regard to the commercial value of the defendant's
sand. Newport Sand Bank Co. v. Monarch Sand Mining Co.,
144 Ky .7,137 S. W. 784,34 L. R. A. (N.S.) 1040.

isexpresslydesignated.'
NEXT FRIEND .- " No person shallsue as next friend unless

accordingto the provisionsof this chapter." Civil Code,

146 Ky . 153 , 142 S. W. 207.

ing that in which such reference is made , unless when some

Under the provisionsof the localoption law of August 6,
1892, requiringthe county judge to make an order directingan

NOISES . The necessitiesof commerce demand the opera-

thesenoises, and there can be no nuisance in the operationof
a railroad or of its switch-yards, unless the noises created
thereby are unnecessary in itsoperation. L. & N. R. Co. v .
Com ., 158 Ky . 773, 166 S. W. 237.
Although the ordinary noisesof moving trains are not an
element of damage , if, on account of the manner of the con-
structionof the road at a particularpoint, though rendered

granted were forfeitedby nonuser. Cincinnati, N. O. & T.
P. Ry .Co.v.Baughman ,116 Ky .479,76 S. W. 351, 25 R. 705.
NONUSER OF LAW .- When the intention of the law-
maker is clearlymanifest in the law itselfnonuser can not
invalidateit. Ex parte Herrick, 78 Ky . 23.
asNOON . In determining whether the word " noon ,"
used in a written contract of insurance, meant twelve o'clock
standard time, the following guide should be given to the
jury: " If the jury believefrom the evidencethatat the time
the policy of insurance was issued there existed in the place
where the contractwas made a custom or usage with reference
to the meaning of the word noon , so well settled, uniformly
acted upon , and of such continuance as to raisea presumption
that plaintiffsand defendants knew of it, and entered into
the contract of insurance sued on with referenceto it, such
usage will govern the jury in arriving at their conclusions."

752 , 87 S. W. 1115, 89 S. W. 3 , 27 R. 1155, 1 L. R. A.
(N.S.) 364.
Noon is merely a shorter expressionthan twelve o'clockin
the daytime . Id .

confinement in the State penitentiaryfor not less than two

NORMAL .- Use of word normal as applied to water rate.

NORTH .- "North " and " northwardly," in common par-
lance,do not mean thesame thing. "North" conveysa definite
idea; " northwardly" does not. The latter, both in propriety
of speech and in common parlance, means " towards, or
approachingtowards the north, ratherthan towards any of
the other cardinalpoints." Craig v . Hawkins ' Heirs, 1 Bibb .
54. See, also, Simpkins ' Admrs . v . Wells, 42 S. W. 348, 19
R. 881 .
NOSCITUR EX SOCIIS.- Nosciturex sociis, quinon cog-
noscitur ex se. He who can not be known from himself may
be known from his associates; so the meaning of a word may
be ascertained from the context. Cochran's Law Lexicon .
This doctrineapplied in U. S. v . L. & N. R. Co., 236 U. S.
334 .
A full discussionof the meaning and applicationof this
maxim may be found in Broom's Legal Maxims .

NOTE . It isprovidedin NegotiableInst. Law , Ky . Stats.
(1915), Sec. 3720b, Subsec. 190, that, unless the context other-
wise requires, the word " note" means negotiablepromissory

Under a devise of bank stocks and bonds, stocks in other

794 , 139 S. W. 968 .
" A negotiablepromissory note within the meaning of this

to order or to bearer . Where a note is drawn to the maker's

able Inst. Law , Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec. 3720b, Subsec. 184.

In an action on a note defendant allegedin his answer that

1321, Ky . Stats. Held , that the answer was insufficient, as it

A note payable to A., "administrator" of B., will be

NOT DOING A THING .- The statement of an injured

NOT DOUBTING . “ The words 'desire,' 'willand desire,'

Major v . Herndon , 78 Ky . 128 .
NOT EXCEEDING.The amount due upon the mortgage

It was claimed that the advertisement of an election did

Haven v . Hardinsburg Graded Common School DistrictNo.
4, 164 Ky . 511, 175 S. W. 994.
Under a contractbetween A. and B., whereby B. agrees to

NOT EXCUSABLE . An instruction is erroneous which

Halsey v. Com ., 10 Ky . Opin . 671.
NOT FOUND .- The return of " not found and not to be

up v.Bacon,1 Mon. 108.
In an action against M. and others the summons was

NOT GUILTY .- An entry on the record of the plea of not
guilty may be substantiallyin the following form : "The

The defendant in a penal action should not be required to

ant had failed and refused to listfor taxation for the year

having made a motion that the statement be made more

A plea of not guilty puts in issue an allegationin an

NOT IN SELF -DEFENSE.- Under Ky .Stats.,Sec.4,giving
a rightof actionto the widow and minor childrenof a person
killed by the careless, wanton , or malicious use of firearms,

the killing was in self-defense or was accidental is admissible

where the defendant, having affirmativelypleadedboth those

do the shooting in self-defense, and it was thereforeerror to

NOT LESS .- Under the statute which provides that a
person convicted the second time of felony shallbe confined
in the penitentiary" not less" than double the time of the first
conviction, if the punishment awarded for the second offense
be equal to or exceeds double the time of the former convic-
tion, judgment isto be rendered only for the punishment fixed
for the second offense. If, however , it be for lessthan double
the time of the former conviction it is to be increased to that
time. Chenowith v . Com ., 11 R. 561.
NOT LESS THAN A BLOCK .- See BLOCK .
NOT REQUIRED TO BE DEPOSITED .- The provisionof

other officer," means not requiredby the charterto be deposited

NOTCHES ON PISTOL .- Evidence to the effect that

NOTHING BY DESCENT .- Same judgment should be

In an actionat law heirshaving receivednothing by descent

NOTICE .- See, also, IMMEDIATE NOTICE; KNOWLEDGE .
Generally. "Notice may be the existenceof that which

inquiryas ordinaryprudence would suggest, would resultin

One who purchases with noticethat another has an equitable

itors, and would lead to the knowledge of the requisitefact

196;Everidgev.Martin,164 Ky .497,175 S. W. 1004.
Whatever puts a party on inquiryamounts in judgment of

Notice, certainto a common intent, is sufficient. Bradley v.

Certainty to a common intentonly required in notice. Bar-
bour v . Whitlock, 4 Mon. 181.

Meaning of " notice" in Ky . Stats., Sec. 1907, referredto,

Informationwhich shouldput a prudentpersonupon inquiry

An agreementfortheserviceofnoticeupon one of theparties,

Noticerequiredby law must be givenby the personrequired

Vol.II,Acts 1857-58,page 124,chap.518,as amended, incor-
porated a drainingcompany for the reclamationof swamp

Actual and Constructive Notice . Actual notice is such
notice as is required to be given in some particularway to
such owner . Constructive notice is such as results from some
public act required to be done and in a particularmanner ,
and of which the owners of the property upon which the
burdens are imposed are required to take notice. C. & 0 .
Ry . Co. v .Mullins, 94 Ky . 355, 15 R. 139.
Implied or Presumptive Notice. Implied or presumptive
notice may be equally as effectiveas positivenotice; but it
must not be that noticewhich isbarely sufficientto put a party
on inquiry. Suspicion of noticeisnot sufficient. The inference
of a fraudulent intent affectingthe consciencemust be clear
and unquestionable. Willisv . Vallette, 4 Met .189 .
Possessionof Personalty. Possessionofpersonaltyisnotice
to all the world of the holder's title. Hewitt v . Sturtevant,

Facts Sufficientto ExciteInquiry. Factssufficienttoput
one upon inquiry as to an infirmityin an obligationare

Deed as Notice . A deed to be notice must so reasonably

Notices Under Code . " The notices mentioned in this

serve a summons , or by any person who is over the age of

sumption of itsdelivery, but thatfactisproper testimonyto

Want of Notice. Where a party getswhat he asks, he is
not prejudiced by want of notice. Conclin v . Grand Central
Savings & B. Assn ., 144 Ky . 237, 138 S. W. 312.
Notice to Take Depositions. "The noticemust be in
writing,signedby thepartygivingit,or hisattorney; must be

Notice of Dishonor . Under the Negotiable Instruments

scribingwhat shallconstitutenoticeof an infirmityin com-

Survey one month oldnotpresumed to be notorious. Cleland

NOTORIOUS .- Where defendant, on trialfor a felony,

NOVATION .- The substitutionof a new obligationfor an
old one which isthereby extingished, e. g ., the acceptance of a

A novation is the making of a new contract, its elements

" Novation may be effectedin threeways : (1) By the sub-
stitutionof a new obligationbetween the same parties, with
intentto extinguishthe old obligation; (2) by the substitution

the placeof the old one, with intentto transferthe rightsof
the latterto the former." Russellv . Centers, 153 Ky .473, 155
S. W. 1149 .
A novationisa contract,and likeothervalidcontractsmust
be supportedby a consideration, which isthe dischargeof the

Acceptance by creditorof a new in lieu of an old note

of all. Stuart v .Groves, 5 R. 687.

A stipulationthat if the new agreement is not performed

and itsacceptante by the holder in lieu thereof is a complete

lishment by parol proof in the same measure as those of a

The principalpaid the obligeeintereston notespast due

the obligorsforany fixedor definiteperiodor to indulgethem
at all. This was not a novation and the sureties were not

NOW OWING .- Where a grantee assumed " allthe indebted-

NOW RESIDES .- An allegationthat a plaintiff" now

NUDUM PACTUM . A bare contract, i. e., one not under.
seal, and made withoutany considerationupon which, there-
fore,no actionwilllie. Cochran'sLaw Lexicon.
A unilateralexecutorycontractisin law a nudum pactum,

an agreement to choose whether he will proceed or abandon

NUISANCE . Generally. The doing of a lawfulthingin a
carefuland prudent manner can not be a nuisance. L. & N.
R. Co. v. Com ., 158 Ky .773,166 S. W. 237.
Every enjoymentby one of hisown propertywhich conflicts
with the rightsof anotherin an essentialdegreeisa nuisance,
and a use of property which was at common law a nuisance does

statuteand a differentpunishment provided. The party creat-
ing the nuisance may be pursued under eithercommon law or
statutoryremedy . L. & N. R. Co. v . Com ., 16 R. 347.
To constitute the offense of a common nuisance it was not
necessary that the act charged should have been habitual or
periodical. It is the resultof the act, rather than the act itself,
that constitutesa nuisance, and where a singleact produces a
continuing result, it may be sufficientto maintain the indict-
ment . Greenbaum v . Com ., 10 R. 723 .
The common law punishment for a public nuisance, not hav-
ing been changed by statute, is stillin forcein thisState.
Sullivan v . Com ., 13 R. 397.
It is not necessary in order to constitutethe offensethat
actualinjury be sufferedby any person. Cin.R. R. Co. v. Com .,
80 Ky . 137, 3 R. 639.
An indictment is the proper remedy for a common nuisance,
and an individual is entitledto an injunction only when he

of (the foulstench, etc., from a slaughterhouse) was common
to thefiveplaintiffs,doesnotmake ita publicnuisance. Seifried
v .Hays , 81 Ky . 377, 5 R. 369.
While an actionnominallyagainstan officer, but really
against the State, to enforce performance of its obligationin

itspoliticalcapacitycan not be maintained, yet officersand

enjoinedfrom so using such propertyas to createa nuisance.
Herr v. The Central Ky . Lunatic Asylum , 97 Ky .458, 30 S. W.
971, 17 R. 320, 28 L. R. A. 394.
A city council can not, by ordinance, declare a thing to be

Board of Council,117 Ky .199; City of Versaillesv.Ky .High-
land R. Co., 153 Ky . 83, 154 S. W. 388.
A nuisanceper se is an act, or omissionor use of prop-
erty or thing, which is of itselfhurtful to the health, tran-
quilityor morals, or outrages the decency of the community .
Open and gross lewdness, or whatever openly outrages decency
and isinjuriousto publicmorals isa misdemeanor and indict-
able at common law . Thus the livingtogether of a man and
woman unmarried, which is generallyknown throughoutthe

indecency is not a necessaryelement. Adams v . Com ., 162 Ky .

A provision, which requiresa policeman to " remove all

A nuisanceis not necessarilya physicalobstructionplaced

supra. When it requires a policeman to remove a nuisance
which constitutesan obstruction,itishisduty to actuallyremove

han , 149, Ky . 537, 149 S. W. 932.

dated Coal& Coking Co.v.Lynch,141 Ky .711,133 S. W. 763.

lishing a nuisance which inflictsspecial damage on an indi-
vidual remains; and hence, where the factsin an actionagainst

of the community. It is not permissibleor excusableunder
any circumstances. Ehrlickv. Com ., 125 Ky . 742, 102 S. W.
289,31 R. 401,10 L. R. A. (N.S.) 995, 128 Am .St.Rep .269;
quoted in Adams v . Com ., 162 Ку . 76, 80, 171 S. W. 1006.

NUISANCE NUISANCE2345
PublicNuisances- Generally. Publicnuisancesare of two
classes, viz.: physical, tangibleobjects, e. g ., a house or fence
in a public highway ; and those which being merely moral are
intangible, e. g . the assembling and misconduct of persons at
a disorderlyhouse, the keeping of a tipplinghouse, etc. Gray
v.Ayers , 7 Dana , 376.
A private individualcan enjoin or recover damages for a
public nuisance when he shows injury distinct from that
sufferedby the public. LouisvilleHome Tel. Co. v . City of
Louisville, 130 Ky . 611, 113 S. W. 855.
A common nuisance is a misdemeanor at the common law,
and punishableas such under indictment. Com . v. Beals,
119 S. W. 813 .
A public nuisance may be abated by anybody ; a private
nuisance only by those who are injured by it; an instruction
which disregards these distinctionsand tells the jury that
" if the dam was a nuisance defendants had a right to abate
it" ismisleading. Gates v. Blincoe,2 Dana , 159.
A city ordinance penal in its character, the language of
which clearlypoints to a physical nuisance, can not by con-
structionbe made to apply to a moral nuisance. Krickle v .
Com ., 1 B. M. 361.
Though an individualmay be liableto a pecuniary punish-
ment for a prohibitedoffense, yet the habitual practiceof that
offense may constitutethe place or house where it is so
habituallycarriedon a publicnuisance. Smith v. Com ., 6
B. M. 21 ; Wilson v . Com ., 12 В. М. 2.
Private individualsseeking reliefagainst a public nuisance
must show that they sufferan injury distinctfrom that suffered
by the general public, and which the public has not the right
to inflicton them without compensation. Barr v . Stevens, 1
Bibb ,293 ;Cosby v .O. & R. R. R.,10 Bush , 291.
PrivateNuisance. A publicnuisancemay become a private
one where specialinjury arises, and particularlywhen it is
that character of nuisance that is continual and affects the
partycomplainingto a greaterextentthanthepublicgenerally.
In such caseon the applicationof the complainantthe chancel-
lorshould abate the nuisance. Green v.Asher , 10 R. 1006.

NUISANCE NUISANCE2346
Approaches to Railroad Crossing. A railroadcompany
which failsto keep in repair the approaches to a highway
crossingto the extent of itsrightof way is guilty of maintain-
ing a nuisance,whether the railroador highway was established
first. Com .v . L. & N. R. Co., 58 S. W. 478 ,702 ,22 R. 572.
Barbed Wire Fence . A barbed wire fence is not as a matter
of law a nuisance, and the owner is not liablefor an injury
inflictedupon the stock of others which may stray upon the
land and be driven or run against the fence by the owner of
it, when in the lawful and prudent exerciseof his own business
upon his own land. Hoard v .Morris, 10 R. 771.
Base Ball. The courtswillnot enjointhe proposedopera-
tion of a base ball park adjoining residenceproperty, as base
ball is the great American game , and is not per se a nuisance.
Alexander v .Tebeau , 71 S. W. 427, 24 R. 1305 .
Beer Garden . A garden in a cityused as a beer garden
and pleasureresortis not in itselfa nuisance, although itmay
make the adjoiningproperty lesseligibleas a place of residence
forsome people,and may subjectthe neighbors to inconvenience
and annoyance . Pfingstv.Senn ,94 Ky .556,15 R. 325 .
Blacksmith Shop . A blacksmithshop is not of itselfa
nuisance. Stone v .Burkhead , 161 Ky . 745, 171 S. W. 417.
A blacksmith shop in the residence portion of a city or
town is not a nuisance per se; and hence a nuisance is not
created by its operation in the ordinary and usual manner .
Morris v . Roberson, 137 Ky . 841, 127 S. W. 481, 136 Am . St.
Rep . 323 .
An injunction will not be granted to prevent the erection
of a blacksmith'sshop near plaintiff'sresidence, as such shop
is not per se a nuisance. Marrs v. Fiddler, 69 S. W. 953,
24 R. 722 .
BuildingOperations. The failureof personserectinga
brick building to safeguard, by barricadesor other means , a
street rendered unsafe by the danger of bricks fallingfrom
the wall of thebuilding, isa nuisance. Young v. Trapp, 118
Ky . 813, 82 S. W. 429,26 R. 752.

NUISANCE NUISANCE2347
Buildings Connected Over Alley. A connectionbetween
two buildings, over a public alley, at such height as not to
interferewith the use of the alley for passage and not to
obstructthe lightand air, is not a public nuisance. Leitchfield
Mercantile Co. v . Com ., 143 Ky . 163, 136 S. W. 639.
A building over an alleywhich does not substantiallyinter-
fere with itsuse by the public is not a nuisance, although it
may be a purpresture. LeitchfieldMercantile Co. v . Com .,
143 Ky . 163, 136 S. W. 639.
City Prison. A cityis not liableto the owner of a lot
adjacentto itscityprisonbecausethe property, by reasonof
the prison, is renderedlessdesirablefor a residence. If the
prisonisbadlykept thekeeperisliableforhisnegligence,but
thecityisnot. A cityprisonisnotper se a nuisance. City of
Bowling Green v . Rogers, 142 Ky . 558, 134 S. W. 921, 34
L. R. A. (N.S.) 461.
Common Law Offense Covered by Statute. In 2 Rober-
son'sKentucky CriminalLaw and Procedure, Sec. 632, it is
said: " Any use of property which was at common law a
nuisance does not cease to be so because the same act is made
an offenseby statuteand a differentpunishmentprovided. The
party creatingthe nuisancemay be pursued under eitherthe
common law or statutoryremedy . And where the act charged
as a common nuisance is an act prohibited by statute, upon
failureto show that it was such as to annoy the public, so as
there can be no convictionfor the allegednuisance, there may
neverthelessbe a conviction under the statute. A public
nuisance is a misdemeanor at common law , and the common
law punishment is by fineor imprisonment, or both, which
has not been changed in this State." In support of the fore-
going text Roberson citesL. & N. R. Co. v . Commonwealth ,
16 R. 347; Greenbaum v. Commonwealth, 10 R. 723, and
Sullivanv . Commonwealth , 13 R. 397 ; Com v . Ky . Distilleries
& Warehouse Co., 154 Ky . 787, 159 S. W. 570.
Dam . The erection of a dam without leave, whereby
another'sland is flooded, is a nuisancefor which an action
forthe tortlies. A mere promiseto pay the damages assessed

NUISANCE NUISANCE2348
in such case would not bar the
will it furnish a basisfor a suit.
action for the nuisance, nor
Cave v. Calmes, 3 Mar. 36.
DisorderlyConduct . Dancing and drinking, accompanied
by swearing, drunkenness, making loud noises, and otherwise
misbehaving, constitutea " common nuisance," indictableas
a public offense. Com . v. Cin., N. O. & T. P. Ry . Co., 139
Ky .429,112 S. W. 613,33 R. 1056.
Electric Plant. Usual operation of electricplant does not
constitutenuisance. Hughes v .General Elec.Co.,54 S. W. 723 ,
21 R. 1202 .
Excavation . An excavationsix or eight feet deep running
up to the edge of a pavement on a streetin a populous city
is a nuisance if leftat night without sufficientlights, guards ,
safe-guards, or barricades to warn passers-by . Matheny v .
Wolffs, 2 Duv . 139.
Gambling House . A gamblinghouse or pool room, where
money or other things of value are bet and won or lost on
horse races, and where tickets, pools, and chances on horse races
run or to be run in the State or in the United States and
Canada are bought and sold, are publicnuisances. Respassv .
Com ., 131 Ky .807, 115 S. W. 1131.
Gaming . All personswho set up, operate, or promote a
common gaming house, including its employes, are guilty of
maintaining a nuisance. Ehrlick v . Com ., 125 Ky . 742, 102
S. W. 289, 31 R. 401, 10 L. R. A. (N.S.) 995.
Grocery- Disorderly. Keepinggrocerywhere personsare in
the habit of assembling on the Sabbath and tipplingand drink-
ing is a public nuisance. Smith v . Com ., 6 В. М. 22.
Hitching Posts. Although trustees of a town have con-
tractedwith a county that the lattermight erecthitching posts
on the publicsquare of a city, the cityisnot estoppedfrom
removing the hitching posts when they become a nuisance.
Mercer County v . City of Harrodsburg , 66 S. W. 10, 23 R.
1744, 56 L. R. A. 583 .

NUISANCE NUISANCE2349
Indictment . To constitutea good indictment it is not
necessary to allegethat the acts were done " to the common
nuisance," etc. Com . v. Enright, 98 Ky . 635, 33 S. W. 1111,
17 R. 1183 .
An indictment for a nuisance which concludes merely
" to the common nuisance of all good citizens" is not good
where the nuisance is committed in carrying on a lawful trade
which may or may not be a nuisance, according to itslocation,
and thereforean indictment for a nuisance in keeping cattle-
pens must conclude " to the common nuisance of all persons
there living and abiding, or there passing and repassing, or
having the right to pass," or the indictment itselfmust show
proximityto human habitationsor highways, and the use of
streams which would be damaged by their pollution. Com . v .
T. J. Megibben Co., 101 Ky . 195, 40 S. W. 694, 19 R. 291.
Obstructing Highways . For obstructinga highway by
cars a railroadcompany may be indictedand convictedof
maintaininga nuisance, notwithstandingKy . Stats., Sec.4335,
providesa penaltyforsuch obstruction,which may be recovered
in a civilaction. Com .v .I. C. R. Co. 92 S. W. 944, 29 R. 102 .
An allegationin an indictment that an obstruction" did
thereby hinder, delay, and obstructthose using said road
to the common nuisance of all good citizens of
Kentucky ," was a sufficientallegationthat itwas to the common
nuisanceof allpersonshaving the rightto pass and repass.
I. C. R. Co. v . Com ., 96 S. W. 467, 29 R. 754.
If one places an obstructionin a public highway , anyone
who isincommoded thereby,may remove it; but,unlesshe has
occasionto make use of the highway , he must leave the publis
injury to be redressed by the public authorities. It is the
existenceof an emergencywhich justifiesthe interferenceof the
individual; and ,generallyspeaking, whenever a public nuisance
obstructstheexerciseof a privateright,thepartyhinderedmay
abate the same, exercisingdue care in doing so. Walter v.
LouisvilleRy . Co., 150 Ky . 652, 150 S. W. 824.
It is not necessary that an indictment for obstructing a
highway should charge in terms a " nuisance" or that the act

NUISANCE NUISANCE2350
done was " to the common nuisance ." L. & N. R. Co. v .
Com ., 5 R. 322.
A fence in a road is a nuisance at common law, and he who
built it might be fined and imprisoned. Gregory v . Com ., 2
Dana , 417.
Any obstructionin or over a highway that substantially
renders it more difficultof passage or less commodious is an
indictable common -law nuisance, but if it does not sub-
stantiallyaffectthe publicuse it isnot indictableas a common-
law nuisance; the gistof the offenseis the hurt to the public.
LeitchfieldMercantile Co. v . Com ., 143 Ky . 163, 136 S. W. 639 .
The occupation of a public highway to the exclusionof all
other people is a public nuisance. Stein v . C. & O. Ry . Co.,
132 Ky . 322,116 S. W. 733.
Any act of an individualdone to a highway, though per-
formed on his own soil, if it detracts from the safety of
travelers, is a nuisance. Matheny v . Wolffs, 2 Duv . 138.
If the owner with a view to repair or build his house
employs his own servants and in the execution of the work
they encroach on the highway , the owner will be guilty of a
nuisance, and if he contractswith a third person to do the
same thing for a sum of money , he will be equally guilty.
Robinson v . Webb , 11 Bush , 476 .
Obstructions in Streets. A city may be indictedfor
maintaining a common nuisance where it permits a street to
be dangerous for travel although the officersof the city may
be liableto indictment therefor. City of Ludlow v . Com ., 147
Ky .706,145 S. W. 406.
Obstructionson the footwalksof the streetsof a municipality
whether created by the accumulation of ice or otherwise,
except such as are temporarily permitted for a lawful purpose
are a nuisance, and the party responsiblethereforis liableto
the individualinjured by his illegalact. L. & N. R. Co. v .
Mulverhill, 147 Ky . 360,144 S. W. 83.
A nuisanceisnot necessarilya physicalobstructionplaced
in the street,and which can be removed by physicalexertion;
it signifiesanything that worketh hurt, inconvenience or

NUISANCE NUISANCE2351
damage. A dangerousholecausedby a sudden caving of the
surface of the streetis a nuisance within the statute, which
requiresa policeman to remove all nuisances from the street.
City of Louisvillev. Lenehan , 149 Ky . 537, 149 S. W. 932.
A barbed wire stretchedacross a streetto prevent travel
on the part of the streetundergoing repairsis a nuisance, and
the city is liablefor an injury to one who , in the absence of
any lightor other warning , runs againstthe wire in the night-
time. City of Glasgow v . Gillenwaters, 113 Ky . 140, 67 S. W.
381 , 23 R. 2375 .
Obstructing Navigable Streams . The obstructionof a
navigablestreamisprima faciea publicnuisance,and any one
sustainingdamages by reason of it may maintain an action.
Simpson v . Kelley, 90 S. W. 241, 28 R. 702.
OffensiveOdors- Cesspools. The owner of premiseshas
no legalrightto throw out,even on hisown premises,decaying
meats and vegetables from which offensiveand unhealthful
odors arise, to the injury of his neighbors, nor has he the
legal right to locate on his premises cesspoolsfrom which
unhealthful and offensiveodors arise, to the injury of his
neighbors and if he does either of these things, his conduct
may be the basis of an individual action against him for a
nuisance. Livezeyv.Schmidt,96 Ky .441;Ky .Dist.& Ware-
house Co. v. Barrett, 112 S. W. 643 ; Cumberland Grocery
Co. v .Baugh's Admr ., 151 Ky . 641, 152 S. W. 565.
Offensiveodors of a loathsome trade, if detrimental to the
comfort of those dwelling around and to passersby, are a
nuisance and may be abated, although not actuallyproducing
disease. Ashbrook v . Com ., 1 Bush , 140.
The pursuit of a noxious trade is lawful only so long as it
does not interferewith the rightsof the public; but when it
does so interfereit becomes illegal, and no length of time can
sanctify it, as its exerciseis a daily renewal of the offense.
Ashbrook v . Com ., 1 Bush , 140.
Pens in a city, wherein are kept large numbers of cattle,
etc.,heldto be a nuisance,notwithstandingsaidpens had been

NUISANCE NUISANCE2352
continuouslykept for thatpurpose for thirtyyears, and when
establishedwere some distance in the country; but by the
extensionof the citysuch pens thereafterbecame a nuisance.
Ashbrook v . Com ., 1 Bush , 140.
The pursuit of a noxious trade is lawful so long as it does
not interferewith the rights of the public, but when it does
so interfereit becomes illegal, and no length of time will
sanctifyit. Ashbrook v . Com ., 1 Bush , 140 .
Smells and offensiveodors of a loathsome trade, if detri-
mental to the comfort of thosedwelling around and to passersby,
is a nuisance and should be abated, although not actually
producingdisease. Ashbrook v.Com ., 1 Bush,140.
Stock yards and pens in a city wherein are kept large
numbers of horses, cattle, hogs, etc., held to be a nuisance,
although kept for thirtyyears, and when firstestablishedwere
outsideof the city. Ashbrook v . Com ., 1 Bush , 140.
Picnics . While it can not be said as a matter of law that
picnics, beer gardens, bowling alleys, and the likeare per se
nuisances, yet they may be so conducted as to become hurtful
to the health and destructiveof the comfort of the community
in which they may be exercised, and when this happens the
hurtful thing must be abated, for no one can have the legal
rightto do that which destroyshis neighbor'sproperty or
health. PalestineB. Assn . v. Minor , 86 S. W. 695, 27 R. 781 .
Pollutionof Spring . Where a person is entitledto the use
of the water of a spring on his neighbor's ground it is a
nuisanceagainsthim to pollutethe water. Tate v. Parish,
7 Mon. 327 .
Prison. A prisonisnot per se a nuisance. City of Bowling
Green v. Rogers, 142 Ky . 558, 134 S. W. 921, 34 L. R. A.
(N.S.) 461.
Prize Fight. A private citizencan not maintain an action
to enjoin a prize fightas a public nuisance, unless he suffers
some specialinjury. LouisvilleAthleticClub v. Nolan, 134
Ky .220, 119 S. W. 800, 23 L. R. A. (N.S.) 1019.

NUISANCE NUISANCE2353
aRailroads- Switch Yard - Noises. The operation of
railroadtrain, and of switching yards in connectiontherewith,
is a lawful occupation, and the carrying on of itsbusinessand
the necessary incidentsthereto, when done in a careful and
reasonableway and without unnecessary noises, can not be a
nuisance. L. & N. R. Co. v . Com ., 158 Ky .773, 166 S. W. 237.
Rock Thrown Into River . Where a railroad and its con-
tractors, by blasting rock into a river and permitting it to
remain there, divert the flow of the water and cause it to flow
on and injure the land of an adjoiningland-owner , the act is
unlawful and wrongful from the outset, and constitutesa
continuing nuisance; and a recovery may be had for each
recurring injury. Turner v . Brooks & Sons, 151 Ky . 310,
151 S. W. 948 .
Sewers Emptied into Stream . The use by municipalities
of a stream as a receptacle for the contents of the sewers of
the city, constitutesboth a public and private nuisance, for
which the owners of adjoining lands may maintain an action
against the city for damages . City of Henderson v . Herron ,
152 Ky . 341, 153 S. W. 440.
Signals for Crossings. An habitualfailureof trainsto
give signalsor warnings as they approach intersectingroads,
isan indictablenuisance. L. & N. R. Co. v .Com ., 13 Bush ,388 .
Stable . The erection of a private stable near a church
will not be enjoined as a nuisance, as the stable is not a
nuisance in itself, and may or may not become so according to
circumstances. Albany Christian Church v . Wilborn , 112 Ky .
507 , 66 S. W. 285 , 23 R. 1820 .
As a stable is not per se a nuisance, and may not become
such if properly managed , injunction will not lie to restrain
defendant from erectinga stable across the streetand forty-
two feet from plaintiff'sresidence. Hyden v . Terry, 108
S. W. 241, 32 R. 1198.
Stationary Engine on Highway . It is an actionable
nuisanceto establishin or upon a publichighway a stationary
steam engine. Smith v .I. C. R. Co., 105 S. W. 96, 31 R. 1323 .

NUL NULLUM TEMPUS2354
Many of the paragraphs under this titleare taken, by
permission, from Digest Kentucky Reports (Morton & Co.).
NUL . No , or none Nul tielagard, or record; no such
award , or record; a plea to an action on an arbitrationbond ,
or judgment , when the defendant denies that there was any
lawful award or judgment rendered. Cochran's Law Lexicon.
NULLA BONA .- (No goods), a returnmade by a sheriff
to a fi. fa., or other writ commanding him to seizethe goods
of a person, when he finds no property to distrain upon .
Cochran's Law Lexicon .
NULLA PACTIONE EFFICI .- Nulla pactione efficipotest
ut dolus praestetur. By no contractcan one effectthat a fraud
shall be maintained . Cochran's Law Lexicon .
NULLUM SIMILE EST IDEM .- Nullum simile est idem
nisiquatuorpedibuscurrit. No likeisidentical, unlessitrun
on " all fours." Cochran's Law Lexicon .
NULLUM TEMPUS OCCURRIT REGI , OR REI PUBLI-
CAE . No time can prejudice the king [or commonwealth ].
In other words , the Statutesof Limitationdo not run against
the crown or the State. Cochran's Law Lexicon .
" The maxim of the common law is, that 'nullum tempus
occuritregi.' Besides, itis a settledprinciplethat the king
isnot bound by a statute, withoutbeing expresslynamed ."
Com .v .M'Gowan , 4 Bibb , 63.
" The prerogativeof royalty in regard to the limitationof
actionsextendsto republicangovernments, and the maxim ,
'Nullum tempus occurritregi,' appliesin the United States,
both as to the Federal Government and as to the several
States, except where express statutory provisionsto the con-
traryexist." Seibertv. City of Louisville, 125 Ky . 296, 101
S. W. 325 .
This maxim isquoted in Beeler v . Coy ,9 B. Mon. 315.
A full discussionof the meaning and applicationof this
maxim may be found in Broom's Legal Maxims .

NULLUS , NULLA 2355 NUMERICAL WEIGHT
NULLUS , NULLA .- No , or none .
NULLUS COMMODUM .- Nullus commodum capere potest
de injuriasua propria. No one can obtain an advantage by his
own wrong . Cochran's Law Lexicon .
NULLUS HOMO CAPIATUR . “ What is meant by a
taking may be understoodfrom the expositionsof Magna Charta,
in England . The privilegessecuredby the twenty-ninth chapter
of Magna Charta, begin thus " Nullus liber homo capiatur,
vel imprisonetur,' etc. 'No freeman shallbe taken or impris-
oned,' etc. In the commentary upon this chapter, second
institute, 46, Lord Coke says, 'attached and arrested, are
comprehended herein.' No man shall be taken, that is
restrainedof liberty. Those definitionsand commentaries upon
the words 'taken,' 'capiatur,' (or capias) and 'arrest,' explain
theirlegalsignification. They do not comprehend the service
of a process,by which no imprisonment , no restraintof liberty,
no bail is required, but only a notice, or copy of the process."
LeGrand v . Bedinger, 4 T. B. Monroe , 540 .
NUL TIEL RECORD.- (No such record). Nul tielrecord
concludes to the court and is tried by the court. Carson v .
Pearl, 4 J. J. M. 95 .
NUMBERS.- It is provided in the Tenement House Act
that for the purposes of that Act the singularnumber includes
the plural number , and the word " person" includesa corpora-
tion. Acts 1912, p . 573 ; Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec. 3037g .
An indictment for carrying two weapons concealed is
sustained by proof that the accused carried one of them ;
proof of carrying both is not necessary. Com . v . Howard ,
3 Met . 408. See SINGULAR NUMBER .
NUMERICAL WEIGHT OF EVIDENCE .— The demeanor of
a witness often throws great lightupon his credibility, and the
verdictof a jury having the witnessbefore them is rarely
disturbed simply because the number of witnesseson one side
isgreaterthan on the other. Witnessesare to be weighed, not
counted, and verdictswillnot be setasideon more numerical

NUMERICAL WEIGHT 2356 NUNC PRO TUNC
weight of evidence, unlesssupportedby circumstances. Louis-
villeWater Co. v . Williams, 140 Ky . 320, 131 S. W. 10.
Juriesare the triersof fact. They decidethe weight to be
given the testimony of the witnesses. The fact that the
numerical weight of the testimonyis againsttheir finding
affordsno ground for reversal, ifthereissubstantialevidence
to support it. Harbison & Walker Co. v. White , 114 S. W. 250.
Where the testimonyis sufficientto sustaina verdict, the
Court of Appeals has no power to controlthe judgment of a
jury and say thatitshouldhave believedthe testimonyof one
side and rejectedthat of another. Rutherford v . Bath Co.,
80 S. W. 815, 26 R. 126.
The Court of Appeals will not set aside a verdict because
of mere numericalsuperiorityof witnessesforappellant. Id.
NUNC PRO TUNC .- A proceeding taken, judgment
declared,etc.,now forthen. Where a proceeding,etc.,has been
delayed by the action of the court, or any like ground , the
court may allow it to be dated as if ithad taken place or been
delivered on the earlier date . Cochran's Law Lexicon .
The officeof a nunc pro tunc order isto supply on the record
something which has actuallyoccurred in the court, but omitted
to be noted of record. It can not supply omitted actionby the
court. Jett v .Farmers Bank of Ky ., 76 S. W. 385, 25 R. 817.
The general rule is that when an order or directionof the
court has been omitted by inadvertenceor mistake on the part
of the judge or clerk,and there is record evidence showing that
all the steps necessary to have the omitted order or direction
entered were duly made and taken, and by a referenceto this
record the court without any other evidencecan see what judg-
ment or order was intended to be entered, it may from this
record evidence enter, as of the date when itshould have been
entered, a nunc pro tunc order. Rallsv. Sharp'sAdmr ., 140
Ky . 744, 131 S. W. 998.
The rule is well settledthat the court can not alter, correct
or amend its records at a subsequent term by the recollection
of the judge or the representationof others to him . There
must be at least a minute or a memorandum on the court record

NUNC PRO TUNC 2357 NUNC PRO TUNC
evidencingthe orderor judgment then renderedto justifythe
court at a subsequent term entering a nunc pro tunc order.
Kendrick v. Williams, 157 Ky . 767, 164 S. W. 72 .
Under the maxim that " An act of the court shallprejudice
no one" (Broom's Legal Maxims , p. 115), made to alsoread
by itsfrequentapplicationto thisclassof casesas " A delay
of the courtshallprejudiceno one," the practiceof entering
judgmentsnunc pro tunc exists,and has from very earlytimes.
Doune v. Lewis, 11 Ves. 601. Nor istherea limitationupon
the time when it may be done, unlesssuch be found in the
modern statutesof limitation; one instancebeing recordedwhere
such an entrywas made twenty-threeyearsafterthejudgment
was rendered. Danl . Ch . Pr . 1219. The entry of such judg-
ments are always to protectsome rightthat has arisensince
thejudgment was delivered,as otherwisea judgment now would
be as efficaciousas ifenteredthen. For that reason, and to that
end, in pursuance of the maxims just quoted, the courts will
entera judgment which was in factrendered,but which through
the omissionof the clerk,or othercasualty,has not been recorded;
but, before proceeding to order such judgment entered as
of the date of its rendition, strictevidence is required that
itwas then so rendered. Therefore the rule isthat such judg-
ment can be entered only upon evidence of the factof itsrendi-
tion contained in the record itself. One purpose of entering a
judgment now for then is to supply a matter of evidence, and
not to alteror createfacts. If the court had failedto act, or
had acted erroneously, the matter can not be correctedby a
judgment at a laterterm; but if it had acted, and the record
shows it, but there was not made sufficientlyformal evidence
of the fact,the evidencemay be made to conform to the truth
as shown by record. Montgomery v . Viers, 130 Ky . 700, 114
S. W. 251 .
" From the earliesttimes courts of law and equity have pos-
sessedand exercisedthepower of making entriesof judgments
and decreesnunc pro tunc in caseswhere such entriesare neces-
sary to prevent injusticeto suitors. Mohun's Case, 6 Mod . 59 ;
Mayor of Norwich v . Berry , 4 Burr , 2277 ; Freeman on Judg-
ments,Sec.56. Such power doesnot depend upon any statute,

NUNC PRO TUNC 2358 NUNC PRO TUNC
but itisinherentin the courts. Mitchellv .Overman , 103 U. S.
62. The power is exercisednot for the purpose of enabling
the court to correctjudicialerrors, but to supply matters of
evidence. If the court has not rendered a judgment that it
might or should have rendered, or if it has rendered an imper-
fector improper judgment, ithas no power to remedy these
errors of omissionsby ordering the entry nunc pro tunc of a
proper judgment . Freeman on Judgments , Sec. 68 ; Gray v .
Brignardello, 1 Wall. 627; Smith v.Hood , 25 Pa . St.218, 64
Am . Dec. 692. One of the classesof casesin which judgments
may be entered nunc pro tunc is where the former judgment
has been pronounced by the court, but not entered of record
by reasonof some accidentor mistake, or through the neglect
or omissionor misprisionof the clerk. In such a case the court
renderingthe judgment has the power to order the judgment
sorenderedtobe enterednunc pro tunc,providedtherebe satis-
actory evidence, not only of the rendition, but of the terms of
the judgment . Freeman on Judgments , Sec. 61 ; Whorley v .
Memphis , etc., R. Co., 72 Ala. 20 ; Shephard v. Brenton, 20
Iowa , 41." Chester v . Graves, 159 Ky . 244, 166 S. W. 998.
The mere recollectionof the judge of a court of what took
place at a former term isnot sufficientto authorizean addition
to, or an amendment of, the record in regard to any order or
judgment. There must be somethingin the recordby which
to amend . Boyd County v .Ross, 95 Ky . 167, 15 R. 520 .
The re-enactmentof a cityordinancelevyinga tax, and the
passage of a nunc pro tunc order directingthe clerk to record
the " yea and nay " vote which had been taken on the original
passageof theordinance,curedthedefectin failingtooriginally
record the yeas and nays as required by Sec. 3489, Ky Stats.
City of Pinevillev .Burchfield,19 R. 984,42 S. W. 340.
The lastday of the term at which judgment was rendered,
a billwas signedby the judge , and indorsed by the clerk" filed
in court," with the date of filing, but the clerk failedto enter
an ordershowingthefiling. At the nextterm,on motion,after
notice,a nunc pro tuncordercuringtheomissionwas proper.
L. & N. R. Co. v. Reed , 10 R. 495.

A nunc pro tunc order filinga billof exceptionsentered in

can not be considered on a petitionfor rehearing. Louisville
Bridge Co.v.Neafus,62 S. W. 2,63 S. W. 600,23 R. 183.
An entry in the clerk'sminutes giving the styleof the case
and the casenumber, followedby the abbreviation" Judgt.,"

Monarch v .Brey , 51 S. W. 191, 21 R. 279 .
Where , in an actionin equity, the partiesagreedthatsuch
of the testimony as had not previouslybeen taken by depositions
might be orallyheard by the court on the trial, as in an ordi-
nary actionwhere the rightof jury is waived, and such agree-

therewith. Conrad's Exr .v .Conrad ,156 Ky .231,160 S. W. 937.
Where in an adoption proceeding there is among the papers
in the case a judgment containingthe followingnotation: " Let
the above order be entered and of effect from this date . The

Judge," such paper is a sufficientquasi recordto justifythe

NUNCUPATIVE WILL .- See WILLS .
NURSING , WASHING .-Though under the statute no
person other than the keeper of a tavern or house of private

promise to pay thereforbe established, there may be an
impliedcontractto pay for" nursing,washing, feedingstock,"

NYMPHOMANIA . “ But, while the sensesare apparently
sound and true, the affectionsmay be perverted, or the moral
sentiments unhinged in such a degree as to subjugate the will

to some morbid appetiteor ungovernablepassion, and thus

mania, homicidalmania, etc., now well-definedand recognized
as irresponsibleinsanity." St. Louis Mutual Life Ins. Co. v .

OATH . It is provided in Civil Code , Sec. 732, that in the
constructionof the Code the word " oath" includesaffirmation,
in any case in which itmay be substitutedfor an oath, and in
like cases the word " sworn " includes affirmed.
It isprovidedin Ky . Stats., Secs.446,451, thatin the con-
structionof statutes, " the word 'oath' shall be construed to
include affirmationin allcases in which an affirmationmay be
substitutedfor an oath; and in like cases, the word 'sworn '
shall be construed to include the word 'affirm.' '"
The oath for allofficers, members of the General Assembly
and members of the bar, is prescribedby Ky . Constitution
(1891), Sec.228 .
" An oath required by this Code may be substitutedby the
affirmationof a person who isconscientiouslyopposed to taking
an oath." CivilCode, Sec. 680.
A magistrate is not authorizedto issue a warrant of arrest
without oath or affirmation made before him of facts sufficient
to constitutereasonablegrounds for believingthat the accused
has been guiltyof a public offense. The personalknowledge of
the magistratewillnot justifythe issualof a warrant . He may
himselfreduceto writtenform the informationthus received,
or may take an affidavit, and in eitherevent willnot be liable
under Sec.1372,Ky .Stats.;but whether he reduces the informa-
tion so obtained on oath or affirmationto written form or not,
he isnot liablein civildamages ,where he has in factso obtained
it. Clark v . Hampton , 163 Ky . 698, 174 S. W. 490.
Under Ky . Stats.,Sec.2157,providingthatupon an inquest
of lunacy the personalpresenceof the personchargedmay be

dispensed with if certain facts are made to appear " by the
oath" of two regular practicingphysicians, the oral testimony
of two physiciansshowing the necessaryfactsis sufficient, writ-
ten affidavitsnot being required. Oster v.Meyer , 113 Ky . 181,
67 S. W. 851, 23 R. 2455.
An oath to the jury to try the case and a true verdictrender
isa substantialcompliancewith Sec. 217, CriminalCode, pro-
viding for an oath to " well and truly try the issueand a true
verdictrender." Young v.Com .,42 S. W. 1141, 19 R. 929.
A witnessisto be sworn in such form as he considersbinding
on his conscience; but if a witness is, without objection, sworn
in the usual form ,itshould be presumed that the mode conforms
to his conscience, and he can not thereafterplead his scruples
as a defense to a charge of perjury. Com . v . Jarboe, 89 Ky .

The terms " corporal oath" and " solemn oath," are now to
be understoodas synonymous. Com .v.Jarboe,89 Ky . 143, 11
R. 344 .
OBEDIENT .- An undertaking " that the defendant will
render himself obedientto the orders and processof the court"
is equivalent to " that he shall at all times render himself
amenable," etc. Sec. 77, Criminal Code. It is sufficientif
the undertakingbe substantiallyas required. Millerv. Com .,
1 Duv . 15 .
" Obedient is to be 'submissive to authority, yielding com-
pliance with commands , orders or injunctions; performing
what is required, or abstainingfrom what is forbid.'" Id.
That the bond of special bail stipulatesthat defendant
" will render himself amenable to the court thereupon," the
words " process of" being omitted does not make the bond
invalid. In substance and import it is sufficient. Abbott v .
Daniel, 3 Met . 340.
OBITER DICTUM .- A saying by the way . An opinion of
a judge , not necessary to the judgment given of record, and
consequently of lessauthority. Cochran's Law Lexicon.
An expressionused in deliveringan opinion must be under-
stoodas limitedto the casepresentedin the record, if not to

that stated in the opinion, not as concluding any right not
litigated. Conwell v. Sandidge, 8 Dana , 277.
Where the Court of Appeals must interpret a statute, an
explanation of what is necessary to bring the case under the

139 Ky . 693 , 132 S. W. 413 .
OBJECTIONS .-See , also, EXCEPTIONS .
" 1. An exceptionis an objectiontaken to a decisionof the
court upon a matter of law.
" 2.A party may , withouta previousobjectionexceptto a
decisionagainsthim , unlessit be made at the instance of the
adverse party.
" 3. But a party can not except to a decisionmade at the
instanceof the adverse party, unless objectionsshallhave been
made to the motion, offeror request of the adverse party."
Civil Code , Sec. 333.
Where a voter merely asked the judge if he would be
required to answer a question asking him for whom he had
voted in a primary election, he sufficientlyraised the question
of his privilegeagainst incrimination. Heitzman v . Voiers,
155 Ky . 39 , 159 S. W. 625 .
Neither objection nor exception is necessary to enable
plaintiffto complain of error in giving peremptory instruction.
Loving v . Warren Co.,14 Bush , 316 ; Collinsv. Potts, 9 R. 536 .
Objections and Exceptions to Instructions. In order to
take advantage on appeal of an error of the court in giving
or refusing an instructionwhich was offeredby the adverse
party, there should be both an objectionto the instructionand
an exceptionto theactionof thecourtin givingit. CivilCode,
Sec. 333 ; Newman's Pleading and Practice, Sec. 669, note 9 ;
Kennedy v . Cunningham , 2 Met . 540 ; Letton v . Young , 2 Met .
564 ; Garrott v .Ratliff, 83 Ky . 386, 7 R. 463 ; Davis v . Bailey,
53 S. W. 31, 21 R. 839.
Objection to the giving of such instruction, without except-
ing to the decision of the court thereon, is not sufficient.
CivilCode, Sec.333; Cox v. Winston, 3 Met. 577; L. & N. R.
Co. v .Bocock , 107 Ky . 223, 53 S. W. 262, 21 R. 896 ; L. & N.
R. Co. v . Graves,78 Ky .74 ; Reed v .Com ., 7 Bush , 641.

OBJECTIVE OBLIGATION2363
Where the court gives an instructionon itsown motion, no
objectionis necessary; an exception is sufficient. And where
neither party asks specificinstructions, but each requests the
court to give the law to the jury, and the court thereupon

for review alleged errors in the charge. LouisvilleSo. Ry .
Co. v .Hooe , 35 S. W. 266, 18 R. 521,
Reed v. Com ., 7 Bush , 642, in which the court held that the

afterthey were given, isoverruledby Poston v . Smith , 8 Bush ,

OBLIGATION OF CONTRACTS .—“ No ex postfactolaw,
nor any law impairing the obligationof contracts, shall be
enacted." Ky . Constitution (1891), Sec. 19.
The obligationof a contractis that which obligesa person
to perform his contract, or to repair the injury done by a
failureto perform it. Collinsv . Collins,79 Ky .88, 1 R. 323 .
Our " fourmostapprovedlexicographers,Johnson,Sheridan,
Walker and Jones, all in substance define the word to mean
the bindingpower of any oath, vow , duty or contract; an act
which binds any man to some performance. The obligationof
a contractis then, itsbinding power ; it is that which compels
itsperformance ; in other words ,itisas definedby the Supreme
Court of the United States, 2 Wheaton 197, " the law of the
contract." Blair v . Williams, 4 Litt. 65.
Congress has no power to pass laws impairing the obligation
of contractsbeyond the expressly granted power over bank-
ruptcy. Griswold v . Hepburn , 2 Duv . 45.
That clause in the Federal Constitution which declares that
no stateshallpass any law impairing the obligationof contracts
refersto the legalobligation, and not to the obligationarising
from consciencealone. Blair v . Williams, 4 Litt. 35.
The only differencenow (as to the consideration) between
an obligationand an assumpsit is, that in the latterthe onus
ison the plaintiff,in the former itis on thedefendant. Conway
v .Bank U. S., 6 J. J. M. 132 .
The legalobligationof a contractconsistsin the legalremedy
for enforcingit. Blair v. Williams, 4 Litt. 36; Lapsley v.
Brashears,4 Litt.54.
The word impairing in the Constitutiondoes not mean
destroying; consequently every law which weakens the obliga-
tionof a contractpreviouslymade ,or rendersitlessoperative,
or so changes the remedy as to impair the obligation, isa viola-
tion of the Constitution. Lapsley v. Brashears, 4 Litt. 53 ;
Berry v.Ransdell,4 Met.294.

lativeact thatmakes the rightlessvaluableand availableimpairs
the obligationof the contract. Griswold v .Hepburn , 2 Duv .44 .
An officeestablishedand held for the public good is not a
contract,nor isitstenure secured by any binding contract. Any

itbe a contractwhich can not be impaired by legislation. Standi-

Marriage, though in some respectsa contract, isnot within

v . Johnson, 3 B. M. 90 ; Cabell v . Cabell, 1 Met . 326.

assume the characterof binding contracts, which the legislature

Although the Constitutionof the United States forbids any

contract,thereisthe exceptionto thisgeneral rulethat the State
can not contractaway itspolicepower , or itsright to abrogate
or annul contractsithas made in contraventionof thispower.
Id .
A charter granted by the State is a contract between the
State and the partiesto whom itisgranted. Id .

shall engage in business other than that expressly authorized
by itscharter,or thelaw under which itmay have been or here

OBSTRUCTING ELECTION .- The sheriff of the election

to do so. Therefore, where a policeman excluded an inspector,

OBSTRUCTING 2369 OBSTRUCTING JUS .
OBSTRUCTING HIGHWAY .-The common law remedy for

Chap . 110, Ky . Stats. Com . v . American Tel. & Tel. Co., 84
S. W. 519, 27 R. 29 .
Where plaintiffwas injured in driving by strikinghis buggy
againsta pileof lumber which extended fiveor six feetinto the

unobstructed, the court properly instructedthe jury that " it
was the duty of defendant to keep itsroad in reasonably safe
conditionfor travel, and free from obstructionsof such width
thatwillpermit vehiclesto pass each otherwith safetyby the use
of ordinary care" ; and plaintiffwas not prejudiced by the

OBSTRUCTING JUSTICE .-He who knows that another

ventinghistestimony,isguiltyof obstructingjustice,although

The obstructionof legaljusticewas a misdemeanor at com-
mon law, and the spiritingaway of a witnessfrom testifying

Where money isplaced in a person'shands to be held till

appearon thetrialof a pendingcase,and then tobe paid over

galitydid not affecthis rightas a principalto recoverthe

116 S. W. 1196 .

OBSTRUCTING JUS . 2370 OBSTRUCTING RAIL .

pany and others. Com . v . Southern Express Co., 160 Ky . 1 ,

Where a person, in aid of a contemplated suit, endeavors
to persuade a party that he has been injured by the negligence
of another,when in factthe party has not been injured,and this
is known to the person prevailingon him to say he is, such
person is guiltyof the common -law misdemeanor of obstructing
justiceand may be punished for thisoffenseby a fine in any
amount or by imprisonmentforany time or both by such fine
and imprisonment . And this offense is complete when the
person attempts by fraudulent methods to secure the institution
of the suit,although itmay never be brought. Melton v . Com .,
160 Ky . 642,170 S. W. 37.
An indictment charging that the defendant corruptly and

and falsestatement, which he would not otherwisehave made ,

to obstructjustice. Ruark v . Com ., 150 Ky .47, 150 S. W. 5.
" Any contractto impede the due courseof publicjusticeis

settleand adjust offersesof a criminal nature, the punishment
