OBSTRUCTION OF STREETS .- The streetsand alleys
are public highways . An encroachment upon them substan-
tiallyinterferingwith the public use is a public nuisance. But
to constitutea public nuisance there must be a substantial
interferenceor obstructionof the public right. Leitchfield
Mercantile Co. v. Com ., 143 Ky . 163, 136 S. W. 639.
The citizenhas a rightto the use of the streetor the right to
complainifany unreasonableobstructionisplacedinit,but this
rightto the use of the streetissubjectto the rightof the owner
of propertyto a reasonableuse of the streetin improvinghis
property. How far the property-owner may obstructthe street
inimprovinghispropertydependsupon thecircumstances. He
may not make an obstructionwhich the municipal authorities
forbid, but the consent of the municipal authoritieswill not
legalizean obstructionwhich thelaw forbids;thatis,itwillnot
authorize an unreasonable obstruction of the street. But the
obstructionof sidewalks in front of large buildings which are
in processof erectionisunavoidable. Button v . City of Louis-
ville, 118 S. W. 977.
In matters of thissortsome weight must be given the judg-
ment of the municipal authoritiesand to the universalcustom
in the erectionof buildingsin cities. Id .
A wire three-eighthsof an inch thick, running from a point
in the ground eighteen inches out in a twenty-foot alley in a
city and along a fence so near itscolor as to render the wire
practicallyundiscernible, may be regardedas an obstruction
dangerous to individualsand vehiclespassing over or through
the alley. LouisvilleHome Tel. Co. v .Gasper , 123 Ky .128, 93
S. W. 1057,29 R. 578, 9 L. R. A. (N.S.) 548.
The obstructionof a streetby an abutting owner must be
temporary in itsnature and such as is reasonablynecessary for
the transactionof businessor the enjoyment of the premises, and
the obstruction must also be reasonable with reference to the
rights of the public. If an obstructioncontinues for such a
period as to amount to an unreasonable interfenence with the
public travel, it is unlawful and a nuisance. The question
whether an obstructionisallowableor a nuisanceisgenerally

OBSTRUCTION OF 2372 OBSTRUCTING SUIT
a question of fact to be determined by the facts of each
particular case. Stratton & Terstegge Co. v . Meriwether,
154 Ky .839,159 S. W. 613.
Where the city authorizeswork to be done, or a part of a
streetto be obstructedwhile the work isgoing on, itmust take
notice of the obstructionwhich it has authorized, and see that
the highway iskept reasonablysafe. City of Louisvillev .Keher ,
117 Ky .841 ; Board of Councilmen v . Allen, 26 R. 583 ; Blocher
v. Dieco, 99 S. W. 606 ; Grider v . JeffersonRealty Co., 116
S. W. 691 ; Τown of Bellevue v .Rentz, 152 Ky .426, 153 S. W.
732 .
To the grant by a cityof the rightof way upon itsstreetsand
alleysthe law attachesthe conditionthat the use by the grantee
shallnot " prevent,obstruct,or unreasonably impede the passage
of persons, wagons , or other vehicles," and when the grantee
has the rightto use a streetor alleyas often as he pleases, and
every time he uses it such use totallyobstructsfor the time,
however short, the ordinary public travel, the obstructionis an
unreasonable one and the grant is unauthorized. 92 Ky . 149,
13 R. 705 .
A citymust keep itsstreetsin a reasonably safe condition,
and where in making improvements it becomes necessary to
placeobstructionstherein,ismust use reasonablecareto protect
persons using the streetat night from injury by giving notice,
by the use of lightsor other means , reasonably sufficientto
warn the travelingpublic of the presence of the obstructions.
City of Georgetown v . Groff, 136 Ky . 662, 124 S. W. 888.
Citiesand towns must keep theirstreetsin reasonablysafe
conditionfor travel, but in repairinga streetthey may
placethereinmaterial, etc.,properlyused in the work, though
reasonablecareshould be used to avoid injury therefrom. Elam
v . City of Mt. Sterling,132 Ky .657,117 S. W. 250, 20 L. R. A.
(N.S.) 512 .
See also, NUISANCE .
OBSTRUCTING SUIT.—Ky. Stats., Sec.2532, which saves
the right of action as againstdebtors who obstruct the action
or concealthemselvesso that they can not be sued, applies

OBSTRUCTING 2373 OBSTRUCTING SUIT
only as against residentsof thisState. Aultman & Taylor Co.
v .Meade , 89 S. W. 137, 28 R. 208 .
Unless the actsof a surety are in point of fact such as would
hinder and preventthe creditorfrom bringingsuit, notwith-
standing his desireto do so, or unless the surety has by fraud
procured the creditorto delay bringing suit, he can not be said
to " obstruct or hinder " the creditor within the meaning of the
statute, which providesthatifthe surety" obstructor hinder
hisbeing sued, the time of such obstructionshallnot be com-
puted as part of the time of limitation." Reid v .Hamilton , 92
Ky . 619, 13 R. 849.
The statuteof limitationonly saves the right as against a
party who has obstructeda suitagainsthimself;itdoes not apply
to a case where a defendant had interfered with an action
against another party for the same cause of action. Smith v .
Jones, 3 Dana , 89.
The surety on a note givenby an insolventto a bank procured
the debtor to depositmoney in the bank ,which the latterappro-
priatedto the note. Subsequentlyothercreditorsof the insol-
vent brought an action, in which it was adjudged that this
appropriationconstituteda fraudulentpreference, and the bank
was compelled to pay back the money , lessitspro rata share.
Held,thatunderKy .Stats.,sec.2552,providingthatifa surety
shallobstructor hinder his being sued, the time of such obstruc-
tionshallnot be computed as part of the time of limitation,
theperiodinterveningbetweenthe bank'sappropriationof the
deposit and the termination of the suit to have such deposit
declared an illegalpreference,should be deducted from the per-
iod of limitationrunning in favor of the surety. Exchange Bank
of Ky .v. Thomas, 115 Ky .832,74 S. W. 1086,75 S. W. 283,
25 R. 228 .
" The terms used in this latter statute are, or 'otherwise
obstructor hinder'; and not, as in the former,'obstructand
defeat.' There is certainlya distinctionbetween the words
defeatand hinder. The latter, says Webster, means 'to inter-
poseobstaclesor impediments,'whilsttheformermeans 'frustra-
tion; a renderingnull and void; to resistwith success. "
Walker v . Sayers, 5 Bush , 582.

OBSTRUCTING OBSTRUCTION2374
Where one residing in Alabama and owing debts in New
York absconded and took up his residencein Kentucky , where
he livedunder an assumed name for twenty-fiveyears unknown
tohiscreditors, itisheld thatthey were not,withinthemean-
ing of the statute, prevented or obstructedby the fraudulent
change of hisname from suing in thisstate. McDonald v. Under-
hill, 10 Bush , 585.
An action isnot " obstructedor hindered," within the mean-
ing of the statute,by a verbal requestto forbear, or by a verbal
promise to pay at a future date. The words , " or otherwise
obstruct or hinder his being sued," as used in the statute,
importssuch actsas would hinder and preventthe creditor
from suing, notwithstandinghe desiredto do so. (Overruling
Walker v. Sayres, 5 Bush, 579) ;Kennedy v. Foster, 14 Bush,
479 .
A defensemade in good faithto a demand , whatever time
may be employed in exerting it, is no " obstruction" in the
meaning of the statute. Phillipsv .Shipp ,81 Ky .436, 5 R. 460 .
Under Ky . Stats., Sec. 2532 , a debtor's absenting himself
from the State is such an " obstruction " of the statute as sus-
pends itsoperation. So. Contract Co.'sAssignee v. Newhouse ,
119 Ky . 704, 66 S. W. 730, 23 R. 2141.
Actions to subjectestatedescended must be brought on the
originalliability, and therefore Ky . Stats., Sec. 2532 , as to
obstructionof suit, is applicable. Id .
OBSTRUCTION .- Meaning of " obstruction" of creek or
ditch, as used in drainage act. See Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec.
2412a .
" Obstructions within the meaning of this chapter (Public
Roads Act of 1914) shall include trees which have been cut
or have falleneither on adjacent land or within the bounds
of the public roads in such manner as to interferewith travel
thereon; limbs or trees which have fallen within the public
road or branches of treesoverhanging the public road so as to
interferewith the travelthereon, landslides, carcassesof dead
animals, lumber, wood, or logspiledwithinthe bounds of the

OBSTRUCTION 2375 OBSTRUCTION OF
public road, fences, building or other obstructionswithin the
bounds of the public road, earth,stone or other materialplaced
in any ditch or waterway along the publicroads; telegraph,
telephone,trolleyor otherpolesand wiresconnectedtherewith,
erected on the. public road in such a way as to interferewith
the use thereof, or any other thing which shall prevent the
easy, safe and convenient use of such public road for public
travel." [Acts 1914,p.368.] Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec.4338.
A torpedo placed on a railroad track to warn a train of
another ahead isnot an obstructionthe placing of which on the
track constitutesnegligence, rendering the railroad liablefor
injuriesto a sectionhand caused by itsexplosion. Mize v. L. &
N. R. Co., 127 Ky . 496, 105 S. W. 908, 32 R. 415, 16 L. R. A.
(N.S.) 1084.
OBSTRUCTION OF EASEMENT .— The temporary placing
of material for railroadconstructionin a streetabout thirty-
two feetfrom the lineof plaintiff'sabuttingproperty, which
does not preventingressor egress, isnot such an obstruction
of his reasonablerightof passway for which damage willlie.
Chesapeake& O. Ry . Co. v.Kobs, 30 S. W. 6, 17 R. 130.
Plaintiffowned a passway over defendant'sland, sixteen
feetwide, which had been used for a long time and was well
marked . Defendant built his fence so as to make the path
straight, which changed its course somewhat . Both parties'
deeds referredto the passway as " then established." Held,
that such fence was an obstruction. Calvert v. Weddle , 44 S.
W. 648 , 19 R. 1883.
The obstructionby a railroadcompany of an easement across
itstracksby permitting cars to stand thereon for an unreason-
able length of time, is not affectedby Sec. 4354 , Ky . Stats.,
imposinga fineof ten dollarsupon " any personwho shallput
any obstructionsin a passway, or shallprop open, pulldown ,
injure, or leave open a gate erected across the same ," since
the obstructions referred to in the statute are held to amount
toa nuisancewhich may be abatedby theowner of thepassway,
and the owner of the passway would not have the right to go

OBSTRUCTION OF 2376 OBSTRUCTIONS ON
upon the railroad'srightof way and remove itscars. Zweigart
v . C. & O. Ry . Co., 161 Ky . 463, 170 S. W. 1194.
OBSTRUCTIONS ON RAILROAD RIGHT OF WAY.-
Obstructions on a railroadright of way which interferewith
the view of the traveleron the highway , so as to endanger life,
are improper if, by the exerciseof ordinarycare, they may
be removed; but the failureto remove cutsis not negligence,
though the railroadwas builtafterthe highway was established.
L. & N. R. Co. v . Breeden , 111 Ky . 729, 64 S. W. 667, 23 R.
1021 .
Where plaintiffcomplained of obstructionsto the view of a
railroadcrossing, the principalone of which was a cut, the
court should have instructed the jury that defendant was
not responsiblefor that obstruction, and that if by reason
thereofthe crossingwas especiallydangerous, both the rail-
road company and the traveleron the highway were required
to exerciseincreasedcare in proportionto the danger. Id.
It is actionablenegligenceto allow the rightof way to grow
up in weeds so as to obstructthe view of persons approaching
a crossing. L. & N. R. Co. v . Park's Admr ., 154 Ky . 269 , 157
S. W. 27 .
The instructionsshould cast on the travelerthe same degree
of careas on the railroadcompany. L. & N. R. Co. v. Park's
Admr ., 154 Ky .269, 157 S. W. 27.
It was proper to instructthat the company had the right
to erectdepots, stock pens, and other structuresnecessary for
itsuse at the places where it did erectthem . L. & N. R. Co.
v.Lucas'Admr .,98 S. W. 308,99 S. W. 359, 30 R. 359,539.
Where a woman on horseback is about to cross a railroad
at a crossingwhere there is a deep cut which prevents a view
of an approachingtrain, it is not contributorynegligence,
per se,to onlybringher horseto a slowwalk in orderto listen
for a train, insteadof coming to a fullstop. Ill. Cent. R.
Co. v.Mizell, 100 Ky . 235, 38 S. W. 5, 18 R. 738.
Where the court instructed that decedent in crossing a
railroadtrackwas requiredto look and listen, defendant was

OBSTRUCTION OF 2377 OBVIOUS DAN .
not prejudiced by the failureof the court to instructthat if
a pile of dirt prevented decedent from seeing an approaching
train, then it was her duty on that account to exercisegreater
care in going on or across the track, and such care as was
commensurate with the increaseddanger . C. & O. Ry . Co. v .
Wilson, 102 S. W. 810, 31 R. 500.
OBSTRUCTION OF STREAM . A boom built in a navi-
gable stream without authority of law is an unlawful obstruc-
tion of the stream and no damages may be recovered by the
owners of the boom from one who negligently injures it
by cuttingtreesintothe stream. McCown v. Langhorne, 147
Ky . 354, 144 S. W. 39.
OBTAINED.- Where theplaintiffmerely refersto thejudg-
ment ordering the sale of the land collaterallyto show how
he derived title, the averment that the judgment was " ob-
tained" should be treated as a substantialcompliance with
Sec. 122 of the Code , which says that it shallbe sufficientto
state that the judgment " was duly given or made ." Arnold
v. Stephens,13 R. 622.
OBTAINING MONEY BY FALSE PRETENSES . See
FALSE PRETENSES .
OBVIOUS DANGER.- Where a person drives through a
door or under a trestle, right before his eyes, he must take
notice of the sizeof the door or of the trestle, and not place
himselfin a positionwhere there is a liabilityof his being
struck. Paducah Pole & Timber Co. v . Brockwell, 161 Ky .
423 , 170 S. W. 970.
From the generalrule imposingupon the master the legal
duty of furnishingreasonablysafeplacesand appliances,there
isexcepted that classof employments that are in and of them-
selves dangerous, and the master is further exempted from
liabilityin respectto dangers which are open, visible, and
obvious to the comprehension of the servant, considering his
age,experience, and understanding. Louisville& A. R. Co. v.

OBVIOUSLY DAN . 2378 OCCUPATION
Wilson , 99 S. W. 634, 30 R. 734. See MASTER AND SERVANT , in
Digests.
OBVIOUSLY DANGEROUS .- Where the engineer of an
engine bound for a wreck has been informed of the wreck
and knows where it is, and the wreck is only two and one-
half miles distant and the track between the engine and the
wreck is closed, and no other collisionis to be anticipated,
it can not be said as a matter of law that the positionof one
who , in obedience to the order of the foreman in charge to
" ride the engine," securely fixes himself on the crossbeam
of the pilotin preference to riding with other employees on
the coal on the tender, is so obviouslydangerous that a person
of ordinary prudence would have refused to take it. I. C. R.
Co. v . Carter, 154 Ky . 373, 157 S. W. 719.
OCCASIONAL CARELESSNESS .- The instruction that a
servant assumes the ordinary risks incident to his service,
which includes the " occasional" carelessness, etc., of fellow
servants,and thatifplaintiff'sintestate,whilein the employ of
a telephone company , came to his death either from his own
negligence or from that of a fellow servant, it would not be
liable, was not misleading, though the word " occasional"
shouldnot have been used,otherinstructionshaving specifically
stated that plaintiffcould not recover if intestate'sdeath
was produced by the negligenceof fellowservants. Cumber-
land Tel. & Tel. Co. v . Ware , 115 Ky . 581, 74 S. W. 289, 24
R. 2519 .
OCCUPATION .- (Vocation.) Insured, a druggist, held
not engaged in hazardous occupation of hunting, where he
hunted forpleasure, fora shorttime only. Union Casualty&
S. Co. v . Goddard , 76 S. W. 832, 25 R. 1035.
The fact that plaintiff, who was insured as a " grocer, with
desk and counter duties," was injured while hunting, does not
deprive him of the right to recover in the absence of anything
to show that he was followingthe " occupation" of a hunter .
Ky .Life & Acc .Ins.Co. v .Franklin,102 Ky .512,43 S. W. 709 ,
19 R. 1573 .

OCCUPATION 2379 OCCUPATION TAX
Under a policy providing that insured should not keep a
liquorsaloon,and thatifhe shoulddieby reasonof the viola-
tion of that condition only the reserve value of the policy
should be paid,the fact that insured owned a half interestin a
saloondoes not limitthe recoveryto the reservevalue, as he
did not " keep " the saloon at his death, though he had done
so up to the time he became an invalid. Union Central Life
Ins.Co. v .Hughes 'Admr .,110 Ky .26,60 S. W. 850,22 R. 1549.
Employment of a boy under sixteenyears of age as a section
hand on a railroadis within Ky .Stats.,Sec.333-2a, Subsec. 11,
prohibiting employment of children under sixteen in " any
occupation dangerous or injurious to health or morals or to
livesor limbs." L. H. & St.L. Ry .Co. v.Lyons, 155 Ky .396,
159 S. W. 971 .
OCCUPATION .- (Of Land .) The maintenanceof a pipe
line for gas held to be an occupation of land. Richardson v .
Chenault'sAdmr .,31 S. W. 143,17 R. 372. See,also,ADVERSE
POSSESSION ; OCCUPIED .
OCCUPATION TAX . An ordinance imposing a licensetax
on " everyinsuranceadjuster" isan occupationtax,and isnot
a tax on the corporateexistenceor rightto do business- of
a corporationengaged in such business. French v. City of
Louisville, 152 Ky .12, 153 S. W. 42 .
A franchisetax is not a licenseor occupation tax, but an
ad valorem or property tax. City of Newport v . South Coving-
ton & CincinnatiSt. Ry . Co., 156 Ky . 403, 161 S. W. 222.
The constructionby the Court of Appeals of Kentucky that
the tax upon person compounding or blending spiritsisa license
tax or occupationtax, and not a property tax, willbe followed
by the Supreme Court of the United States. Brown -Forman
Co. v . Commonwealth , 217 U. S. 563.
In indictmentfor sellingliquor, etc., not necessaryto add
to the defendant'sname , his occupation, estate, or degree, as
yeoman , laborer,etc. Com .v .Rucker ,14 В. М. 229.
An ordinance requiring each barber shop to pay a license
of $5 per year and $2 additionalfor each chair where more

OCCUPATION TAX 2380 OCCUPIED
than two chairswere used was not a property tax, but one on
the occupation. City of Louisvillev. Schnell, 131 Ky . 104. 114
S. W. 742 .
A municipal corporation of the third class can not under
Sec. 3290, Subsec. 10, impose a licensefor revenue purposes
upon automobiles not used or let for hire. The statute in
questionis limitedby Sec.181 of the Constitution; and as such
a tax would not be an occupationtax, itcouldnot be upheld
under the statutein question. City of Henderson v . Lockett,
157 Ky . 366, 163 S. W. 199.
OCCUPIED.- Where plaintiff,suingunder Sec. 2, Chap . 5,
Gen. Stats., is the mere occupant of the land, and not the
owner , he must allege that neither he nor the owner has
received compensation for fencing it; it is not sufficientto
allege that he has not received such compensation; and from
the allegationthat he " occupied" the premises the presump-
tion arises that he was not the real owner . L. & N. R. Co. v .
Belcher, 89 Ky . 193, 11 R. 393 .
A statuteexempting from taxationthe property of an insti-
tution so long as it is " occupied" by the institutionfor the
purposesof itsorganization, does not exempt such portionsof
the property as may be rented out, although the rentsmay be
appliedto suchpurposes. Cityof Louisvillev.Board of Trade,
90 Ky .409 , 12 R. 397.
The fact that a tenant of insured and his servants had for
two days before the fire, which occurred in the night, been
cleaningthe insureddwellinghouse preparatoryto itsoccupa-
tiondid not constitutean occupation within the meaning of the
policy. Thomas v. Hartford Fire Ins. Co., 53 S. W. 297, 21
R. 914 .
A petitionwas not defectivein failingto use the word
" actual" in describing" possession," as itallegedthatplaintiff
had " occupied" the land for more than fifteenyears. The
use of the word " occupied" was tantamount to the word
" actual." Hall v . Roberts, 74 S. W. 199, 24 R. 2362.
It is providedin the Tenement House Act, applicableto
citiesof the firstclassthatwhenever the words " isoccupied"

OCCUPYING CLAIM . 2381 OF VALUE
are used in this act, applying to a building, such words shall
be construed as if followed by the words " or is intended,
arranged or designated (designed) to be occupied." Tenement
House Act , Ky . Stats. (1915 ), Sec. 3037g .
Where a policyinsuring a building " occupied as a dwelling
house" provided that it should be void " if the premises
described" shouldbe unoccupiedfor more than ten days, the
" premises" referredto were not the variousbuildingson the
tractof land on which the insured dwelling house was located,
but thedwellinghouse itself. Thomas v.Hartford Fire Ins.Co.,
53 S. W. 297 , 21 R. 914.
Where a policydescribesthe houseas " occupiedas a family
residence," and in anotherclauseprovidesthatitshallbecome
void if the house " shallbe or become vacantor unoccupied,"
the firstwords are but a representationas to the then use of
thehouse,and thelatterwords as but an undertakingthatthe
house shallnot be without an occupant during the lifeof the
policy. ImperialFireIns.Co.v.Kiernan,83 Ky .468,7 R. 542.
See noteof opinionof chancellorinsame case,4 R. 1009.
OCCUPYING CLAIMANTS .-For authorities as to who are
and who are not consideredoccupyingclaimantsseeKy .Stats.
(1915), Sec. 3728 , and notes.
ODIOSA ET INHONESTA .- Odiosa et inhonesta non sunt
in legepraesumenda. Odious and dishonestthingsare not to
be presumed in law . Cochran's Law Lexicon.
OF .- Signed by adding name of principalpreceded by
" of." See note on thissubjectin 42 L. R. A. (N.S.) 49.
" Of " read as " or," in phrase " squares of lots." Haller
v . City of Louisville,107 S. W. 741, 32 R. 1045 .
OF VALUE.- Under the statutewhich providesa punish-
ment for defacing, injuring, etc.," any property, realor per-
sonal, or other thing of value," if the thing injured fallswithin
the classof realor personalestate, itisnot necessaryto allege
that it was " of value" ; itsvalue is assumed . Com . v. Sears,
7 R. 219. See, also, VALUE .

OF VIRGINIA 2382 OFFENSE
OF VIRGINIA .- A complainant's stylinghimself, " of Vir-
ginia," in his bill, and statingthat he is " a residentof Vir-
ginia," in his replication, is a sufficientallegationof non-
residence, to entitlehim to sue in the general court. Banks
v .Fowler , 3 Litt.332.
OFF .- Under Sec. 170, CriminalCode, providingthat ifa
demurrer is sustained to an indictment on any other ground
than that it contains matter which is a legal defense or bar
to the indictment the case may be sent to another grand jury,
an indictment for maiming allegingthat defendant did " bite
of" insteadof "bite off" the ear of a certainperson, should
be resubmitted to the grand jury when a demurrer has been
sustainedfor such mistake. Com . v . Shelby, 38 S. W. 490, 18
R. 781 .
OFFENDING PARTY .- Where the policyprovidesthat the
insurance shall not be recovered if the insured is killed in a
quarrel in which he is the " offendingparty," the insured is
the " offendingparty" if he brings on the difficultythat ends
in his death by assaultingthe person who killshim , unlesshe
believesor has reasonablegrounds to believethat it was neces-
sary to act in the manner he did to protect himself. In an
action to recover the amount of an insurance policy, where it
was sought to defeat it upon the ground that the insured
was killed in a quarrel in which he was the offending party,
itwas not competent to prove his general reputation for peace
and quiet or that he was a violent, dangerous and quarrel-
some man . Knights ofMaccabeesof the World v. Shields, 156
Ky . 270, 160 S. W. 1043.
OFFENSE.- The word " offense" in Civil Code , Sec. 241,
which provides " A convictioncan not be had upon the testi-
mony of an accomplice, unless corroboratedby other evidence
tending to connect the defendant with the commission of the
offense; and thecorroborationisnot sufficient,ifitmerely show
thattheoffensewas committed,and thecircumstancesthereof,"

OFFENSE OFFENSIVE TO2383
includesboth feloniesand misdemeanors . Com . v .Barton , 153
Ky . 465, 156 S. W. 113.
Sec. 168, Ky . Constitution, providingthat " no municipal
ordinance shallfixa penalty for a violationthereofat lessthan
thatimposedby statuteforthesame offense," and that" a con-
victionor acquittalunder eithershallconstitutea bar to another
prosecution for the same offense," applies only to statutory
offenses; and thereforea conviction, under a municipalordi-
nance, of the offenseof maintaining a nuisance by operating
a pool room is not a bar to a prosecutionin the Circuit Court
for the same acts, constitutinga nuisance at common law, no
penalty being prescribedthereforby statute. Respass v. Com .,
107 Ky . 139, 53 S. W. 24, 21 R. 789.
Sec. 168, Ky . Constitution, providingthat "No municipal
ordinanceshallfixa penaltyfora violationthereofat lessthan
that imposed by statutefor the same offense" appliesto offenses
only for which a statutorypenalty has been fixed. Where the
offenseispunishableonly at common law the municipalordi-
nance may fixa penalty thereforat lessthan the common law
penalty. Cityof Owensborov.Simms ,99 Ky .49,34 S.W. 1085,
17 R. 1393 .
The Code in referenceto all " offensesof homicide" refers
todegreesrecognizedatcommon law. Conner v.Com .,13 Bush,
714 .
OFFENSIVE TO OTHER PASSENGERS .- The jury were
not properlyinstructed, as the instructiongiven erroneously
advised them that appellant'strainmen had no right to refuse
appellee entrance into the train unless he was so intoxicated
as to be " offensiveto other passengerson the train," or
unable to " care for himself." Whereas , they should have been
instructedthat if, when he attempted to enter the train,
appelleewas so intoxicatedas to affecthis conduct, make him
offensiveto other passengers, or render him unable to care for
himself, appellant'sservantshad the right to prevent him
from enteringthe train. C. & O. Ry . Co. v .Gatewood , 155 Ky .
102, 159 S. W. 660.

OFFER OFFICE2384
OFFER . A propositionor offerimposes no obligationon
the party making itunless it is acceptedby the party to whom
it ismade , and no obligationis then imposed unless the accept-
ance closesthe contract,so that itisbinding upon both parties;
for where the promise of one is the considerationof the other,
neither party can be bound unless the other is bound . Moxley
v . Moxley , 2 Met . 59 ; Second National Bank of Ashland v .
Rouse , 142 Ky . 612 ; New York Life Ins. Co. v . Levy , 122 Ky .
457 ; CitizensNational Life Ins. Co. v. Murphy , 154 Ky . 88,
156 S. W. 1069 .
Since an offeris not a contract the party making it may
withdraw it at any time before acceptance. Even though it
is in writing, and by itsterms is to stand open for a specified
period, the resultis the same . With no money consideration,
and no corresponding promise from the person to whom it is
made , the promise not to withdraw it has no binding force.
If a considerationfor the undertaking to leave the offeropen
is given and accepted, this constitutesof itselfa contract, and
the offercan not be withdrawn . Citizens National Life Ins.
Co. v . Murphy , 154 Ky . 88, 156 S. W. 1069. See, also, CON-
TRACT .
OFFERED BY DEFENDANT .- The assignment as ground
for new trial that the court erred in granting instructions
" offeredby defendant" isnot sufficientto authorizethe review
of instructionsgiven by the court on its own motion. Bailey
v .L. & N. R. Co.,44 S. W. 105,19 R. 1617.
OFFER TO CONFESS JUDGMENT .— Code provisionas to
offer. See CivilCode , Sec.640.
OFFICE . An " office" isa publicchargeor employment-
the right to exercisea private or public employment and to
take the fees and emoluments thereunto belonging. It implies
an authority to exercisesome portion of the sovereign power
of the State, eitherin making , administering, or executing the
laws. The power and jurisdictionof an officeconstitutethe
office, and are inseparablefrom it; and the true test of a
public officeseems to be that it is a part of the administration

OFFICE OFFICE2385
of government, civilor military. Com . v. Bush, 131 Ky . 384,
115 S. W. 249 .
An appointmentto an officeby thegovernorisnot the same
thing,ina constitutionalsense,as an electiontoan office. Speed
v . Crawford , 3 Met . 209.
An appointmentwithinthe meaning of the Constitutionis
completed when the commission is made out, signed by the
governor, and transmittedto the person appointed. Justices
of Jeffersonv. Clark, 1 Mon. 84.

"' An officerde facto is one whose acts, though not those of

to or invokehisaction,supposing him to be the officerhe assumed
to be . Second, under color of a known and valid appointment
or election,but where the officerhad failedto conform to some
precedent, requirement, or condition, as to take an oath, give
a bond , or the like. Third, under color of a known election
or appointment, void because the officerwas not eligible, or
because there was a want of power in the electingor appoint-
ing body , or by reason of some defect or irregularityin its
exercise,such ineligibility,want of power ,or defectbeing known
to the public. Fourth , under colorof an electionor an appoint-
ment by or pursuant to a public, unconstitutionallaw, before
the same is adjudged to be such.' These definitionsappear to
us to be exceptionallywell stated, and to cover fully every
state of case in which the authorityof a de facto officermay
be exercised." Wendt v . Berry , 154 Ky . 586, 157 S. W. 1115 .

District, 152 Ky . 519,153 S. W. 748.
A de facto officeris not entitled to the emoluments of the
office. Eubanks v . Montgomery County , 127 Ky . 261. While
his actsare validuntilthe statuteisdeclaredunconstitutional,
his subsequent actswillbe invalid. Nagel v .Bosworth , 148 Ky .
807, 147 S. W. 940.
The judge appointed under an unconstitutionalact, good on
itsface, is a de facto officer, and his acts are valid as to third
persons, but he is entitledto no compensation. Nagel v . Bos-
worth, 148 Ky .807, 147 S. W. 940.
De facto officerin case of officehaving potentialexistence.
See note on his subjectin 15 L. R. A. (N.S.) 104.
Right of de facto officerto salary of office. See note on this
subjectin 32 L. R. A. (N.S.) 949.
If an officeris eligibleand has taken such oath as is required
by law (the law creatingthe officenot requiring the dueling-
oath prescribed in the Constitution), his acts are not void
because he did not take said dueling-oath. Morgan v . Vance ,
4 Bush , 325 .
If the officerbe eligibleand he has taken such oaths as he
supposed to be required, and regards his installationas regular,
he might be deemed an officerde jure as well as de facto
until a regular proceeding and judgment declaring his office
vacant . Id .

OFFICERS OF 2399 OFFICERS OF THE
OFFICERS OF COURTS . It is provided in Civil Code ,
Sec.732, that in the constructionof the Code the word " court"
refersto the court in which an actionor proceeding ispending,
ormay be brought;and thewords" judge," " justice," "clerk,"
refers to officersof such court .
In action on bond for costs payable to defendant and the
" officersof the court," the officersmust unite, not in their
official, but private charactersand names . Ham v . Tinchener,
3 Mon. 198 ; Chilesv. Smith, 3 Mon. 199.
A trustee in bankruptcy is defined by the bankrupt act as
an officer, and is in a certainrestrictedsense an officerof the
court,but he is not an officerof the court in any such sense as
a receiver. He takesthe legaltitleto the property and in
respectto suitsstands in the same general positionas a trustee
of an expreestrustor an executor. TigerShoe Mfg .Co.'sTrus-
tee v. Shanklin, 125 Ky . 719 ,102 S. W. 295.
OFFICERS OF ELECTION . It is providedin Ky . Stats.,
Sec . 1442 , in the chapter on " Elections," that the words
" Officerof election' as used in thischapter, means a judge,
clerk or sheriff, or person acting as sheriffat any election, also
a member of the board for canvassing the returns."
Under Ky . Stats., Sec. 1442, defining an " officerof elec-
tion" to mean a judge, clerk, sheriff, or person acting as
sheriffand a member of the board for canvassing returns, and
Sec. 1481, providing for inspectorsat elections, and that the
county executivecommittee of each party designate a suitable
person to be present at, witness, and inspect the counting of
the vote in each precinct, who shallbe admitted to the voting
place, to which no other person, exceptthe electionofficers,
shall be admitted, when read in connection with Sec. 1476 ,
making it a felony for an electionofficerto mutilate or place
distinguishingmarks on any ballot, an inspector is not an
" electionofficer" within the meaning of that term used in
Sec. 1476. Com . v . Goulet, 137 Ky . 464, 125 S. W. 1083.
OFFICERS OF THE STATE .- See DISTRICT OR STATE OFFI-
CERS; STATE OFFICERS.

OFFICER'S SHADOW 2400 OFFICIAL DEFALCA .
OFFICER'S SHADOW . The deputy " is but the officer's
shadow,and doth allthingsin the name of the officerhimself,
and nothing in his own name , and his grantor (principal)
shallanswer forhim." Talbott'sDeviseesv.Hooser, 12 Bush,
414 .
OFFICIAL ACTS .- Officialactsas appliedto the conduct
of ministerialofficers, are only such as are done in the execu-
tion of some legalprocess, or of some positivecommand of the
law . Jones v .Van Bever, 164 Ky .80,174 S. W. 795.
A bank pass-book regularly and accurately kept by the
statetreasurer, in connection with the discharge of his duties,
was competent to go to the jury as a part of his officialtrans-
actionsin an action againsthis suretieson his bond . Com . v.
Tate, 89 Ky .587, 12 R. 1 .
OFFICIAL BOND .- Every bond executed by sheriff, by
which his suretiesundertake that he shalldischarge a public
duty , is an officialbond . Com . v . Adams , 3 Bush , 46 ; Ander-
son v . Thompson , 10 Bush , 137 .
Sureties on the official bond of a master commissioner are
not liablefor a defalcationoccurring after the expiration of
the term for which the bond was given. United StatesFidelity
& Guaranty Co. v .Faulkner,144 Ky . 629, 139 S. W. 853.
It is not essentialto the validityof a bond executed by a
public officialthat it should be accepted or approved by the
officialdesignatedby statuteto do so. Com .v .Hinson , 143 Ky .
428, 136 S. W. 912.
OFFICIAL CAPACITY .- An allegationin a petition that
an arrestwas made by a deputy sheriffin his officialcapacity,
is a mere legalconclusion or inference, without the facts on
which itwas founded ,and adds nothing to the petition. Jones
v. Van Bever, 164 Ky . 80, 174 S. W. 795.
OFFICIAL DEFALCATIONS .- A sheriff and his sureties
are liableonly for officialdefalcations, for failingto do what
the law enjoins, and not for a failureto do what it does not
enjoin, and much less what it inhibits. Rodes v . Young , 5
Dana , 566 .

OFFICIAL RESI . 2401 OHIO RIVER
OFFICIAL RESIDENCE .- See RESIDENCE .
OFFICINA JUSTITIAE .—-The workshop or business place
of justice; a term formerly applied to chancery, in English
law, because all writs issued from it, under the great seal,
returnable into the courts of common law . Cochran's Law
Lexicon .
In England a writ of error coram vobis was not issued from
the same court,but from another officer, to-wit, from chancery,
the officinajustitiae; and it operated as a commission to the
court to which itwas directed, authorizingthem to examine the
record- Todd's Prac . 1,051. Here the inferiorcourts have
no need of such commission . The Governor v . Bacon's Execu-
tors 1 Litt . 285 .
OFFICIUM NEMINI DEBET ESSE DAMNOSUM .- An
officeought to be injuriousto no one (employed in it). Coch-
ran's Law Lexicon .
OFFSET . The affidavit of the claimant must state that
there is " no offset," etc. It will not do to state that there
is " no just offset," etc., the word just being omitted from
the affidavitrequired by the General Statutes. Ky . Stats.
(1915 ), Sec. 3870. Leach v. Kendall, 13 Bush , 424 ; Prewett
v .Wortham ,79 Ky .287, 2 R. 282 ; Spradlin v.Stanley, 124 Ky .
701 , 99 S. W. 965 .
Where the obligorin a note accompanies it with a certificate
that it is a bona fidedebt againsthim , " that there is no offset,
discount, or counterclaim, or defense against the same ," and
that it will be paid at maturity, he is not estopped, even as
against one who has purchased the note upon the faithof the
certificate, to impeach the note for fraud, provided he shows
that the certificatewas also obtained by fraud. Hill v .
Thixton, 94 Ky . 96, 14 R. 900 ; Rev. Sup . Ct ., 13 R. 333, and
distinguishingCrabtreev.Atchison, 93 Ky . 338.
OHIO RIVER .-The courtstake judicialknowledge of the
character of the Ohio River. Thurman v . Morrison, 14 B.
Μ . 370 .

Ο . Κ . 2402 OMITTED PROP .
The Commonwealth of Kentucky has jurisdictionover all
kinds of property, and the right to the soil under the low
watermark on the northwest side of the Ohio river. Louisville
Bridge Co. v . City of Louisville, 81 Ky . 189, 5 R. 16, 473 .
O. K. The indorsement of " O. K. " on an order was con-
strued as possiblyoperating as a consent to it. Edwards v .
Logan , 114 Ky . 332.
"OLD LINE " POLICY .- Where the policyof insuranceis
an " old line" policy, in the absence of a provisionin the con-
tractto the contrary, the designationof a beneficiary, valid in
its inception, remains so, although the insurable interestor
relationshipof the beneficiaryhas ceased; therefore, is not
affectedby a divorce. Sea v . Conrad , 155 Ky . 51, 159 S.
W. 622 .
OMISSION . " The omission of those things which are
silentlyexpressed is of no consequence." Warfield v . Gard-
ner'sAdmr ., 79 Ky . 586, 3 R. 423 .
OMITTED . A charge in a petitionfor reformation of a
contract " that some of the terms of the contract were omitted ,"
is not a sufficientallegationof fraud or mistake. Johnson v .
Shannon , 4 Ky . Opin . 236.
OMITTED PROPERTY .— The " omittedproperty" contem-
plated by our assessment statutes is property which is not
assessedat all; they do not mean that property which by mis-
take has been apportioned to the wrong subdivisionor munici-
pality may be treated as " omitted property" by the county
or subdivision where it should have been assessed. In such
casesthe county or subdivisionso wronged may appear before
the board authorized to make the assessment and have the
errorcorrected, and if that body refusesit may be required
to do so by mandamus . Com .v .Ashland Coal & Iron Ry . Co.,
154 Ky . 673, 159 S. W. 538.
One's land can not be assessedas omittedproperty, there
having been a validassessmentof allof itfor the same year.
Ward v.Wentz,130 Ky .705, 113 S. W. 892.

OMITTED PROP . 2403 OMITTED PROP .
Property that has been undervaluedby the owner is not
property omitted from taxationwithin the meaning of the
statute. American Tobacco Co. v . Com ., 162 Ky . 716, 172 S.
W. 1085 ; City of Georgetown v . Graves ' Admr ., 165 Ky . 676,
178 S. W. 1035 .
It is incumbent on the taxpayer in listinghis property for
taxationto make, under oath, a fulland fairdisclosure, by
items, of allhis property subject to taxation, and if he does
this and the assessorplaces a valuation on it that is too low,
the cityor State isbound by it; but if he failsto do this, and
gives itsvaluationin a lump without being sworn and without
disclosingthe items, to the extent that the property is thereby
underestimated, it is omitted property in the meaning of the
law, and the negligenceof the officerin not requiringsuch dis-
closurewillnot avail the delinquent taxpayer. Bell's Trustee
v. City of Lexington, 120 Ky .199, 85 S. W. 1081, 27 R. 591.
It is only property omitted from taxationby the owner or
person charged with the duty of listingit, that the revenue
agent may cause to be listedand taxed. And unless the state-
ment containsthe names of the owners of theproperty itcan not
be identifiedas property omitted from taxation, for it is essen-
tial to know the names of the owners in order for the court to
ascertainwhether or not the property has been listedand taxed,
and ifthe names of the owners are not given, a cause of action
is not stated. Com . v . Tabbs Storage Warehouse & Freight
Transfer Line, 150 Ky . 465, 150 S. W. 525.
The proceedingto assessomittedpropertyunder Ky . Stats.,
Sec. 4260, institutedby the filingof a statement of the omitted
propertyby a revenueagent, isa proceedingon behalfof the
Commonwealth and the Commonwealth is the real party in
interest, althoughthe revenue agent, if successful, obtainsa
compensationfor his efforts, and the proceedingisneitheran
actionnor a specialproceedingas definedby the statute, but
issimply a supplementalmeans for assessingomittedproperty.
Com . v .Glover, 132 Ky . 588, 116 S. W. 769.
In a proceedingby an auditor'sagentin the County Court
to listomittedpropertyfor taxation,held, first,itisnot neces-
sary to itsvaliditythat the judgment should statethe rate of

OMNE MAJUS IN 2404 OMNIA PRAESUM .
taxation, as the ratefor eachof theyearsisfixedby law;
second, thepropertyissufficientlydescribedas " notes, mort-
gages, and cash" ; and, third, the recitalthat defendanthad
failedto properly listhis personalproperty for the years
involvedto the extent of $7000, and an adjudicationthat it be
listedfor theseyears, amounts to a judgment that the property
had been " omitted" for the years mentioned. Sebree v . Nutter,
87 S. W. 1072, 27 R. 1080.
OMNE MAJUS IN SE CONTINET MINUS .- The greater
contains the less. Cochran's Law Lexicon .
OMNE QUOD SOLO INAEDIFICATUR SOLO CEDIT.-
Every thing which is built upon the soilbelongs to the soil.
Cochran's Law Lexicon .
OMNIA PRAESUMUNTUR CONTRA SPOLIATOREM.-
All things are presumed againsta spoliator; e. g., if he
wrongfullywithholdsor destroysevidencein his possession, it
will be presumed to be adverse. Cochran's Law Lexicon.
" The generalrule isomnia praesumuntur contra spoilatorem.
His conduct is attributedto his supposed knowledge that the
truth would have operated against him ." McClure v . Mc-
Clintock, 150 Ky . 265, 150 S. W. 332.
OMNIA PRAESUMUNTUR . Omnia praesumuntur rite et
solemniteresseactadonec probeturin contrarium. All things
are presumed to have been done properly and with due
formalities, untilitbe proved to the contrary. Cochran's Law
Lexicon .
" Time can never authorizethe presumptionthat an exist-
ing record,apparently complete and perfect, isnot substantially
what it always was , and especiallythat anything which it
expressesor imports is false. The legal maxim omnia praes-
umuntur riteet solemniteresseacta donec probeturin con-
trarium,' appliesnot to such a case." Shaefer v. Gates, 2
B. Mon. 457. See, also, Kenton Co. Ct. v. Turnpike Co., 10
Bush , 536 .

OMNES LICENTIAM 2405 ON AND AFTER
OMNES LICENTIAM . Omnes licentiamhabent iis, quae
pro se introductasunt, renunciare. Every one has the rightto
renounce those stipulationswhich have been introduced for
his own benefit . Cochran's Law Lexicon .
OMNIS INNOVATIO .— Omnis innovatioplus novitateper-
turbat quam utilitateprodest. Every innovationoccasionsmore
harm by itsnovelty than benefitby itsutility. Cochran's Law
Lexicon .
OMNIS RATIHABITIO .- Omnis ratihabitio retro -trahitur et
mandato priori aequiparatur. Every ratificationhas a retro-
spectiveeffect, and is equivalentto a previous request. Coch-
ran's Law Lexicon .
OMNIUM CONTRIBUTIONE . Omnium contributione sar-
ciatur quod pro omnibus datum est. That which is given for
all should be made good by the contributionof all. A prin-
cipleof the law of general average. Cochran's Law Lexicon.
ON .- Cases interpretingterm " in or on." See note on
this subjectin 37 L. R. A. (N.S.) 620.
ON ACCOUNT OF .— A “due bill" signed by the president
of the corporation, with the words describinghimselfas such,
the body of the obligationrecitingthat the amount for which
it is given is the balance due the payee " on account of" the
corporation, is the obligationof the corporation. Humber v .
C. O. & O. T. P. Co., 13 R. 327.
Notes promising, " on behalf of," or " on account of." See
note on thissubject in 19 L. R. A. 677.
ON AND AFTER .- The act authorizing contracts for the
payment of interestat that rate provides by the 8th section
as follows : " this act shall take effectand be in force from and
after the firstday of September , 1871." It was not in force
on the firstday of September, 1871. The words " from" and
" after" are used in direct connection, and they fix the day
named as a point or period of time after which the rule of
actionprescribedby the act is to prevail. If the intentionhad

ON BEHALF OF 2406 ON DUTY
been to include the firstday of September the language would
have been " on and after" that day , etc. Handley v . Cun-
ningham's Trustees, 12 Bush , 403.
ON BEHALF OF .- Notes promising, " on behalf of," or
" on account of." See note on thissubjectin 19 L. R. A. 677.
ON BOARD ITS CARS .- Where one contracts to deliver
stone to a railroadcompany " on board its cars" at a desig-
nated point on its road, the mere failureof the company to
furnish the cars is no excuse for not deliveringthe stone at
or near the place of delivery. C. O. & S. W. R. Co. v . Cruger ,
7 R. 103 .
ON DEMAND.- " An instrument is payable on demand :
(1) Where itisexpressedto be payable on demand , or at sight,
or on presentation; or (2) in which no time for payment is
expressed. Where an instrument isissued,accepted or endorsed
when overdue, it is, as regards the person so issuing, accepting
or endorsingit, payableon demand ." NegotiableInst. Law ,
Ky . Stats. (1915), Sec. 3720b, Subsec. 7.
A note payable" on demand " isdue immediately, and the
statutebeginstorun from thedateof thepaper. Gerke Brewing
Co. v .Bussee, 11 R. 322.
A failureto demand a note payabie" on demand " for two
yearsafteritwas givenreleasesthe assignors. Gerke Brewing
Co. v . Bussee,11 R. 322.
Allegation to pay a horse on demand , and proof to pay a
horse, without saying more , are not variant in legal effect.
Anderson v . Waller, 3 Mon. 236.
Covenant to pay property " on demand , to be paid in four
months after date," construed to mean that the obligor may
be hastened by a demand to pay within the four months , and
at the expiration of that time an action accrues without demand .
Stucker v .Miller, 5 Litt.235.
ON DUTY . " A watchman asleepon duty, thatis,during
his turn, is not doing his duty, but if one be offthe boat while
he is employed to be there, he isnot even on duty." St. Paul
Fire & Marine Ins.Co.v.Kendle, 163 Ky .146,173 S. W. 373.

ONE STORY ON TERMS AS2407
ONE STORY BUILDING .- See HIGH BUILDING .
ON GIVING NOTICE TO THAT EFFECT .- Where the
policy provides that the insurance may be terminated at any
time at the option of the company " on giving notice to that
effect," a noticeby the company to itsagent is not sufficient;
the notice must be to the insured . London & Lancashire Fire
Ins. Co. v . Turnbull, 86 Ky .230,9 R. 544.
ON OR BEFORE . The words " on or before," insertedin
a stipulationto do an act or pay money , entitlesthe obligor
to perform at any time before the day , and upon performance
or tender and refusalhe is vested with all the rights which
would have attached if performed on the day . Wall v .
Simpson , 6 J. J. M. 156.
ON RECEIPT .- A policyprovidedthat it should become
void if the insured should sellthe property or assign the policy
without the consent of the company indorsed thereon. The
insured soldand informed the company's solicitor, who referred
him to the superintendent, who sent the insured a blank guar-
antee for the purchaser to sign and return, saying, " On receipt
of same , allbeing satisfactory, we willgive the matter prompt
attention." This letter was never received by the insured .
Held , no consent was shown , and there was nothing indicating
a waiver. Mays v . ContinentalIns. Co., 7 R. 524. See infra,
100 .
ON RELATION .- See AT THE RELATION .
ON REQUEST .- Where the covenant is to convey land
on request, a specialrequest is necessary. Gibbs v . Stone, 7
Mon. 303 .
ON TERMS AS MUTUALLY AGREED ON .- Where a con-
tractbetween a city and a water company , made in 1883, pro-
vided that the company should supply the city for twenty-five
years, and if the citydid not purchase the water plant at the
end of that time the contract to be renewed for twenty-five
years longer, " on terms as mutually agreed on at that time,"

ON THE DAY 2408 ON OR NEAR
the renewal provision was valid, and meant that reasonable
and equitableterms were to be fixedby the court if the parties
could not agree thereto. Slade v . City of Lexington, 141 Ky .
214 , 132 S. W. 404 .
ON THE DAY .- The averment that an offense was com-
mitted " on the - day of July, 1883," imports the commission
of it anterior to the 24th day of the same month , when the
indictment was found . Vowells v . Com ., 84 Ky . 52 , 8 R. 74 .
ON THE FARM .- As the husband provided for the wife
liberallyand was kind to her, and the only reason assigned
by her for refusing to live with him " on the farm " was
thathe would not build a largerhouse,therewas no just ground
for a separationfrom bed and board. Woolfolk v . Woolfolk,
96 Ky . 657.
ON THE MERITS . If any court err in sustaining a
demurrer and entering judgment for defendant thereon, when
the complaint is sufficient, the judgment is nevertheless" on
the merits." Cain v . Union Central Life Ins. Co., 123 Ky . 64 ,
93 S. W. 622 .
ON THE PART OF THEIR MOTHER .- Meaning of these
words in statuterelatingto inheritancefrom bastards. Croan
v . Phelps' Admr ., 94 Ky . 215.
ON THE PETITION .— Under CivilCode , Sec.57 , providing
that, if the name and place of residenceof a non-resident
defendant be unknown to plaintiff, the clerkshallmake on the
petitionan orderwarning defendantto defend the action,such
order need not be actuallymade on the petition, but may be
written on a separate paper and attached thereto. Harlam-
mert v .Moody , 15 R. 839.
ON OR NEAR .- Where the decree of sale directed the
officerto advertise by posting a notice " on or near" the
premises, hisreturnthathe posteda notice" on or near" the
property shows a compliance with the decree. Slaughter v .
Graham , 5 R. 324.

ONCE A MORTGAGE 2409 ONLY HEIRS
" In Commonwealth v . City of Somerset, 14 Ky . L. R.
238, the Superior Court held that while an indictment for a
nuisance must describe the location of the nuisance with
certainty, yet an indictment against a city for permitting a
nuisance, which alleges the city permitted a certain street,
naming it, to become filledwith mud ,stonesand othermaterial,
which preventedpublictravelalongsaid street, issufficiently
specificas to the locationof thenuisance, as the fairinference
is that the city had permitted the whole streetto become a
nuisance. It further held, however , that had the indictment
charged that the citycaused or permitteda nuisance" on"
or " near " the streetnamed , without. further describing the
location, it would have been defective." King v. Com ., 154
Ky . 829, 159 S. W. 593.
ONCE A MORTGAGE .- Once a mortgage always a mort-
gage . Threlkeld v . Walker , 141 Ky . 739, 133 S. W. 772 ;
Schollv .Hopper , 134 Ky .92 .
ONCE A HIGHWAY ALWAYS A HIGHWAY . " In the case
of Louisvillev . Brewer , 24 Ky . L. R. 1671 (72 S. W. 9), it is
decided that a public thoroughfare coming into the city by
annexationor upon itsorganizationas a city, remainsa public
thoroughfare, and the old maxim 'once a highway always a
highway' is applied, although the city might not formally
declare it to be a public thoroughfare, or exerciseany act of
controlover it." City of Oakdalev. Sanders' Extx., 155 Ку .
352 , 159 S. W. 812 .
ONE DOLLAR CONSIDERATION .- " One dollar considera-
tion," though sufficientbetween grantor and grantee, is
considerednominal only as to creditors. Ward v. Trotter,
3 Mon. 3 .
ONE SUING FOR ALL .- See СомMON OR GENERAL INTEREST .
ONLY HEIRS . In an actionby collateralrelativesof an
intestatefor property alleged to have descended to them ,
they must allegeand prove if denied, that there are no other

OONTZ 2410 OPEN ,NOTORIOUS .
relativesentitledto take in preferenceto them . The allegation
that they are the " only heirs" of the intestateis but a con-
clusion of the pleader and is insufficient. Montgomery v .
White , 10 R. 905.
OONTZ .- The game of " oontz," played with dice on a table
by bettors, does not come within the meaning of the Act of
1886 , making it a felony to " set up , etc., a keno -bank , faro-
bank , or other machine or contrivance," etc. Com . v .
Kammerer , 11 R. 777. See, also, GAMING .
ONUS PROBANDI .- The burden of proof.
OPEN ACCOUNTS .- Assignment of " allestateof whatever
nature or kind soever" willpass to the assignee, open accounts
due assignor, and every thing else in the form of property
owned by him . Forepaugh v .Appold , 17 B. M. 360.
OPEN SHOP .- Controversy over " open" or " closed" shop
as justificationfor means employed to aid strike. See note on
thissubjectin 17 L. R. A. (N.S.) 162 ; 35 L. R. A. (N.S ) 787.
OPEN UP.- Where a licensed saloon keeper who kept a
family grocery, which opened into his saloon by a swinging
door, had in his employ a clerk who attended to both the
groceryand saloon,gave ordersto hisclerkon Sunday morning
to " open up ," not indicatingwhether he meant the grocery or
the saloon or both, itwas competent on the trialof the owner
of the saloon for sellingliquors on Sunday to prove that on
previous Sundays , with the knowledge and consent of the
owner , his clerkhad opened the saloonand sold whisky therein,
for the purpose of proving that the sale of such liquorswas
within the scope of his clerk'semployment . City of Paducah
v .Jones,104 S. W. 971, 31 R. 1203.
OPENLY , NOTORIOUSLY AND SCANDALOUSLY .- An
indictmentisnot fatallydefectivebecause itfailsto charge that
the acts constituting the alleged nuisance were " openly,
notoriouslyand scandalously" committed. Adams v. Com .,
162 Ky .76, 171 S. W. 1006.

OPERA HOUSE 2411 OPERATION
OPERA HOUSE .- Under an indictment for breaking the
warehouse of W. with intent to stealtherefrom it was proper
to give an instructionauthorizing a conviction if the jury
believed that defendant had broken the " opera house" of W.
and stolentherefromthe articlesfound in his posession, the
evidence showing such opera house to be a warehouse within
the statute. Hunter v . Com ., 48 S. W. 1077, 20 R. 1165.
OPERATED.- A sleepingcar owned by the Pullman Car
Company , manned and controlledwholly by itsservantsand
the fares for berths and seats in which were exclusively
receivedand owned by that company ,was not,because attached
to and hauled by a passenger train of defendant company ,
operated by said company within the meaning of Ky . Stats.,
Sec. 795 (the separate coach law ), and as the carrier is not
required to furnish sleepingcars under that statute, it is not
liablefor hauling a sleeping car which contains no separate
compartments , or for failingto require a colored passenger in
such sleeper to enter the compartment in the day coach set
apart for his race, where the passenger had , before entering
the State, bought a ticketwhich entitledhim to ride in the
sleeper, and also a ticket which entitledhim to be carried
through the State on defendant's train. Com . v . I. C. R. Co.,
141 Ky .502,133 S. W. 1158.
OPERATING .-A provision in the charter of a railroad
corporation empowering the corporation to " make contracts
with individuals, corporations, and other railroad companies
for the building, completion, and operatingof said road or
any part thereof," empowered the corporation to lease its
road,but not so as to relieveit from liabilityfor the negligence
of the lesseein the operation of a train whereby a person on
the track was struck and killed. McCabe's Admx .v .Maysville
& B. S. R. Co.,112 Ky .861, 66 S. W. 1054,23 R. 2328.
OPERATION .- A telegraphmessage reading, " Wire me to
Toledo care trainthreehundred and nine Big Four if operation
successful," was insufficientin itselfto give notice to the
telegraph company that the operation referred to was a
surgicaloperationto be performed on the wife of the sender,

OPERATION OF RAIL 2412 OPINION
and thatmental sufferingwould resultto the sender by a failure
to transmit and deliverthe message, and hence damages for
mental sufferingwere not recoverablefor failureto deliverthe
message withinthe proper time. Western Union Tel. Co. v.
Glover, 138 Ky . 500, 128 S. W. 587. See, also, TRANSACTION .
A contractto restoresalarywhen a plant should be in
" operation," did not require " full operation." Frankfort
Modes Glass Works v. Arbogast, 148 Ky . 4, 145 S. W. 1122.
OPERATION OF RAILROAD . It was error, in instructing
the jury as to the permanent damages recoverableon account
of the continuedoperationof a railroad,not to qualify the word
" operation" by the words " skillfuland prudent." C. & 0 .
R. R. Co. v .Gross,43 S. W. 203, 19 R. 1926.

OPTION . An option to sellis a standing offerto sellto

to be compliedwithby thepayment or tenderof the considera-

S. W. 964, 31 R. 176, 10 L. R. A. (N.S.) 195.
An option for the saleof a tractof land consistsof two ele-

Although the option given is without consideration, yet if

to sell, in such a way , that performance of the conditionsmay
be requiredof him , itbecomes an enforciblecontract. Stamper

The exerciseof an option to sellland and acceptance of the

In the construction of a will the word " or " will be construed
as " and " where it is necessaryto do so in order to effectuate
themanifestintentionof thetestator. Moore'sAdmr .v. Sleet,
113 Ky .600, 68 S. W. 642,24 R. 426 .

no heirs, then the portionthat would go to him or them should

died withoutissuebeforethe periodof distribution, hisshare

26 R. 1197 .
Where a willdevised land to testator'ssons, and empowered

leaving issuehis share should go to the survivors, the word

In construingwills, courtsmay give to disjunctivewords

After verdict, that interpretationshould be given a pleading
which will sustain the verdict if it can be done by any reason-

ever, sufficientbail be not offered, the sum in which bail is
required must be stated in the order of commitment ." Crim .
Code , Sec. 67.
ORDER OF DELIVERY .- For Code provisionsrelatingto
ordersof delivery,seeCivilCode,Sec.180,etseq.
An order of deliverymay be obtained for specificpersonal
property, but itmay not be had for an undividedinterestin
personalproperty where the plaintiffonly shows that he owns
such an interest. Hibbard v . Estridge, 156 Ky .122, 160 S. W.
746 .
ORDER FOR GOODS .— " An order is merely an offer; it
issignedby the would-be purchaseronly; it is merely a re-
quest, on hispart,thathe send him,sellhim or shiphim, the
goods desired." American SeedingMachine Co. v. Com ., 152
Ky . 594, 153 S. W. 972; see, also, Simons v. American Box
Ball Co., 158 Ky . 631, 166 S. W. 224.

ORDER NOTIFY 2426 ORDERS
ORDER NOTIFY .- When goods are shipped by the con-
signorsconsigned to " our order, notify H " and H is notified,
but refusesto accept the goods, it is incumbent on the carrier
to notify the shippers of this and failingto do so he is liable
for the lossof the goods while lying in his warehouse by reason
of a fireoccurringwithouthisfault. Nashville,C. & St.L. Ry .
Co. v . Dreyfuss-Weil Co., 150 Ky . 333, 150 S. W. 321. See,
also, CARRIERS, in Digest.
ORDER OR PROMISE TO DO AN ACT .- A condition in
a note otherwisenegotiable, that was secured by collateral, that
the maker " willdeposit and pledge with said bank such addi-
tional securityas it may from time to time require, and in
default of such deposit this note, at the option of the bank ,
shallbecome due and payable," did not make the instrument
non -negotiable. It is not, strictlyspeaking, " an order or
promise to do an act in additon to the payment of money ,"
but is rather an order or promise to do an act that will better
secure the promise to pay the money stipulatedat the time fixed
in the note. Finley v . Smith , 165 Ky . 452, 177 S. W. 262.
ORDERED.- Where a justice of the peace was clerk as
well, an executionissuedby him was " orderedby the court"
within Sec. 4 , Art . 1, Chap . 38, Gen. Stats., providing that
unless so ordered no executionshallissueuntil ten days after
judgment rendered. Galotv.Pearce,38 S. W. 892,18 R. 1004.
ORDERS . It is not necessary that a superior should give
an order in express words . It is sufficientif the words and
conduct of the superior are such as to warrant a servant of
ordinary prudence under the circumstancesto understand that
he is directedto do the work , and when the superior saw that
the servant so understood him and acquiescedin the servant's
constructionof what he had said, his acquiescencewas equiva-
lent to a priorcommand . Cumberland Tel.& Tel.Co. v .Graves,
104 S. W. 356, 31 R. 972.
A carpenter, injured while carrying a ventilatorto the roof
of a building,can not recoveron the ground that he was directed
to carry the ventilatorby his superior, where thiswas not a

ORDERS 2427 ORDERS OF COURT
peremptory order but was merely a directiongiven in the usual
course of the employment and in the usual manner in which
directionsand orders are given to laborers; there being no
hurry , no emergency , and no assurance of safety. Foreman v .
L. & N. R. Co.,142 Ky . 63,133 S. W. 964.
Distinction between " order " and " request." See note cn
thissubjectin 32 L. R. A. (N.S.) 327.
For additionalauthoritiesseeDigest Kentucky Reports (Mor-
ton & Co.), titleMaster and Servant.
aORDERS OF COURT.- “A directionor decisionof
court or judge enteredupon the order-book or made in writing,
in an action,and not final,isan order." CivilCode, Sec.622.
It is provided in Civil Code , Sec. 732 , Subsec. 27, that in
the construction of the Code the word " writ " means an order
or precept in writing,issuedby a court, clerkor judicialofficer.
The docket book and minute book are record books of the
County Court, but they are not the ones in which are recorded
the ordersof thecourt. Each County Court has an orderbook,
and in thisbook the actsand doings of the court are preserved
and authenticatedby the signatureof the presidingjudge.
When orders of the court are spoken of, referenceis made to
the order book, and not to the docket book or minute book.
Rallsv. Sharp'sAdmr ., 140 Ky . 744,131 S. W. 998.
An order of a FiscalCourt which was never read publicly
by the clerk, nor signed by the county or presidingjudge, with
the approval of the justicespresent, is not a valid order for
any purpose. Fox v . Lantrip, 162 Ky . 178, 172 S. W. 133.
The Fiscal Court is one of the courts provided for in the
Constitutionof the State, and is given charge and direction
of the fiscalaffairsof the county. By the statutesof thisState
it is required to be a court of record, and it speaks only by
itsrecords, and can not do otherwise. A mere meeting together
of the persons composing the membership of the Fiscal Court,
and determining in parol as to what they are to do and not to
do , isnot an act of said court. Talbot v .Marshall, 2 Mar. 603 ;
McDonald v . Franklin Co., 125 Ky .205, 100 S. W. 861 ; Fox v.
Lantrip, 162 Ky .178, 172 S. W. 133.

ORDERS OF COURT 2428 ORDINANCES
The orderor judgment made in a habeascorpusproceeding
isnot a judgment of a court, or finaldeterminationby a court
under our practice. Bedford v.Hamilton, 153 Ky . 429, 155
S. W. 1128 .
Where the clerk certifieson appeal that the copy of the
ordersof the court is a fulland complete copy of allthe orders
made in a cause,and sincethey could not be orders untilsigned
by the judge,theCourt of Appeals willassume thatthe clerk's
certificateis correct, and that such orderswere duly signed.
Johnson v.Dearen, 9 Ky .Opin. 272.
The recordsof the proceedingsof a court do not become
orders, in the legalsenseof thatterm, untilthey are signed
by the judge and when he signsthe recordthe whole of that
day'sprocedingsis therebyvitalized, and each order becomes
operativeat thesame moment . Revillv. Claxon,12 Bush ,560.
The signingby the judge of the minutesof the lastday of
the term, and leavingthem for the clerkafteradjournment
and invacationtowriteup at length,insertingwhat he chooses,
constituteshim pro tantoa judicialofficer. Such attempt to
give the minute book the force of a record should not be
tolerated. Johnson v. Com ., 80 Ky . 377, 4 R. 210.
Appellantfound guiltyof murder was orallysentencedbut
neitherverdictnor judgment was spreadon theorderbook. On
the minute book of the lastday of the term was the style of
the case with the words " written order," from which the clerk
in vacationspread the order at lengthupon the order book,
which was notsignedby thejudge. This practiceiscondemned,
and a new trialordered. Johnson v . Com .,80 Ky .377,4 R. 210.
See, also, JUDGMENTS .
ORDINANCES .-See , also, RESOLUTION.
An ordinance is a law of the municipality, and those who
must obey it, as well as those whose duty it is to enforce it,
must be able to know when it has been violated. C. & O. Ry .
Co. v. Mellon, 162 Ky . 738, 172 S. W. 1067.
An ordinanceof a town prescribinga punishment for its
violation, must be sufficientlycertainas to the portion of the
town where it is intendedto be in force, that personsmay

ORDINANCES ORDINANCES2429
know certainlywhen they have violatedit, or else it is void
for want of certainty. C. & O. Ry . Co. v .Mellon, 162 Ky .738,
172 S. W. 1067 .
When the charter of a city provides in terms, or by neces-
sary implication, that legislationshallbe enacted by ordinance,
the council is without authority to enact legislationby joint
resolution,but if the charterissilenton thissubjectthe council
may legislateeitherby resolutionor ordinance. City of Louis-
villev. Parsons, 150 Ky . 420, 150 S. W. 498.
In the charter of towns of the sixth class the words " ordi-
nance " and " resolution" seem to be used as synonymous terms,
the word " ordinance" being more generallyemployed in such
of itsprovisionsas confer authority upon the municipality to
collectlicensefees, impose taxes, make contracts, incur lia-
bilities,make appropriationsexceeding$50.00, and in legislating
in the exerciseof the policepower. Bernheim v. Town of
Anchorage , 159 Ky . 315, 167 S. W. 139.
Sec. 51 of the Constitutionwhich provides in part that no
law enacted by the General Assembly shallrelateto more than
one subject and that shall be expressed in the title, was , is
obvious from its language, intended to apply alone to laws
enacted by the General Assembly . Kentucky Light & Power Co.
v.Jas.H. Williams,124 S. W. 840 ; Tuggles v.Com .,30 R. 1071,
in the opinion of which it is said: Swann v . City of Murray ,
146 Ky . 148 , 142 S. W. 244.
It is provided in the Tenement House Act , applicable to
citiesof the firstclass, that wherever the word " ordinance"
occurs in that Act it shallbe construed as if followed by the
words " of the city of the firstclassin which tenement house
issituated." Ky .Stats. (1915), Sec.3037g .
It isnot necessarythat the noticeshould designateaccurately
or in detailthe characterof businessthat willbe brought before
the council, but neither should the purpose of the statutebe
defeated by a loose constructionthat would leave it without
meaning or effect. As practicallyallthe businesstransacted
by the councilisin the form of ordinances,a noticethat " ordi-
nances will be considered " is not sufficientto authorize the

ORDINANCES ORDINANCES2430
considerationof any ordinance. Meacham Contracting Co. v .
Kleiderer, 146 Ky . 441, 142 S. W. 720 .
Under Sec. 2834, Ky . Stats., providing that at least two
weeks shallelapse between the passage from one board of the
general council to another of an ordinance of a city of the
firstclassfor an originalstreetimprovement ,such an ordinance
passed by theboard of councilmen March 17th,and by the board
of aldermen March 31st, is valid. City of Louisvillev . Selvage,
51 S. W. 447, 21 R. 349.
When the aid of the courts is invoked to declare an ordinance
void, itmust clearlyappear that itisinherentlyviolativeof the
law , or some of the well-settledprinciplesthat are generally
recognized as limitationsupon the power of municipalitiesin
theenactmentof ordinances,or iftheordinanceisnotinherently
defectiveas coming withinthoseinhibitions, then the person
attacking it must affirmativelyshow that, as applied to him ,
it isunreasonable, unfair,or oppressive. Wells v . Town of Mt.
Olivet, 102 S. W. 1182,31 R. 576.
An ordinance passed by a municipality can not be invalid
upon any other ground than its illegality. It is not within
the province of the court to say that a validordinance isunwise
or impolitic; those questionsmust be addressed solelyto the
General Council. Gathright v . Byllesby & Co., 154 Ky . 106,
157 S. W. 45 .
The courts are reluctantto declare a municipal ordinance
relatingto a subjectupon which the municipalcouncilhas
statutoryauthorityto legislate, invalidon the ground that it
isunreasonable, arbitraryor oppressive. But they willdo so,
if it is patent that such willbe the effectof putting the ordi-
nance into operation. City of Versaillesv . Ky . Highland R.
Co.,153 Ky .83, 154 S. W. 388, and casescited.
Ordinancesof a cityprovidingmerely for the incurringof
an indebtedness are not measures for raisingrevenue, as the
indebtednessis to be paid in the future by the levy and collec-
tion of taxes to be thereafterfixedand imposed by subsequent
ordinances . Bills or ordinances for raising revenue are con-
finedto such as levy taxes in the strictsense of the word and

ORDINANCE OR 2431 ORDINARY CARE
do not embrace billsor ordinances for other purposes, which
incidentallycreaterevenue. CentralConstructionCo. v. City
of Lexington, 162 Ky . 286, 172 S. W. 648.
An ordinance directingthe improvement of a streetat the
expense of the property owners, the citynot to be liablethere-
for, but providing that the city may advance the necessary
money , taking an assignment from the contractorof his liens
on abuttingproperty, is not an ordinanceappropriatingor
involvingthe expenditureof money within Sec.3304,Ky .Stats.,
requiring such ordinance to be engrossed by the clerk and
presented to the mayor for approval. Becker v. City of Hen-
derson, 100 Ky . 450, 38 S. W. 857, 18 R. 881.
An ordinance of a municipality which is in contravention
of the terms of a statuteof the State,general in itsapplication,
isvoid. Com .v. Preston, 165 Ку . 200, 176 S. W. 963.
Running a train in violationof a city ordinance regulating
the rate of speed isnot necessarilynegligence. L. & N. R. Co.
v . Redmon's Admr ., 122 Ky . 399, 91 S. W. 722, 28 R. 1293,
and cases there cited .
ORDINANCE OR RESOLUTION .- Where the charter pro-
vides that a city legislativebody shallby ordinance or resolu-
tionfixand determinewhat lotsare benefittedby a proposed
sewer, preliminary to its construction, an allegationin a suit
to enjoin the constructionof a sewer that no such ordinance
has been passed is insufficient. City of Newport v . Lang , 155
Ky . 776, 160 S. W. 499.
ORDINARY ACTIONS . " Unless otherwise provided by
this Code or other statute-
1. Actions of which Courts of Chancery had jurisdiction
before the firstday of August , 1851, may be equitable; and
actionsof which such jurisdictionwas exclusivemust be equit-
able .
2. All other actionsmust be ordinary." CivilCode , Sec. 6.
ORDINARY CARE .-Ordinary care means that degree of
care usuallyexercisedby ordinarilyprudent persons under cir-
cumstances likeor similarto those proven in this case. L. H.

ORDINARY CARE 2432 ORDINARY CARE
& St.L. Ry .Co.v.Stillwell,142 Ky .330,134 S. W. 202; L. &
N. R. Co.v.Lynch,137 Ky .696,126 S. W. 362;Ewing & Sons
v.Callahan,105 S. W. 387,32 R. 46;Creamer v.LouisvilleRy .
Co.,142 Ky .340,134 S. W. 193;L. & N. R. Co.v.Lucas,98 S.
W. 308, 30 R. 359; InterstateCoal Co. v. Love, 153 Ky . 323,
155 S. W. 746 .
" This courthas adopted a plainand satisfactorydefinition
of ordinary care in many cases,among which may be mentioned
thoseof West Kentucky Coal Co. v .Davis, 138 Ky .667,and C.,
N. O. & T. P. Ry .Co. v.McElroy,146 Ky .668. In substance
the definitionof ordinary care is" such care as an ordinarily
prudent person will usually exerciseunder circumstances like
or similarto thoseproven in thiscase." Owensboro CityRy .
Co. v. Tucker,148 Ky .844,147 S. W. 916.
Ordinary care is such care as one of ordinary prudence
usuallyexercisesunder givencircumstances. Cincinnati, N. O.
& T. P. Ry . Co. v . Evans ' Admr ., 12 Ky . 152 , 110 S. W. 844,
33 R. 596 .
Ordinary care isthatdegree of care which ordinarilyprudent
personswould usuallyexerciseif surroundedby the same or
similarcircumstancesto thoseproven in the case. Cross v .I.С.
R. Co., 110 S. W. 290, 33 R. 432 .
What is reasonable or ordinary care- and both of these words
mean in this connectionsubstantiallythe same thing- depends
on the circumstancesand facts of each particularcase as they
are developed by the evidence. Sharp v . Layne , 117 S. W. 292.
Ordinary care is such care as a man of ordinary prudence
might reasonably be expected to exerciseunder like circum-
stances. L. & N. R. Co.v.Logsdon,114 Ky .746,71 S. W. 905,
24 R. 1566 .
Reasonable or ordinary care is such care as an ordinarily
prudent person will usually use under circumstances the same
or similarto thoseproven in a particularcase. West Kentucky
Coal Co. v .Davis, 138 Ky . 667,128 S. W. 1074.
Where the words " reasonable diligence" and " reasonable
care" are used in the instructionsthe court should tellthe jury
that these terms mean ordinary care. Greene v . LouisvilleRy .
Co., 84 S. W. 1154, 27 R. 316.

ORDINARY CARE 2433 ORDINARY CARE
It was not error for the court to use the words " reasonable
care," insteadof " ordinary care," in an instructionsettingout
the care requiredby defendant'sservantsin the inspectionof a
cut through which the track ran. L. & N. R. Co. v .Pointer,113
Ky . 952, 69 S. W. 1108, 24 R. 772 .
The care which partiesare required to use in the discharge
of theirrespectiveduties variesso much with the situationof
the partiesand the circumstancesof each particularcase that
itis the policy of the law to leave questionsof thiskind to the
jury. Henderson Tobacco Extract Works v. Wheeler, 116 Ky .
322, 76 S. W. 34, 25 R. 495 .
Ordinary care is such care as an ordinarilyprudent person
would ordinarilyuse under likecircumstances. Straight Creek
Coal Co. v . Huddleston's Admr ., 147 Ky . 94, 143 S. W. 775 .
The additionof the words " but no more ," to an instruction
defining ordinary care, while surplusage and not necessary to
the sense, were not erroneous; the court merely tellingthe jury
that ordinary care, as applied to plaintiff, meant that degree
of care which might be reasonablylooked for in a boy of such
age and intelligence, but no more ." Chess & Wymond Co. v .
Gohagan's Gdn ., 105 S. W. 890, 32 R. 372.
It was held not error to defineordinary care as such care as
an ordinarily prudent person would usually exercise under
similarcircumstances" in matters involvinghis own interest,"
though the trial court should adhere to the definitionof
ordinarycareapprovedby the Court of Appeals. L. & N. R.
Co. v .Allen, 153 Ky . 252, 154 S. W. 1095.
An instructionthat ordinary care is that degree of care
which ordinarilyprudent and " skillful" men usually exercise,
iferroneous, isonly technicallyso, and not prejudicial. Sou-
thernRy .Co. in Ky .v.Otis'Admr .,78 S. W. 480,25 R. 1686.
An instructiondefiningordinary care to be such care as an
ordinary person would usuallyobserveunder the same or
similarcircumstancesis erroneous. Henderson City Ry . Co. v.
Lockett, 98 S. W. 303, 30 R. 321.
In an actionagainsta streetrailwaycompany for personal
injuries, an instructiontellingthejury that"by ordinarycare
ismeant such careas isusuallyexercisedby ordinarilyprudent

ORDINARY CARE 2434 ORDINARY CARE
persons, and by negligenceis meant the absenceof ordinary
care," there should have been added the qualifyingclause" of
like or similar conditions." The standard of ordinary care
varieswith varying circumstances the greater the danger the
greater the care, the lessthe danger the lessthe care. Owens-
boro CityRy .Co.v. Tucker,148 Ky .844,147 S. W. 916.
Where , in an action for damages for personal injuriesto
plaintifffrom being thrown from her buggy as a resultof her
horse becoming frightened at a pile of rubbish in the street
of a town , defendant pleaded that plaintiffwas guilty of
contributory negligence in driving a horse she knew to be
unsafe, with weak and rotten harness, defendant was confined
to proof of such actsof negligence, and it was not error for the
court to failto embrace in its instructionsthe question as to
whether or not plaintiffwas exercisingordinary care at the
time she received her injuries. Board of Councilmen of
Nicholasvillev. Fain ,99 S. W. 275, 30 R. 564.
Reasonable or ordinary care is such care as may be usually
expectedof ordinarilyprudent persons under likecircumstances.
Cincinnati, etc., R. Co. v . Hill, 89 S. W. 523,28 R. 530 .
One drivingon to a railroadat a crossing, where the gates
have negligentlybeen leftup though a train is approaching,
has no right to rely exclusivelyon the operativesof the gates
or of the train in looking out for his safety and giving him
notice, but he must use " ordinary care"-such care as an
ordinarilyprudent person would use under the same or similar
circumstances to discover the approach of the train and for
his own safety; and if he does so rely on the others, without
using such care,he is guilty of contributorynegligence. L. &
N. R. Co. v .Roth , 130 Ky .759, 114 S. W. 264.
In an action against a streetcar company for injuriesto
the driver of a wagon in a collisionbetween the wagon and a
street car, it was proper for the court to instruct that
" ordinary care," as applied to the motorman , is meant the
degree of skilland care which men of average prudence and
skill, engaged in operatingstreetcars by electricpower in a
citylikethat in which the accidentoccurred, on a streetlike
that on which the accident occurred, usually exerciseunder

ORDINARY CARE 2435 ORDINARY CARE
similarcircumstances. LouisvilleRy . Co. v. Boutellier, 110
S. W. 357, 33 R. 484.
An instructionwhich , afterdefiningordinary care, says that
" extraordinarycare is such care as an extraordinarilyprudent
man would use under the circumstancesproven in the case,"
was misleading. If afterseeingthe child'sdanger the motor-
man in control of the street car used all the means at his
command to stop the car, nothing more was required. Paducah
StreetRy . Co. v .Adkins ,14 R. 425.
In an action against a railroad company to recover for
the loss of plaintiff'sproperty by fire communicated from
defendant's depot, which it is claimed was set on firethrough
defendant'snegligence, the court properly instructedthat itwas
defendant's duty to exercisesuch care as " men of ordinary
prudence under like circumstances would employ to prevent
injury to their own property." L. & N. R. Co. v. Blair, 11
R. 578 .
Before a man deals with a gun or pistolas if it were not
charged, it is incumbent upon him to ascertainwhether it is
so or not, and if he does not use reasonable caution in this
respect, and afterwards, upon pullingthe trigger, itunexpec-
tedly explodes and killsa person, it will be manslaughter.
Barnard v . Com ., 8 Ky . Opin . 764.
The question of what constitutesordinary care on the part
of one using a public crossing is one for the jury, and the
courts willnot lay down any rule definingit. L. & N. R. Co.
v .Price, 76 S. W. 836, 25 R. 1033.
What is ordinary care in regard to one speciesof property
may not be so in regard to other property; it may vary on
account of the subjectitself, or on account of the circumstances
in which it isplaced. Swigert v . Graham , 7 B. M. 663 .
Ordinary care is such as is commonly exercisedby persons
of ordinarilyprudent habits when placed under like circum-
stances. Needham v . L. & N. R. Co., 85 Ky . 423, 9 R. 61, 8
R. 869. But as the danger to which one is exposed must
