affectthe degree of care in each case, it resultsthat what
would be ordinary care in one kind of business would be

ORDINARY CARE 2436 ORDINARY CARE
gross negligence in another. L. & N. R. Co. v . McCoy , 81
Ky . 403, 5 R. 397.
Ordinary care by employes of railroadsis that degree of
care which a majority of men of prudent and careful habits
would exerciseunder like circumstances to avoid injury to
their own persons from the same risksthat others undergo in
obedience to orders or by reason of this hazardous business.
It isgoing too far to requirethat care which such person would
takeof " hisfamily" placedunder likecircumstances. L. & N.
R. Co. v .McCoy , 81 Ky .403 ,5 R. 397.
In operation of streetcars only ordinary care is required
as to persons not passengers. Ky . Traction & Terminal Co. v .
Bain , 161 Ky . 44, 170 S. W. 499.
While trialcourtsshould adhere to the definitionof ordinary
careadoptedand recognizedby thiscourt,itwas notprejudicial
error to give an instructiondefining ordinary care as " such
care as an ordinarilyprudent person would usually exercise
under similar circumstances in matters involving his own
interest." L. & N. R. Co. v .Allen,153 Ky .252,154 S. W. 1095.
Ordinary Care on Part of a Child. "The court instructs
the jury that 'ordinary care' as to the decedent, G., is that
degree of care that boys of his age, capacity and experience
usually exerciseunder like or similar circumstances. Negli-
gence is the failureto exerciseordinary care." Approved in
Goldstein'sAdmr .v . LouisvilleRy . Co., 115 S. W. 194.
An instruction telling the jury that the intestate was
required to use ordinary care, was not prejudicialbecause it
omitted the qualificationconsideringhis age, capacity and
intelligence; where he was seventeen years old, weighed 150
pounds ,was a bright intelligentboy, and was killedby running
in front of a streetcar, a danger he well understood. Stone's
Admr .v . Lexington. Ry . Co., 157 Ky . 121, 162 S. W. 778 .
Ordinary Care of Physician or Surgeon . The care and
skillrequiredof a physicianin treatinga patientisnot to be
measured by thatexercisedby " ordinarilyskillfuland prudent
physiciansin that (particular) vicinityin treatinga like
injury," but by such as isexercisedgenerallyby physiciansof

ORDINARY CARE 2437 ORDINARY CARE
ordinary care and skillin similarcommunities. Burk v .Foster,
114 Ky . 20, 69 S. W. 1096, 24 R. 791.
The mere fact that the resultof a patient'streatment " is
as good as isusuallyobtainedin likecasessimilarlysituated"
will not preclude a recovery by the patient against the
physician for negligence and lack of skill, the patient being
entitledto the chance for the betterresultswhich might come
from proper treatment. Id .
Action based on surgeon leaving gauze sponge in wound .
Samuels v. Willis, 133 Ky . 459, 118 S. W. 339.
For discussionof criminal liabilityof physiciansand
surgeons, see Roberson's Ky . Crim . Law , Secs. 203-206.
Ordinary Care of Motorman and Pedestrian Distinguished.
" By the term 'ordinary care' as I have used it in the instruc-
tionsgiven you as applied to the motorman ,ismeant the degree
of care which men of average prudence and skill, engaged in
operating streetcars by electricpower in a citylikeLouisville,
and on a streetlike Market Street, between E. and C. streets
usually exerciseunder similarcircumstances. As applied to the
plaintiffit means the degree of care which a man of average
prudence, driving a wagon in such a city and along such a
street, usually exercisesunder such circumstances for his own
safety. 'Negligence means the failure to exercise ordinary
care.' " Approved in LouisvilleRy . Co. v . Boutellier, 110
S. W. 357, 33 R. 484, in which case the court held that in that
action for injuriesto the driver of a wagon by being struck
by a street car, the above instructionproperly defined
" ordinary care" as applied to the driver of the wagon , and
to the motorman ; that in such case it is improper to point out
in an instructionthe particularthings concerning which it is
the duty of the travelerto exerciseordinary care, but it is
proper to point out and define with more particularitythe
duty resting upon persons in charge of engines and cars;
that thisdistinctionismade because of the dangerous character
of the machines they are operating, and it has become a
firmly fixed law that the duty of lookout and warning is
included in the ordinary care required of persons operating
engines or cars.

ORDINARY CARE 2438 ORDINARY DILI .
It was proper for the court to refuse to instruct that it
was the duty of plaintiffto use ordinary care for his own safety,
and to exerciseall of his senses as persons of ordinary care
usually do under the same or similarcircumstancesto ascertain
whether or not a car was approaching, and it was his duty not
to change his course and turn across the track without exercis-
ing ordinary care to discoverwhether or not he was in danger
of collidingwith streetcars or other vehicleson the street, as
this instructionimproperly pointed out the particularthings
concerning which it was the duty of the travelerto exercise
ordinary care. LouisvilleRy . Co. v . Boutellier, 110 S. W. 357,
33 R. 484 .
Operation of Automobile . In an action for injuriesto a
boy by being run over by an automobile in a street, the court
held that the following instructionas to ordinary care was
proper : " Ordinary care," as used in these instructionswith
referenceto the agent of the defendant in charge of the auto-
mobile, means that degree of care which ordinarilycarefuland
prudent persons, operatingautomobilesof like characterto
that used by defendant, usually exerciseunder likeor similar
circumstances, and negligence is the failure to exercisesuch
care. Fulkerson v .Akers , 145 Ky . 187, 140 S. W. 167.
Use of Word Skillful. An instructionusing the word
" skillful," definingordinary care and negligence, was not
objectionablein an actionto recoverfor injuriessustainedby
a fallingtelephonepole, the businessof operatinga franchise
along a highway necessarilyrequiringa certainamount of
skilland prudence. Cumberland Tel. & Tel. Co. v. Warner,
79 S. W. 199, 25 R. 1843.
An instructionthat ordinary care is that degree of care
which ordinarilyprudent and " skillful" men usuallyexercise,
if erroneous, is only technicallyso, and not prejudicial.
Southern Ry . Co. in Ky . v. Otis' Admr ., 78 S. W. 480, 25
R. 1686 .
ORDINARY DILIGENCE .- Ordinary diligencemeans that
degreeof careor attentionor exertionwhich under the circum-
stancesa man of ordinary prudence and discretionwould

ORDINARY INCON . 2439 ORDINARY RISKS
exercisein referenceto the particularthing were it his own
property. Swigert v . Graham , 7 B. M. 663.
ORDINARY INCONVENIENCE AND DAMAGE .- The
diminution in value of the entire tract is as much a taking
within the meaning of the Constitutionas is the stripof land
upon which the road-bed lies, and the generalenhancement of
the value of the tractnot taken by reason of the improvement
can not be set-off against thisdiminution. But the " ordinary
inconvenienceand damage " that may resultfrom the prudent
operationof the road may be set-off by the benefitsand
advantages, ifany ,that may be reasonablyanticipatedfrom the
constructionand operation of the road. L. & N. R. Co. v .
Ingram , 12 R. 456 .
ORDINARY MEANING OF WORDS .- Words in a contract
must be interpretedaccording to their ordinary and popular
sense, unless from the context it appears to have been the
intentionof the parties that they should be understood in
a differentsense, or by the usage of trade or the custom of
the country they have been understood by the particularor
peculiar meaning as distinguishedfrom their ordinary mean-
ing. Spring Garden Ins. Co. v . Imperial Tobacco Co., 132 Ky .
7,116 S. W. 234,20 L. R. A. (N.S.) 277,136 Am .St. Rep .164 .
ORDINARY NEGLIGENCE .-See , also, NEGLIGENCE.
Under Ky . Stats., Sec. 6 , there may be a recovery against
the master for the death of a servant resulting from the
ordinary negligenceof a superiorservant engaged in the
same employment , though there could have been no recovery
for the injury if it had not resultedin death. Cincinnati,
N. O. & T. P. Ry . Co. v . Cook , 113 Ky . 161, 67 S. W. 383,
23 R. 2410 .
ORDINARY RISKS . An instructionon assumed riskby a
servant, that ordinary risks of servicemeans such risks as
were known to the servant at the time he entered upon the
service, althougherroneous, was held not to misleadthe jury,
in view of otherinstructiongiven. Lexington & Big Sandy
R. Co. v . Joера, 6 Ку . Opin . 662.

ORDINARY ROOF 2440 ORGANIZATION TAX
ORDINARY ROOF.- An ordinary roof is a roof that can be
made reasonablysafeby the ordinary propping usuallydone by
the miner himself. Duncan Coal Co. v . Thompson's Admr .,157
Ky . 304, 162 S. W. 1139.
ORDINARILY SAFE . “ There must be some point short
of perfectionthat isnot actionablenegligence; that such depres
sions of an inch or so are quite common in allcities, and that
that which is so customary may be regarded as ordinarilysafe
and as the standard. Teager v . City of Flemingsburg , 109
Ky .746." City of Lexington v . Cooper, 148 Ky . 17, 145 S. W.
1127 .
Customarily the question of whether a sidewalk or street
is in an ordinarilysafe condition is for the jury; and yet it
would be manifestlybegging the questionto say that, no
matterhow slightthe inequalityof surface,or how immaterial
the obstruction, the case should go to the jury. City of Cov-
ington v . Belser, 137 Ky . 125 ; City of Louisvillev . Uebelhar,
142 Ky . 151 ; City of Lexington v . Cooper, 148 Ky . 17, 145
S. W. 1127 .
ORGANIZATION AND GOVERNMENT .- The annexation
of territoryto citiesrelatesto their " organizationand govern-
ment," withinSec. 156, Ky . Constitution, providingfor the
classificationof citiesfor that purpose, and the laws in rela-
tion to the annexation of territoryto citiesof the various
classesmay differ. Lewis v. Town of Brandenburg, 105 Ky.
14, 47 S. W. 862, 48 S. W. 978, 20 R. 1011.
ORGANIZATION TAX . Organization tax on corporations
is one-tenth of one per cent. of capital stock. Ky . Stats.,
Sec . 4225 .
A corporation which has once paid the organizationtax
as provided in Sec. 4225, Kentucky Statutes, shallnot be
requiredto againpay an organizationtax when itextendsits
corporateexistenceby amendment as provided in Sec. 559,
Kentucky Statutes. Ohio ValleyTie Co. v. Bruner, 148 Ку .
358 , 146 S. W. 749 .
Sec.4225,Kentucky Statutes, imposingan organizationtax
on corporationsisnot retroactive. The tax is imposedon the

ORGANIZE ORIGINAL CON .2441
capitalstock of corporationsorganized after itsenactment and
upon the increaseof stock issuesmade after itsenactment by
all corporations. Com . v . Southern PacificCompany , 164 Ky .
818, 176 S. W. 375.
ORGANIZE.- To put in working order; to arrange in order
for the normal exerciseof its appropriate functions. Oliver
Co. v . LouisvilleRealty Co., 156 Ky . 628, 161 S. W. 570, in
dissentingopinion,citingBlack's Law Dictionary.
ORGANIZED .- Ky . Stats., Sec. 547, provides that stock-
holders of corporationsnot " organized" for educationalpur-
poses, or for operatingwater, gas, or electricplants, shall
be individuallyresponsiblefor their liabilitiesto the extent
of the amount of their stock at par , in addition to the
amount of such stock; and Sec. 573 declares that after Sep-
tember 28, 1897, the provisionsof the chapter containing
the previous sectionshallapply to all corporations organized
in the State, if such provisions would be applicable to
them if organized under such chapter. Held , that where
a corporation previously organized for the purpose of " ex-
ploitingor promoting" the fireand policetelegraphbusi-
ness,and to purchaseand otherwiseacquirestocks, bonds, and
other obligationsof other corporations, etc., continued its
businessafter Sec. 573 went into effect, its stockholderswere
subjectto the double liabilityprescribedby Sec. 547. Game-
well Fire Alarm Tel. Co. v . Fire & Police Tel. Co., 116 Ky .
759, 76 S. W. 862, 25 R. 1010.
ORIGINAL CAPITAL STOCK . By the term " original
capitalstock," used in the fourth sectionof the act of Febru-
ary 10, 1869, is clearlymeant the amount of stock subscribed,
or issuedand sold, which has been actuallyexpended for the
accomplishmentof the objectof the charter- that is the con-
structionof the road. Bank Lick Turnpike Co. v. Phelps, 81
Ky . 615.
ORIGINAL CONSTRUCTION , OR IMPROVEMENT . See,
also, CONSTRUCTED ; RECONSTRUCTION .

ORIGINAL CON . 2442 ORIGINAL CON .
A streetis not originallyconstructeduntilit is improved
at the costof the adjoiningpropertyowners. City of Louis-
villev . Stoll, 159 Ky . 138, 166 S. W. 811.
The repairing of an old turnpike road which had been
taken into a city along with other territorydid not constitute
an originalconstructionwithin the meaning of the charter of
citiesof the firstclass, which makes abutting property owners
liablefor the improvement of a streetonly in case of original
construction, so as to preclude the city from assessing the
cost of a subsequent improvement againstthe property owners .
Wymond v . Barber Asphalt Paving Co., 77 S. W. 203, 25 R.
1125 .
The spreading of cinders and ashes upon a graded street
can not be called originalconstruction; it is only temporary
in itsnature, and only intended to serve the passing needs of
the city until such time as a permanent improvement can
be made . A subsequent improvement of such streetby paving
with brick is an original construction. Adams v . City of
Ashland , 80 S. W. 1105, 26 R. 184 .
The fact that a drain existed in a street is not sufficient to
show that a sewer subsequentlyconstructedwas not an original
improvement in the absence of evidence showing that the drain
was a sewer within the purview of the statute, and that the
cost thereof had been assessedagainst the abutting property
owners. Gesser v . John B. McLane & Co., 156 Ky . 743, 161
S. W. 1118 .
Ky . Stats., Sec. 3100, provides that no error in the proceed-
ings of the general council shall exempt from payment after
the work is done as required by the ordinance or contract,
but that no ordinance for any original improvements men-
tioned in the act shallpass both branches of the general coun-
cilat the same meeting, and at leasttwo weeks shallintervene
between its passage in the two branches, and section 3105
of the act the generalcouncilisgiven power to contractfor
sewers. Held , that the constructionfor the firsttime of a
sewer in front of defendant's property was an " original
improvement " within the statute. Thomas v . Woods , 128 Ky .
555 , 108 S. W. 878 , 32 R. 1405 .

ORIGINAL CON . 2443 ORIGINAL CON .
The regrading of an old turnpike road taken into a city
by the extension of its limits is an original constructionof
the streetwithin Sec. 2833, Ky . Stats. McHenry v . Selvage,
99 Ky .232, 35 S. W. 645, 18 R. 473 .
Under Sec. 3561, Ky . Stats., a turnpikeroad taken into
a cityby an extensionof itsboundary and acceptedby the
city becomes a street thereof, but an improvement subse-
quently made is an originalconstructionand not the recon-
struction of a street. Mackin v . Wilson, 45 S. W. 663, 20
R. 218 .
Until property once bears specificallythe cost of building
a pavement abutting it, done on the order of the council of
the cityor by the city'sexpresspermission, indicatedby act
of its legislativebody , there has not been an original con-
structionof such improvement within a statute allowing the
city to order the originalconstructionof such improvement
at the cost of the abutting owners . Guilfoyle'sExr . v . City
of Maysville, 129 Ky . 532, 112 S. W. 666.
A city ordering the grading and paving of a streeta part
of which had been improved by an abutting owner at his own
cost, on his own initiative, orders the originalconstructionof
the street, and the abutting owners may be taxed for the cost
thereof. Sparks v. Barber Asphalt Paving Co., 129 Ky . 769,
112 S. W. 830, 22 L. R. A. (N.S.) 877, 130 Am . St. Rep . 492.
The constructionof a streetupon such a highway is not a
reconstructionof it,but an originalconstruction, and the prop-
erty owner is liablefor the cost. McHenry v . Selvage, 99 Ky .
232; Sparks v. Barber Asphalt Paving Co., 129 Ky . 769;
Gernert v . City of Louisville, 155 Ky . 589, 159 S. W. 1163.
It is well settledin thisjurisdictionthat when a public
highway is taken into a city, it becomes ipso facto a street
of the city; and , that the constructionof a streetupon such
a highway is not a reconstructionof it, but an originalcon-
struction. Gernert v . City of Louisville, 155 Ky . 591 ; Town
of Erlanger v . Cody , 158 Ky . 625, 166 S. W. 202.
A street, withoutregardto itspreviouscondition, may be
originallyimproved once at the expense of the abuuting owner ,
and where the property of the abutting owner has never been
assessedfor streetconstruction, and the work so far as the

ORIGINAL CON . 2444 ORIGINAL CON .
owner is concernedis originalconstruction, the cityhas the
rightto charge them with the expense of it. Kimbley v .Hick-
man , 163 Ky . 713, 174 S. W. 484.
A street improvement made by paving a streetwith fire-
clay brick in place of a macadamized road laid some thirty
years previous, was an original construction, and properly
assessedunder Ky . Stats., Sec. 3572, providing that an original
constructionof any streetshallbe made at the exclusivecost
of the owners of abutting lots, and not a reconstruction, the
costof which under the provisionsof Sec. 3565 is to be borne
exclusivelyby the city. City of Catlettsburgv. Self, 115 Ky .
669, 74 S. W. 1064 ,25 R. 161.
Ky . Stats., Sec.3096, provides for the constructionor recon-
struction of the streets, alleys, and other public ways and
sidewalksof citiesof the second class,and stipulatesthat where
originalconstructionof " publicways " is made of improved
material it shall be at the exclusivecost of the owners , and
reconstructionat half the costof the owners, the cost of making
sidewalks to be apportioned to the owners and paid by them.
Held , that abutting owners are liablefor the whole cost of
originalor reconstructionof sidewalks. Mudge v . Walker , 90
S. W. 1046, 28 R. 996.
Where a streethad formerly been a turnpike and had been
for many years macadamized from curb to curb by putting
rock on it from time to time, paving the roadway between the
curbings with vitrifiedbrick under an ordinance by which the
abutting owners were charged with part of the cost, was not
a reconstruction,but originalconstruction, withinthe purview
of the statute. Lindsey v . Brawner , 97 S. W. 1, 29 R. 1236.
An ordinary dirt road was paved with macadam eighteen
feet wide and twelve inches deep ; there was no curbing or
guttering, the originaldirtroad remaining outsidethe eighteen-
foot macadam pike. The road remained in this condition,
being kept up by the county for about twenty years, when
itwas taken into a city, afterwhich itwas graded, curbed,
and paved with vitrifiedbrick from curb to curb, the way
thus improvedbeing fortyfeetwide. Held, thatthe fact
that the road had been improved twenty years before as a
countyhighway,and had thereafterbeenkeptup as such, did

ORIGINAL ENTRIES 2445 OSTENSIBLE AU .
not constitutethe latterimprovement a reconstruction. Heim
v .Figg , 89 S. W. 301, 28 R. 396.
ORIGINAL ENTRIES .- Two of the requisitesof admissi-
bilityof originalentriesin account books are: (1) that the
entriesmust be original, and (2) the party who made them
must , as a general rule, have had personal knowledge of the
factsrecorded. Cincinnati, etc., Ry . Co. v . Smith & Johnson ,
155 Ky . 481, 159 S. W. 987.
ORIGINAL IMPROVEMENT .-See ORIGINAL CONSTRUC-
TION .
ORIGINAL PACKAGE .— “ The doctrine(of originalpack-
age) has no applicationwhere the manufacturer puts up the
package with the express intentof evading the laws of another
State, and is enabled to carry out his purpose by the facile
agency of an express company and the connivance of his
consignee." Grigler v . Com ., 120 Ky . 526, 87 S. W. 276, 27
R. 918 .
ORIGINATE , CARRY ON AND CONSUMMATE . In an
action by a servant where he admitted that he had made a
settlementcovering all of his claim for damages growing out
of the accidentexcept for injury to his eye, and admitted that
he had capacity to " originate, carry on and consummate " a
settlementto a certainextent, and the only question was , did
he have capacity to understand whether or not the settlement
he made was in full of allclaims for injury, plaintiffcan not
complain because the court instructedthat " the mental condi-
tion of the plaintiffis to be considered only as bearing on
the abilityof plaintiffto understand that the settlementwas
or was not in full of all of plaintiff'sclaim for injury."
Wells v.Royer Wheel Co., 114 S. W. 737 .
ORPHAN .- " An orphan, in legalparlance, is a fatherless
child." Poston v. Young , 7 J. J. Marshall, 501.
OSTENSIBLE AUTHORITY .- Where one, by want of
ordinarycare, inducesthirdpersonsto believethat another
ishisagent,empowered to perform certainacts, althoughsuch
ostensibleagent is not an agent in fact, such one is bound

OSTEOPATHY OTHER BRANCH2446
by the acts of the apparent or ostensibleagent. Liability
arisesfor the actsof such ostensibleagent, not becausehe is
in point of fact an agent, but because the principal will not
be permittedto deny it, to the injuryof thirdpersonswho
have in good faithand in the exerciseof reasonableprudence
dealt with the agent upon the faith of his apparent and
ostensibleauthority. Roberts v . Akers , 162 Ky . 840, 173 S.
W. 164 .
OSTEOPATHY.- " Osteopathy" teachesneithertherapeu-
tics, materia medica , surgery, nor bacteriology. It reliesen-
tirelyon manipulation of the body for the cure of diseases.
It administersno drugs. It uses no knife. Nelson v. State
Board of Health, 57 S. W. 501 , 22 R. 438 ,50 L. R. A. 383.
OTHER ARTICLES .- Printed inhibitionagainstkeeping
certain articlescan not be relied on to avoid a policy that
insures the holder against the loss of those very articles. A
policyupon a stock of dry goods " and such other articlesas
are usually kept in a country store" was not avoided by
keeping gunpowder and coal oil, although a condition in the
policy prohibited the keeping of these articlesunless the
consentof the company shouldbe indorsedon the policy; it
appearing from the proof that gunpowder and coal oil are
" such articlesas are usuallykept in a country store." Amer-
ican Fire Ins. Co. v . Nugent , 7 R. 597, 8 R. 119.
OTHER BANK . These words held to embrace only incor-
porated or legalizedbanks. Campbell's Exr .v .Bank , 10 Bush ,
155 .
OTHER BRANCH ROADS . While the Legislaturehas the
power to grant a railroadcompany the rightto take land
already appropriatedto another public use, yet such an
intentionwillnot be presumed , but must be shown by express
words or by necessaryimplication. Therefore a grant of power
to lay out a railroad between certain termini, where the
precisecourseand directionare not prescribed, but are left
to the corporationto be locatedbetween the termini,does not
give authorityprima facieto take the roadbed of a public
highway as the track of the railroador to destroy the high

OTHER CAUSE 2447 OTHER DELE .
way by removing itssupport. And the fact that the corpora-
tion is authorized in general terms to construct" other branch
roads" does not enlarge its power in this regard. L. & N.
R. Co. v. Whitley County Court, 95 Ky . 215, 15 R. 734.
OTHER CAUSE . An unfounded charge by the husband
of unchastity on the part of the wife does not entitleher to
an absolutedivorce, but issufficientto authorizea divorce from
bed and board, under Sec. 2121 Ky . Stats., authorizing the
court to grant a divorce from bed and board for any cause
named in the statute, or for any other cause considered
sufficient. Kefauver v .Kefauver , 57 S. W. 467, 22 R. 386.
OTHER COUNTRY . It is provided in CivilCode, Sec.
732 , that in the construction of the Code the words " other
country," " foreigncorporation," and words of likeimport,
referto any part of the world out of this State.
OTHER COUNTY BUILDINGS.- See COUNTY BUILDINGS .
OTHER CRIMES . Under the Constitution, which declares
that laws shall be made to exclude from officeand suffrage
those convictedof bribery, perjury, forgery, " or other crimes
or lightmisdemeanors ," the Legislaturemay pass laws exclud.
ing from suffrage not only those convicted of crimes which
by the common law render the perpetrator infamous, but
those who may be convicted of any other crime or high mis-
demeanor . Anderson v . Winfree,85 Ky .597, 9 R. 181.
The statutewhich providesthat " any person convictedof
robbery, forgery, counterfeiting, or any other crime shall
forfeithis rightof suffrageand right to hold office," deprives
one convicted of grand larceny of the right to vote. Id .
OTHER CROPS .- A mortgage of " my presentcrop of
tobacco and other crops with other property to me belonging"
istoo indefinitein descriptionto pass titleto any thing but
the tobacco. Boulware v .Pendleton, 6 R. 727.
OTHER DELETERIOUS SUBSTANCES .- The phrase
" Other deleterioussubstances" held not sufficientlyspecificto
satisfythe rulesof good pleading. Eaton v . Green River Coal
& Coke Co., 157 Ky . 159, 162 S. W. 807.

OTHER EXPENSES 2448 OTHER INDUSTRIAL
OTHER EXPENSES NEEDFUL .- Ky . Stats., Sec. 4464,
provides for an electionupon the questionof levying a taxto
maintain a graded common schooland for erectingor repairing
suitablebuildingsthereforifnecessary. Sec.4481 (Sec.5758),
makes it the duty of the districttrusteesto provide funds for
purchasing suitablebuildingsor for erectingor repairingthem,
and for other expenses needful in conducting a good graded
common school, and permits them to use such part of the
proceeds of the tax as they may deem necessary. Held , that
the phrase " and for other expenses needful in conductinga
good graded common school" impliesthe power to renta suit-
able building temporarily and until one can be secured or
builtby the trustees, and a petitionfor the election, reciting
that the proposed tax was to be used for the purpose of main-
taining a graded common school and for the erecting, purchas-
ing, leasing, and repairingof suitablebuildingsthereforif
necessary, was no more than the two sectionsof the statute
authorized, meaning that the trusteeswere to buy a building
if they could, and if not, to buy a lot and build a house, to
repair any buildings bought or built by them , and in the
meantime to leasea suitablebuildingfor the continuationofthe
school, and did not render the petitioninsufficient. JeffriesV.
Board of Trusteesof Columbia Graded Common School, 135
Ky .488,122 S. W. 813.
OTHER INDEBTEDNESS.- Where a railroadcorporation,
afteracquiringallthe stockof anotherrailroadcoorporation,
accepteda transferof allthelatter'sproperty, subjecttoits
bonded indebtednessand all other indebtedness," without
affectingthe rightsof " creditors" therein, the purchaser
became liablefor the tortsof the vendor theretoforecommitted,
as the word " indebtedness" should be construed in itsbroadest
sense, as includingclaimsforunliquidateddamages, and not
merely contractual liabilities, and the word " creditors" as
includingall personsholdingclaimsagainstthe grantor.
L.& N. R. Co.v.Biddell,112Ky .494,66 S.W. 34,23 R.1702.
OTHER INDUSTRIAL PURSUITS .- Annotated Statutes,
SouthDakota,1901,Sec. 3812, authorizingtheformationof

OTHER INJURIES 2449 OTHER MUNIC .
corporationsfor mining ,manufacturing , mechanical, quarrying,
" and other industrial pursuits, and for any other lawful
business," does not limit the formation of corporations to
purposessimilarto those enumerated. Vokes v. Eaton, 119
Ky . 913, 85 S. W. 174, 27 R. 358.
OTHER INJURIES .- See INTERNAL INJURIES .
OTHER INSURANCE .— Where a policyprocured by the
owner provides that it should be void " if now or hereafter
there be other insurance on any property hereby insured,"
the fact that a mortgagee subsequently procures insurance
upon his interestdoes not render the policy void, the interest
thus insured being distinctfrom the owner's interest; and
especially is this true where neither the owner nor the
mortgagee has any knowledge of the insurance procured by
the other. Home Ins. Co. v. Koob , 113 Ky . 360, 68 S. W. 453,
24 R. 223 , 58 L. R. A. 58 .
Fire policiesissued without request are not additional
insurance. Ins. Co. v . Robertson, 134 Ky . 529, 121 S. W. 630.
OTHER MACHINES AND CONTRIVANCES .- Under
Criminal Code , Sec. 124, providing that an indictment must
correctlyand certainlydescribethe party, offense, county, and
circumstances, an indictment charging defendant with setting
up a faro bank , and specifyingthe settingup of a faro bank
and " other machines and contrivances" for betting, was not
bad for duplicity, the settingup of a faro bank being the
only offensecharged and the only one on which a conviction
could be had , and the allegationsas to other machines and
contrivancesbeing surplusage. Miller v. Com ., 117 Ky . 80, 77
S. W. 682, 79 S. W. 250, 25 R. 1236, 25 R. 1931.
OTHER MUNICIPALITIES . " In City of Covington v.
District of Highlands , 113 Ky . 612, we also held that the
right of the districtto levy and collecttaxes could not be
questioned, thisconclusionbeingbasedprincipallyupon the
factthat the provisionsof the Constitutionrelatingto taxation,
in using the terms 'towns, cities, taxing districts, and other
municipalities,' contemplatedthe continuedexistenceof cer

OTHER OFFICERS 2450 OTHER PERSONS
tain municipalitiesin the Commonwealth which could not be
classifiedas 'citiesor towns' but are embraced by the phrase
'other municipalities.'" Gleason v . Weber , 155 Ky . 431, 159
S. W. 976 .
While itisobviouslytrue that the words " citiesand towns,"
as used in the Constitution and statutes, do not embrace a
districtor quasi municipality such as the districtof Clifton,
the latterisone of the " other municipalities" mentioned in the
Constitution and Statutes. It is of like character to the
Districtof the Highlands, of which itwas said by thiscourt in
City of Covingtonv.DistrictofHighlands,113 Ky .612: " The
districtis not a town , and , therefore, could not be properly
classifiedas such, as the Legislature was required by the
Constitutionto do ." Gleason v . Weber , 155 Ky . 431, 159
S. W. 976 .
OTHER OFFICERS . Sec. 160, Constitution, expressly
makes two years the term of officeof city legislativeboards
generally. In the next clause the manner of electing such
boards in citiesof the firstand second classesis prescribed,
and in the succeeding clause provides that " other officers" of
towns or cities, if elected by the voters thereof, shall hold
officefor " four years and until theirsuccessorsshallbe quali-
fied. Held ,that the provisionas to the terms of officeof " other
officers" was plainlyexclusiveof the provisionas to the terms
of city legislativeboards, and was not open to construction.
McDermott v. City of Lou ., 98 Ky .50,32 S. W. 264, 17 R. 617.
The omission of Sec. 160, Constitution, providing that city
legislativeboards shall hold officefor two years, to further
provide that they shallhold until their successorsare chosen,
willnot be suppliedby the courts. Id.
OTHER PERSONS . Sec. 4171, Ky . Stats., providingthat
suitsand motions againstsheriffs, clerks, " and allother persons
requiredto pay money into the State treasury," may be
institutedin the Franklin CircuitCourt and prosecutedas
prescribedby law, authorizesan actionby the Commonwealth
againsta corporationto recoverfranchisetaxes which are
payableintothe State treasury. Cent. Ry . & Bridge Co. v.
Com ., 49 S. W. 456, 20 R. 1890.

OTHER PRO . 2451 OTHER STATE
OTHER PROCEEDINGS.- Where a judgment is reversed
on appeal the case does not stand for " trial" or for " other
proceedings" at the next succeeding term of the lower court,
unless the mandate of the appellatecourt has been filedin the
clerk'sofficeof the lower court and noticethereofgiven to the
oppositeparty ten days before the commencement of such term ;
and thisthough the mandate directsa particularjudgment to
be entered. Lloyd v. Matthews , 92 Ky . 300, 13 R. 537 .
OTHER PERSONAL PROPERTY .- Notes and accounts
belonging to a debtor, but found in the hands of another, are
exempt to the debtor under the Act of April 8, 1884, allowing
a bona fidehousekeeper, with a family, " other personal prop-
erty" in lieu of exempted articlesnot on hand . Miller v .
Mahoney , 16 R. 799, 16 R. 400 .
OTHER PROCESS . An Act declaringthat originalwrits
and " other process" may be executed at any time before the
return day , will obviously include the writ of scire facias.
Patrick v. Newell, 1 Bibb ,323 .
OTHER PROPERTY .- Where the articlesof personalty
belonging to the estate, which are directedby statuteto be set
apart to the widow as exempt , are not on hand , she is not
entitled to their value out of the real estate. The statute
directing that the widow shall have the value of such articles
out of " other property" refersto personal property. Bowling
v . Shepherd , 91 Ky . 273, 12 R. 831.
OTHER RAILROAD PURPOSES .- See RAILROAD PURPOSES .
OTHER STATE OFFICIAL . Sec. 114,Ky . Stats., instruct-
ing the attorney-general to collectall claims in favor of the
Commonwealth and authorizinghim to employ such attorneys
" in the variouscounties" as may be necessary, isnot limited
in effectby Sec. 115,declaringthatthe governorshallemploy
counsel in certain cases only and providing in a subsequent
clausethat no " otherStateofficialshall" employ counselto
representthe Commonwealth in any actionbrought or pending
in the Franklin CircuitCourt. Rogers v. Bradley, 100 Ky .
344, 38 S. W. 501, 19 R. 114.

OTHER SUITABLE 2452 OTHER YEARS
OTHER SUITABLE SIGNALS .- An instruction which
authorizesthe jury to find for the plaintiffin an action for
damages forpersonalinjuryifthey believethatthedefendant
failedto give reasonableand timely warnings of itsapproach,
by ringing its bell or sounding its whistle, or by " other
suitablesignals," is not prejudicialto defendant, but rather
beneficialto it. L. & N. R. Co. v . Penrod , 66 S. W. 1013,
1042 , 24 R. 50 .
OTHER TESTIMONY .- Confessions out of court must
be corroborated. The meaning of Sec. 238, Crim . Code , is,
that besidesthe proof of confession, unlessmade in open court,
there must , to warrant a conviction, be other evidence showing
thatsuch an offensehad been committed, and not inconsistent
with the defendant's guilt; and not merely some " other testi-
mony ," which might have a tendency to establishthe charge.
Cunningham v. Com .,9 Bush ,150 .
OTHER VALUABLES . An indictment for taking money ,
" saddlepockets" and " othervaluables," where it isproved the
saddlepocketswere taken, isheld good to sustainfindingof
defendant guilty. Nichols v . Com ., 3 Ky . Opin . 258.
OTHER WITNESSES .- Where the record shows that the
plaintiffoffered to introduce " other witnesses" who would
testify, in substance,as three witnesseshad theretoforetestified,
and the court refused to hear further testimony from the
plaintiff, therewas a sufficientavowal upon the part of the
plaintiffto present the action of the trialcourt for review.
Eaton v . Green River Coal & Coke Co., 157 Ky . 159 , 162
S. W. 807 .
OTHER YEARS .- Where a Federal Court held that an
unreversed judgment of a State Court dismissing an action
against a bank to recover taxes for the years 1893 and 1894
was a bar to the recovery of taxes against the same bank for
certainsubsequent years or any other years," and enjoined
the collectionof taxes for such years, and thereafter, the
judgment of the State court as to taxesfor the years 1893
and 1894 was reversedon appeal; thejudgment of the Federal

OTHERS ACTING IN 2453 OTHERWISE
Court was not a bar to a recovery of taxes for those years,
upon the return of that case to the lower court. Board of
Council v . Deposit Bank of Frankfort, 111 Ky . 950, 65 S. W.
10 , 23 R. 1285 .
OTHERS ACTING IN CONCERT WITH HER .- Where
defendant claimed that the alleged assaultwas committed in
repellingan assaultby plaintiffand certain members of her
family whom the testimony tended to show were acting in
concert with her, it was error, in instructingthe jury, to
limit defendant's right to use force in self-defense to such
force as was necessary to repel the assaultof plaintiffalone,
defendant being entitledto use such force as was apparently
necessary to protect him from personal violence about to be
inflictedon him by plaintiff" and othersacting in concert with
her." Thornton v. Taylor, 54 S. W. 16, 21 R. 1082.
OTHERWISE .- M ., a creditor of an insolvent railroad
corporation, employed an attorney to bring suit upon his
claim, agreeing to pay him a certainper cent. of any amount
realizedfrom theclaim,"whetherby suit,compromise,or other-
wise." The attorney, having obtained judgment and return
of " no property," attached the property of the company ,
which was sold under decree to satisfyall liensupon it, and
purchased by a syndicate of which M. was a member . Liens
prior to the attachment swallowed up the purchase money .
Held , that the attorneywas not entitled, as againsta purchaser
without notice, to a lien upon stock issued to theirclientby a
new corporation formed by the purchasers, whatever might
be the rights of the attorney if M. had not sold the stock.
Morton v .Hallam , 89 Ky . 165, 11 R. 447 .
A creditor employed attorneys to bring suit on a claim
against an insolventcorporation, agreeing to pay them a sum
equal to ten per cent. of any amount that should thereafter
be realizedfrom the claim, " whether by suit, compromise , or
otherwise." Held , that the words " or otherwise" were
intended to refer to what might be realizedby the creditor
by the effortsof his counsel, and that theircompensation should
be confinedto the value of theirservicesin bringing the several

OTHERWISE GREAT . 2454 OUGHT
actions, unless theirservicescontributedto enable the creditor
to realizehis debt. Simrall v . Morton , 6 R. 735.
The defendant was not prejudiced by an instructiontelling
the jury that any deprivationof the libertyof plaintiffby
defendant, " whether it was by actual violence, threats, or
otherwise," constitutesan arrest, for while the use of the
word " otherwise" was rather too indefinite, it could not under
the circumstancesof thiscase,have misled the jury. Miller v .
Ashcraft, 98 Ky . 314, 32 S. W. 1085,17 R. 894.
OTHERWISE GREATLY HURT AND WOUNDED .-The
allegationin a petitionof specificinjuries, followed by the
words " and he was otherwise greatly hurt and wounded ,"
does not authorizeplaintiffto prove injuriesother than those
specificallyalleged; and it was not error to refuse to require
plaintiffto make his petitionmore specific. Chesapeake &
NashvilleRy . Co. v . Hanmer , 66 S. W. 375, 23 R. 1846.
OTHERWISE OBSTRUCT OR HINDER . " The terms
used in thislatterstatuteare, or 'otherwiseobstructor hinder';
and not, as in the former, obstruct and defeat. There is
certainlya distinctionbetween the words defeat and hinder.
The latter, says Webster , means 'to interpose obstaclesor
impediments,' whilst the former means 'frustration; a render-
ing null and void; to resistwith success. Walker v . Sayers,
5 Bush ,582. See, also, titleOBSTRUCT , in thisbook.
OTHERWISE THAN ACCORDING TO LAW .- An indict-
ment for the offenseof arrestinganother, otherwisethan accord-
ing to law , under Sec. 1221 of the Kentucky Statutes, which
charges that the defendant did unlawfully, willfully and
feloniouslyarrestand detain Luther Neal againsthis will,
" otherwise than according to law ," is bad on demurrer .
Carrollv . Com ., 164 Ky .599, 175 S. W. 1043.
OUGHT . It was error for the court to instruct that the
jury " ought" award punitivedamages in the event grounds
existed therefor, as the inflictionof such damages is within
the discretionof thejury solely. L. & N. R. Co. v.Cottengim,
104 S. W. 280 , 31 R. 871 .

OUR HEIRS OUTAGE2455
The right to recover punitive damages for willfulneglectis
a question for the jury, and it is improper to instructthem
that they " ought" to give such damages , and such instruction
is not correctedby another informing them that they " could"
give punitive damages . Ky . Central R. R. Co. v . Gastineau,
83 Ky . 119, 7 R. 3. See, also, MusT .
OUR HEIRS .- Evidence examined and held to show that
the words " our heirs" was insertedby mistake for " his heirs."
Wilson v . Reynolds, 154 Ky . 159, 156 S. W. 1036.
OUR INTEREST .- A guardian,jointowner with hiswards
and othersof a tractof land which he was authorizedby an
act of the Assembly to sell, with others signed a bond to
convey " our (their) interest," gave possessionand a general
warranty deed. Held , that thisbond , signed by the guardian
without referringto his fiduciarycharacter, imposed no obliga-
tion on him or his wards to convey their title. Johnson v .
Johnson , 1 Dana , 364.
OUSTER . An entry on the land of another, to amount to
an ouster, must be made under a claim or colorof right; other-
wise it is a mere trespass. It must be made with the express
and manifest intention of taking possession, and must be
followed by such acts as indicatean intentionof making the
possessionpermanent . L. & N. R. Co. v . Sparks, 14 R. 398.
See, also, ADVERSE POSSESSION.
OUT OF . A legacy of a certainsum " out of" a designated
fund isin general a demonstrativelegacy,pledging a particular
fund as collateralsecurity. Smith v . Lampton , 8 Dana , 71.
OUT OF THE PROCEEDS . A partner who isentitledto a
statedsalary for servicesas manager of the partnership busi-
ness, to be paid " out of the proceeds of said business," can not
sue his co-partner at law for his salarypast due , in the absence
of a settlementof the firm accounts. Stone v . Mattingly, 14
R. 113 .
OUTAGE .- " Outage " is a term applied to leakage from
barrelsof whiskey. Oswald v . Morris, 92 Ky . 50.

OUTCAST 2456 OUTSIDE MAN
OUTCAST.- A publication charging that a man was a
" henchman " of a lawlesscharacter, a " fugitivefrom justice"
and " an outcast" was libelousper se, as these epithetsare
calculatedto bring the person to whom they are applied into
odium and contempt, degrade him in the estimationof society,
as well as reflecton his character. Herald Publishing Company
v .Feltner,158 Ky . 35, 164 S. W. 370.
OUTHOUSE .- A bail bond and order of recognizance
describing the offenseagainst the principal as " breaking an
outhouseand stealingtherefrom" was sufficient, though the
offensedenounced by the statuteis the feloniousbreaking " any
outhouse belonging to or used with a dwelling house," as the
descriptionindicatesin a generalway the nature of the charge,
which is all that is required. Main v . Com ., 56 S. W. 970,
22 R. 228 .
A chicken house on the same lot as the dwelling house, and
owned and used by the same person, is an outhouse, within
the statuteproviding that any person who shall break into a
dwelling house, or " any outhouse belonging to or used with any
dwellinghouse," and carry away anything of value, shall
be guiltyof felony. Price v. Com ., 15 R. 847.
A house used for storinggrain and other things from which
wheat was taken was an " outhouse " within the meaning of
Sec.4,Art .5,Chap .29,Gen. Stats. McHatton v. Com .,7 R. 47.
Any outhouse belonging to or used with any dwelling house,
as used in Sec. 4, Art . 5, Chap . 29, Gen. Stats., embraces
such structures as are ordinarily attached to and used in
connection with dwelling houses, and does not include a
tobacco barn . A tobacco barn is a storehouse or warehouse in
the meaning of Sec. 4, Art . 6 , and one who feloniously
breaksintosucha houseand stealsanythingof valueispunish-
able under that section. White v . Com ., 87 Ку . 454, 10 R. 422.
OUTLAWRY .- A defendant in this State could never be
proceeded against to outlawry. Sneed v . Wiester, 2 Mar. 278.
OUTSIDE MAN .- Where a generalagent of an insurance
company employed a subagent as an " outsideman " to solicit

OVERDRAFTS OVERFLOW2457
applications, receivepremiums , and deliverpolicies, and he was
the only man with whom the public came into contact, and
so far as the public could know had fullpower to representthe
company in allmatters pertainingto insurance, a person who is
induced by him to take insurancemay , in the absence of notice
to the contrary, regard him as an agent of the company with
authority to bind it. Vertressv . Head & Matthews , 138 Ky .
83, 127 S. W. 523.
OVERDRAFTS .- Where a bank cashier allows a customer
to overdraw without authority, and the bank requires him to
make good the overdraft, he is not a volunteer in paying the
debt,but ispayinghisown debt. Mentz'sAssigneev.Mahoney ,
150 Ky . 409, 150 S. W. 503.
When a bank cashier suffered a customer to overdraw
without authorityand the bank repudiated his action, squaring
the account of the customer and taking his note for the over-
draft, the debt to the bank must be regardedas paid, and the
cashiermay maintain an action against the customer for the
money . Mentz's Assignee v . Mahoney , 150 Ky . 409, 150 S. W.
503 .
OVERDUE.- A check drawn on Saturday and negotiated
on the followingMonday was not overdue. Asbury v . Taube ,
151 Ky . 142, 151 S. W. 372. See, also, DUE .
OVERFLOW . Insurance againstall" directloss or damage
by tornado,windstormor cyclone," except"forlossor damage
•occasioneddirectlyor indirectlyby or through high water or
overflow," does not embrace damage caused to insured
buildingssituatedalong a stream in a territorysubjectto
overflow,where duringan unusualrisein thestreamthehouses
are surrounded by water and a wind storm caused the waves
to wash againstthe housesand injurethem. NationalFire
Ins.Co.v. Crutchfield,160 Ky .802,170 S. W. 187.
In an action to recover for overflow of property, where all
the evidence shows that it was an unprecedented and extra-
ordinaryrain and there is no proof to show that ordinary
prudence couldhave providedagainstit, thereis no liability.
Greiner v . Alfred Struck Co., 161 Ky . 793, 171 S. W. 405 .

OVERHEAD CROSS . 2458 OVERSEER
OVERHEAD CROSSINGS .- In Missouri PacificRy . Co. v .
City of Omaha , 235 U. S. - , and cases citedin that opinion,
will be found a discussionof the power of citiesto require
railroadcompaniesto constructoverheadcrossings, or viaducts.
OVER HEATED.- Where a servant,whileperformingnight
work with a fellowservantfor the master, in loadingcinders
from a cinder pit on cars, was overcome by the extreme heat
of the weather and thereby injured, and the evidence showed
that the place of work , appliances for performing it, and
the manner of itsperformance,were reasonablysafe,and that
the work was such as had always been done by two men and
had , for three months , been performed by the injured servant
and another,the master isnot liablein damages for the injuries
sustainedby the former. L. & N. R. Co. v . Williams, 165 Ky .
386, 176 S. W. 1186.
OVER THE SAME LINE . A jointtrafficarrangement, by
which connecting carriershaul from a point on one road for
lessthan the firstcarriercharges from the same point on its
road to itsterminus, between the points, is not in violationof
Ky . Stats., Sec.820, making itan offensefor a carrierto charge
more for hauling for a shorter than for a longer distance
" over the same line" in the same direction, the shorter being
included in the longer distance. Com . v . C. & O. Ry . Co., 115
Ky . 57, 72 S. W. 361, 24 R. 1883. See, also, LONG AND SHORT
HAUL .
OVERRULING DECISIONS .- Decisions on questions of
law can not be overruled by loose expressionsof the court,
used in the course of opinions in later cases in which such
questionswere not considered, or before the court. Cooper v .
Cardwell, 151 Ky . 620, 152 S. W. 589.
OVERSEER .- Ky . Stats., Sec. 4311, requiresthe overseers
of roads to work in the roads in the manner directedby the
FiscalCourt. The FiscalCourt setaparta sum of money forroad
work , and placed it in the hands of the treasureras the road
and bridge fund . The clerk of the Fiscal Court was required
to issuevouchers againstthe fund on the written certificateof

OVERVALUATION 2459 OWN HAND
the various overseersof the county as work was done . The
method was followed except in a few instanceswhere persons
signing the certificateor order failedto add to his signature
the word " overseer." Held that,as itwould be presumed that
the FiscalCourt would perform itsduty to the county, the
treasurerof the county paying ordersof the clerkwas protected,
and was not responsiblefor such payments. Harrison v.
Logan County , 129 Ky . 48, 110 S. W. 377, 33 R. 465 .
OVERVALUATION . Overvaluationof the propertyby the
assured, when made in good faith, can not amount to a
warranty . To avoid the policy for substantialmisrepresenta-
tion the company must show that the assured placed an
overvaluationon the property for the purpose of defrauding
the company . ContinentalIns. Co. v . Ware , 3 R. 621 ; Kenton
Ins. Co. v . Wigginton , 89 Ky . 330, 11 R. 539, 10 R. 587 ;
Dwelling House Ins. Co. v .Freeman , 10 R. 496, 12 R. 894.
See, also,VALUE .
OVERT ACTS . A criminal conspiracy is a corrupt com-
bination of two or more persons by concerted action to do an
unlawful act, or a lawful act by unlawful means , or an act
which would tend to prejudicethe generalpublic. Sparks v.
Com ., 89 Ky . 644, 7 R. 163, 288. Overt acts are not necessary
to the consummation of the offense,and ifsuch actsare charged
they are to be regarded merely as matters in aggravation.
Com .v . Ward , 92 Ky . 158, 13 R. 422.
OWING . Where a grantee assumed " all the indebtedness
now owing at thistime" by the grantorto otherpersons, this
will not embrace a liabilityof the grantor as surety on the
bond of a trustee, where the trusteewas not then in default.
Sellerv. Thompson,52 S. W. 968, 21 R. 698.
OWN HAND AND ACT .- Where the proof of death fur-
nishedby plaintiffshows that the insuredcame to his death
by his " own hand and act," no recovery can be had under a
policyexemptingfrom liabilityfor such a death, withoutsome
satisfactoryexplanationof the proof, or untilitiswithdrawn.
And ifthestatementof the replythatthe proofwas furnished
merely as evidenceof death, and not as proof of the cause,be

OWN HAND OWNER2460
a satisfactoryexplanation,yet, being deniedby rejoinder, the
burden on the issueis on the plaintiff. Prudential Ins. Co. v .
Breustle'sAdmr ., 41 S. W. 9, 19 R. 544. See, also, SUICIDE.
OWNER . See, also, OWNERSHIP .
The word " owner " in Ky . Stats., Sec. 2361, authorizingthe
owner of land, though not in actualpossession, to sue for
trespassthereon, means one who owns the land by a titleof
record deduciblefrom the Commonwealth or who has acquired
ownership by actual adverse possessionof the land to a well-
defined boundary for fifteenyears. Scroggins v . Nave , 133
Ky .793,119 S. W. 158.
An answer averring that defendant is the owner of a home-
stead in the land claimed by plaintiff, and that he is a house-
keeper with a family residing on the land, is not good on
demurrer , there being no averment that he is the owner of the
land, and it appearing that he could not have derived the
homestead through his wife, as she is stillliving. Buffington
v . Mosby , 51 S. W. 192, 21 R. 297.
Evidence of plaintiffthat he was the owner of property
injured, when received without objection, is sufficientproof
of titleto maintain an action for damages caused by blasting.
Lexington & Eastern Ry . Co. v . Baker , 156 Ky . 431, 161
S. W. 228 .
Under the Code , it is sufficientin a suit in ejectment for
the plaintiffsto allegethat they are the owners and entitledto
possessionof the land described. It is not necessary to allege
and show how title was derived . This should come in the
evidence to support the allegationof ownership. War Fork
Land Co. v . Spivey, 162 Ky . 600, 172 S. W. 1042.
Where plaintiff, suing under Sec. 2, Chap . 5, Gen. Stats., is
the mere occupant of the land, and not the owner , he must
allegethat neitherhe nor the owner has receivedcompensation
for fencing it; it is not sufficientto allegethat he has not
receivedsuch compensation; and from the allegationthat he
" occupied" the premises the presumption arisesthat he was
not the real owner . L. & N. R. Co. v. Belcher, 89 Ky . 193,
11 R. 393 .

OWNER OWNER2461
The situs of evidences of indebtedness belonging to non-
residents must be treated as located with the owner . The
" owner" is the person in whom the title, eitherabsoluteor
qualified, is vested. The personal estate of a decedent is
annexed to the person of the administrator; if he lives in
thisstatethe situsof evidencesof indebtednessbelonging to the
estate is here and not with the distributeeswho may live in
another state. Baldwin v . Shine, 84 Ky .502, 8 R. 496.
The person who isthe equitableowner of property when the
15th of September begins is liablefor the tax, as between him
and one who on that day became such owner . Kenton v .
Cormick , 11 R. 487.
Acts 1906, page 115, Chap . 22, Art . 3, Sec. 1, makes it the
duty of an owner or claimant of land to pay allthe taxes which
have been for should have been assessedagainst him for the
years 1901-1905 inclusive; and Sec. 2 provides that the ascer-
tainment of the amount of taxes unpaid and the assessments
requiredby the preceding sectionshallbe made by the County
Court on applicationby the owner by verifiedpetition, in
which the land shall be described so as to be identified.
petitionfiledunder Sec. 2 allegedthat the petitionerclaimed
to be the owner of the therein-describedtractsof land, but not
of the improvements thereon nor of the surfaceof certainparts
of each tract, followingwhich was a descriptionby callsand
distances, and the petitionthen set out the patents and instru-
ments through which petitionerderived titleand alleged that
the lands had not been listedfor taxationfor any of the years
1901-1905 inclusive, by petitioner, but that parts had been
listedby othersand thetaxespaid. Held,thatthepetitionwas
defectiveinthatitshowed onlythatpetitionerwas " an owner,"
and did not show of what part or in what proportionand by
whom the remainingportionswere owned. Eastern Ky . Coal
Lands Corporationv. Com ., 127 Ку . 667, 106 S. W. 260, 108
S. W. 1138,32 R. 129, 33 R. 49, 219 U. S. 140.
A
An indictmentchargingthatthe defendantwas the owner,
occupier, and controllerof a house and ten-pin alley, at which
games were played and money and otherthingsbet, won , and

OWNER OWNER2462
lostby his permission, and that he and others participated, is
sufficientunder the statute. Com .v .Fraize, 5 Bush , 325 ; Rev.
Stat., Chap . 42, Secs. 6 and 7 ; Gen. Stat., Chap . 47 , Secs. 6
and 7 .
A statute requiring that the " owner or owners," of a
building sought to be abated as a nuisance must be summoned
as partiesto the proceedings, includes tenants as well as the
landlord. Fay v . City of Louisville, 6 Ky . Opin . 532 .
Where executors were vested with the titleto property,
they were the " owners " of it, under Ky . Stats., Sec. 3567.
City of Winchester v . Bush , 141 Ky . 711.
Where lands of a non -residentare sold and purchased by
the State for non -payment of taxes, and the State, in an
equitableactionto recoverpossessionof the lands, is adjudged
to be the owner thereof, and is awarded a writ of possession,
the State can not forfeit the lands for a failure of the
owner to listthem for taxationfor fivesuccessiveyears, or
a failureto pay the taxes thereon for five successiveyears,
when during certain of these years the titleto the land was
in the Commonwealth , and the owner was not, therefore,
required to listthe land for taxation. Buffum v . Com ., 153
Ky . 192 ,154 S. W. 1082.
In an action involving the titleto land, it was error to
adjudge partiesto be the owners of land which they did not
claim. Parsons v . Dills, 159 Ky . 471, 167 S. W. 415.
" Owners " of a steamboat sued by the general appellation
are not partiesto the suitunlessdesignatedby name and served
with process actuallyor constructively. Kountz v . Brown , 16
В. М. 585. But the owners having answered (without indi-
vidualizing their respectivenames ) waived all objectionsto
the form of the actions, yet as judgment can not be rendered
without naming them , the plaintiffshould amend his petition
by naming the owners, but no new summons will be necessary.
Rake v. Owners of Steamboat Potomac , 6 Bush , 26 .
Where appelleeallegedthat he was the owner of lands, and
appellantdenied this and pleaded that he was the owner , this

OWNER OWNER2463
allegationwas but an affirmativedenial, which did not require
a reply. Cravens v .Despain , 79 S. W. 276, 25 R. 2018.
The equitableowner of land over which a road is opened,
and not the holder of the legaltitle, is entitledto the damages
allowed for opening the road. Grider v . Porter, 7 R. 47.
Consignee as Owner . Where the consigneesuesfordam-
ages for injury to the goods he need not aver in his petition
that he is the owner , as the law will presume , when nothing
appears to the contrary, that such is the fact, and that the
contract for transportationof the goods was made with him
as such owner . U. S. Mail Line Co. v . Carrollton Furniture
Mfg . Co., 101 Ky . 658, 42 S. W. 342, 19 R. 833.
The rule that the consignee, nothing else appearing, is
presumed to be the owner of the goods and is the proper
person to sue for a misdelivery, has no applicationwhere other
persons have wrongfully ordered the goods in the name of
the consignee, who refusesto receivethem , as they remain the
property of the consignor and he may sue for a misdelivery.
L. & N. R. Co. v. Ft . Wayne ElectricLight Co., 55 S. W.
918 , 21 R. 1544 .
Where a person ships goods consigned to himself, it is
presumed he is the owner of the goods and the billof lading
while the goods are in the possessionof the carrier. C., N. O
& T. P. Ry . Co. v. Steel, 140 Ky . 383, 131 S. W. 22 .
Baggage . Where a traveler is not the owner of goods
which he checks as baggage , but is liableto such owner for
any lossor damage to them , he may be treatedas their owner
for the purposes of an action against the carrierfor damage
to such goods in itshands . I. C. Ry . Co. v .Matthews , 114 Ky .
973, 72 S. W. 302, 24 R. 1766, 60 L. R. A. 864.
Owner in Possession. The term " owner in possession"
of premises, within a statuteprescribinga penalty against
such persons, where intoxicantsare unlawfullysold on their
premises, includes a tenant for a term of years, or of any
freehold or greater estate. Com . v . Smith & McGuiar , 127
Ky . 171, 105 S. W. 397, 32 R. 35.
Owner or Holder. As the purchaserof land at a judicial
saleis not entitledto rents and profitsbefore the confirmation

OWNER OWNERSHIP2464
of the sale, he is not liablefor taxes assessedbefore that time
under a city charter providing that the assessment may be
made againstthe " owner or holder," and therefore, though the
property of a bank was exempt by statutefrom city taxation,
land purchased by it at a judicialsale was subject to a lien
for city taxes assessedbefore the salewas confirmed, the assess-
ment having been made before it became the " owner or
holder" of the property. German Bank v . City of Lou ., 56
S. W. 504, 22 R. 9.
Mechanic's Lien Law . Under Sec.2463,Ky .Stats., giving
a mechanic's lien on real estate for the cost of work done
" by contract with or by the written consent of the owner,"
one who is in possessionof land under a contract for its
purchase is " the owner." Waddy Creamery Co. v. Davis-
Rankin Co., 103 Ky . 579, 45 S. W. 895, 20 R. 259.
Possession. The possessionof personalpropertyis prima
facie, though not conclusive, evidence of ownership. Bracken
v. Com ., 1 Ky . Opin . 22.
A factor or commission merchant is one who has the actual
or technicalpossessionof the goods or wares of another. The
possessionof the property is prima facie evidence of owner-
ship, and being thus in possessionfor the purpose of salea
factor may sellit in his own name and collectthe proceeds
without disclosingthe name of the principal. J. M. Robin-
son, Norton Co. v . Corsicana Cotton Factory, 124 Ky . 435,
99 S. W. 305, 30 R. 580, 8 L. R. A. (N.S.) 474. See, also,
POSSESSION .
OWNERSHIP .- " Ownership of land in thisState, so far
as we are advised, can only be obtained in two ways- one, by
what is calleda paper titletracingback to the Commonwealth ,
and the other, by adverse possession." Frazier v . Ison, 161
Ky .382,170 S. W. 977.
Ownership of land in thisState can only be obtained in two
ways , (1 ) by what is calleda paper title, and (2 ) by adverse
possession. Whitley County Land Co. v. Powers' Heirs, 146
Ky . 801, 144 S. W. 2.

OWNERSHIP OWNERSHIP2465
One who owns land owns it by an absoluteright from the
centerusque ad coelum. Langhorne v. Turman , 141 Ky . 809,
133 S. W. 1008, 34 L. R. A. (N.S.) 211.
Where in an action to recover damages for the cutting and
removal of timber from a certaintract of land, the defendant
denies the plaintiff'sownership of the land and assertsowner-
ship in himself, admitting the acts alleged as constitutingthe
trespass, the real issue is the question of ownership of the
land. Benge v . Martin , 159 Ky . 302, 166 S. W. 1003.
Where partiesare in controversyover the ownership of land,
and all claim titlethrough a common vendor, it is not neces-
sary to consider the chain of title, further back, than to the
common stem, in determining the ownership. I. C. R. Co. v .
Taylor, 164 Ky . 150, 175 S. W. 26.
In an action for destroying a railroadcrossingan averment
by plaintiffsthat a farm is theirsis equivalentto saying that
they own the land. Zook v . I. C. R. Co., 80 S. W. 211, 25
R. 2194 .
Where there is a conflictas to ownership of land, proof by
one party that he and his ancestorslistedthe property for
taxation and paid taxes on it, and the other party did not
listitor pay taxespriorto a certaindate, may be admitted,
as thisis an act of claim of ownership, to be given such weight
as the jury may determine. Walling v . Eggers , 78 S. W. 428,
25 R. 1563 .
An indictment for breaking into the warehouse of an ice
company is supported by evidence that defendant broke into
an apartment in the warehouse which was leased from the ice
company by a brewing company . Com . v. Ballard, 38 S. W.
678,18 R. 782 .
In an indictment for robbery the statement of the owner-
ship is only a part of the descriptionof the stolen property;
and if it is otherwise identified, the failureto charge in the
indictment the ownership would not be material under Ky .
